topics.title: Basics of Energy Transformation: Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Energy Transformation: Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔋 Module 1 — Basics of Energy Transformation: Transducers

## 1.1 What is a Transducer? (Formal Definition)

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCPT205)**, a **Transducer** is broadly defined as a device that converts energy from one physical domain (form) into another physical domain. It is the fundamental energy-conversion element upon which the entire field of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** is built, because a CPS cannot perceive or manipulate the physical world without transducers acting as the "bi-lingual translators" between the non-electrical real world and the electrical computational world.

In the most general engineering sense, a transducer is defined as:

> **Transducer** — *A device that transforms a physical quantity (the measurand) from one energy domain into an equivalent quantity in another energy domain, normally with a well-defined, repeatable, and ideally linear mathematical relationship between the input stimulus and the output response.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The course treats a *transducer* as the **superset** term. A **sensor** is a *transducer whose output is electrical and whose function is the detection/measurement* of a physical stimulus. An **actuator** is *a transducer whose input is electrical and whose function is to produce a physical action/force/motion*. Hence:
> $$\text{Transducer} = \text{Sensor} \cup \text{Actuator}$$
> Every sensor and every actuator is a transducer, but the converse is not always true.

## 1.2 The Six Fundamental Energy Domains

A transducer always operates *between* two energy domains. The KTU syllabus recognises **six** canonical physical energy domains that form the alphabet of energy transformation:

1. **Mechanical** — force, pressure, displacement, velocity, acceleration, torque.
2. **Thermal** — temperature, heat flux.
3. **Electrical** — voltage, current, charge, resistance, capacitance, inductance.
4. **Magnetic** — magnetic field, flux density.
5. **Chemical** — concentration, pH, humidity, gas composition.
6. **Optical (Radiant)** — light intensity, wavelength, refractive index.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Translator" 🗣️

Imagine a **United Nations interpreter booth** at an international summit. The Russian delegate speaks Russian, the Japanese delegate speaks Japanese, the Brazilian delegate speaks Portuguese. None of them understands the others. The interpreter sitting in the booth **listens to one language** and **immediately speaks out another**, with the constraint that the **meaning must be preserved** (a phrase about trade must come out as a phrase about trade, not a phrase about weather).

A transducer is exactly that interpreter:

| Interpreting Booth | Transducer |
|---|---|
| Speaker 1 (e.g., Russian) | Input energy domain (e.g., Mechanical) |
| Microphone picks up sound | Sensing element |
| Interpreter processes meaning | Conversion mechanism (transduction principle) |
| Speaker 2 outputs (e.g., English) | Output energy domain (e.g., Electrical) |
| Faithful translation | Linear transfer function |
| Slight accent/mistakes | Static errors (hysteresis, non-linearity) |

Just as a bad interpreter distorts meaning, a poor transducer distorts the measurand. The quality of a transducer is judged by how *faithfully* (linearly, repeatably, and accurately) it performs the conversion.

## 1.4 Energy Transformation — The Governing Principle

The physical principle that makes a transducer work is the **Conservation of Energy** combined with the **Coupling of Energy Domains** through material properties. Every transducer exploits a constitutive relationship that couples two domains. For example:

- The **piezoelectric effect** couples **Mechanical stress** ↔ **Electrical charge** (domains: Mechanical ↔ Electrical).
- The **Seebeck effect** couples **Thermal gradient** → **Electrical voltage** (domains: Thermal → Electrical).
- The **photovoltaic effect** couples **Optical photons** → **Electrical current** (domains: Optical → Electrical).
- The **electromagnetic force** couples **Electrical current** → **Mechanical force** (domains: Electrical → Mechanical) — this is the heart of *actuators*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Measurand:** The *measurand* is the specific physical quantity that a transducer is designed to detect or measure (e.g., temperature, pressure, humidity). It is the *input* to the transducer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transducers (KTU-Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires mastery of the following classification tree. Each branch is described below with its transduction principle and a representative engineering example.

### 2.1.1 Based on the Direction of Energy Conversion

**(a) Direct Transducer (Sensing / Forward Path)**
Converts *non-electrical energy* into *electrical energy* (or any other measurable form). The classical definition used in instrumentation.

$$\text{Physical Stimulus (non-electrical)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrical Output}$$

*Examples:*
- **Thermocouple** — Thermal energy → Electrical voltage (Seebeck effect).
- **LVDT** — Mechanical displacement → Electrical voltage (mutual-inductance coupling).
- **Piezoelectric crystal** — Mechanical force → Electrical charge (piezoelectric effect).
- **Photodiode** — Optical light → Electrical current (photovoltaic / photoconductive effect).

**(b) Inverse Transducer (Actuating / Reverse Path)**
Converts *electrical energy* into *non-electrical (physical) energy*. These are the *actuators* of the cyber-physical world.

$$\text{Electrical Input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Physical Output (force, motion, heat, light)}$$

*Examples:*
- **DC Motor / Solenoid** — Electrical current → Mechanical rotation / linear force.
- **Heater coil** — Electrical current → Thermal energy (Joule heating, $P = I^2 R$).
- **LED** — Electrical current → Optical photons (electroluminescence).
- **Piezo buzzer** — Electrical voltage → Mechanical vibration (inverse piezoelectric effect).

### 2.1.2 Based on Energy Source Requirement

**(a) Active Transducer (Self-Generating / Energy-Converter)**
- **Definition:** A transducer that *generates its own electrical output signal* directly from the measurand, **without needing any external auxiliary power supply**.
- **Working principle:** The measurand itself is the energy source for the output.
- **Examples:** Tachogenerator (mechanical → electrical), Thermocouple (thermal → electrical), Piezoelectric pickup (mechanical → electrical), Photovoltaic cell (optical → electrical).
- **KTU keyword:** *"Energy conversion device."*

**(b) Passive Transducer (Energy-Modulating)**
- **Definition:** A transducer whose output energy is *derived from an external auxiliary power source*; the measurand only *modulates* (changes) a circuit parameter such as resistance, capacitance, inductance, or reluctance.
- **Working principle:** The measurand cannot generate electricity on its own; it merely changes an electrical property.
- **Examples:** RTD / Thermistor (temperature modulates resistance), Strain gauge (strain modulates resistance), LVDT (displacement modulates mutual inductance), Capacitive pressure sensor (pressure modulates capacitance).
- **KTU keyword:** *"Energy controlling device."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Active transducer ⇒ Self-generating ⇒ No external power needed.**
> - **Passive transducer ⇒ Externally excited ⇒ Needs a separate power source / bridge circuit.**

### 2.1.3 Based on Output Signal Type

- **Analog Transducer** — Output is a continuous function of the measurand (e.g., thermocouple voltage, RTD resistance).
- **Digital Transducer** — Output is in discrete steps / digital code (e.g., optical encoder, digital flow meter).

### 2.1.4 Based on the Physical Principle Used

- **Resistive** — resistance change (RTD, strain gauge, LDR, potentiometer).
- **Capacitive** — capacitance change (capacitive displacement sensor, capacitive humidity sensor).
- **Inductive** — inductance / mutual-inductance change (LVDT, eddy-current probe).
- **Piezoelectric** — charge generation (force / pressure sensor).
- **Semiconductor (P-N junction)** — photodiode, Hall sensor.
- **Thermoelectric** — Seebeck / Peltier effect.
- **Electrochemical** — pH probe, gas sensor.

### 2.1.5 Primary vs Secondary Transducer

- **Primary Transducer** — the *sensing element* that makes first contact with the measurand and converts it into a more convenient mechanical or intermediate form (e.g., the Bourdon tube in a pressure gauge).
- **Secondary Transducer** — takes the output of the primary transducer and converts it into a usable electrical signal (e.g., the strain gauge bonded to the Bourdon tube).

## 2.2 The Transducer as a Two-Port Energy System

A transducer is universally modelled as a **two-port energy-coupling element**:

| Port | Quantity | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Input port | Measurand (physical quantity) | $x_i$ |
| Output port | Electrical signal (or vice versa) | $y_o$ |
| Transfer behaviour | Mathematical mapping | $y_o = f(x_i)$ |

For an *ideal* transducer, this mapping is linear:

$$y_o = K \cdot x_i$$

where $K$ is the **Sensitivity (or Scale Factor / Transfer Coefficient)** of the transducer.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **every** equation, definition and unit you are expected to recall in the KTU University Examination for this topic. Master this table.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units (SI) | Notes for KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transfer function (static) | $y = f(x)$ | — | Defines the input–output relationship. |
| 2 | Sensitivity (Scale Factor) | $K = \dfrac{dy}{dx} \;\Big\vert_{x=x_0}$ | $\dfrac{\text{output unit}}{\text{input unit}}$ | Slope of the calibration curve at the operating point. |
| 3 | Span (Full-scale input range) | $\text{Span} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | input unit | The operating input range. |
| 4 | Dynamic range | $\dfrac{x_{\max}}{x_{\min}}$ | dimensionless (dB) | Ratio of largest to smallest detectable input. |
| 5 | Linearity error (Non-linearity) | $\varepsilon_L = \dfrac{\vert y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{ideal line}} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | % of FS | Best-fit straight line deviation. |
| 6 | Hysteresis error | $\varepsilon_H = \dfrac{\vert y_{\text{increasing}} - y_{\text{decreasing}} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | % of FS | Maximum separation between up and down strokes. |
| 7 | Repeatability / Precision | $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{cycles}}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | % of FS | Inability to reproduce the same output for the same input. |
| 8 | Dead zone / Threshold | Smallest input that produces a measurable output. | input unit | The "blind" input region. |
| 9 | Resolution | Smallest *discernible* change in input. | input unit | Step size of an ADC, or the smallest physically detectable change. |
| 10 | Accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{\vert y_{\text{measured}} - y_{\text{true}} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | % of FS | Inclusively accounts for linearity, hysteresis, repeatability. |
| 11 | Drift (Zero / Sensitivity) | Slow change in output with time / temperature when input is held constant. | output unit / time | Two kinds: *zero drift* and *sensitivity drift*. |
| 12 | Active transducer output power | $P_{\text{out}} \le P_{\text{measurand}}$ | W | Restricted by energy conservation. |
| 13 | Thermocouple voltage | $V = S_{AB}(T) \cdot \Delta T$ | V | $S_{AB}$ is the Seebeck coefficient (V/°C). |
| 14 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{ij} \cdot F$ | C | $d_{ij}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N). |
| 15 | Resistance change in strain gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | $G$ = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain. |
| 16 | RTD resistance vs. temperature | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta T^2 + \dots\right]$ | $\Omega$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The above static characteristics determine the *fitness* of a transducer for a given CPS application. A transducer embedded in a medical ventilator, for example, requires ultra-high accuracy and negligible hysteresis, whereas a transducer in a toy robot can tolerate much higher non-linearity. KTU questions often test your ability to *select* the right static characteristic for a given scenario.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

The KTU University Examination regularly tests the analytical reasoning behind transducer behaviour. The following derivations are the most frequently required.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linearised Transfer Function (Sensitivity Equation)

**Statement to Prove:** *For a transducer operating over a small range near an operating point $x_0$, the static response $y(x)$ can be approximated by a linear function whose slope is the sensitivity $K$.*

### Step 1 — General Non-Linear Response

Every real transducer exhibits a non-linear relationship between the measurand $x$ and the electrical output $y$:

$$y(x) = f(x)$$

### Step 2 — Taylor Series Expansion about the Operating Point

To linearise, we expand $f(x)$ as a Taylor series around the chosen operating (quiescent) point $x = x_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(x) &= f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0) + \frac{1}{2!}\left.\frac{d^2 f}{dx^2}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0)^2 + \frac{1}{3!}\left.\frac{d^3 f}{dx^3}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0)^3 + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Operating-Point Output

Let $y_0 = f(x_0)$ denote the output when the input equals the operating-point value. Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(x) &= y_0 + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0) + \frac{1}{2!}\left.\frac{d^2 f}{dx^2}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0)^2 + \frac{1}{3!}\left.\frac{d^3 f}{dx^3}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0)^3 + \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Small-Signal Approximation

When $(x - x_0) \ll 1$, the higher-order terms $(x - x_0)^2$, $(x - x_0)^3$, $\dots$ become negligibly small. Dropping all second- and higher-order terms gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(x) &\approx y_0 + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x_0}\,(x - x_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Sensitivity

By definition, the **sensitivity** is the slope of the response at the operating point:

$$K = \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x_0}$$

### Step 6 — Final Linearised Equation

Substituting $K$:

$$
\boxed{\,y(x) = y_0 + K\,(x - x_0)\,}
$$

> This is the **canonical static transfer function** of a linearised transducer. It is the equation on which every KTU problem on sensitivity, span, and static error rests.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Sensitivity from a Calibration Curve

**Problem (KTU pattern):** A thermocouple has the following calibration readings:

| Temperature $T$ (°C) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $V$ (mV) | 0.00 | 2.05 | 4.10 | 6.12 | 8.20 |

*Find the sensitivity and the output at $T = 75^\circ\text{C}$.*

### Step 1 — Compute the Slope (Sensitivity)

Because the data is nearly linear, use the full-scale endpoints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} = \frac{V_{200} - V_{0}}{T_{200} - T_{0}} \\
  &= \frac{8.20 - 0.00}{200 - 0} \\
  &= 0.041 \;\text{mV/°C} \\
  &= 41 \;\mu\text{V/°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Output at $T = 75^\circ\text{C}$

Using the linearised transfer function with $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(75) &= V_0 + K \cdot (T - T_0) \\
      &= 0 + (0.041\;\text{mV/°C}) \times (75 - 0)\;\text{°C} \\
      &= 3.075\;\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Non-Linearity Check

The reading at $T = 150$ °C is **6.12 mV**, but a perfectly linear sensor would give $0.041 \times 150 = 6.15$ mV. The deviation is $\vert 6.12 - 6.15 \vert = 0.03$ mV, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_L &= \frac{\vert V_{\text{actual}} - V_{\text{ideal}} \vert}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\% \\
              &= \frac{0.03}{8.20} \times 100\% \\
              &\approx 0.366\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *non-linearity* error of about $0.37\%$ of full scale.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Total Static Error Composition

**Problem:** A pressure transducer has full-scale output $V_{\text{FS}} = 10$ V. It exhibits a linearity error of $\pm 0.05$ V, a hysteresis error of $\pm 0.03$ V, and a repeatability error (standard deviation over 5 cycles) of $0.02$ V. Compute the *worst-case total static error* and the *root-sum-square (RSS)* estimate.

### Step 1 — Convert Each Error to a Percentage of Full Scale

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_L &= \frac{0.05}{10} \times 100\% = 0.50\% \\
\varepsilon_H &= \frac{0.03}{10} \times 100\% = 0.30\% \\
\varepsilon_R &= \frac{0.02}{10} \times 100\% = 0.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Worst-Case (Arithmetic Sum)

Used for *safety-critical* CPS applications (e.g., medical, aerospace):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{worst}} &= \varepsilon_L + \varepsilon_H + \varepsilon_R \\
                           &= 0.50\% + 0.30\% + 0.20\% \\
                           &= 1.00\% \;\text{of FS}
\end{aligned}
$$

In voltage terms: $\Delta V = 0.01 \times 10 = 0.10$ V.

### Step 3 — Root-Sum-Square (Statistical / RSS)

Used for *typical* estimates, assuming the errors are statistically independent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{RSS}} &= \sqrt{\varepsilon_L^{\,2} + \varepsilon_H^{\,2} + \varepsilon_R^{\,2}} \\
                         &= \sqrt{(0.50)^2 + (0.30)^2 + (0.20)^2} \\
                         &= \sqrt{0.25 + 0.09 + 0.04} \\
                         &= \sqrt{0.38} \\
                         &= 0.616\% \;\text{of FS}
\end{aligned}
$$

In voltage terms: $\Delta V \approx 0.00616 \times 10 \approx 0.0616$ V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The RSS estimate (0.616 %) is smaller than the worst-case (1.00 %). In *safety-critical* cyber-physical systems, the worst-case sum is the appropriate metric; in *consumer-grade* applications, the RSS estimate is acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Transducer Principles to Energy Domains

The matrix below maps each major transduction principle to (i) its input energy domain, (ii) its output energy domain, (iii) the governing physical effect, and (iv) a typical KTU-grade example. This is the *spine* of the topic.

| Transduction Principle | Input Domain | Output Domain | Governing Physical Effect | Representative Device |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric | Mechanical | Electrical | Charge generation under stress | Quartz force sensor |
| Inverse Piezoelectric | Electrical | Mechanical | Strain under applied voltage | Piezo buzzer / injector |
| Thermoelectric (Seebeck) | Thermal | Electrical | Junction EMF ∝ ΔT | Thermocouple (Type-K) |
| Peltier | Electrical | Thermal | Heat absorption/emission at junction | Thermoelectric cooler |
| Photovoltaic | Optical | Electrical | Photon-induced e⁻–h⁺ pair | Solar cell, photodiode |
| Electroluminescent | Electrical | Optical | Recombination radiation | LED, laser diode |
| Electromagnetic Induction | Mechanical | Electrical | $e = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | LVDT, Tachogenerator |
| Electromagnetic Force | Electrical | Mechanical | $F = BIL$ | DC motor, voice coil |
| Piezoresistive | Mechanical | Electrical | $\Delta R / R = G \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge |
| Capacitive | Mechanical | Electrical | $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$ | Capacitive pressure sensor |
| Inductive | Mechanical | Electrical | $L = N^2 \mu A / l$ | LVDT, eddy-current probe |
| Hall Effect | Magnetic | Electrical | $V_H = \dfrac{IB}{qn t}$ | Hall sensor, current probe |
| Chemisorption | Chemical | Electrical | Surface resistance change | Metal-oxide gas sensor |
| Electrochemical | Chemical | Electrical | Nernst potential | pH probe, lambda sensor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Transducer as a Two-Port Energy Transformer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["PHYSICAL WORLD\nMEASURAND SOURCE\nMechanical / Thermal /\nOptical / Chemical /\nMagnetic Input"]
    pIn["Input Port\nPhysical Quantity x"]
    tBlock["TRANSDUCER\nEnergy-Conversion\nMechanism\nf x → y"]
    pOut["Output Port\nElectrical Quantity y"]
    load["ELECTRICAL WORLD\nSignal Conditioner /\nADC / Controller /\nActuator Driver"]
    fb["Feedback Path\nOptional CPS Loop"]

    src -->|Stimulus| pIn
    pIn --> tBlock
    tBlock -->|Converted Signal| pOut
    pOut --> load
    load -.->|Actuation / Control Action| fb
    fb -.-> src
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The *physical measurand* enters from the left, is converted by the transducer into an electrical signal in the middle, and is consumed by the electrical world (signal conditioner, ADC, controller) on the right. The dashed feedback loop is the hallmark of a *closed-loop* cyber-physical system.

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Transducers (KTU-Syllabus Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["TRANSDUCER\nEnergy Conversion Device"]
    dirCls{"Based on\nDirection of\nConversion"}
    typeCls{"Based on\nPower Source"}
    outCls{"Based on\nOutput Form"}

    root --> dirCls
    dirCls -->|Non-Electrical to Electrical| direct["DIRECT\nTransducer\n(Sensor)"]
    dirCls -->|Electrical to Non-Electrical| inverse["INVERSE\nTransducer\n(Actuator)"]

    direct --> ex1["Example\nThermocouple\nLVDT\nPhotodiode"]
    inverse --> ex2["Example\nDC Motor\nSolenoid\nHeater Coil"]

    root --> typeCls
    typeCls -->|Self Generating\nNo External Power| active["ACTIVE\nTransducer"]
    typeCls -->|Needs Auxiliary\nPower Source| passive["PASSIVE\nTransducer"]

    active --> ex3["Example\nPiezo Pickup\nTachogenerator\nPV Cell"]
    passive --> ex4["Example\nRTD / Thermistor\nStrain Gauge\nLVDT"]

    root --> outCls
    outCls -->|Continuous Output| analog["ANALOG\nTransducer"]
    outCls -->|Discrete Steps| digital["DIGITAL\nTransducer"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The transducer universe is split along three independent axes — *direction*, *power source*, and *output form* — each of which the KTU examiner uses to test classification skills.

## 4.3 Energy-Domain Coupling Map (Six Domains × Six Domains)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mech["MECHANICAL\nForce, Pressure\nDisplacement, Velocity"]
    therm["THERMAL\nTemperature\nHeat Flux"]
    elec["ELECTRICAL\nVoltage, Current\nResistance"]
    magn["MAGNETIC\nField B\nFlux Phi"]
    chem["CHEMICAL\nConcentration\npH, Humidity"]
    opt["OPTICAL\nIntensity\nWavelength"]

    mech <-->|Piezoelectric| elec
    therm <-->|Seebeck / Peltier| elec
    opt <-->|Photovoltaic / LED| elec
    magn <-->|EM Induction / Force| elec
    chem <-->|Electrochemical| elec
    mech <-->|Capacitive| elec
    mech <-->|Inductive| elec
    mech <-->|Piezoresistive| elec
    therm -->|Radiation| opt
    magn -->|Faraday Rotation| opt
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that *all* real-world transducers ultimately couple their specific non-electrical domain to the **electrical** domain — that is why *electricity* is the lingua franca of cyber-physical systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> The following questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern for *PCCPT205 – Sensors and Actuators (Cyber Physical)*, Module 1. They include the canonical *Part A* (3 marks) and *Part B* (14 marks with internal choice) formats.

---

## 📘 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]`
**Define the term *transducer*. With the help of a single block diagram, distinguish clearly between a *sensor* and an *actuator* within the transducer family. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **transducer** is a device that converts energy from one physical form into another. The two most important sub-classes of transducers used in cyber-physical systems are **sensors** and **actuators**:

| Property | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Detects / measures a physical quantity | Produces a physical action |
| Energy flow | Non-electrical → Electrical | Electrical → Non-electrical |
| Direction | *Direct* transducer | *Inverse* transducer |
| Example | Thermocouple, LVDT, Strain gauge | DC motor, solenoid, heater |
| Role in CPS | *Senses* the physical world | *Acts* upon the physical world |

A simplified block diagram is:

```
  [Physical Stimulus] --(SENSOR)--> [Electrical Signal] --(ADC)--> [Controller]
                                                                          |
                                                                          v
  [Physical Action]   <--(ACTUATOR)-- [Electrical Command] <----------(DAC)
```

(3 marks: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the direction difference, 1 mark for the example/diagram.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`
**Differentiate between *active* and *passive* transducers. Give one example of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| External power source | **Not required** (self-generating) | **Required** (externally excited) |
| Working principle | Energy *conversion* from measurand | Energy *modulation* of an external source |
| Output | Directly generated by the measurand | Derived from the auxiliary supply, controlled by the measurand |
| Circuit complexity | Simple, output measured directly | Requires bridge / excitation circuit |
| Example | Thermocouple (thermal → electrical) | Strain gauge (resistance change needs a Wheatstone bridge) |
| Example | Piezoelectric pickup | RTD (resistance change needs excitation) |

(3 marks: 2 marks for the tabulated difference, 1 mark for the examples.)

---

## 📕 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`
**"(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the working principle of any **two** active transducers. List the energy domains involved and the governing physical effect in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**"(b)** A transducer delivers an output of $4.20$ mV for an input of $140$ °C and $6.05$ mV for an input of $200$ °C. Assuming a linear response, determine:
    (i) the sensitivity of the transducer,
    (ii) the output at $T = 175$ °C,
    (iii) the non-linearity error if the actual output at $T = 175$ °C is $5.20$ mV and the full-scale output is $8.00$ mV. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A(a) — Two Active Transducers

**1. Thermocouple (Thermal → Electrical)**

A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metal wires (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for a Type-K thermocouple) joined at one end (the *hot* or *measuring* junction). The other ends are kept at a reference temperature (the *cold* or *reference* junction).

When the hot junction is heated:
- Charge carriers in metal A have a higher diffusion rate than those in metal B, producing a contact potential at the hot junction.
- A net EMF $V$ is generated that is a function of the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{ref}}$.
- The governing physical effect is the **Seebeck effect**.
- The output voltage is given by:
$$V = S_{AB}\,\Delta T$$
where $S_{AB}$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient of the thermocouple pair (typically in the range $10$ – $70\;\mu\text{V/°C}$).

- **Input domain:** Thermal (temperature)
- **Output domain:** Electrical (voltage)
- **Energy flow:** Self-generating, no external power.

**2. Piezoelectric Pickup (Mechanical → Electrical)**

A piezoelectric crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT ceramic) is sandwiched between two metallic electrodes. When a mechanical force $F$ is applied:

- The crystal lattice deforms, separating the centres of positive and negative charge, producing a net surface charge $Q$.
- The governing physical effect is the **direct piezoelectric effect**.
- The relationship is:
$$Q = d_{ij} \cdot F$$
where $d_{ij}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (units: C/N).
- The output voltage is $V = Q / C_p$, where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the crystal-plus-electrode system.

- **Input domain:** Mechanical (force / pressure / vibration)
- **Output domain:** Electrical (charge / voltage)
- **Energy flow:** Self-generating, no external power.

(7 marks: 2 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for the working principle, 2 marks for the energy domain identification, 1 mark for the example detail.)

---

#### Solution to Q-A(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** Two calibration points — $(T_1, V_1) = (140, 4.20)$ mV and $(T_2, V_2) = (200, 6.05)$ mV. Full-scale output $V_{\text{FS}} = 8.00$ mV. Actual output at $175$ °C is $5.20$ mV.

**(i) Sensitivity $K$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{V_2 - V_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{6.05 - 4.20}{200 - 140} = \frac{1.85}{60} \\
  &\approx 0.03083 \;\text{mV/°C} \\
  &\approx 30.83\;\mu\text{V/°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Output at $T = 175$ °C (ideal linear)**

The linearised transfer function is $V = V_1 + K(T - T_1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{175} &= 4.20 + 0.03083 \times (175 - 140) \\
        &= 4.20 + 0.03083 \times 35 \\
        &= 4.20 + 1.079 \\
        &\approx 5.28 \;\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Non-linearity Error**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_L &= \frac{\vert V_{\text{actual}} - V_{\text{ideal}} \vert}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\% \\
              &= \frac{\vert 5.20 - 5.28 \vert}{8.00} \times 100\% \\
              &= \frac{0.08}{8.00} \times 100\% \\
              &= 1.00\% \;\text{of FS}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for sensitivity, 2 marks for the linearised output, 2 marks for the non-linearity computation, 1 mark for the final percentage form.)

---

### 🔹 Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`
**"(a)** Define **sensitivity, hysteresis, repeatability, and drift** as applied to transducers. For each, write its formula and SI / practical unit, and state one engineering situation where that static characteristic dominates the design choice. **(7 Marks)**

**"(b)** A thermistor has a resistance–temperature characteristic given by the Steinhart–Hart equation:
$$\frac{1}{T} = a + b\,\ln R + c\,(\ln R)^3$$
where $T$ is in Kelvin, $R$ in ohms, and $a$, $b$, $c$ are device constants. For a particular thermistor, $a = 1.40 \times 10^{-3}$, $b = 2.55 \times 10^{-4}$, $c = 1.05 \times 10^{-7}$. Determine:
    (i) the temperature $T$ in °C when $R = 10\;\text{k}\Omega$,
    (ii) the sensitivity $\dfrac{dT}{dR}$ at this operating point.
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-B(a) — Static Characteristics Definitions

| # | Characteristic | Definition | Formula | Unit | Situation Where it Dominates |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | The slope of the calibration curve at a given operating point — i.e., the change in output per unit change in input. | $K = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\vert_{x_0}$ | $\dfrac{\text{output unit}}{\text{input unit}}$ | A medical ECG sensor must have *high sensitivity* to detect micro-volt cardiac signals. |
| 2 | **Hysteresis** | The maximum difference in output for the same input, depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. | $\varepsilon_H = \dfrac{\vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | % of FS | A pressure transducer used in a *prosthetic limb controller* must have low hysteresis to avoid drift in grip force. |
| 3 | **Repeatability** | The ability of a transducer to produce the same output for the same input, measured over multiple cycles under identical conditions. | $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{cycles}}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\%$ | % of FS | A *flow meter* in a chemical plant must have high repeatability for batch-to-batch consistency. |
| 4 | **Drift** | The slow, unwanted change in output with time or ambient conditions, while the input is held constant. | Drift $= \dfrac{y(t_2) - y(t_1)}{t_2 - t_1}$ | $\dfrac{\text{output unit}}{\text{time}}$ | An *industrial weighing scale* exposed to ambient temperature swings suffers from sensitivity drift and must be re-calibrated. |

(7 marks: 4 marks for the four definitions + formulas, 2 marks for the units, 1 mark for the engineering situations.)

---

#### Solution to Q-B(b) — Steinhart–Hart Thermistor

**Given:** $a = 1.40 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$, $b = 2.55 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K}^{-1}$, $c = 1.05 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{K}^{-1}$, and $R = 10\;\text{k}\Omega = 10^4\;\Omega$.

**(i) Temperature at $R = 10\;\text{k}\Omega$**

Compute $\ln R = \ln(10^4) = 4 \ln 10 \approx 4 \times 2.302585 = 9.21034$.

Compute $(\ln R)^3 = (9.21034)^3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(9.21034)^2 &= 84.830 \\
(9.21034)^3 &= 84.830 \times 9.21034 \approx 781.33
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute into the Steinhart–Hart equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{T} &= a + b\,\ln R + c\,(\ln R)^3 \\
            &= 1.40 \times 10^{-3} + (2.55 \times 10^{-4}) \times 9.21034 + (1.05 \times 10^{-7}) \times 781.33 \\
            &= 1.40 \times 10^{-3} + 2.3486 \times 10^{-3} + 8.204 \times 10^{-5} \\
            &= (1.400 + 2.3486 + 0.0820) \times 10^{-3} \\
            &= 3.8306 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{3.8306 \times 10^{-3}} \;\text{K} \\
  &\approx 261.06\;\text{K} \\
  &\approx 261.06 - 273.15 \\
  &\approx -12.09\;^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Sensitivity $\dfrac{dT}{dR}$**

Differentiate the Steinhart–Hart equation implicitly with respect to $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{1}{T^2}\,\frac{dT}{dR} &= b \cdot \frac{1}{R} + 3c\,(\ln R)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{R} \\
                              &= \frac{1}{R}\left[b + 3c\,(\ln R)^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dT}{dR} &= -R\,T^2\left[b + 3c\,(\ln R)^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(\ln R)^2 = (9.21034)^2 = 84.830$.

Compute the bracketed term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b + 3c\,(\ln R)^2 &= 2.55 \times 10^{-4} + 3 \times (1.05 \times 10^{-7}) \times 84.830 \\
                  &= 2.55 \times 10^{-4} + 2.674 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-1}\,? \text{ (recheck)} \\
                  &= 2.55 \times 10^{-4} + 2.674 \times 10^{-5} \\
                  &= 2.817 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Corrected arithmetic:*
$3 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-7} \times 84.830 = 2.674 \times 10^{-5}$ (dimensionless since $c$ has units of $\text{K}^{-1}$ — handled by convention).

$$
\begin{aligned}
b + 3c(\ln R)^2 &= 2.55 \times 10^{-4} + 2.674 \times 10^{-5} \\
                &= 2.817 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dT}{dR} &= -R\,T^2\,\left[b + 3c(\ln R)^2\right] \\
              &= -10^4 \times (261.06)^2 \times 2.817 \times 10^{-4} \\
              &= -10^4 \times 68\,152.4 \times 2.817 \times 10^{-4} \\
              &\approx -10^4 \times 19.197 \\
              &\approx -1.92 \times 10^5 \;\text{K/}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently, the *magnitude* of the sensitivity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\vert \frac{dT}{dR} \right\vert &\approx 1.92 \times 10^5 \;\text{K/}\Omega \\
                                     &\approx 192 \;\text{K/k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates the **inverse** relationship — as $R$ increases, $T$ decreases, which is the well-known NTC (negative temperature coefficient) behaviour of a thermistor.

(7 marks: 3 marks for the temperature computation, 2 marks for the implicit differentiation, 2 marks for the numerical evaluation of $dT/dR$.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on this Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing *transducer* with *sensor*.** Many answers use the two terms interchangeably. A *sensor* is a *sub-class* of transducer (specifically, a *direct* transducer that produces an electrical output). A *transducer* also includes *actuators* (inverse transducers). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this misuse.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the external power distinction.** In *active vs passive* questions, students frequently say "active transducers don't need an external power supply" but forget to add the *second* half of the statement — that the measurand itself is the energy source. A complete answer is worth **2 marks**; a half-answer is worth **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Mixing up *accuracy* and *precision* (repeatability).** Accuracy is closeness to the *true* value. Precision/Repeatability is closeness *among repeated measurements* of the same value. They are *not* the same.
>
> 4. **Skipping the units in numerical answers.** Every computed quantity (sensitivity, error percentage) must be written with its unit. KTU examiners frequently dock **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
>
> 5. **Linearising without stating the assumption.** In derivations, you must *explicitly* state that the higher-order Taylor terms are dropped under the small-signal assumption. A derivation that jumps to $y = y_0 + K(x - x_0)$ without justification loses **1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Using *hysteresis* and *dead zone* interchangeably.** Dead zone is a *zero-output* region near zero input. Hysteresis is a *loop* in the calibration curve. They are distinct static characteristics.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam!)**

- A **transducer** is a device that converts energy from one physical domain to another. It is the **superset** of sensors and actuators.
- A **sensor** is a *direct* transducer (non-electrical → electrical). An **actuator** is an *inverse* transducer (electrical → non-electrical).
- The **six canonical energy domains** are: mechanical, thermal, electrical, magnetic, chemical, and optical (radiant).
- **Active transducers** are *self-generating* and need **no** external power (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric pickup, photovoltaic cell, tachogenerator).
- **Passive transducers** need an **external** power source and *modulate* a circuit parameter (e.g., RTD, thermistor, strain gauge, LVDT, capacitive sensor).
- The **linearised static transfer function** is $y = y_0 + K(x - x_0)$, derived from the **Taylor series** of $f(x)$ about the operating point, dropping second-and-higher-order terms.
- **Sensitivity $K$** is the slope of the calibration curve at the operating point: $K = dy/dx$ at $x = x_0$.
- **Static characteristics** to memorise: accuracy, precision/repeatability, resolution, span, range, linearity/non-linearity, hysteresis, dead zone, drift (zero & sensitivity), threshold.
- **Dynamic characteristics** (preview for Module 2): first-order and second-order time response, rise time, settling time, time constant, natural frequency, damping ratio.
- **Typical exam formulae** to write on the first page of your answer sheet: $V = S_{AB} \Delta T$ (thermocouple), $Q = d F$ (piezo), $\Delta R / R = G \varepsilon$ (strain gauge), $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ (RTD), $y = y_0 + K(x - x_0)$ (generic linearised).
- **Pitfall to avoid:** always state the **SI unit** of every computed quantity; always state the **assumptions** (small-signal, linear, isothermal, etc.); always distinguish between **active vs passive** using the *energy source* criterion, not the *direction* criterion.

> 📌 **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When answering a "define and classify" question, structure your response as: **Definition → Classification Tree → One Example Per Class → Energy-Domain Mapping → Governing Physical Effect**. This is the template valued by the KTU Board Examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL) > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Sensor? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > A **sensor** is a physical device that detects, measures, and responds to a physical stimulus (such as temperature, light, pressure, motion, or chemical composition) and converts it into an electrical signal that can be read by an observer or by an electronic instrument. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, sensors form the **"sensing layer"** of the system. They are the interface between the physical world and the digital computation engine. Without sensors, a CPS is essentially "blind" — it cannot perceive the environment it is supposed to monitor or control. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the human body. Your **eyes, ears, skin, and nose** are biological sensors. They detect light, sound, pressure, and chemicals, and convert them into nerve impulses (electrical signals) that your brain processes. An engineering sensor plays exactly the same role for a machine. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A sensor does NOT create energy out of nothing. It *transduces* (converts) one form of energy into another — typically into a measurable electrical quantity (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency). ## 1.2 What is an Actuator? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > An **actuator** is a device that takes an electrical signal from a controller and converts it into a physical action — such as motion, heat, light, or force — thereby manipulating its environment in a controlled manner. Actuators form the **"actuation layer"** of a CPS. They are the *muscles* of the system — they take commands from the digital brain and produce real-world effect. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Continuing the human body analogy: if sensors are the senses, **actuators are the muscles and limbs**. When your brain decides to pick up a cup, it sends nerve signals to your arm muscles (actuators) which produce the physical motion. ## 1.3 Sensors vs. Actuators — The Fundamental Distinction | Aspect | Sensor | Actuator | |---|---|---| | **Direction of Energy Flow** | Physical world → Electrical signal | Electrical signal → Physical world | | **Role in CPS** | Perception (Input) | Action (Output) | | **Biological Analogy** | Eyes, ears, skin | Muscles, limbs | | **Example** | Thermistor (temperature) | DC Motor (rotation) | | **Output Type** | Measurable electrical quantity | Mechanical motion, heat, light | | **Power Level** | Typically low power | Typically high power | ## 1.4 Transduction — The Unifying Principle The single most important concept that ties together all sensors and actuators is **transduction**. > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** *Transduction* is the process of converting one form of energy into another. The element that performs this conversion is called a **transducer**. **Critical distinction for KTU exams:** - **All sensors are transducers** (they convert physical energy → electrical energy). - **All actuators are also transducers** (they convert electrical energy → physical energy). - The term *transducer* is the *superset*; *sensor* and *actuator* are *subsets* based on the direction of energy flow. ### Classification of Transducers 1. **Active Transducers** (Self-generating) - Do NOT require external power. - Generate their own electrical output signal directly from the physical stimulus. - Example: **Piezoelectric crystal**, **Thermocouple**, **Photovoltaic cell**. - Operating principle: based on direct energy conversion (e.g., Faraday's law, Seebeck effect, piezoelectric effect). 2. **Passive Transducers** (Externally powered) - REQUIRE an external excitation power source. - They modify the external signal in response to the stimulus. - Example: **Thermistor**, **Strain gauge**, **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)**. - The output is a *change* in a circuit parameter (R, L, C) which is then measured. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** In 14-mark questions, examiners frequently ask students to classify a given sensor/actuator as active or passive. Always remember — **piezoelectric sensors and thermocouples are ACTIVE; thermistors and strain gauges are PASSIVE.** ## 1.5 Sensor / Actuator in a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) In a CPS, sensors and actuators do not work in isolation. They are integrated into a feedback loop: ``` Physical World ⟷ Sensor → Controller/Computation → Actuator ⟷ Physical World ``` This is the same structure as a classical control system. The **sensor** measures a physical variable, the **controller** (often a microcontroller or processor) decides what to do, and the **actuator** executes the decision. This continuous loop is what gives a CPS its *cyber-physical* nature. ## 1.6 Standard Performance Metrics (Constants & Metrics) > [!NOTE] > These metrics are tested almost every KTU exam cycle. Memorize the definitions and units. - **Sensitivity (S):** Ratio of output change to input change. Unit: V/unit of stimulus (e.g., V/°C). - **Range:** The minimum and maximum input values for which the sensor works reliably. - **Accuracy:** How close the output is to the true value. Expressed as a percentage of full-scale reading. - **Precision:** The repeatability of measurements under identical conditions. - **Resolution:** The smallest detectable change in input. - **Linearity:** How closely the output follows a straight-line relationship with input (ideal sensor is perfectly linear). - **Hysteresis:** The difference in output for the same input depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. - **Dynamic Response:** Time constant, rise time, settling time, and bandwidth — all measured in **seconds (s)** or **Hertz (Hz)**. - **Drift:** Slow change in output over time when the input is constant. - **Dead Band / Dead Zone:** Range of input values that produce no change in output. - **Span (Full-Scale Output, FSO):** The difference between maximum and minimum output values. ## 1.7 Real-World Examples (Industry-Grade) - **Automotive:** Oxygen sensor in the exhaust manifold, ABS wheel-speed sensor, fuel injector (actuator). - **Healthcare:** Pulse oximeter sensor, insulin pump actuator. - **Industrial IoT:** Vibration sensor on a turbine, servo valve (actuator). - **Smart Home:** PIR motion sensor, smart thermostat's relay (actuator). - **Aerospace:** Pitot tube (pressure sensor), hydraulic actuator for flight control surfaces. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Modelling of a Sensor Every sensor can be represented mathematically as a function: $$ y = f(x) $$ Where: - $x$ = physical input stimulus (temperature, pressure, etc.) - $y$ = electrical output (voltage, current, resistance) - $f$ = the sensor's transfer function For an **ideal linear sensor**: $$ y = a \cdot x + b $$ Where: - $a$ = slope = **sensitivity** $S$ - $b$ = offset (output when input is zero) For a **non-ideal sensor**, additional terms appear: $$ y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + \ldots $$ The coefficients $a_2, a_3, \ldots$ represent **non-linearities** — the deviation from ideal linear behaviour. ## 2.2 Static Characteristics — The Complete Set ### 2.2.1 Sensitivity $$ S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx} $$ Units depend on input and output: V/°C, mV/kPa, A/lumen, etc. ### 2.2.2 Span (Full-Scale Output) $$ \text{Span} = y_{\max} - y_{\min} $$ ### 2.2.3 Linearity (Non-linearity Error) The maximum deviation of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage: $$ \%\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\% $$ Where $\Delta y_{\max}$ is the maximum deviation in output, and $y_{\text{FS}}$ is the full-scale output. ### 2.2.4 Hysteresis Error $$ \%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{\vert y_{x \uparrow} - y_{x \downarrow} \vert_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100\% $$ ### 2.2.5 Accuracy and Precision - **Accuracy** = $\dfrac{\vert \text{Measured} - \text{True} \vert}{\text{True}} \times 100\%$ - **Precision** = repeatability under identical conditions, often expressed as standard deviation. ## 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics When the input changes rapidly with time, the sensor's output cannot follow instantaneously. The dynamic behaviour is modelled using **differential equations**. ### 2.3.1 Zero-Order System $$ y(t) = K \cdot x(t) $$ A perfect, instantaneous sensor with no dynamics. Does not exist in practice, but is the ideal. ### 2.3.2 First-Order System (e.g., temperature sensor with thermal mass) $$ \tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t) $$ Where: - $\tau$ = time constant (seconds) - $K$ = static sensitivity For a step input $x(t) = X_u$ at $t = 0$: $$ y(t) = K X_u (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Viva/Exam Trap:** At $t = \tau$, the response reaches $63.2\%$ of its final value. At $t = 5\tau$, the response is $\approx 99.3\%$ of final value (practically settled). ### 2.3.3 Second-Order System (e.g., accelerometer with seismic mass) $$ \frac{1}{\omega_n^2} \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t) $$ Where: - $\omega_n$ = natural (undamped) frequency, **rad/s** - $\zeta$ = damping ratio (dimensionless) - $K$ = static sensitivity The step response depends on $\zeta$: - $\zeta = 0$: undamped oscillation - $0 < \zeta < 1$: underdamped (oscillatory) - $\zeta = 1$: critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory) - $\zeta > 1$: overdamped (slow, no oscillation) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | V/°C, mV/kPa | Slope of transfer curve | | 2 | Span (FSO) | $\text{Span} = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | V, mA | Full-scale output range | | 3 | Non-linearity error | $\%\text{NL} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | % | Static characteristic | | 4 | Hysteresis error | $\%\text{Hyst} = \frac{\max \vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | % | Static characteristic | | 5 | Accuracy | $\frac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100$ | % | $M$ = measured, $T$ = true | | 6 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K X_u (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Output units | $\tau$ = time constant | | 7 | 63.2% rule | $y(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot y_{\infty}$ | — | Memorize this value | | 8 | Settling time | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ to $5\tau$ | s | For first-order system | | 9 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | For second-order mechanical sensor | | 10 | Damped frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s | For underdamped response | | 11 | Resolution | $\Delta x_{\min} = \dfrac{\text{output resolution}}{S}$ | Input units | Smallest detectable change | | 12 | Repeatability | $\%\text{Rep} = \dfrac{\pm \sigma}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | % | $\sigma$ = std. deviation | > [!NOTE] > **Critical:** In the table above, the absolute-value bars use the word `vert` notation-style vertical bars (e.g., `vert y_up - y_down vert`). In your answer sheet, write them as standard $\vert \cdot \vert$. ## 2.5 Why These Metrics Matter in Engineering - **Safety-critical systems** (aircraft, medical devices): *Accuracy, reliability, and dynamic response* dominate. - **Process control** (chemical plants): *Linearity and repeatability* are critical for closed-loop control stability. - **Consumer electronics** (smartphones): *Size, cost, and power consumption* dominate over extreme accuracy. - **Industrial automation** (robotics): *Hysteresis and dead-band* critically affect positioning accuracy. ## 2.6 Sensor Calibration > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** *Calibration* is the process of comparing a sensor's output against a known standard and deriving correction factors. A typical calibration involves: 1. Apply **known inputs** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ (e.g., ice water for 0°C, boiling water for 100°C). 2. Record the sensor's **outputs** $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$. 3. Use **least-squares fitting** to find the best straight line $y = a + bx$. 4. Compute the residuals (deviations) and the **regression error**. The **least-squares estimate** of slope $a_1$ and intercept $a_0$ are: $$ a_1 = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} $$ $$ a_0 = \bar{y} - a_1 \bar{x} $$ Where $\bar{x}$ and $\bar{y}$ are the mean values of the input and output datasets. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Sensor Response to Step Input We start with the standard first-order differential equation that models a sensor with thermal mass, electrical inertia, or mechanical damping: $$ \tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t) $$ Assume a step input: $x(t) = X_u$ for $t \ge 0$ and $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. Also assume zero initial condition: $y(0) = 0$. **Step 1:** Substitute $x(t) = X_u$: $$ \tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K X_u $$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to standard form: $$ \frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau} y = \frac{K X_u}{\tau} $$ **Step 3:** This is a first-order linear ODE. The integrating factor is $e^{t/\tau}$: $$ \frac{d}{dt} \left( y \cdot e^{t/\tau} \right) = \frac{K X_u}{\tau} e^{t/\tau} $$ **Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$: $$ y(t) e^{t/\tau} - y(0) \cdot 1 = \frac{K X_u}{\tau} \int_0^t e^{s/\tau} \, ds $$ **Step 5:** Since $y(0) = 0$: $$ y(t) e^{t/\tau} = \frac{K X_u}{\tau} \cdot \tau \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right) $$ **Step 6:** Simplify the right-hand side: $$ y(t) e^{t/\tau} = K X_u \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right) $$ **Step 7:** Divide both sides by $e^{t/\tau}$: $$ y(t) = K X_u \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ This is the complete step-response of a first-order sensor. The final (steady-state) value is: $$ y(\infty) = K X_u $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Time to Reach 63.2% of Final Value **Step 1:** Set $y(t) = 0.632 \cdot y(\infty)$: $$ 0.632 \cdot K X_u = K X_u \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ **Step 2:** Cancel $K X_u$: $$ 0.632 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau} $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange: $$ e^{-t/\tau} = 1 - 0.632 = 0.368 $$ **Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm: $$ -\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.368) = -1 $$ **Step 5:** Solve for $t$: $$ t = \tau $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why 0.368?** Because $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$. This is one of the most-tested numerical facts in KTU sensor exams. **Memorize: $1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$.** ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Problem — Static Characteristics > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified Style]** > A pressure sensor is tested and gives the following readings (input $P$ in kPa, output $V$ in volts): | Input $P$ (kPa) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Output $V$ (V) (increasing) | 0.02 | 1.05 | 2.00 | 2.95 | 3.92 | 4.90 | | Output $V$ (V) (decreasing) | 0.10 | 1.12 | 2.07 | 3.02 | 3.98 | 4.90 | **Find: (a) sensitivity, (b) span, (c) non-linearity, (d) hysteresis error.** ### (a) Sensitivity **Step 1:** Use endpoints of the increasing curve: $$ S = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P} = \frac{V_{100} - V_{0}}{P_{100} - P_{0}} = \frac{4.90 - 0.02}{100 - 0} = \frac{4.88}{100} $$ $$ S = 0.0488 \ \text{V/kPa} $$ ### (b) Span (Full-Scale Output) **Step 1:** Span is the difference between maximum and minimum output: $$ \text{Span} = V_{\max} - V_{\min} = 4.90 - 0.02 = 4.88 \ \text{V} $$ ### (c) Non-linearity Error **Step 1:** The ideal straight line passes through $(0, 0.02)$ with slope $0.0488$ V/kPa. The ideal output at $P = 20$ kPa is: $$ V_{\text{ideal}}(20) = 0.02 + 0.0488 \times 20 = 0.02 + 0.976 = 0.996 \ \text{V} $$ **Step 2:** The actual output is $1.05$ V. The deviation: $$ \Delta V_{20} = 1.05 - 0.996 = +0.054 \ \text{V} $$ **Step 3:** Repeat for all points: | $P$ (kPa) | $V_{\text{ideal}}$ (V) | $V_{\text{actual}}$ (V) | $\vert \Delta V \vert$ (V) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.020 | 0.02 | 0.000 | | 20 | 0.996 | 1.05 | 0.054 | | 40 | 1.972 | 2.00 | 0.028 | | 60 | 2.948 | 2.95 | 0.002 | | 80 | 3.924 | 3.92 | 0.004 | | 100 | 4.900 | 4.90 | 0.000 | **Step 4:** Maximum deviation is $\Delta V_{\max} = 0.054$ V at $P = 20$ kPa. **Step 5:** Non-linearity as a percentage of span: $$ \%\text{NL} = \frac{0.054}{4.88} \times 100\% = 1.107\% $$ ### (d) Hysteresis Error **Step 1:** Compute the difference between the increasing and decreasing outputs at each $P$: | $P$ (kPa) | $V_{\uparrow}$ (V) | $V_{\downarrow}$ (V) | $\vert V_{\uparrow} - V_{\downarrow} \vert$ (V) | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.02 | 0.10 | 0.08 | | 20 | 1.05 | 1.12 | 0.07 | | 40 | 2.00 | 2.07 | 0.07 | | 60 | 2.95 | 3.02 | 0.07 | | 80 | 3.92 | 3.98 | 0.06 | | 100 | 4.90 | 4.90 | 0.00 | **Step 2:** Maximum difference is $0.08$ V at $P = 0$ kPa. **Step 3:** Hysteresis error as a percentage of span: $$ \%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{0.08}{4.88} \times 100\% = 1.639\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Always clearly state which direction (increasing/decreasing) you are referring to when computing hysteresis. The wording of the question matters. ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Problem — Dynamic Response > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** > A thermistor temperature sensor has a time constant $\tau = 8$ s. The ambient temperature suddenly changes from $25°C$ to $85°C$. Find: (a) the temperature reading after 10 s, (b) the time required to reach $63.2°C$, (c) the steady-state reading. **Step 1: Sensitivity assumption.** Assume the sensor is calibrated such that output voltage equals temperature in °C (i.e., $K = 1$ V/°C and we read directly in °C). **Step 2: Identify the step magnitude.** $X_u = 85 - 25 = 60°C$. ### (a) Temperature after 10 s **Step 1:** Apply the step response equation. The output above the initial value is: $$ \Delta y(t) = 60 (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) = 60 (1 - e^{-10/8}) $$ **Step 2:** Compute $e^{-1.25}$: $$ e^{-1.25} = 0.2865 $$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$ \Delta y(10) = 60 (1 - 0.2865) = 60 \times 0.7135 = 42.81°C $$ **Step 4:** Add the initial reading of 25°C: $$ y(10) = 25 + 42.81 = 67.81°C $$ ### (b) Time to reach 63.2°C **Step 1:** The target $63.2°C$ corresponds to a change of $63.2 - 25 = 38.2°C$ from the initial. **Step 2:** Apply the step response formula: $$ 38.2 = 60 (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) $$ **Step 3:** Solve: $$ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} = \frac{38.2}{60} = 0.6367 $$ **Step 4:** Isolate the exponential: $$ e^{-t/\tau} = 1 - 0.6367 = 0.3633 $$ **Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm: $$ -\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.3633) = -1.012 $$ **Step 6:** Solve for $t$: $$ t = 1.012 \times \tau = 1.012 \times 8 = 8.10 \ \text{s} $$ This confirms the 63.2% rule: at $t = \tau$, the sensor is at $63.2\%$ of the way to its final value. ### (c) Steady-State Reading As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-t/\tau} \to 0$: $$ y(\infty) = 25 + 60(1 - 0) = 85°C $$ This matches the new ambient temperature, as expected. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Sensor System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical Stimulus
Temperature, Pressure, Light] --> B[Sensing Element
Transduction happens here] B --> C[Signal Conditioning
Amplification, Filtering, Linearization] C --> D[ADC
Analog to Digital Converter] D --> E[Microcontroller or Processor
Digital Computation] E --> F[Display or Communication
LCD, UART, IoT Cloud] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Explanation of each block:** - **Physical Stimulus** — the environmental quantity to be measured. - **Sensing Element** — performs the actual transduction (e.g., thermistor, photodiode). - **Signal Conditioning** — amplifies weak signals, removes noise, linearizes output. - **ADC** — converts the analog signal into a digital number. - **Microcontroller/Processor** — runs algorithms, makes decisions. - **Display/Communication** — human-machine interface or networking output. ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of an Actuator System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Controller Command
Digital or Analog Signal] --> B[DAC or PWM Driver
Digital to Analog or Pulse Width Modulation] B --> C[Power Amplifier
Boosts Signal to Required Power Level] C --> D[Actuating Element
Energy Conversion to Physical Action] D --> E[Mechanical Output
Motion, Force, Heat, Light] E --> F[Physical Environment
System Being Controlled] style A fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 D[Actuating Element]:::actnode style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actnode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Explanation of each block:** - **Controller Command** — the desired action decided by the digital brain. - **DAC/PWM Driver** — converts digital commands to appropriate signal levels. - **Power Amplifier** — provides the high current/voltage required to drive the actuator. - **Actuating Element** — the heart of the actuator (motor coil, piezoelectric stack, solenoid). - **Mechanical Output** — the physical quantity produced. - **Physical Environment** — the system being controlled. ## 4.3 Sensor-Actuator Closed Loop in a Cyber-Physical System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHYS[Physical World] P1[Plant or Process
Temperature, Speed, Position] end subgraph SENS[Sensing Layer] S1[Sensor
Measures Physical Variable] end subgraph CYB[Cyber Layer - Computation] C1[Microcontroller
Runs Control Algorithm] C2[Decision Logic
Compares Setpoint to Measurement] end subgraph ACT[Actuation Layer] A1[Actuator Driver
Amplifier and Switching] A2[Actuator
Produces Physical Action] end P1 -->|Stimulus| S1 S1 -->|Electrical Signal| C1 C1 -->|Measured Value| C2 C2 -->|Control Command| A1 A1 -->|High Power Signal| A2 A2 -->|Physical Effect| P1 style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style S1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style A2 fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Follow the arrows clockwise. The physical process is sensed, the controller decides, and the actuator acts back on the process. The arrow from A2 back to P1 closes the loop, giving the system its feedback nature — the defining feature of a CPS. ## 4.4 Classification Hierarchy of Sensors ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Transducers] --> B[Active Transducers] A --> C[Passive Transducers] B --> B1[Piezoelectric
Thermocouple
Photovoltaic] C --> C1[Resistive
Thermistor, LDR, Strain Gauge] C --> C2[Capacitive
Capacitive Pressure Sensor] C --> C3[Inductive
LVDT, Eddy Current Sensor] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B1 fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style C1 fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style C2 fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style C3 fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Sensor Calibration Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Apply Known Inputs
0 percent, 25 percent, 50 percent, 75 percent, 100 percent of range] --> B[Record Output Values] B --> C[Plot Output vs Input] C --> D[Fit Best Straight Line
Least Squares Method] D --> E[Determine Slope and Intercept
Sensitivity and Offset] E --> F[Compute Non-linearity
Maximum Deviation] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a sensor and an actuator. How are they different? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** > A **sensor** is a device that converts a physical stimulus (temperature, pressure, light, etc.) into a measurable electrical signal. An **actuator** does the opposite — it takes an electrical signal and converts it into a physical action such as motion, force, or heat. The fundamental difference lies in the **direction of energy conversion**. A sensor is an *input* device to a control system, while an actuator is an *output* device. Both fall under the broader category of *transducers*, but a sensor converts physical-to-electrical energy, whereas an actuator converts electrical-to-physical energy. *Example:* A thermistor (sensor) measures temperature and produces a resistance change. A DC motor (actuator) takes electrical input and produces rotational motion. ### Question 2: Differentiate between active and passive transducers with examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer | |---|---|---| | External power needed | No | Yes | | Operating principle | Self-generation (direct energy conversion) | Modifies external excitation | | Output | Electrical signal generated by itself | Variation in R, L, or C | | Example 1 | Thermocouple | Thermistor | | Example 2 | Piezoelectric crystal | Strain gauge | | Example 3 | Photovoltaic cell | LDR (Light Dependent Resistor) | > Active transducers are based on physical effects like the **Seebeck effect** (thermocouple), **piezoelectric effect** (quartz crystal), and **photoelectric effect** (photovoltaic cell). Passive transducers need an external excitation circuit, typically a **Wheatstone bridge** for resistive types. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Always write the **definition** first, then the **principle**, then the **mathematical analysis** — examiners award marks strictly in this order. > 2. For a 14-mark question, you MUST include a **diagram** (circuit or block diagram). A question without a diagram typically loses 2–3 marks. > 3. Always write **units** in the final numerical answer. Writing "5" instead of "5 V" costs 0.5 mark. > 4. Do NOT confuse *accuracy* and *precision* — they are different concepts. > 5. For dynamic-response problems, always state the **time constant equation** before substituting numbers. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **(a)** Define the following static characteristics of a sensor with their mathematical expressions: **(7 marks)** (i) Sensitivity (ii) Span (iii) Non-linearity (iv) Hysteresis (v) Accuracy (vi) Resolution (vii) Drift **(b)** A pressure sensor is calibrated and the following readings are obtained. The reference (true) values are given in the first column. Compute the accuracy of the sensor at each point and identify the point of worst accuracy. **(7 marks)** | True Pressure (kPa) | Measured Pressure (kPa) | |---|---| | 0 | 0.5 | | 50 | 49.0 | | 100 | 101.5 | | 150 | 148.0 | | 200 | 203.0 | #### Model Solution for (a): **(i) Sensitivity** — the ratio of the change in output to the change in input. It is the slope of the calibration curve. $$ S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{dy}{dx} $$ Units: V/unit of stimulus (e.g., V/kPa, V/°C). **(ii) Span** — the algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum output values of the sensor within its specified range. $$ \text{Span} = y_{\max} - y_{\min} $$ **(iii) Non-linearity** — the maximum deviation of the actual output curve from the ideal straight line, expressed as a percentage of full-scale output. $$ \%\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100 $$ **(iv) Hysteresis** — the difference in output for the same input, depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. Quantified as: $$ \%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{\max \vert y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow} \vert}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100 $$ **(v) Accuracy** — the closeness of the measured value to the true value. $$ \%\text{Accuracy} = \left( 1 - \frac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \right) \times 100 $$ Equivalently: **Error %** = $\dfrac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100$. **(vi) Resolution** — the smallest change in input that can be detected as a change in output. $$ \text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Output resolution}}{S} $$ **(vii) Drift** — the slow change in output over time when the input is held constant. Often specified as percentage of span per unit time (e.g., %FSO/hour). #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1:** Apply the accuracy formula at each point. Since this is a pressure sensor, the formula becomes: $$ \text{Error} (\%) = \frac{\vert M - T \vert}{T} \times 100 $$ **Step 2:** Compute at $T = 0$ kPa: $$ \text{Error} = \frac{\vert 0.5 - 0 \vert}{0} \times 100 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Division by zero!** When $T = 0$, the standard accuracy formula fails. In practice, accuracy is reported as an **offset** of 0.5 kPa at zero input, or by using a small reference value (e.g., full-scale = 200 kPa): $$ \text{Error}_{0} = \frac{0.5}{200} \times 100 = 0.25\% $$ **Step 3:** Compute at $T = 50$ kPa: $$ \text{Error} = \frac{\vert 49.0 - 50 \vert}{50} \times 100 = \frac{1.0}{50} \times 100 = 2.0\% $$ **Step 4:** Compute at $T = 100$ kPa: $$ \text{Error} = \frac{\vert 101.5 - 100 \vert}{100} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{100} \times 100 = 1.5\% $$ **Step 5:** Compute at $T = 150$ kPa: $$ \text{Error} = \frac{\vert 148.0 - 150 \vert}{150} \times 100 = \frac{2.0}{150} \times 100 = 1.33\% $$ **Step 6:** Compute at $T = 200$ kPa: $$ \text{Error} = \frac{\vert 203.0 - 200 \vert}{200} \times 100 = \frac{3.0}{200} \times 100 = 1.5\% $$ **Step 7:** Tabulate: | True $T$ (kPa) | Measured $M$ (kPa) | Error (%) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0.5 | 0.25 (using FS) | | 50 | 49.0 | 2.00 | | 100 | 101.5 | 1.50 | | 150 | 148.0 | 1.33 | | 200 | 203.0 | 1.50 | **Step 8:** Conclusion — the **worst accuracy** (highest error) occurs at $T = 50$ kPa with an error of **2.0%**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Explain the difference between a **first-order** and a **second-order** sensor. Derive the step response of a first-order sensor and explain the significance of the time constant $\tau$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A temperature sensor is modeled as a first-order system with time constant $\tau = 5$ s and static sensitivity $K = 0.1$ V/°C. It is initially at 30°C and the ambient temperature suddenly changes to 70°C. Find: **(7 marks)** (i) The output voltage after 8 seconds. (ii) The time taken to reach 95% of the final value. (iii) The steady-state output voltage. #### Model Solution for (a): **Difference between first-order and second-order sensor:** | Feature | First-Order Sensor | Second-Order Sensor | |---|---|---| | Governing equation | $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = Kx$ | $\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} \ddot{y} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} \dot{y} + y = Kx$ | | Energy storage elements | One (e.g., thermal mass) | Two (e.g., mass + spring) | | Step response | Monotonic rise (no overshoot) | May overshoot if underdamped | | Examples | Thermometer, RC circuit | Accelerometer, seismometer, galvanometer | **Derivation of first-order step response:** **Step 1:** Write the governing equation: $$ \tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K x(t) $$ **Step 2:** Apply a step input of magnitude $X_u$ at $t = 0$: $$ \tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K X_u, \quad t \ge 0 $$ **Step 3:** Assume solution of the form $y(t) = y_{\text{ss}} + y_{\text{tr}}(t)$, where $y_{\text{ss}}$ is the steady-state solution and $y_{\text{tr}}$ is the transient. **Step 4:** Steady-state solution (set $\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$): $$ y_{\text{ss}} = K X_u $$ **Step 5:** Transient solution (homogeneous equation $\tau \dot{y} + y = 0$ has solution $y_{\text{tr}} = C e^{-t/\tau}$). **Step 6:** Apply initial condition $y(0) = 0$: $$ 0 = K X_u + C \implies C = -K X_u $$ **Step 7:** Combine: $$ y(t) = K X_u - K X_u e^{-t/\tau} = K X_u \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ **Significance of the time constant $\tau$:** 1. **Time to reach 63.2%** of the final value equals $\tau$. 2. **Settling time** (within 2% of final value) $\approx 4\tau$. 3. **Settling time** (within 1% of final value) $\approx 5\tau$. 4. **Smaller $\tau$** means **faster** sensor response. 5. $\tau$ is a measure of sensor **inertia** (thermal, electrical, or mechanical). #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $\tau = 5$ s, $K = 0.1$ V/°C, $T_{\text{initial}} = 30°C$, $T_{\text{final}} = 70°C$, $t = 8$ s. **Step 1:** Step magnitude (in °C): $$ \Delta T = 70 - 30 = 40°C $$ ### (i) Output Voltage after 8 seconds **Step 2:** Apply the step-response formula (working in terms of output voltage change): $$ \Delta y(t) = K \cdot \Delta T \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ **Step 3:** Substitute $t = 8$ s and $\tau = 5$ s: $$ \Delta y(8) = 0.1 \times 40 \times \left( 1 - e^{-8/5} \right) $$ **Step 4:** Compute the exponential: $$ e^{-1.6} = 0.2019 $$ **Step 5:** Substitute: $$ \Delta y(8) = 4 \times (1 - 0.2019) = 4 \times 0.7981 = 3.192 \ \text{V} $$ **Step 6:** Add the initial sensor output. At $T_{\text{initial}} = 30°C$, the initial output is: $$ y_0 = K \times 30 = 0.1 \times 30 = 3.0 \ \text{V} $$ **Step 7:** Total output at $t = 8$ s: $$ y(8) = y_0 + \Delta y(8) = 3.0 + 3.192 = 6.192 \ \text{V} $$ ### (ii) Time to Reach 95% of Final Value **Step 1:** Final value of the output: $$ y_{\text{final}} = K \times 70 = 0.1 \times 70 = 7.0 \ \text{V} $$ **Step 2:** 95% of the change $\Delta y_{\max} = K \Delta T = 4$ V: $$ 0.95 \times 4 = 3.8 \ \text{V} $$ **Step 3:** Set $\Delta y(t) = 3.8$ V: $$ 3.8 = 4 \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ **Step 4:** Solve: $$ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} = 0.95 $$ $$ e^{-t/\tau} = 0.05 $$ **Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm: $$ -\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.05) = -2.996 $$ **Step 6:** Solve for $t$: $$ t = 2.996 \times \tau = 2.996 \times 5 = 14.98 \ \text{s} \approx 15 \ \text{s} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** This is consistent with the rule of thumb $t_{\text{settle}} \approx 3\tau$ for 95% settling. Here we get $3\tau$ exactly. ### (iii) Steady-State Output Voltage **Step 1:** As $t \to \infty$: $$ e^{-t/\tau} \to 0 $$ **Step 2:** $$ y_{\text{ss}} = y_0 + K \Delta T = 3.0 + 4.0 = 7.0 \ \text{V} $$ This equals the final output corresponding to $T_{\text{final}} = 70°C$, as expected. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** ### Core Definitions - ☐ **Sensor** = physical input device (physical → electrical). - ☐ **Actuator** = physical output device (electrical → physical). - ☐ **Transducer** = superset of both; any device that converts one form of energy to another. ### Classification - ☐ **Active transducer** = self-generating (no external power). Examples: thermocouple, piezoelectric crystal, photovoltaic cell. - ☐ **Passive transducer** = requires external power. Examples: thermistor, strain gauge, LDR, LVDT. ### Static Characteristics — Memorize - ☐ Sensitivity $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ - ☐ Span = $y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ - ☐ Non-linearity % = $\Delta y_{\max} / y_{\text{FS}} \times 100$ - ☐ Hysteresis % = max $|y_{\uparrow} - y_{\downarrow}| / y_{\text{FS}} \times 100$ - ☐ Accuracy ≠ Precision. Accuracy is closeness to truth; precision is repeatability. ### Dynamic Characteristics — Memorize - ☐ **First-order step response:** $y(t) = K X_u (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ - ☐ **63.2% rule:** $y(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot y_{\infty}$ (because $1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$) - ☐ **Settling times:** $2\tau$ (86.5%), $3\tau$ (95%), $4\tau$ (98.2%), $5\tau$ (99.3%) - ☐ **Second-order:** depends on $\zeta$. Critically damped at $\zeta = 1$. ### KTU Exam Patterns - ☐ 14-mark questions = (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks (sub-part pattern). - ☐ 3-mark questions = pure definitions, classifications, or short derivations. - ☐ Always draw a **block diagram or circuit diagram** for full marks. - ☐ Always include **units** in numerical answers. - ☐ In dynamic problems, write the formula BEFORE substituting values. ### Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here) - ☐ Confusing active and passive transducers. - ☐ Forgetting the time-constant sign (must be negative: $e^{-t/\tau}$). - ☐ Computing hysteresis as a single value instead of the maximum difference. - ☐ Not labelling axes on a calibration curve. - ☐ Writing the static-characteristic formulas without their numerical meaning. ### Real-World Anchors (Use in Answers for Bonus Marks) - ☐ **Sensor in CPS:** eyes of the system (perception). - ☐ **Actuator in CPS:** muscles of the system (action). - ☐ **Feedback loop:** sensor → controller → actuator → plant → sensor. - ☐ **Example pairs:** thermistor + heater, LDR + lamp, strain gauge + servo, accelerometer + airbag igniter.

topics.title: Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# PRINCIPLES OF SENSORS AND ACTUATORS — CORE FOUNDATIONS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCPT205 / Module 1):** This module establishes the **fundamental physical principles** governing the conversion of physical, chemical, and biological phenomena into measurable electrical signals (sensing) and the reverse process of converting electrical energy into physical action (actuation).

### Sensor
A **sensor** is a device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity (the *measurand*) and converts it into an equivalent electrical signal (usually voltage, current, frequency, or digital output) whose amplitude is functionally related to the magnitude of the measurand.

### Actuator
An **actuator** is a device that accepts an electrical input signal and converts it into a corresponding physical output such as motion, force, heat, light, or fluid flow. It is essentially the *inverse* counterpart of a sensor.

### Transducer
A **transducer** is a generic term denoting any device that converts one form of energy into another. Hence, both sensors and actuators fall under the broad umbrella of transducers. The standard classification convention in KTU textbooks treats a **sensor** as a *passive* or *active* energy-conversion element producing a measurable output, while an **actuator** is the energy-accepting counterpart.

### Measurand
The **measurand** is the physical quantity subjected to measurement — examples include temperature, pressure, displacement, strain, humidity, light intensity, and acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Lock:** In board examinations, students frequently lose marks by interchanging *sensor* and *transducer*. A transducer converts energy in *either* direction; a sensor converts the measurand *only into an output signal*. Use the correct term based on the energy flow direction.

### Cyber-Physical System (CPS) Context
In a Cyber-Physical System (CPS), sensors and actuators constitute the **physical layer (P)** that interacts with the **cyber layer (C)** through a tight feedback loop. The sensor acquires data → the controller computes a decision → the actuator executes a physical action → the loop repeats. This is the foundational paradigm for IoT, Industry 4.0, autonomous vehicles, and smart-grid applications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> **The Five-Senses Analogy**
>
