
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The serpentine (zigzag) pattern multiplies the effective length of the gauge by roughly 50–100× within a compact area. This is the geometric trick that makes strain gauges practical — without it, a 1 cm straight wire would barely register any measurable resistance change.

## 4.4 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Bridge Configuration] --> B{How Many Active\nGauges Available?}
    B -->|One Active Gauge| C[Quarter Bridge\nSensitivity Factor 1.0\nFormula: epsilon equals 4 Vo over Vin times GF]
    B -->|Two Active Gauges| D[Half Bridge\nSensitivity Factor 2.0\nFormula: epsilon equals 2 Vo over Vin times GF]
    B -->|Four Active Gauges| E[Full Bridge\nSensitivity Factor 4.0\nFormula: epsilon equals Vo over Vin times GF]

    C --> F1[Pros: Simple, One Active Gauge\nCons: Low Sensitivity, Sensitive to Temperature]
    D --> F2[Pros: Temperature Compensation, Doubled Sensitivity\nCons: Requires Two Active Gauges]
    E --> F3[Pros: Maximum Sensitivity, Built-in Temperature Compensation\nCons: Most Expensive, Complex Wiring]

    classDef decisionStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000

    class A,B decisionStyle

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Gauge Factor. Why is it important in strain gauge measurements?

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance to the mechanical strain producing it:

$$
GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. For metallic gauges (e.g., constantan), $GF \approx 2.0–2.2$, while for semiconductor gauges, $GF \approx 100–200$.

**Importance:**
1. It quantifies the **sensitivity** of the strain gauge — higher GF means larger $\Delta R$ for a given strain, making measurement easier.
2. It is essential for **converting measured voltage** back to actual strain values in bridge circuits.
3. It determines the **selection** of gauge material for specific applications (metallic for precision, semiconductor for high sensitivity).

---

### Question 2: What is the role of a dummy gauge in a Wheatstone bridge?

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A **dummy gauge** is an identical strain gauge that is mounted on an **unstrained (or unstrained part of the same) material** and placed in an adjacent arm of the Wheatstone bridge.

**Roles:**
1. **Temperature Compensation:** When temperature changes, both the active and dummy gauges experience identical resistance changes due to thermal expansion. Since they are in adjacent arms, the common-mode resistance change cancels out in the differential output.
2. **Lead Wire Compensation:** It compensates for resistance changes in long lead wires connecting the gauge to the bridge.
3. **Improved Linearity:** It reduces the nonlinearity in the bridge output by keeping the bridge closer to balance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: (a) Derive an expression for the Gauge Factor of a resistance strain gauge starting from the basic resistance equation.

**(7 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Basic Resistance Equation**

$$
R = \frac{\rho \, L}{A}
$$

**Step 2: Logarithmic Differentiation**

$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate with respect to strain**

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

**Step 4: Apply strain relations**

Since $\varepsilon = \dfrac{dL}{L}$ and the transverse strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon
$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify**

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon = \varepsilon \left( 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} \right)
$$

**Step 6: Compare with definition**

$$
\boxed{GF = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The gauge factor has three components — the length effect (1), the area/Poisson effect ($2\nu$), and the piezoresistive effect. For metallic alloys like constantan, the piezoresistive term is near zero, giving $GF \approx 2.0$. For semiconductors, this term dominates, yielding $GF \approx 100–200$.

---

### Question A: (b) A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit uses a single active gauge of resistance 120 Ω and gauge factor 2.05. The bridge is excited by 5 V. The output voltage is measured as 1.2 mV. Calculate (i) the strain, (ii) the change in resistance, and (iii) the stress if Young's modulus of the specimen material is 200 GPa.

**(7 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 120 \, \Omega$
- $GF = 2.05$
- $V_{in} = 5 \, V$
- $V_o = 1.2 \, mV = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \, V$
- $E = 200 \, GPa = 200 \times 10^9 \, Pa$

**Step 1: Calculate the strain using the quarter-bridge formula**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{4 V_o}{V_{in} \cdot GF} = \frac{4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 2.05}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-3}}{10.25} = 4.683 \times 10^{-4} = 468.3 \, \mu\varepsilon
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the change in resistance**

$$
\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 2.05 \times 4.683 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\Delta R = 0.1152 \, \Omega \approx 0.115 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the stress using Hooke's Law**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 200 \times 10^9 \times 4.683 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\sigma = 93.66 \times 10^6 \, Pa = 93.66 \, MPa
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Strain: $\varepsilon \approx 468.3 \, \mu\varepsilon$
- (ii) Resistance change: $\Delta R \approx 0.115 \, \Omega$
- (iii) Stress: $\sigma \approx 93.66 \, MPa$

---

### Question B: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a bonded metal foil strain gauge. List its advantages and limitations.

**(7 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

A bonded metal foil strain gauge consists of:
1. **Grid (Foil):** Thin metallic foil (typically constantan, 3–6 µm thick) arranged in a serpentine pattern on a carrier.
2. **Backing (Carrier):** Thin polyimide or epoxy backing (~25–50 µm) that supports the foil and isolates it from the test specimen mechanically.
3. **Adhesive Layer:** Special cement (cyanoacrylate or epoxy) bonds the gauge to the test specimen.
4. **Lead Wires:** Soldered to the terminal pads to connect to the bridge circuit.
5. **Protective Coating:** Optional moisture-resistant coating for harsh environments.

**Working:**

When the test specimen is subjected to mechanical stress:
1. The strain is **transmitted** through the adhesive to the foil grid.
2. The foil stretches or compresses in the longitudinal direction, changing its length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$.
3. According to $R = \rho L / A$, the resistance changes.
4. This $\Delta R$ is detected by a Wheatstone bridge and converted to a voltage output.
5. The output voltage is proportional to the strain, which can be calibrated to the applied force or stress.

**Advantages:**
- High accuracy and linearity ($GF \approx 2.0$ is very stable)
- Small size, low cost, and easy installation
- Excellent fatigue life (>10⁶ cycles)
- Good temperature stability (especially constantan)
- Wide strain range (± 3000 µε)

**Limitations:**
- Very small $\Delta R$ (fraction of an ohm) — requires sensitive bridge circuits
- Sensitive to temperature variations without compensation
- Single-axis (measures strain in one direction only — rosettes needed for multi-axis)
- Non-recoverable if overloaded beyond elastic limit

---

### Question B: (b) Explain the temperature compensation techniques used in strain gauge measurements. What is the purpose of using a dummy gauge?

**(7 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of Temperature Error:**

1. **Apparent Strain:** Thermal expansion of the specimen (different from gauge material) creates a false strain reading.
2. **Resistance Change:** Temperature changes the resistivity $\rho$ of the gauge material.
3. **Gauge Factor Variation:** GF itself varies slightly with temperature.

**Compensation Techniques:**

**1. Self-Temperature Compensation (STC) Gauges:**
The gauge alloy is selected such that its thermal expansion matches that of common test materials (e.g., constantan matches steel closely). This minimizes apparent strain at a specific reference temperature.

**2. Dummy Gauge Method (Adjacent Arm Compensation):**
A second identical gauge (dummy) is mounted on a **separate unstressed piece** of the same material as the test specimen. Both gauges are connected in adjacent arms of the Wheatstone bridge. When temperature changes:
- Both gauges experience identical $\Delta R$ due to temperature.
- Since they are in adjacent arms, the differential output $V_o$ is **unaffected** by this common-mode change.
- Only the **mechanical strain** on the active gauge produces a true differential signal.

**3. Half-Bridge and Full-Bridge Configurations:**
Using two or four active gauges in the bridge automatically compensates for temperature, since all gauges change identically with temperature.

**Purpose of Dummy Gauge:**
- Acts as a **reference** that "sees" only the thermal effect, not the mechanical strain.
- Cancels **common-mode noise** (temperature, lead wire resistance) in the bridge.
- Improves the **stability** and **accuracy** of long-duration measurements.
- Reduces the **zero drift** in the readout instrument.

**Practical Note:** In industrial applications, the dummy gauge is often mounted on the same specimen but in a direction perpendicular to the principal strain — this also compensates for **transverse sensitivity** errors.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the $2\nu$ term:** Many students forget that the area change contributes $2\nu$ (not $\nu$) because area is a 2D quantity and both perpendicular dimensions change.
> 2. **Confusing bridge configurations:** Students often write the formula for a full-bridge but use it for a quarter-bridge problem, leading to a **4× error** in the final answer. Always identify the configuration first.
> 3. **Unit errors in $\Delta R$:** $\Delta R$ is in **ohms** ($\Omega$), not milliohms. $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless but is often expressed in **µε** for readability. Always include units in your final answer.
> 4. **Sign convention:** Tensile strain gives **positive** $V_o$ (active gauge resistance increases), compressive strain gives **negative** $V_o$ (resistance decreases). Failing to specify the sign loses a half-mark.
> 5. **Forgetting Poisson's ratio correction:** In half-bridge and quarter-bridge setups where the dummy gauge is unstrained, the transverse sensitivity of the active gauge introduces a $(1-\nu)$ or $(1+\nu)$ correction. Read the problem statement carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gauge Factor (GF):** The fundamental parameter of a strain gauge. $GF = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$. For constantan: $GF \approx 2.0$. For silicon: $GF \approx 100–200$.
- **Basic Resistance Formula:** $R = \rho L / A$. Strain changes all three quantities, but for metallic gauges, only $L$ and $A$ matter significantly.
- **Quarter-Bridge Output:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$. Sensitivity factor = 1.
- **Half-Bridge Output:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{2}$. Sensitivity factor = 2 (twice as sensitive as quarter-bridge).
- **Full-Bridge Output:** $V_o = V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$. Sensitivity factor = 4 (most sensitive configuration).
- **Poisson's Effect:** Transverse strain $\varepsilon_a = -\nu \, \varepsilon_l$. Always negative for positive longitudinal strain.
- **Temperature Compensation:** Achieved via dummy gauge, STC gauge selection, or multi-active-gauge bridge configurations.
- **Semiconductor vs. Metallic:** Semiconductors have **much higher GF** (sensitivity) but are **more temperature-sensitive** and **more nonlinear**.
- **Typical Applications:** Load cells, pressure transducers, torque sensors, accelerometers, structural health monitoring, weighing scales.
- **Standard Nominal Resistances:** **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 1000 Ω** — choose based on bridge impedance and lead length.
- **Key Derivation to Memorize:** The gauge factor derivation from $R = \rho L / A$ using logarithmic differentiation (5 lines, can be done in <2 minutes in the exam).
- **Common Misconception:** The strain gauge does **not** measure force directly — it measures strain. Force/stress must be calculated using $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ if Young's modulus is known.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (10 minutes before exam):** Memorize the gauge factor formula (4), the three bridge sensitivity formulas (9, 10, 11), and the quarter-bridge numerical example pattern. These cover ~70% of the marks on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semiconductor Strain Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Strain Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Semiconductor Strain Gauges — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Semiconductor Strain Gauge (SSG)** is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a proportional change in electrical resistance, utilizing the **piezoresistive effect** — the phenomenon by which the bulk electrical resistivity of a semiconductor crystal changes when subjected to mechanical stress.

Unlike metallic foil strain gauges which rely predominantly on the geometric change of the conductor, a semiconductor strain gauge exploits a fundamental **change in the resistivity ($\rho$)** of the doped semiconductor element (typically monocrystalline Silicon or Germanium). This makes the device intrinsically far more sensitive than its metallic counterparts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCPT205 – Module 1):**
> Semiconductor strain gauges must be studied under three core sub-domains:
> 1. **Piezoresistive effect** in doped Silicon and Germanium.
> 2. **Gauge Factor ($G_f$)** — its magnitude, sign (positive/negative), and physical origin.
> 3. **Temperature dependence and cross-sensitivity compensation** (Wheatstone bridge nulling, dummy gauge techniques).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge and the Highway"

Imagine a **highway built on a soft sponge**:

* When you step on the sponge, the road **deforms** (geometric change).
* More importantly, the **traffic flow (electric current) gets severely disrupted** because the internal pathways inside the sponge are squeezed shut.

A **metal strain gauge** behaves like a road built on a *firm rubber sheet* — only the road geometry deforms slightly, so the change in traffic (resistance) is small.

A **semiconductor strain gauge**, however, behaves like a road built on a *loose sponge full of internal tunnels*. Stepping on it not only bends the road but also **collapses the internal tunnels** (atomic lattice distortion alters the energy band structure), dramatically reducing the traffic flow (resistivity skyrockets).

> **Key takeaway:** In semiconductors, the **internal atomic restructuring** (resistivity change) dominates over the simple **shape change** (geometric factor). This is the heart of the **piezoresistive effect**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized in KTU board problems and must be memorized:

* **Young's Modulus of Silicon (single crystal, $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction):** $E \approx 187 \text{ GPa}$
* **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon:** $\nu \approx 0.28$
* **Resistivity of doped Silicon (typical gauge):** $\rho \approx 0.001$ to $0.01 \ \Omega \cdot \text{m}$
* **Standard Gauge Factor range:** $G_f \approx \mathbf{+50}$ to $\mathbf{+200}$ (n-type, longitudinal), or $\mathbf{-100}$ to $\mathbf{-200}$ (p-type, transverse)
* **Operating Temperature:** $-50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+150^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typical)
* **Carrier mobility electrons (Si):** $\mu_n \approx 1400 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$
* **Carrier mobility holes (Si):** $\mu_p \approx 470 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V}\cdot\text{s}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why memorize Gauge Factor magnitudes?**
> In KTU numerical problems, the expected $G_f$ for doped silicon is **two orders of magnitude higher** than for metallic gauges ($\approx 2.0$). Always quote this range to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Piezoresistive Effect — Operational Logic

The piezoresistive effect is a **quantum-mechanical phenomenon** rooted in the band structure of semiconductors. When mechanical stress is applied:

1. **Lattice spacing changes** — inter-atomic distance alters.
2. **Energy band curvature shifts** — the effective mass $m^*$ of charge carriers changes.
3. **Mobility ($\mu$) changes** — scattering rates and mean free path are altered.
4. **Resistivity ($\rho$) changes** — since $\rho = \dfrac{1}{q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)}$ for an intrinsic semiconductor.
5. **Net resistance** $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ increases or decreases depending on carrier type and crystal orientation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **n-type silicon** under longitudinal tensile strain, $G_f$ is **positive and large**. In **p-type silicon** under transverse strain, $G_f$ can be **negative** — meaning the resistance *decreases* with tensile strain. This sign inversion is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

## 2.2 Construction of a Typical Semiconductor Strain Gauge

* **Active element:** A thin strip or rectangular bar of doped Si (often $\sim 0.1$ mm thick, $1\text{–}5$ mm long).
* **Lead wires:** Gold or aluminum bonding wires (avoiding dissimilar-metal thermocouple effects).
* **Substrate mounting:** Epoxied or diffused onto the test specimen; alternatively, microfabricated as part of an MEMS die.
* **Orientation:** Crystallographic axis ($\langle 111 \rangle$, $\langle 110 \rangle$, or $\langle 100 \rangle$) is carefully aligned to the principal stress direction to maximize sensitivity.

## 2.3 Gauge Factor — Physical Decomposition

For a uniform resistive element of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$
R = \frac{\rho L}{A}
$$

Taking the logarithmic differential:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

For an isotropic material under uniaxial strain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{dL}{L}$, and using Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu\varepsilon
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

The **Gauge Factor ($G_f$)** is defined as the fractional change in resistance per unit strain:

$$
G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

Hence:

$$
G_f = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

where the second term is the **piezoresistive contribution**.

| Contribution Term | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude (Metals) | Typical Magnitude (Semiconductors) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $(1 + 2\nu)$ | Geometric (shape change) | $\approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ | $\approx 1.5$ (negligible vs. piezoresistive term) |
| $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ | Piezoresistive (resistivity change) | $\approx 0.5$ (small) | $\approx +100$ to $+200$ (dominant) |
| **Total $G_f$** | — | $\approx 2.0$ | $\approx 100$ to $200$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize for ESE)

| Formula | Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resistance of gauge | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $\rho$ in $\Omega\cdot$m, $L$ in m, $A$ in m² | Baseline design |
| Gauge Factor (general) | $G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Dimensionless | Sensitivity specification |
| Gauge Factor (decomposed) | $G_f = (1+2\nu) + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ | Dimensionless | Identifies dominant physical effect |
| Piezoresistive coefficient | $\pi_l = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\dfrac{\Delta \rho_l}{\rho}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ or $\mu\varepsilon^{-1}$ | Material selection |
| Output voltage (Wheatstone bridge) | $V_o = V_{ex}\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | $V$ in volts | Interfacing circuit design |
| Active-dummy compensation | $\Delta V_o \approx V_{ex}\,\varepsilon\,G_f$ (single active gauge) | $V$ in volts | Temperature-compensated read-out |
| Longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient (Si, n-type, $\langle 111 \rangle$) | $\pi_l \approx +400 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Numerical problem input |
| Transverse coefficient (Si, p-type, $\langle 100 \rangle$) | $\pi_t \approx -700 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ | Numerical problem input |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note:** Never confuse the **piezoresistive coefficient $\pi$** (units of $\text{Pa}^{-1}$) with the **gauge factor $G_f$** (dimensionless). They are related by $\pi \cdot E \approx G_f$ when expressed per unit strain instead of per unit stress. KTU questions test this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive:** Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS), airbag accelerometers, engine manifold pressure sensors.
* **Aerospace:** Wing load monitoring, structural fatigue sensors in composite airframes.
* **Biomedical:** Microfabricated blood pressure catheters, neural probes.
* **Industrial:** Load cells in high-precision weighing scales (5–500 kg range), robotic force/torque sensors.
* **MEMS:** Integrated pressure sensors (e.g., MPX series by NXP/Freescale) where the SSG is diffused directly into a silicon diaphragm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Gauge Factor from First Principles

### Starting Premise
A uniform resistive strip with length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and bulk resistivity $\rho$ obeys Ohm's law in the form:

$$
R = \frac{\rho L}{A}
$$

### Step 1 — Apply logarithmic differentiation
Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to strain $\varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{d\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{d\varepsilon} + \frac{1}{L}\frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{A}\frac{dA}{d\varepsilon}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify each derivative
The strain along the gauge axis is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{L}\frac{dL}{d\varepsilon} = 1
$$

The lateral strain (transverse contraction) is governed by Poisson's ratio:

$$
\varepsilon_t = -\nu\varepsilon \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu\varepsilon
$$

Hence:

$$
\frac{1}{A}\frac{dA}{d\varepsilon} = -2\nu
$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the Gauge Factor definition
By definition:

$$
G_f = \frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

Substituting the assembled differential equation:

$$
G_f = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret for semiconductor materials
For doped silicon under moderate strain ($\varepsilon \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$), the piezoresistive term $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$ is on the order of **100 to 200**, while $(1 + 2\nu) \approx 1.5$. Therefore:

$$
G_f^{\text{Si}} \approx \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{d\rho}{\rho} \approx 100 \text{ to } 200
$$

For metals (constantan, nichrome), the resistivity term is negligible:

$$
G_f^{\text{metal}} \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The dominance of the piezoresistive term in semiconductors arises because the mobility $\mu$ depends strongly on lattice spacing. Stress-induced band warping reshapes the conduction/valence band curvature, dramatically shifting $\mu$, and hence $\rho = 1/(q n \mu)$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Output Voltage of Quarter-Bridge SSG Circuit

### Circuit Topology
A single active semiconductor strain gauge $R_g$ is mounted in **arm 1** of a Wheatstone bridge. The other three arms are fixed precision resistors $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$.

### Step 1 — Initial balanced bridge condition
At zero strain, $R_g = R$ and the bridge is balanced:

$$
V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R}{R+R} - \frac{R}{R+R}\right) = 0
$$

### Step 2 — Apply strain
The gauge resistance changes to:

$$
R_g = R + \Delta R \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \varepsilon
$$

### Step 3 — Compute unbalanced output
The output voltage between the two midpoints becomes:

$$
V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_g}{R_g + R} - \frac{R}{R + R}\right)
$$

Substituting $R_g = R(1 + G_f \varepsilon)$:

$$
V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R(1 + G_f\varepsilon)}{R(1 + G_f\varepsilon) + R} - \frac{1}{2}\right)
$$

$$
V_o = V_{ex}\left(\frac{1 + G_f\varepsilon}{2 + G_f\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply small-strain approximation ($G_f \varepsilon \ll 1$)
Using the binomial series:

$$
\frac{1 + G_f\varepsilon}{2 + G_f\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{1 + G_f\varepsilon}{1 + \frac{1}{2}G_f\varepsilon} \approx \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}G_f\varepsilon\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_o \approx V_{ex}\cdot\frac{G_f\varepsilon}{4}
$$

### Step 5 — Final linearized result

$$
\boxed{\,V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}\,G_f\,\varepsilon}{4}\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A silicon strain gauge with $G_f = 120$ is bonded to a steel beam subjected to a strain of $800\ \mu\varepsilon$. The excitation voltage is $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$. Find the bridge output voltage.

**Solution:**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}\,G_f\,\varepsilon}{4} = \frac{5 \times 120 \times 800\times 10^{-6}}{4}
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{5 \times 0.096}{4} = \frac{0.48}{4} = 0.12 \text{ V} = 120 \text{ mV}
$$

**Answer:** $V_o = 120 \text{ mV}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Property | Metal Foil Gauge | Semiconductor Strain Gauge |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Gauge Factor $G_f$ | $2.0$ | $100$ to $200$ |
| Strain range | $\pm 30{,}000\ \mu\varepsilon$ | $\pm 3000\ \mu\varepsilon$ |
| Hysteresis | Very low ($\approx 0.1\%$) | Moderate ($\approx 0.5$ to $1\%$) |
| Temperature sensitivity ($\Delta R/R$ per °C) | $\approx 10\ \text{ppm}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\approx 100\text{ to }1000\ \text{ppm}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Fatigue life | $> 10^7$ cycles | $> 10^6$ cycles |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Linearity | Excellent | Moderate (non-linear at high strain) |
| Active material | Constantan, Karma alloy | Doped Si, Ge, InSb, GaAs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the signal flow from a physical strain input through a semiconductor gauge to a conditioned electrical output, highlighting temperature compensation pathways and the role of the piezoresistive transducer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Strain Input ε] --> B[Host Structure Beam or Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Active SSG Element Doped Silicon Strip]
    D[Thermal Environment T] --> C
    C --> E[Piezoresistive Effect Resistivity Change drho]
    E --> F[Resistance Change dR = Gf times R times ε]
    F --> G[Wheatstone Bridge Interfacing]
    G --> H[Differential Output Voltage Vo]
    I[Dummy Gauge Temperature Reference] --> G
    J[Excitation Voltage Source Vex] --> G
    H --> K[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain Stage]
    K --> L[ADC Conversion]
    L --> M[Microprocessor Digital Readout in microstrain]
    I --> N[Temperature Compensation Network]
    N --> G
```

## 4.2 Crystal Orientation & Anisotropy Flow

The gauge factor in silicon is **anisotropic** — it depends on the crystal direction relative to the applied stress. The following schematic shows the standard crystallographic axes and typical directional gauge factor signs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crystal Direction Selection] --> B[Longitudinal 111 axis]
    A --> C[Transverse 110 axis]
    A --> D[Shear 100 axis]
    B --> E[N-type pi_l plus 400 times 10 to power minus 12 per Pa]
    B --> F[P-type pi_l minus 660 times 10 to power minus 12 per Pa]
    C --> G[N-type pi_t minus 250 times 10 to power minus 12 per Pa]
    C --> H[P-type pi_t plus 700 times 10 to power minus 12 per Pa]
    D --> I[Shear coefficient pi_44 about 1380 times 10 to power minus 12 per Pa]
    E --> J[Optimal High Sensitivity SSG Design]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Quarter-Bridge Circuit Schematic (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vex Positive Supply] --> B[Node A Top Left]
    B --> C[Active SSG Rg plus delta R]
    B --> D[Fixed Resistor R]
    C --> E[Node C Bottom Left Output Positive]
    D --> F[Node D Bottom Right Output Negative]
    E --> G[Differential Amplifier IN plus]
    F --> H[Differential Amplifier IN minus]
    I[Vex Negative Ground] --> J[Node B Top Right]
    J --> D
    J --> C
    G --> K[Instrumentation Amplifier Output Vo]
    H --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Viva:**
> When asked to explain the signal chain, always narrate in the order: **Strain → Lattice distortion → Band warping → Mobility change → Resistivity change → Resistance change → Bridge unbalance → Differential voltage → Amplification → ADC → Digital readout.** This sequence captures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the piezoresistive effect. How does it differ fundamentally from the strain sensitivity observed in metallic strain gauges?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **piezoresistive effect** is the change in the electrical resistivity of a semiconductor material when subjected to mechanical stress, arising from stress-induced changes in the energy band structure and charge carrier mobility.

**Key distinction (2 marks):**
* In **metallic strain gauges**, sensitivity originates primarily from **geometric deformation** (change in length and cross-sectional area), yielding a gauge factor $G_f \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2.0$.
* In **semiconductor strain gauges**, the dominant contribution comes from the **change in resistivity** $\rho$, which is two orders of magnitude larger than the geometric term. Hence $G_f \approx 100$ to $200$.

The metallic gauge is governed by classical mechanics (geometry), while the semiconductor gauge is governed by **quantum-mechanical band theory** (mobility modulation).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Why is the temperature sensitivity of a semiconductor strain gauge significantly higher than that of a metallic gauge? List one compensation technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Semiconductors exhibit a strong temperature coefficient of resistivity because:
1. **Carrier concentration ($n$, $p$)** increases exponentially with temperature (intrinsic regime) or varies with doping-dependent activation.
2. **Mobility ($\mu$)** decreases with temperature due to enhanced phonon scattering.
3. **Combined effect** amplifies $\Delta \rho / \rho$ per °C by 10 to 100× compared to metals.

**Compensation technique (1 mark):**
* Use a **dummy gauge** in the adjacent arm of the Wheatstone bridge, mounted on the same material but oriented perpendicular to the strain axis. The dummy gauge experiences identical temperature change but no strain, canceling thermal drift at the bridge output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\pi$ (piezoresistive coefficient) with $G_f$ (gauge factor).** They differ by a factor of $E$ (Young's modulus). Marks are deducted if units are mixed.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign** of $G_f$ for p-type silicon (negative in transverse orientation). KTU problems frequently ask for the polarity.
> 3. **Skipping the small-strain approximation** $G_f \varepsilon \ll 1$ in the Wheatstone bridge derivation. The full non-linear expression $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{ex}} = \dfrac{G_f\varepsilon}{4 + 2G_f\varepsilon}$ loses no marks, but the simplified form is preferred.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers. Always state $V_o$ in mV or $\mu$V, and strain in $\mu\varepsilon$.
> 5. **Not mentioning crystal anisotropy.** For silicon, $G_f$ depends strongly on whether the stress is along $\langle 111 \rangle$, $\langle 110 \rangle$, or $\langle 100 \rangle$.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge starting from $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$. Show that for semiconductors the piezoresistive term dominates over the geometric term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon semiconductor strain gauge has a nominal resistance of $120\ \Omega$ and a gauge factor of $130$. It is mounted in one arm of a quarter Wheatstone bridge with the other three arms being $120\ \Omega$ fixed resistors. The bridge excitation is $5\ \text{V}$ DC. Calculate the output voltage when the gauge experiences a strain of $1200\ \mu\varepsilon$. Also find the change in resistance of the gauge. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Start with $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$. Apply logarithmic differentiation to obtain:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute strain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{dL}{L}$ and lateral contraction $\dfrac{dA}{A} = -2\nu\varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define gauge factor:

$$
G_f = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For silicon, the piezoresistive term $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} \approx 100$ to $200$, vastly exceeding $(1 + 2\nu) \approx 1.5$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,G_f^{\text{Si}} \approx \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{d\rho}{\rho} \gg (1 + 2\nu)\,}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $R = 120\ \Omega$, $G_f = 130$, $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$, $\varepsilon = 1200 \times 10^{-6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Change in resistance:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \varepsilon = 130 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 0.156
$$

$$
\Delta R = 0.156 \times 120 = 18.72 \ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Bridge output formula (quarter-bridge, single active gauge):**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}\,G_f\,\varepsilon}{4}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute and evaluate:**

$$
V_o = \frac{5 \times 130 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4}
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{0.78}{4} = 0.195 \text{ V} = 195 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final answers:**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta R = 18.72\ \Omega \quad ; \quad V_o = 195\ \text{mV}\,}
$$

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with relevant equations the working principle of a semiconductor strain gauge based on the piezoresistive effect. Discuss how doping type (n-type vs p-type) and crystal orientation affect the magnitude and sign of the gauge factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diffused silicon pressure sensor uses four identical p-type strain gauges diffused on a thin silicon diaphragm. Two gauges are oriented along the $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction at the diaphragm edge (tangential) and two along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction at the diaphragm center (radial). When a pressure of $50\ \text{kPa}$ is applied, the tangential strain is $+400\ \mu\varepsilon$ and the radial strain is $-200\ \mu\varepsilon$. Given $G_f^{\text{tangential}} = +120$ and $G_f^{\text{radial}} = -90$, and bridge excitation $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$, compute the full-bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (2 marks):** When a semiconductor crystal is mechanically strained, the inter-atomic spacing changes, modifying the **energy band curvature** and hence the **effective mass $m^*$** of charge carriers. The mobility $\mu$ depends on $m^*$ and scattering time $\tau$ as $\mu = q\tau/m^*$. Since $\rho = 1/(q n \mu)$, any change in $\mu$ directly alters $\rho$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Define the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_l$ such that:

$$
\frac{\Delta \rho_l}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t
$$

where $\sigma_l$ and $\sigma_t$ are longitudinal and transverse stresses.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Doping dependence:**

* **n-type silicon:** Carriers are electrons. Tensile longitudinal strain raises the conductivity effective mass asymmetry, producing a **positive $G_f$** on the order of $+100$ to $+200$ along $\langle 111 \rangle$.
* **p-type silicon:** Carriers are holes. Due to the degenerate valence band (heavy-hole and light-hole branches), the response is **strongly anisotropic**. Along certain transverse orientations, $G_f$ can be **negative** (resistance decreases with tensile strain).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Crystal orientation determines which piezoresistive coefficient ($\pi_{11}$, $\pi_{12}$, $\pi_{44}$) is activated, producing the observed $G_f$ variation across orientations.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** Four active gauges in a **full Wheatstone bridge** configuration:
* $R_1$ (tangential, +ε₁ = +400 με, $G_f^{(1)} = +120$)
* $R_2$ (radial, –ε₂ = –200 με, $G_f^{(2)} = –90$)
* $R_3$ (tangential, +ε₃ = +400 με, $G_f^{(3)} = +120$)
* $R_4$ (radial, –ε₄ = –200 με, $G_f^{(4)} = –90$)
* Arranged so adjacent arms have **opposite strain signs** → maximum sensitivity.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Resistance changes:**

For gauge 1 (tangential, tensile):
$$
\frac{\Delta R_1}{R} = (+120)(+400 \times 10^{-6}) = +0.048
$$

For gauge 2 (radial, compressive):
$$
\frac{\Delta R_2}{R} = (-90)(-200 \times 10^{-6}) = +0.018
$$

Both $\Delta R_1/R$ and $\Delta R_2/R$ are **positive**, which is the hallmark of a **push-pull** configuration where adjacent arms increase together. This maximizes bridge sensitivity.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Full-bridge output formula:**

For a full bridge with all four gauges active and arranged for additive output:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R}\right)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute:**

$$
V_o = \frac{10}{4}\bigl[(+0.048) - (+0.018) + (+0.048) - (+0.018)\bigr]
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{10}{4}\bigl[2 \times 0.048 + 2 \times (-0.018)\bigr]
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{10}{4}\bigl[0.096 - 0.036\bigr] = \frac{10 \times 0.060}{4} = 0.15 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_o = 150 \text{ mV}\,}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* **Definition to recall:** A semiconductor strain gauge uses the **piezoresistive effect** — change in bulk resistivity due to stress — to transduce strain into resistance change.
* **Core formula to memorize:** $G_f = (1+2\nu) + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho}$.
* **Magnitude benchmark:** $G_f^{\text{metal}} \approx 2.0$; $G_f^{\text{semiconductor}} \approx 100$ to $200$.
* **Dominant physics:** In semiconductors, the **piezoresistive (resistivity-change) term dominates** over the geometric term by two orders of magnitude.
* **Sign convention:** n-type Si typically yields **positive $G_f$** along longitudinal $\langle 111 \rangle$; p-type Si can yield **negative $G_f$** along certain transverse orientations.
* **Standard output equation:** For a quarter bridge, $V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}\,G_f\,\varepsilon}{4}$.
* **Material:** Doped monocrystalline **Silicon** (most common), Germanium, GaAs, InSb (less common).
* **Key advantage:** Very high sensitivity (≈50× to 100× metal gauges).
* **Key disadvantage:** High temperature sensitivity, limited strain range (±3000 με), higher cost.
* **Compensation techniques:** Dummy gauge in adjacent bridge arm; constant-current excitation; active bridge temperature control; use of matched dual-gauge rosettes.
* **Anisotropy:** Gauge factor depends on crystallographic orientation — always specify $\langle 111 \rangle$, $\langle 110 \rangle$, or $\langle 100 \rangle$ in answers.
* **Units to quote:** Strain in $\mu\varepsilon$, output voltage in mV, $G_f$ dimensionless, $\pi$ in $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ or $\mu\varepsilon^{-1}$.
* **KTU buzzwords for full marks:** "Piezoresistive effect," "energy band warping," "mobility modulation," "Wheatstone bridge nulling," "crystal anisotropy," "dummy gauge compensation."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 – INTRODUCTION: TEMPERATURE & THERMAL SENSING DOMAIN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 Scope)**
> **Temperature** is a fundamental physical quantity that characterizes the average kinetic energy of the microscopic particles (atoms/molecules) within a physical system. It is the *intensive thermodynamic property* that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies in thermal contact. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, temperature sensing forms the *perceptual front-end* of thermal regulation loops, providing measurable electrical signals proportional to thermal state changes in industrial, biomedical, and embedded applications.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stepping into a swimming pool. The instant your foot touches the water, your skin's nerve endings translate the *speed of water molecules colliding with your skin* into an electrical signal your brain interprets as "cold" or "warm." You have just acted as a biological temperature sensor. A physical temperature sensor does the *exact same job* — it converts the invisible, atomic-level kinetic motion into a **measurable electrical quantity** (Voltage, Resistance, or Current) that a microcontroller (like an Arduino or ESP32) can read and process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Module 2 begin with Temperature?**
> Temperature is the **most frequently measured process variable** in industrial automation (accounting for approximately **40–50%** of all sensor deployments worldwide). Mastering thermal sensors builds the foundation for understanding *all resistive*, *voltage-generating*, and *semiconductor-based* transducers encountered later in the course.

### Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Absolute Zero:** $0\text{ K} = -273.15^\circ\text{C} = -459.67^\circ\text{F}$ (the theoretical lower limit of thermodynamic temperature where molecular kinetic motion ceases).
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_{ref} = 0^\circ\text{C} = 273.15\text{ K}$ (used as the baseline for most temperature-to-electrical calibrations).
- **Triple Point of Water:** $0.01^\circ\text{C} = 273.16\text{ K}$ (the defining fixed point of the modern thermodynamic temperature scale, ITS-90).
- **Seebeck Coefficient baseline (standard):** Approximately **$40\text{ }\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$** for Type K thermocouples (used as a quick-mental-reference value).
- **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR or $\alpha$):** Measured in **$\Omega/\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$** or **%/°C**, indicating fractional resistance change per degree.

### The Four Pillars of Thermal Sensing (Module 2 Roadmap)

| # | Sensing Principle | Output Signal | Representative Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thermoelectric Effect** (Seebeck) | Voltage ($\mu$V) | Thermocouple |
| 2 | **Resistance vs. Temperature** (Ohmic) | Resistance ($\Omega$) | RTD, Thermistor |
| 3 | **Semiconductor Junction Voltage** | Voltage (V) | IC Sensor (LM35, AD590) |
| 4 | **Radiative / Pyroelectric** | Voltage / Charge | Pyrometer, Bolometer |

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU Module 2 derivations and problems are framed around these four pillars. You are expected to be able to *classify* any given temperature sensor into one of these categories during examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Temperature Cannot Be Measured Directly

A fundamental KTU conceptual favorite:

- Temperature has **no physical substance** — it is a *statistical* property describing the *distribution* of kinetic energy.
- Therefore, we **never measure temperature directly**. We always measure a *secondary effect* (volume change of mercury, EMF in a junction, resistance drift in a metal, or voltage shift in a semiconductor bandgap) and *infer* the temperature via a calibrated mathematical model.
- This is why **every temperature sensor requires a reference point** (cold-junction compensation for thermocouples, ice-bath calibration for legacy RTDs).

## 2.2 The Three Primary Temperature Scales

A $3$-equation conversion set is essential for KTU problems:

### 2.2.1 Celsius $\leftrightarrow$ Kelvin

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$$

### 2.2.2 Celsius $\leftrightarrow$ Fahrenheit

$$T(^\circ\text{F}) = \left[\frac{9}{5} \times T(^\circ\text{C})\right] + 32$$

### 2.2.3 Fahrenheit $\leftrightarrow$ Kelvin

$$T(\text{K}) = \left[\frac{5}{9} \times \left(T(^\circ\text{F}) - 32\right)\right] + 273.15$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** The ratio $\frac{9}{5}$ (or equivalently $1.8$) governs the *size* of a degree, while the offset $32$ governs the *zero-point shift* between Celsius and Fahrenheit.

## 2.3 Static and Dynamic Characteristics of a Temperature Sensor

Every KTU sensor problem (Module 2) eventually requires you to characterize the sensor using these static metrics:

| Parameter | Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Min & Max temperature the sensor can measure | $^\circ\text{C}$ or K |
| **Span** | Difference between max and min range | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness to true value (max error) | $^\circ\text{C}$ or %FS |
| **Sensitivity** | Output change per unit input change ($\Delta V / \Delta T$) | $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Linearity** | Deviation from ideal straight-line output | % of FS |
| **Resolution** | Smallest measurable temperature step | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Repeatability** | Ability to produce same output for same input | % of FS |
| **Hysteresis** | Output difference at the same input on rising vs. falling ramps | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Response Time ($\tau$)** | Time to reach $63.2\%$ of a step change | seconds |

## 2.4 The First-Order Thermal Model (Time Constant)

When a sensor is suddenly exposed to a temperature step, it does **not** respond instantly. The thermal system behaves like a first-order RC circuit, where *thermal resistance* $R_{th}$ is analogous to electrical resistance, and *thermal capacitance* $C_{th}$ is analogous to capacitance.

### Derivation of the Time-Constant Response

Let $T_s(t)$ be the sensor's sensed temperature, and $T_0$ the initial sensor temperature, and $T_\infty$ the ambient (step) temperature.

The governing differential equation from energy balance:

$$C_{th} \frac{dT_s}{dt} = \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{R_{th}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT_s}{dt} + \frac{T_s}{R_{th} C_{th}} = \frac{T_\infty}{R_{th} C_{th}}$$

Define the **thermal time constant** $\tau = R_{th} C_{th}$ (measured in seconds). The solution to this ODE, assuming initial condition $T_s(0) = T_0$, is:

$$T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The value $t = \tau$ corresponds to a sensor reaching $63.2\%$ of the total step change. This is a guaranteed $2$–$3$ mark question in KTU Module $2$ exams.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 2 - Introduction)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Celsius to Kelvin | $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ | Temperature conversion | K |
| 2 | Celsius to Fahrenheit | $T_F = \frac{9}{5} T_C + 32$ | Temperature conversion | $^\circ\text{F}$ |
| 3 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$ | Linear approximation | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | Steinhart-Hart simplified | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Thermocouple EMF | $\mathcal{E} = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck coefficient × ΔT | mV |
| 6 | IC Sensor Output (LM35) | $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C} \times T_C$ | Linear in Celsius | V |
| 7 | Thermal Time Constant | $T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$ | First-order response | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$ | Slope of transfer curve | $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 9 | Linearity Error | $e_L = \frac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Non-linearity quantification | % FS |
| 10 | Heat Transfer (Steady) | $Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$ | Fourier's law analog | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Convention:** All temperatures inside exponential and logarithmic thermistor equations **must be in Kelvin**. Celsius will produce mathematically incorrect results.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal sensors are the *backbone* of:

- **HVAC systems** in smart buildings (zone-wise temperature control).
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** in electric vehicles (cell monitoring between $-20^\circ\text{C}$ and $60^\circ\text{C}$).
- **Industrial process control** (refinery, pharmaceutical, semiconductor fabrication ovens).
- **Medical instrumentation** (clinical thermometers, incubators, cryogenic preservation).
- **Aerospace telemetry** (engine exhaust pyrometers exceeding $1200^\circ\text{C}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Temperature Scale Conversion

### Given
A process is operating at $T = 86^\circ\text{F}$. Find the equivalent temperature in Kelvin.

### Step 1 — Identify the target scale
We need the final answer in **Kelvin**, so the chain of conversion is: $^\circ\text{F} \rightarrow ^\circ\text{C} \rightarrow \text{K}$.

### Step 2 — Convert Fahrenheit to Celsius
Using the inverse of the standard formula:

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \left[ T(^\circ\text{F}) - 32 \right]$$

Substituting $T(^\circ\text{F}) = 86$:

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \left[ 86 - 32 \right]$$

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \times 54$$

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{270}{9} = 30^\circ\text{C}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Celsius to Kelvin

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$$

$$T(\text{K}) = 30 + 273.15$$

$$T(\text{K}) = 303.15 \text{ K}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{T = 303.15 \text{ K}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — First-Order Thermal Response

### Problem Statement
A thermistor with thermal time constant $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$ is initially at $T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C}$ and is suddenly immersed in a fluid at $T_\infty = 95^\circ\text{C}$. Find (a) the temperature of the sensor after $t = 10 \text{ s}$, and (b) the time required to reach $90\%$ of the final temperature.

### Part (a) — Temperature at $t = 10 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Identify given variables.**
$T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 95^\circ\text{C}$, $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$, $t = 10 \text{ s}$.

**Step 2 — Write the first-order response equation.**

$$T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature difference (driving force).**

$$\Delta T = T_\infty - T_0 = 95 - 25 = 70^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\frac{t}{\tau} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponential term.**

$$e^{-1.25} \approx 0.2865$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the response equation.**

$$T_s(10) = 95 - (70 \times 0.2865)$$

$$T_s(10) = 95 - 20.055$$

$$T_s(10) = 74.945^\circ\text{C}$$

### Part (a) Final Answer
$$\boxed{T_s(10) \approx 74.95^\circ\text{C}}$$

### Part (b) — Time to reach $90\%$ of the final temperature

**Step 1 — Define "90% of final" mathematically.**

We require the sensor to reach $90\%$ of the *temperature change*:

$$T_s(t) - T_0 = 0.90 \times (T_\infty - T_0)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the general equation.**

$$T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau} - T_0 = 0.90 (T_\infty - T_0)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the left-hand side.**

$$(T_\infty - T_0) \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right] = 0.90 (T_\infty - T_0)$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $(T_\infty - T_0)$ from both sides** (since it is non-zero).

$$1 - e^{-t/\tau} = 0.90$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the exponential term.**

$$e^{-t/\tau} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

**Step 6 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.10)$$

$$\frac{t}{\tau} = -\ln(0.10) = 2.3026$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t$.**

$$t = 2.3026 \times \tau = 2.3026 \times 8$$

$$t = 18.42 \text{ s}$$

### Part (b) Final Answer
$$\boxed{t_{90\%} \approx 18.42 \text{ s}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Sensitivity & Linearity Error

### Problem Statement
A temperature sensor produces $V_{out} = 0.5 \text{ V}$ at $T = 20^\circ\text{C}$ and $V_{out} = 2.5 \text{ V}$ at $T = 70^\circ\text{C}$. The ideal (theoretical) line gives $V_{ideal} = 2.2 \text{ V}$ at $T = 50^\circ\text{C}$. If the full-scale output is $V_{FS} = 4.0 \text{ V}$, compute (a) the sensitivity, and (b) the linearity error at $50^\circ\text{C}$.

### Part (a) — Sensitivity

$$S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T} = \frac{V_{out,2} - V_{out,1}}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$S = \frac{2.5 - 0.5}{70 - 20} = \frac{2.0}{50}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.04 \text{ V}/^\circ\text{C} = 40 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}}$$

### Part (b) — Linearity Error

$$e_L = \frac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = \frac{\vert 2.2 - V_{actual} \vert}{4.0} \times 100\%$$

The actual output at $50^\circ\text{C}$ using the linear interpolation from the two calibration points:

$$V_{actual}(50) = V_{out,1} + S \times (50 - 20) = 0.5 + 0.04 \times 30 = 1.7 \text{ V}$$

Therefore:

$$e_L = \frac{\vert 1.7 - 2.2 \vert}{4.0} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = \frac{0.5}{4.0} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{e_L = 12.5\% \text{ FS}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection at a Glance

| Criterion | Thermocouple | RTD (Pt100) | Thermistor (NTC) | IC Sensor (LM35) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $-200^\circ\text{C}$ to $2300^\circ\text{C}$ | $-200^\circ\text{C}$ to $850^\circ\text{C}$ | $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $150^\circ\text{C}$ | $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $150^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Sensitivity** | $\sim 40 \text{ }\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ (low) | $\sim 0.385 \text{ }\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$ (medium) | $-4\% /^\circ\text{C}$ (very high) | $10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}$ (high) |
| **Linearity** | Moderate | Excellent | Poor (exponential) | Excellent |
| **Self-Powered?** | Yes (Seebeck) | No (needs excitation) | No (needs excitation) | No (needs excitation) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium–High | Low | Low |
| **Typical Use** | Furnaces, exhaust | Industrial labs | Inrush protection, HVAC | Microcontroller interfacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb:**
> - **High temperature + harsh environment** → Thermocouple.
> - **High accuracy + industrial process** → RTD (Pt100/Pt1000).
> - **High sensitivity + narrow range** → NTC Thermistor.
> - **Direct ADC interface + prototyping** → IC Sensor (LM35 / DS18B20).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Temperature Sensor Signal Chain in a CPS

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the signal flow from a physical temperature source to a digital display/actuator command in a Cyber-Physical System.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    physSource["PHYSICAL HEAT SOURCE"]
    sensElement["SENSOR ELEMENT RTD TC NTC IC"]
    condCircuit["CONDITIONING CIRCUIT AMPLIFIER FILTER BRIDGE"]
    adcNode["ADC MODULE MICROCONTROLLER"]
    procNode["PROCESSING UNIT ARDUINO ESP32 RASPBERRY PI"]
    displayNode["OUTPUT HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE ACTUATOR COMMAND"]
    fbNode["FEEDBACK LOOP TO CONTROL SYSTEM"]

    physSource -->|Thermal Energy| sensElement
    sensElement -->|Millivolt OR Resistance| condCircuit
    condCircuit -->|0 to 5 Volt Signal| adcNode
    adcNode -->|Digital Code 10 bit 12 bit| procNode
    procNode -->|Display OR PWM OR Decision| displayNode
    displayNode --> fbNode
    fbNode -->|Closed Loop Control Action| physSource
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Sensor Classification Logic for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["TEMPERATURE INPUT"]
    qSelf{"IS IT SELF POWERED VOLTAGE OUTPUT"}
    qRes{"DOES RESISTANCE CHANGE LINEARLY WITH T"}
    qSemi{"IS IT A SEMICONDUCTOR BANDGAP DEVICE"}
    qRad{"DOES IT MEASURE EMITTED INFRARED RADIATION"}
    outTC["THERMOCOUPLE SEEBECK EFFECT"]
    outRTD["RTD METALLIC RESISTANCE PT100"]
    outTHERM["THERMISTOR NTC OR PTC"]
    outIC["IC SENSOR LM35 DS18B20 AD590"]
    outRAD["PYROMETER OR BOLOMETER"]

    startA --> qSelf
    qSelf -->|YES| outTC
    qSelf -->|NO| qRes
    qRes -->|YES POSITIVE COEFFICIENT| outRTD
    qRes -->|NO EXPONENTIAL RESPONSE| qSemi
    qSemi -->|YES BANDGAP JUNCTION| outIC
    qSemi -->|NO RESISTIVE CERAMIC| outTHERM
    qRes -->|NONE EM RADIATION| qRad
    qRad -->|YES| outRAD
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Thermal CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANT["PHYSICAL PLANT DOMAIN"]
        heater1["HEATER ELEMENT"]
        tank1["WATER TANK"]
    end

    subgraph SENSING["SENSING DOMAIN MODULE 2"]
        ts1["TEMPERATURE SENSOR PT100"]
        sig1["WHEATSTONE BRIDGE"]
        amp1["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    end

    subgraph CYBER["CYBER DOMAIN"]
        mcu1["MCU WITH ADC AND PID ALGORITHM"]
        dac1["PWM OR DAC OUTPUT"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION["ACTUATION DOMAIN"]
        driver1["POWER DRIVER MOSFET OR SSR"]
        heaterActuator["HEATER COIL OR PELTIER MODULE"]
    end

    heater1 --> tank1
    tank1 -->|Heat Transfer| ts1
    ts1 -->|Resistance| sig1
    sig1 -->|Millivolt| amp1
    amp1 -->|0 to 5 Volt| mcu1
    mcu1 -->|Control Signal| dac1
    dac1 -->|Gate Drive| driver1
    driver1 -->|Electrical Power| heaterActuator
    heaterActuator -->|Thermal Output| heater1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the CPS Diagram:** The sensor is the *eyes* of the system, the microcontroller is the *brain*, and the actuator is the *muscle*. KTU Module 2 focuses almost entirely on the **Sensing Domain** block.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define temperature. Why is it considered an intensive thermodynamic property? List the four major classes of temperature sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Temperature is the physical quantity that quantifies the average kinetic energy of the microscopic particles of a thermodynamic system. It is classified as an **intensive property** because it does **not depend on the size or amount of matter** in the system; for example, two cups of water at $80^\circ\text{C}$ and a swimming pool at $80^\circ\text{C}$ share the same temperature, even though their thermal energies differ vastly.

The four major classes are:
1. **Thermoelectric sensors** — Thermocouple.
2. **Resistive sensors** — RTD (Pt100) and Thermistor.
3. **Semiconductor junction sensors** — LM35, AD590.
4. **Radiation / Pyroelectric sensors** — Pyrometer, Bolometer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write "transducer" instead of "sensor" and lose a mark. KTU strictly uses the term **sensor** in Module 2.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the static characteristics: Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Linearity. State their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** — The maximum deviation of the sensor output from the true (ideal) value. Mathematically: $\text{Accuracy} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$.
- **Sensitivity** — The ratio of the change in output to the change in input. Mathematically: $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$, units: $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Linearity** — The closeness of the calibration curve to a straight line. The linearity error is: $e_L = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions offer an internal choice. The KTU pattern (Module 2) typically provides *one compulsory 7-mark part* and a *choice between two 7-mark sub-parts*. To best serve your preparation, the full $14$-mark version is provided below with a parallel alternative.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a closed-loop temperature control system used in a Cyber-Physical System. Identify the role of the **temperature sensor** in the loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor is initially at $T_0 = 30^\circ\text{C}$. It is placed in an oven at $T_\infty = 180^\circ\text{C}$. The sensor exhibits a first-order response with a thermal time constant $\tau = 12 \text{ s}$. Determine:
- (i) The sensor temperature after $t = 24 \text{ s}$.
- (ii) The time required to reach within $5^\circ\text{C}$ of the final temperature.
- (iii) Justify whether the sensor has reached $99.3\%$ of the final temperature at $t = 5\tau$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The closed-loop thermal CPS consists of **four domains**:

1. **Plant Domain** — Heater coil / furnace, where heat is generated.
2. **Sensing Domain** — Temperature sensor (e.g., Pt100 RTD) that converts thermal energy to a measurable electrical quantity.
3. **Cyber Domain** — Microcontroller / PLC that reads the ADC, executes the PID algorithm, and outputs a control signal.
4. **Actuation Domain** — Power driver (MOSFET / SSR) that modulates the heater power.

**Role of the temperature sensor:** It is the *perceptual front-end* of the loop. Without it, the cyber layer has no knowledge of the plant's thermal state, making **feedback control impossible**. The sensor *transduces* the physical temperature into a precise electrical signal (voltage / resistance) that is proportional, repeatable, and low in noise.

A labelled block diagram is provided in **Section 4.3** of these notes — refer to the *Functional Architecture – Closed-Loop Thermal CPS* figure.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $T_0 = 30^\circ\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 180^\circ\text{C}$, $\tau = 12 \text{ s}$.

**(i) Sensor temperature at $t = 24 \text{ s}$:**

The first-order response equation is:

$$T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

Substituting:

$$T_s(24) = 180 - (180 - 30) e^{-24/12}$$

$$T_s(24) = 180 - 150 \, e^{-2}$$

$$T_s(24) = 180 - 150 \times 0.1353$$

$$T_s(24) = 180 - 20.30$$

$$\boxed{T_s(24) \approx 159.70^\circ\text{C}}$$

**(ii) Time to reach within $5^\circ\text{C}$ of $T_\infty$:**

We require $T_s(t) \geq T_\infty - 5 = 175^\circ\text{C}$:

$$175 = 180 - 150 \, e^{-t/12}$$

$$150 \, e^{-t/12} = 5$$

$$e^{-t/12} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.03333$$

$$-\frac{t}{12} = \ln(0.03333) = -3.4012$$

$$t = 12 \times 3.4012$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 40.81 \text{ s}}$$

**(iii) Justification of $99.3\%$ at $t = 5\tau$:**

At $t = 5\tau$:

$$T_s(5\tau) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-5}$$

$$T_s(5\tau) = 180 - 150 \times 0.00674$$

$$T_s(5\tau) = 180 - 1.011 \approx 178.99^\circ\text{C}$$

The fraction of total change achieved:

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{T_s - T_0}{T_\infty - T_0} = \frac{178.99 - 30}{150} = \frac{148.99}{150} = 0.9933 = 99.33\%$$

> Therefore, at $t = 5\tau$, the sensor has indeed reached $99.3\%$ of the final temperature. This confirms the universal **KTU rule-of-thumb** that *five time constants* is the practical settling time for a first-order sensor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with reference to temperature sensors, and write the governing mathematical expression for each: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Thermal Time Constant
- (ii) Sensitivity
- (iii) Linearity Error

**(b)** A Pt100 RTD has a resistance of $138.5 \text{ }\Omega$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$. Given that the resistance at $0^\circ\text{C}$ is $R_0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$, determine:
- (i) The temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha$.
- (ii) The expected resistance of the same RTD at $T = 250^\circ\text{C}$ (assuming linear behaviour).
- (iii) The actual resistance at $250^\circ\text{C}$ using the more accurate Callendar-Van Dusen equation (first three terms). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

- **(i) Thermal Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time required for a first-order temperature sensor to reach $63.2\%$ of its final steady-state value when subjected to a step change in temperature. Mathematical expression: $T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **(ii) Sensitivity ($S$):** The ratio of the change in sensor output to the corresponding change in temperature. Mathematical expression: $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$, units $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- **(iii) Linearity Error ($e_L$):** The maximum deviation of the actual sensor output from the ideal (straight-line) output, expressed as a percentage of full-scale. Mathematical expression: $e_L = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $R_0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$, $R_{100} = 138.5 \text{ }\Omega$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$.

**(i) Compute $\alpha$:**

The linear RTD equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$

Substituting $T = 100^\circ\text{C}$, $T_0 = 0^\circ\text{C}$:

$$138.5 = 100 \left[ 1 + \alpha (100 - 0) \right]$$

$$138.5 = 100 + 10000 \alpha$$

$$10000 \alpha = 38.5$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.00385 \text{ } \Omega/\Omega/^\circ\text{C} = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} /^\circ\text{C}}$$

> This is the standard nominal $\alpha$ for the European Curve (IEC 60751) Pt100 RTD, confirming the calibration.

**(ii) Linear estimate of $R$ at $250^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$R_{250} = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot 250 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.00385 \times 250 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.9625 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \times 1.9625$$

$$\boxed{R_{250,\,linear} = 196.25 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**(iii) Callendar-Van Dusen correction (first three terms):**

The Callendar-Van Dusen equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 \right)$$

For $T \geq 0^\circ\text{C}$, the standard coefficients for Pt100 are:
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$

Substituting $T = 250$:

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(250) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(250)^2 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.977075 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(62500) \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.977075 - 0.036094 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \times 1.940981$$

$$\boxed{R_{250,\,CVD} \approx 194.10 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**Comparison:** The linear estimate ($196.25 \text{ }\Omega$) differs from the accurate CVD prediction ($194.10 \text{ }\Omega$) by $2.15 \text{ }\Omega$, which is the *non-linearity* introduced at higher temperatures. This validates the necessity of the Callendar-Van Dusen correction in high-precision industrial RTDs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 2 (Introduction)**
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Always state units. Writing "$R = 100$" instead of "$R = 100 \text{ }\Omega$" costs a half-mark in KTU valuation.
> - **Kelvin vs Celsius in Exponentials:** Thermistor and CVD equations **require Kelvin**. Using Celsius in $e^{1/T}$ will produce a numerical answer that is wrong by a factor of hundreds, and the examiner will mark the entire step as zero.
> - **Skipping Intermediate Steps:** KTU expects $3$–$4$ visible steps in any $7$-mark derivation. Jumping directly to the final answer without substitution steps leads to **partial marking only**.
> - **Confusing Sensitivity with Linearity:** Sensitivity is the *slope*; Linearity is the *deviation from straightness*. Do not interchange them.
> - **Forgetting Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Thermocouples are a higher-order topic, but even in Module 2's introductory questions, students lose marks by not mentioning that the **reference junction must be compensated** to obtain an absolute temperature.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist – Module 2 Introduction**

- ☐ Temperature is an *intensive* thermodynamic property; we **infer** it via secondary effects.
- ☐ The three scales are Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin. Conversions use the $9/5$ ratio and offsets of $32$ and $273.15$.
- ☐ **Four pillars** of temperature sensing: Thermoelectric, Resistive, Semiconductor, and Radiative.
- ☐ **Static characteristics** to memorize: Range, Span, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Linearity, Resolution, Repeatability, Hysteresis.
- ☐ **First-order thermal response** $T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ☐ At $t = \tau$, the sensor has reached **$63.2\%$** of the total change.
- ☐ At $t = 5\tau$, the sensor is considered to have **practically settled** ($99.3\%$).
- ☐ The thermal time constant $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$, analogous to an electrical RC circuit.
- ☐ Pt100 RTD has a nominal $\alpha = 0.00385 /^\circ\text{C}$ (European Curve, IEC 60751).
- ☐ LM35 IC sensor outputs $10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}$ with linear scaling in Celsius.
- ☐ **Always use Kelvin** in thermistor exponential equations.
- ☐ Sensitivity = slope = $\Delta V / \Delta T$, in units of $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- ☐ Linearity error is expressed as a **% of full-scale (FS)** output.
- ☐ A temperature sensor is the *perceptual front-end* of any Cyber-Physical thermal control loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Thermometric Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gas Thermometric Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gas Thermometric Sensor** is a primary temperature measurement device that exploits the predictable thermodynamic relationship between the pressure, volume, and temperature of an ideal (or near-ideal) gas to infer the unknown temperature of a system. The fundamental governing relation is the **Ideal Gas Law**, expressed as:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

where the symbols carry their standard thermodynamic meaning. In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, gas thermometric sensors are classified under **Primary Thermometers** because they directly relate a measurable macroscopic property (pressure or volume) to the thermodynamic temperature scale, without requiring prior calibration against another temperature standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Gas thermometric sensors are studied as part of the *Temperature Sensors* cluster. They form the *thermodynamic foundation* upon which practical temperature scales (ITS-90, IPTS-68) are anchored. The **triple point of water (273.16 K)** is the defining reference.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **balloon sealed inside an air-conditioned room**. If you switch the AC off and the room heats up, the gas molecules inside the balloon move faster and push outward more vigorously. The balloon expands (volume changes at constant pressure). Conversely, if you instead keep the balloon trapped inside a rigid glass box (constant volume) and cool the room, the molecules slow down and the pressure they exert on the glass walls drops. A gas thermometer does precisely this — it either *watches the pressure change at fixed volume*, or *watches the volume change at fixed pressure*, and converts that change into a temperature reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Anchor:** The gas itself is the *sensing element*. Unlike a thermocouple (which depends on Seebeck coefficient calibration) or an RTD (which depends on resistance-temperature mapping), the gas thermometer relies on a *fundamental physical law*. This makes it a **primary standard** in metrology.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following constants and reference points are *non-negotiable* for any KTU answer on this topic:

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann Constant (k):** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Triple Point of Water:** $T_{tp} = 273.16 \ \text{K}$ (exactly, by SI definition)
- **Absolute Zero:** $0 \ \text{K} = -273.15 \ \degree\text{C}$
- **Standard Reference:** The gas thermometer defines the **Kelvin** through the fixed points of the **International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)**.

## 1.4 Classification of Gas Thermometers

Gas thermometric sensors are broadly classified into two operative modes:

| Mode | Fixed Variable | Measured Variable | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant-Volume Gas Thermometer (CVGT)** | Volume (V) | Pressure (P) | Highest precision, primary standards laboratory |
| **Constant-Pressure Gas Thermometer (CPGT)** | Pressure (P) | Volume (V) | Less common, illustrative/educational |
| **Filled-System Thermometer** | Bulb + capillary volume | Pressure of working gas | Industrial process measurement |
| **Partial Pressure (Vapor Pressure) Thermometer** | — | Vapor pressure of condensed fluid | Cryogenic range (e.g., helium vapor pressure) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Logical Chain

The working of a gas thermometric sensor can be decomposed into a precise cause-and-effect chain. Each link must be understood in sequence because KTU questions frequently test *which variable is held constant* and *which is measured*.

**Step 1 — Establish a Closed Gas System:**
A fixed mass of gas (typically helium, hydrogen, or nitrogen) is sealed inside a bulb connected to a manometer or pressure transducer. The number of moles $n$ is therefore a *time-invariant constant*.

**Step 2 — Expose the Bulb to the Unknown Temperature:**
The gas in the bulb comes into thermal equilibrium with the medium whose temperature is to be measured. The connecting capillary is kept at a *known reference temperature* to avoid spurious thermal expansion errors.

**Step 3 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law:**
For the same mass of gas at two different states (initial and final), the combined gas law is invoked:

$$
\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Hold One Variable Constant and Measure the Other:**
- In **CVGT**: $V_1 = V_2$, so $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (P_2 / P_1)$
- In **CPGT**: $P_1 = P_2$, so $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (V_2 / V_1)$

**Step 5 — Convert to Engineering Units:**
The resulting absolute temperature in Kelvin is reported, then optionally converted to Celsius via $T(^\circ\text{C}) = T(\text{K}) - 273.15$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" Matters:** KTU examiners love to ask *"Why is helium preferred over air in precision gas thermometers?"* The answer is that helium has a **monatomic, inert, near-ideal** character at all practical temperatures, so it obeys $PV = nRT$ with negligible real-gas corrections (van der Waals $a$, $b$ terms are extremely small).

## 2.2 Constant-Volume Gas Thermometer (CVGT) — Detailed Mechanics

A CVGT uses a **rigid bulb** of fixed volume $V$. The gas pressure is measured by a precision manometer (mercury column, electronic transducer, or dead-weight gauge). The temperature is computed by referencing the unknown state to a known state — typically the **triple point of water** because $T_{tp} = 273.16 \ \text{K}$ is a *defining fixed point* with extremely low uncertainty (better than $\pm 0.0001 \ \text{K}$).

The defining temperature relation is:

$$
T = 273.16 \ \text{K} \times \frac{P}{P_{tp}}
$$

where $P$ is the measured pressure at temperature $T$, and $P_{tp}$ is the pressure at the triple point of water. The **dead space** (the volume of gas in the capillary and manometer lines that is *not* at the measured temperature) introduces a small but significant correction term in precision work.

## 2.3 Constant-Pressure Gas Thermometer (CPGT)

A CPGT uses a movable piston or a flexible bellows to maintain constant pressure. The volume of the gas is read directly (or via a calibrated displacement transducer). CPGTs are mechanically more complex than CVGTs and are mainly of *academic* and *historical* interest — they appear in KTU questions to test the student's ability to *choose the correct combined gas law form*.

## 2.4 Filled-System (Pressure-Spring) Gas Thermometer

This is the **industrial** counterpart of the laboratory gas thermometer. It consists of:
- A **bulb** (sensing element) immersed in the process
- A **capillary tube** transmitting pressure
- A **Bourdon tube** or bellows that converts pressure into mechanical displacement
- A **pointer** indicating temperature on a calibrated dial

The filled system is filled with an inert gas (nitrogen) at high pressure. The bulb is *small* (to minimize thermal lag) and the system is *sealed*. This is what an engineer would actually install on a pipe in a chemical plant.

## 2.5 Vapor-Pressure Thermometers (Partial-Pressure Family)

At very low temperatures (cryogenics), the gas thermometer is impractical because real gases deviate strongly from ideality. Instead, a condensed fluid (e.g., liquid helium, liquid hydrogen) is used. The vapor pressure above the liquid is a strong function of temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron relation), and measuring this vapor pressure gives the temperature.

The governing equation is:

$$
\ln P = -\frac{L}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C
$$

where $L$ is the latent heat of vaporization (assumed constant over a small temperature range) and $C$ is an integration constant. Vapor-pressure thermometers are used in the range **$0.5 \ \text{K}$ to about $5 \ \text{K}$** for helium-4 and up to higher ranges for nitrogen/oxygen systems.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Engineering Use / Context |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | Foundation equation for all gas thermometers |
| Combined Gas Law | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Two-state temperature computation |
| CVGT Relation | $T = 273.16 \, \text{K} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{tp}}$ | Primary standard temperature measurement |
| CPGT Relation | $T = T_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ (at constant $P$) | Less common, but appears in derivations |
| Boltzmann Form | $PV = NkT$ (with $N$ molecules) | Microscopic interpretation |
| Real Gas Correction (v.d. Waals) | $\left(P + \dfrac{a n^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT$ | Correction for high-pressure or low-T regimes |
| Vapor Pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) | $\ln P = -\dfrac{L}{R T} + C$ | Cryogenic vapor-pressure thermometer |
| Dead-Space Correction (CVGT) | $T = 273.16 \cdot \dfrac{P \, (1 + \alpha \, T_{ds}/T_{tp})}{P_{tp} + P_{ds}}$ | Precision correction for non-bulb gas volume |
| Celsius to Kelvin | $T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$ | Unit conversion (always required) |
