topics.title: Acoustic Temperature Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Temperature Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Temperature Sensor (ATS):** A non-contact, indirect temperature measurement device that determines the absolute temperature of a gaseous medium by precisely measuring the **time-of-flight (ToF)** of an acoustic wave traversing a known reference path length, exploiting the deterministic dependence of the speed of sound on the absolute temperature of the propagation medium.

In the context of KTU 2024 Cyber-Physical Systems, the acoustic temperature sensor belongs to the class of *primary effect-based indirect transducers*, where the measurand (temperature) is converted into a measurable intermediate variable (acoustic transit time).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Echo Clapping Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are standing in a long, empty hallway and you clap your hands once. You hear the echo come back after a short time. If the air inside the hallway is **cold**, the echo returns **late** (sound is sluggish). If the air is **hot**, the echo returns **quickly** (sound molecules vibrate faster, transmitting the disturbance more rapidly). An Acoustic Temperature Sensor does exactly this — it claps (transmits) an ultrasonic pulse and times how long the echo takes to come back. The "clap time" is mathematically converted into a temperature reading. No thermometer is ever in contact with the air!

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reference Values used in Acoustic Thermometry**
>
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$
> - **Ratio of Specific Heats for Air:** $\gamma = 1.402$ (diatomic ideal gas)
> - **Molar Mass of Air:** $M = 28.97 \text{ g/mol} = 0.02897 \text{ kg/mol}$
> - **Speed of Sound at Standard Conditions (T₀ = 273.15 K):** $c_0 = 331.3 \text{ m/s}$
> - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 0^{\circ}\text{C} = 273.15 \text{ K}$
> - **Pressure Sensitivity Coefficient:** Typically expressed per kPa deviation from $101.325 \text{ kPa}$

### 1.4 Working Principle — The Acoustic Equation of State

The fundamental equation linking the speed of sound in an **ideal gas** to its absolute temperature is derived from Newtonian mechanics and thermodynamic state equations:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T}{M}}$$

where:
- $c$ = speed of sound in the gas (m/s)
- $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ = adiabatic index (ratio of specific heats)
- $R$ = universal gas constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $M$ = molar mass of the gas

Since $R$, $M$, and $\gamma$ are known constants for a given gas (e.g., dry air), the speed of sound $c$ is a **monotonic, single-valued function** of absolute temperature $T$ alone (assuming pressure is compensated for).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The acoustic temperature sensor operates through a deterministic sequence of events, each mathematically traceable to the governing physics:

1. **Pulse Generation:** A high-frequency acoustic burst (typically in the ultrasonic range, **40 kHz to 200 kHz**) is generated by an electro-acoustic transducer (piezoelectric transmitter). The frequency selection minimizes environmental noise interference.

2. **Propagation Through Medium:** The acoustic wavefront propagates through the gaseous medium at a velocity $c$ governed strictly by the medium's thermodynamic state. The medium acts as the *sensing element* itself.

3. **Detection at Receiver:** A second transducer (piezoelectric receiver/microphone) detects the wavefront after it has traversed a precisely known geometric path $L$ (the *baseline*).

4. **Time-of-Flight Measurement:** High-precision timing circuitry (often using TDC — Time-to-Digital Converters, or counters driven by crystal oscillators) measures the elapsed time $t$ between transmission and reception.

5. **Computational Conversion:** A microcontroller applies the inversion of the acoustic velocity equation to compute $T$ from the measured $t$.

6. **Pressure Compensation (Optional):** Since the speed of sound also weakly depends on static pressure $P$, high-precision systems incorporate a co-located pressure sensor to apply a correction term.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Physics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the speed of sound depend on temperature?**
>
> Sound is a mechanical longitudinal wave — it propagates via collisions between adjacent gas molecules. The **average kinetic energy of these molecules is directly proportional to absolute temperature** (kinetic theory of gases). When the gas is hotter, molecules move faster, collide more frequently, and transfer momentum more rapidly, causing the pressure disturbance (sound) to propagate at a higher velocity. This is a direct macroscopic manifestation of the microscopic Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table — All Equations Required for Examination Problems**

| Equation # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|:----------:|:-------------|:------------------|:------------------|:-----------------|
| **Eq. 1** | Ideal Gas Sound Speed | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | $T$ in K, $c$ in m/s | Foundation equation |
| **Eq. 2** | Linearized Air Approximation | $c \approx 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta$ | $\theta$ in °C, $c$ in m/s | Valid for $-20^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+40^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Eq. 3** | Time-of-Flight | $t = \dfrac{L}{c}$ | $L$ in m, $c$ in m/s, $t$ in s | For direct path |
| **Eq. 4** | Round-Trip ToF | $t = \dfrac{2L}{c}$ | Same as Eq. 3 | For reflector-based setup |
| **Eq. 5** | Temperature from Velocity | $T = \dfrac{c^{2} \cdot M}{\gamma \cdot R}$ | $T$ in K | Inverted form of Eq. 1 |
| **Eq. 6** | Temperature from ToF | $T = \dfrac{L^{2} \cdot M}{t^{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot R}$ | $T$ in K | Substituting Eq. 3 into Eq. 5 |
| **Eq. 7** | Pressure Compensation | $c_{\text{corr}} = c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{ref} \cdot P}{P_{ref} \cdot T}}$ | $P$ in Pa | Reduces humidity/pressure error |
| **Eq. 8** | Sensitivity | $\dfrac{dc}{dT} = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R}{M T}}$ | m/s per K | Decreases at higher $T$ |
| **Eq. 9** | Resolution Limit | $\Delta T = \dfrac{2T}{L} \cdot c \cdot \Delta t$ | $\Delta t$ = timing resolution | Sets the minimum detectable $\Delta T$ |
| **Eq. 10** | Molar Mass of Air | $M_{air} = 0.02897 \text{ kg/mol}$ | Constant | Use for dry air at sea level |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial & Research Applications**
>
> - **Power Plant Stack Monitoring:** Continuous exhaust gas temperature measurement without contact with corrosive flue gases.
> - **Indoor Climate Control (HVAC):** Uniform spatial averaging across a room, avoiding localized hot/cold spot errors of point sensors.
> - **Meteorological Stations:** Sonic anemometers and thermometers on weather buoys and towers.
> - **Inert Gas Atmospheres:** Temperature profiling in cleanrooms, semiconductor fabs, and glove boxes (where N₂ or Ar is used).
> - **Aerospace:** High-altitude balloon-borne atmospheric temperature profiling.
> - **Acoustic Calorimetry in Engines:** In-cylinder temperature measurement in IC engines during combustion cycles.

### 2.5 Architectural Block Diagram of a Typical ATS

The sensor system can be decomposed into five functional blocks, each with a specific role in the transduction chain:

> **Input (Physical Domain) → Transduction → Signal Conditioning → Computation → Output (Digital Domain)**
>
> `Temperature in Gas` ➔ `Speed of Sound c(T)` ➔ `Time-of-Flight t` ➔ `Voltage Pulse` ➔ `Digital Count` ➔ `Temperature Reading T`

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Speed of Sound from First Principles (Newton-Laplace Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation connects kinetic theory of gases to the macroscopic speed of sound. KTU examiners frequently award full marks (7 marks) only when the thermodynamic origin of the equation is stated explicitly.

**Starting Point — Newton's Model for an Adiabatic Sound Wave:**

For a sound wave propagating through a gas, we assume the compression and rarefaction occur **adiabatically** (i.e., faster than the timescale of heat conduction, governed by the Péclet number). The pressure and density of a sound wave are related by:

$$P \cdot V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating implicitly with respect to volume $V$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} \cdot V^{\gamma} + P \cdot \gamma \cdot V^{\gamma - 1} = 0$$

Dividing through by $V^{\gamma - 1}$:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} \cdot V + P \cdot \gamma = 0$$

Substituting $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ (where $\rho$ is mass density), and noting that $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = -\dfrac{dV}{V}$:

$$c^{2} = \frac{dP}{d\rho} = \frac{\gamma \cdot P}{\rho}$$

**Step 1: Conversion to Temperature using Ideal Gas Law**

From the ideal gas equation $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ and $\rho = M / V$:

$$P = \frac{\rho \cdot R \cdot T}{M}$$

**Step 2: Substituting the Pressure into the Sound Speed Equation**

$$c^{2} = \frac{\gamma \cdot \rho \cdot R \cdot T / M}{\rho}$$

The $\rho$ terms cancel exactly:

$$c^{2} = \frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T}{M}$$

**Step 3: Taking the Square Root — The Final Governing Equation**

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T}{M}}$$

This is the **Newton-Laplace Equation** for an ideal gas. Every acoustic thermometer ultimately relies on this relation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Linear Approximation for Air Near Standard Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Derivation: Converting the Nonlinear Equation to a Linear Engineering Approximation**

Starting from:

$$c(T) = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$$

Let $T = T_0 + \theta$, where $T_0 = 273.15 \text{ K}$ and $\theta$ is the Celsius temperature.

$$c(\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R (T_0 + \theta)}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_0}{M}} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{\theta}{T_0}}$$

Applying the Taylor series expansion $\sqrt{1 + x} \approx 1 + \dfrac{x}{2}$ for small $x$ (i.e., $\theta \ll T_0$):

$$c(\theta) \approx \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T_0}{M}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\theta}{2 T_0}\right)$$

$$c(\theta) \approx c_0 + \frac{c_0}{2 T_0} \cdot \theta$$

**Numerical Evaluation for Air:**

- $c_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.402 \times 8.314 \times 273.15}{0.02897}} = 331.3 \text{ m/s}$
- Coefficient: $\dfrac{c_0}{2 T_0} = \dfrac{331.3}{2 \times 273.15} = 0.6064 \text{ m/s per }^{\circ}\text{C}$

Therefore, the **linearized engineering form** is:

$$c(\theta) = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

where $\theta$ is in °C.

### 3.3 Derivation: Complete Temperature Recovery from a Time-of-Flight Measurement

**Given:**
- Known path length: $L$ (meters)
- Measured round-trip time: $t$ (seconds)
- For round-trip: $c = \dfrac{2L}{t}$

**Step 1: Square the velocity**

$$c^{2} = \frac{4L^{2}}{t^{2}}$$

**Step 2: Equate to the Newton-Laplace expression**

$$\frac{4L^{2}}{t^{2}} = \frac{\gamma R T}{M}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T$**

$$T = \frac{4 L^{2} M}{\gamma R t^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Celsius if needed**

$$\theta = T - 273.15$$

This is the **complete closed-form solution** for temperature recovery from a single acoustic transit time measurement. KTU 14-mark problems typically ask students to derive this and then plug in numerical values.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** An acoustic temperature sensor uses a baseline path length of $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and a single-pass configuration. The measured transit time is $t = 1.475 \text{ ms}$. The medium is dry air. Calculate the temperature of the air in °C.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- $t = 1.475 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$
- $M = 0.02897 \text{ kg/mol}$
- $\gamma = 1.402$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1: Compute the speed of sound**

$$c = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.5}{1.475 \times 10^{-3}} = 339.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $T$ in Kelvin**

$$T = \frac{c^{2} \cdot M}{\gamma \cdot R} = \frac{(339.0)^{2} \times 0.02897}{1.402 \times 8.314}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate numerator**

$$(339.0)^{2} = 114921 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$114921 \times 0.02897 = 3329.27 \text{ (kg·m}^2\text{)/(s}^2\text{·mol)}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate denominator**

$$1.402 \times 8.314 = 11.656 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5: Final Division**

$$T = \frac{3329.27}{11.656} = 285.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Celsius**

$$\theta = 285.6 - 273.15 = 12.45^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Verification using the linearized form:**

$$c = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad 339.0 = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta$$

$$\theta = \frac{339.0 - 331.3}{0.606} = \frac{7.7}{0.606} = 12.71^{\circ}\text{C}$$

The slight discrepancy ($\approx 0.26^{\circ}\text{C}$) arises from the linearization error inherent in dropping the higher-order Taylor terms. The exact value is preferred for engineering precision.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error

| Error Source | Effect on $T$ | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|:-------------|:--------------|:----------|:-----------|
| Humidity variation in air | $M$ changes ($\downarrow$ as water vapor replaces N₂, O₂) | $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ in $c$ | Co-located humidity sensor |
| Static pressure drift | $P$ deviates from $P_{\text{ref}}$ | $\Delta c \approx 0.6 \text{ m/s per 10 kPa}$ | Pressure sensor compensation |
| Wind / gas flow | Apparent path length changes (Doppler-like effect) | $\propto v_{\text{flow}}$ | Reflective geometry or wind correction |
| Path length thermal expansion | $L$ varies with ambient metal temperature | $\sim 12 \text{ ppm/K}$ (aluminum) | Invar or low-$\alpha$ alloys |
| Electronic timing jitter | $\Delta t$ in ToF measurement | $\sim 10 \text{ ns}$ (typical MCU) | TDC chips with sub-ns resolution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Acoustic Temperature Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller Trigger] --> B[Pulser Driver Circuit]
    B --> C[Ultrasonic Transmitter TX]
    C --> D[Gas Medium Path Length L]
    D --> E[Ultrasonic Receiver RX]
    E --> F[Amplifier and Filter Stage]
    F --> G[Threshold Detector]
    G --> H[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    H --> I[Microcontroller for Computation]
    I --> J[Display and Communication UART or I2C]
    K[Pressure Sensor Optional] --> I
    L[Humidity Sensor Optional] --> I
    M[Crystal Oscillator] --> H
    M --> I
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Signal Chain from Pulse to Temperature Readout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Power On Initialization] --> A1[Configure TDC and TX Frequency]
    A1 --> A2[Generate Ultrasonic Pulse Burst]
    A2 --> A3[Start TDC Timer]
    A3 --> A4[Wait for RX Echo Detection]
    A4 --> A5{Is Echo Detected within Timeout?}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Stop TDC and Latch t value]
    A5 -->|No| A7[Flag Error and Retry]
    A6 --> A8[Read Pressure and Humidity if available]
    A8 --> A9[Apply Compensation Equation]
    A9 --> A10[Compute T using c equals 2L over t]
    A10 --> A11[Convert K to degree C if needed]
    A11 --> A12[Output to Display or Bus]
    A7 --> A1
    style A2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A10 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A12 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Sensor Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Transmit: Trigger Command Received
    Transmit --> Listening: Pulse Emitted, Timer Started
    Listening --> Compute: Echo Received, Timer Stopped
    Listening --> Error: Timeout Exceeded
    Error --> Transmit: Auto Retry Up to N times
    Compute --> PressureComp: Apply Barometric Correction
    PressureComp --> Publish: Push to Display and Bus
    Publish --> Idle: Await Next Cycle
    Error --> Idle: Log Fault, Reset
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Transducer Pair Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensor Head
        TX1[Transmitter Piezo Disc 40 kHz]
        RX1[Receiver Piezo Disc 40 kHz]
    end
    TX1 -->|Acoustic Path L| RX1
    RX1 -->|Electrical Signal| AMP1[Preamp]
    AMP1 --> CMP1[Comparator]
    CMP1 --> MCU1[MCU with TDC Peripheral]
    TX1 -.->|Driven by| MCU1
    style TX1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RX1 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MCU1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Key Design Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two separate transducers in a co-linear arrangement?**
>
> The dual-transducer ("pitch-catch") geometry is preferred over a single transducer with a reflector because it eliminates the need for the transducer to recover from the high-voltage excitation pulse before listening for the weak return echo. This "blind time" would otherwise introduce a measurement dead-zone, severely limiting minimum path length and hence measurement resolution.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define an Acoustic Temperature Sensor and state the underlying physical principle on which it operates.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
An Acoustic Temperature Sensor is a non-contact temperature measurement device that determines the absolute temperature of a gaseous medium by measuring the time-of-flight of an acoustic wave across a known path length. The underlying physical principle is the **temperature dependence of the speed of sound in gases**, governed by the Newton-Laplace equation $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$, which establishes a deterministic one-to-one relationship between the speed of sound and the absolute temperature of the gas (assuming pressure compensation).

**Q2. State the Newton-Laplace equation for the speed of sound in an ideal gas and identify the meaning of each term.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
The Newton-Laplace equation is:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T}{M}}$$

where:
- $c$ = speed of sound in the gas (m/s)
- $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ = ratio of specific heats at constant pressure and volume (dimensionless)
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ = universal gas constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (Kelvin)
- $M$ = molar mass of the gas (kg/mol)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a) Derive the relationship between the speed of sound in an ideal gas and its absolute temperature, starting from first principles of thermodynamics.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumption of Adiabatic Propagation:**
Sound waves in gases are longitudinal pressure waves that propagate on a timescale much faster than thermal diffusion. Therefore, the process is assumed **adiabatic** (no heat exchange with surroundings).

For an adiabatic process: $P \cdot V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate Implicitly:**

$$V^{\gamma} \cdot dP + P \cdot \gamma V^{\gamma-1} \cdot dV = 0$$

Dividing by $V^{\gamma-1}$:

$$V \cdot dP + \gamma P \cdot dV = 0$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of density:**
Since $\rho = M/V$, we have $V = M/\rho$, and $dV/V = -d\rho/\rho$. Substituting:

$$\frac{dP}{d\rho} = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho}$$

The speed of sound is defined by $c^{2} = dP/d\rho$, hence:

$$c^{2} = \frac{\gamma P}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation:**
From $PV = nRT$ and $\rho = nM/V$, we obtain $P = \rho R T / M$.

**Step 5 — Final Substitution:**

$$c^{2} = \frac{\gamma \cdot \rho R T / M}{\rho} = \frac{\gamma R T}{M}$$

$$\boxed{c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}}$$

This is the required Newton-Laplace relationship. **[7 Marks — Awarded for: Assumption, Implicit differentiation, Density substitution, Ideal gas application, Final form]**

---

**Q.A (b) An acoustic temperature sensor with a baseline path length of 0.75 m measures a round-trip transit time of 4.40 ms in dry air. Calculate the temperature in °C. Given: $\gamma = 1.402$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$, $M = 0.02897 \text{ kg/mol}$.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Geometry:**
Round-trip ToF means the acoustic pulse travels $2L$ before returning. Therefore:

$$c = \frac{2L}{t} = \frac{2 \times 0.75}{4.40 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.5}{0.00440} = 340.91 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Invert the Newton-Laplace Equation:**

$$T = \frac{c^{2} \cdot M}{\gamma \cdot R}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator Computation:**

$$c^{2} = (340.91)^{2} = 116219.6 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 116219.6 \times 0.02897 = 3366.88 \text{ (kg·m}^2\text{)}/\text{(s}^2\text{·mol)}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator Computation:**

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.402 \times 8.314 = 11.656 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Final Division:**

$$T = \frac{3366.88}{11.656} = 288.85 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Celsius:**

$$\theta = T - 273.15 = 288.85 - 273.15 = 15.7^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Verification using linear approximation:**

$$c = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac{340.91 - 331.3}{0.606} = 15.84^{\circ}\text{C}$$

The values agree to within $0.14^{\circ}\text{C}$, confirming the result. **[7 Marks — Awarded for: Setup, Inversion, Numerical work, Unit consistency, Final answer]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an acoustic temperature sensor. List the major sub-systems and explain the role of each.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Explanation (Refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4):**

The acoustic temperature sensor consists of **six major sub-systems**:

1. **Acoustic Transmitter Stage:** Consists of a piezoelectric disc driven by a high-voltage pulser circuit. It generates a short ultrasonic burst (typically 8–16 cycles at 40 kHz) when triggered by the microcontroller.

2. **Propagation Medium (Gas Path):** The gaseous medium acts as the *sensing element* — its thermodynamic state (temperature) modulates the velocity of the acoustic wave.

3. **Acoustic Receiver Stage:** A matched piezoelectric transducer converts the arriving pressure wavefront into a small electrical signal (millivolts).

4. **Signal Conditioning Stage:** A low-noise preamplifier boosts the signal, followed by a bandpass filter centered at the transmit frequency (40 kHz) to reject ambient noise. A threshold comparator produces a clean digital pulse at the moment of echo arrival.

5. **Time Measurement Stage:** A Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) or high-frequency counter, referenced to a temperature-compensated crystal oscillator, latches the elapsed time between transmit trigger and received echo.

6. **Computation and Output Stage:** The microcontroller computes $c = 2L/t$, inverts the Newton-Laplace equation to obtain $T$, applies pressure and humidity compensation, and outputs the result via display or digital bus (UART, I²C, SPI).

**Operating Cycle:**
Trigger → Transmit → Propagate → Receive → Time → Compute → Output → Idle.

**[7 Marks — Awarded for: Block diagram clarity, Six sub-systems identified, Role of each, Operating sequence]**

---

**Q.B (b) Discuss the advantages, limitations, and major sources of error in acoustic temperature sensors.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**

1. **No thermal contact required** — ideal for corrosive, hazardous, or moving gas streams.
2. **Spatial averaging** — measures the *bulk* temperature along the path, avoiding local hot/cold spot errors of point sensors.
3. **No self-heating** — unlike thermistors or RTDs, the sensor does not introduce energy into the medium.
4. **Long-term stability** — no drift, no calibration decay, since the underlying physics is governed by universal constants ($\gamma$, $R$, $M$).
5. **Fast response** — limited only by acoustic transit time (milliseconds for meter-scale paths).

**Limitations:**

1. **Gas-specific** — the molar mass $M$ and $\gamma$ depend on gas composition; sensor must be calibrated or reconfigured for the target gas.
2. **Pressure and humidity dependence** — uncorrected, these can introduce errors of 0.5% to 2%.
3. **Limited precision at small path lengths** — because ToF $\propto L$, short paths yield small $t$ values, demanding extremely high time resolution.
4. **Susceptible to acoustic noise and flow** — turbulent eddies and ambient sound can corrupt the signal.
5. **Range constraints** — very high temperatures cause absorption and dispersion of acoustic waves, degrading accuracy.

**Major Sources of Error:**

1. **Path length uncertainty** — thermal expansion of the mechanical structure directly biases $L$ and therefore $T$.
2. **Timing jitter** — electronic noise on the threshold detector translates into time-base error.
3. **Humidity variation** — water vapor ($M = 0.018 \text{ kg/mol}$) replacing dry air molecules ($M \approx 0.029 \text{ kg/mol}$) alters the effective $M$, shifting the inferred $T$.
4. **Static pressure** — at the same temperature, $c$ varies as $\sqrt{P}$.
5. **Gas composition drift** — in mixed gas environments, $M$ and $\gamma$ change.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- Co-located pressure and humidity sensors with compensation equations.
- Invar or low-expansion coefficient structural materials.
- Differential (dual-path) measurement schemes to cancel common-mode errors.
- Digital filtering and signal averaging in the time domain.

**[7 Marks — Awarded for: Balanced coverage of advantages, limitations, and error sources, with at least 3 valid mitigation strategies]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Acoustic Temperature Sensor Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in round-trip ToF:** Many students write $c = L/t$ when the geometry is reflective, losing 1–2 marks. **Always read the problem statement carefully** — "single-pass" vs "round-trip" changes the formula by a factor of 2.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Celsius and Kelvin:** The Newton-Laplace equation strictly requires $T$ in **Kelvin**. If a problem gives a temperature in °C, you **must** add 273.15 before substitution. Forgetting this leads to absurd results (negative or wildly wrong $c$).
>
> 3. **Dropping the linearization step correctly:** When asked to derive the linear approximation $c = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta$, students often forget to mention the **Taylor series expansion** $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + x/2$ and the small-angle assumption $\theta \ll T_0$. Examiners specifically look for this.
>
> 4. **Not specifying the gas:** The speed of sound is gas-specific. An answer valid only for air without stating so will lose partial credit.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in derived expressions:** Always carry units through the calculation. A final answer of "$T = 285$" without a unit (K or °C) is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Acoustic Temperature Sensor**
>
> - ⭐ The governing equation is $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ — the **Newton-Laplace equation**.
> - ⭐ The sensor is **indirect**: it measures *time-of-flight*, not temperature directly.
> - ⭐ The propagation medium itself is the **sensing element** — its temperature is inferred from the wave velocity.
> - ⭐ The standard numerical form for air is $c = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot \theta$ (m/s, with $\theta$ in °C).
> - ⭐ For **round-trip** ToF, use $c = 2L/t$. For **single-pass**, use $c = L/t$.
> - ⭐ Always convert °C to K before applying the Newton-Laplace equation.
> - ⭐ Three major error sources: **path length variation, pressure drift, and humidity change**.
> - ⭐ Two main transducer configurations: **pitch-catch** (separate TX and RX) and **pulse-echo** (single transducer with reflector).
> - ⭐ Typical operating frequency: **40 kHz** (ultrasonic, immune to audible noise).
> - ⭐ Resolution improves with **longer path** $L$ and **better time resolution** $\Delta t$.
> - ⭐ Applications: HVAC, power plant stacks, weather stations, cleanrooms, engine research.
> - ⭐ **KTU Key Insight:** The relation is **monotonic** — there is exactly one temperature for each speed of sound, simplifying calibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 – INTRODUCTION: TEMPERATURE & THERMAL SENSING DOMAIN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 Scope)**
> **Temperature** is a fundamental physical quantity that characterizes the average kinetic energy of the microscopic particles (atoms/molecules) within a physical system. It is the *intensive thermodynamic property* that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies in thermal contact. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, temperature sensing forms the *perceptual front-end* of thermal regulation loops, providing measurable electrical signals proportional to thermal state changes in industrial, biomedical, and embedded applications.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stepping into a swimming pool. The instant your foot touches the water, your skin's nerve endings translate the *speed of water molecules colliding with your skin* into an electrical signal your brain interprets as "cold" or "warm." You have just acted as a biological temperature sensor. A physical temperature sensor does the *exact same job* — it converts the invisible, atomic-level kinetic motion into a **measurable electrical quantity** (Voltage, Resistance, or Current) that a microcontroller (like an Arduino or ESP32) can read and process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Module 2 begin with Temperature?**
> Temperature is the **most frequently measured process variable** in industrial automation (accounting for approximately **40–50%** of all sensor deployments worldwide). Mastering thermal sensors builds the foundation for understanding *all resistive*, *voltage-generating*, and *semiconductor-based* transducers encountered later in the course.

### Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Absolute Zero:** $0\text{ K} = -273.15^\circ\text{C} = -459.67^\circ\text{F}$ (the theoretical lower limit of thermodynamic temperature where molecular kinetic motion ceases).
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_{ref} = 0^\circ\text{C} = 273.15\text{ K}$ (used as the baseline for most temperature-to-electrical calibrations).
- **Triple Point of Water:** $0.01^\circ\text{C} = 273.16\text{ K}$ (the defining fixed point of the modern thermodynamic temperature scale, ITS-90).
- **Seebeck Coefficient baseline (standard):** Approximately **$40\text{ }\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$** for Type K thermocouples (used as a quick-mental-reference value).
- **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR or $\alpha$):** Measured in **$\Omega/\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$** or **%/°C**, indicating fractional resistance change per degree.

### The Four Pillars of Thermal Sensing (Module 2 Roadmap)

| # | Sensing Principle | Output Signal | Representative Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thermoelectric Effect** (Seebeck) | Voltage ($\mu$V) | Thermocouple |
| 2 | **Resistance vs. Temperature** (Ohmic) | Resistance ($\Omega$) | RTD, Thermistor |
| 3 | **Semiconductor Junction Voltage** | Voltage (V) | IC Sensor (LM35, AD590) |
| 4 | **Radiative / Pyroelectric** | Voltage / Charge | Pyrometer, Bolometer |

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU Module 2 derivations and problems are framed around these four pillars. You are expected to be able to *classify* any given temperature sensor into one of these categories during examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Temperature Cannot Be Measured Directly

A fundamental KTU conceptual favorite:

- Temperature has **no physical substance** — it is a *statistical* property describing the *distribution* of kinetic energy.
- Therefore, we **never measure temperature directly**. We always measure a *secondary effect* (volume change of mercury, EMF in a junction, resistance drift in a metal, or voltage shift in a semiconductor bandgap) and *infer* the temperature via a calibrated mathematical model.
- This is why **every temperature sensor requires a reference point** (cold-junction compensation for thermocouples, ice-bath calibration for legacy RTDs).

## 2.2 The Three Primary Temperature Scales

A $3$-equation conversion set is essential for KTU problems:

### 2.2.1 Celsius $\leftrightarrow$ Kelvin

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$$

### 2.2.2 Celsius $\leftrightarrow$ Fahrenheit

$$T(^\circ\text{F}) = \left[\frac{9}{5} \times T(^\circ\text{C})\right] + 32$$

### 2.2.3 Fahrenheit $\leftrightarrow$ Kelvin

$$T(\text{K}) = \left[\frac{5}{9} \times \left(T(^\circ\text{F}) - 32\right)\right] + 273.15$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** The ratio $\frac{9}{5}$ (or equivalently $1.8$) governs the *size* of a degree, while the offset $32$ governs the *zero-point shift* between Celsius and Fahrenheit.

## 2.3 Static and Dynamic Characteristics of a Temperature Sensor

Every KTU sensor problem (Module 2) eventually requires you to characterize the sensor using these static metrics:

| Parameter | Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Min & Max temperature the sensor can measure | $^\circ\text{C}$ or K |
| **Span** | Difference between max and min range | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Accuracy** | Closeness to true value (max error) | $^\circ\text{C}$ or %FS |
| **Sensitivity** | Output change per unit input change ($\Delta V / \Delta T$) | $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Linearity** | Deviation from ideal straight-line output | % of FS |
| **Resolution** | Smallest measurable temperature step | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Repeatability** | Ability to produce same output for same input | % of FS |
| **Hysteresis** | Output difference at the same input on rising vs. falling ramps | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Response Time ($\tau$)** | Time to reach $63.2\%$ of a step change | seconds |

## 2.4 The First-Order Thermal Model (Time Constant)

When a sensor is suddenly exposed to a temperature step, it does **not** respond instantly. The thermal system behaves like a first-order RC circuit, where *thermal resistance* $R_{th}$ is analogous to electrical resistance, and *thermal capacitance* $C_{th}$ is analogous to capacitance.

### Derivation of the Time-Constant Response

Let $T_s(t)$ be the sensor's sensed temperature, and $T_0$ the initial sensor temperature, and $T_\infty$ the ambient (step) temperature.

The governing differential equation from energy balance:

$$C_{th} \frac{dT_s}{dt} = \frac{T_\infty - T_s}{R_{th}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT_s}{dt} + \frac{T_s}{R_{th} C_{th}} = \frac{T_\infty}{R_{th} C_{th}}$$

Define the **thermal time constant** $\tau = R_{th} C_{th}$ (measured in seconds). The solution to this ODE, assuming initial condition $T_s(0) = T_0$, is:

$$T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The value $t = \tau$ corresponds to a sensor reaching $63.2\%$ of the total step change. This is a guaranteed $2$–$3$ mark question in KTU Module $2$ exams.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 2 - Introduction)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Celsius to Kelvin | $T_K = T_C + 273.15$ | Temperature conversion | K |
| 2 | Celsius to Fahrenheit | $T_F = \frac{9}{5} T_C + 32$ | Temperature conversion | $^\circ\text{F}$ |
| 3 | RTD Resistance | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$ | Linear approximation | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R_T = R_0 \, e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | Steinhart-Hart simplified | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Thermocouple EMF | $\mathcal{E} = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck coefficient × ΔT | mV |
| 6 | IC Sensor Output (LM35) | $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C} \times T_C$ | Linear in Celsius | V |
| 7 | Thermal Time Constant | $T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$ | First-order response | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 8 | Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$ | Slope of transfer curve | $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 9 | Linearity Error | $e_L = \frac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$ | Non-linearity quantification | % FS |
| 10 | Heat Transfer (Steady) | $Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$ | Fourier's law analog | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Convention:** All temperatures inside exponential and logarithmic thermistor equations **must be in Kelvin**. Celsius will produce mathematically incorrect results.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal sensors are the *backbone* of:

- **HVAC systems** in smart buildings (zone-wise temperature control).
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** in electric vehicles (cell monitoring between $-20^\circ\text{C}$ and $60^\circ\text{C}$).
- **Industrial process control** (refinery, pharmaceutical, semiconductor fabrication ovens).
- **Medical instrumentation** (clinical thermometers, incubators, cryogenic preservation).
- **Aerospace telemetry** (engine exhaust pyrometers exceeding $1200^\circ\text{C}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Temperature Scale Conversion

### Given
A process is operating at $T = 86^\circ\text{F}$. Find the equivalent temperature in Kelvin.

### Step 1 — Identify the target scale
We need the final answer in **Kelvin**, so the chain of conversion is: $^\circ\text{F} \rightarrow ^\circ\text{C} \rightarrow \text{K}$.

### Step 2 — Convert Fahrenheit to Celsius
Using the inverse of the standard formula:

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \left[ T(^\circ\text{F}) - 32 \right]$$

Substituting $T(^\circ\text{F}) = 86$:

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \left[ 86 - 32 \right]$$

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \times 54$$

$$T(^\circ\text{C}) = \frac{270}{9} = 30^\circ\text{C}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Celsius to Kelvin

$$T(\text{K}) = T(^\circ\text{C}) + 273.15$$

$$T(\text{K}) = 30 + 273.15$$

$$T(\text{K}) = 303.15 \text{ K}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{T = 303.15 \text{ K}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — First-Order Thermal Response

### Problem Statement
A thermistor with thermal time constant $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$ is initially at $T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C}$ and is suddenly immersed in a fluid at $T_\infty = 95^\circ\text{C}$. Find (a) the temperature of the sensor after $t = 10 \text{ s}$, and (b) the time required to reach $90\%$ of the final temperature.

### Part (a) — Temperature at $t = 10 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Identify given variables.**
$T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 95^\circ\text{C}$, $\tau = 8 \text{ s}$, $t = 10 \text{ s}$.

**Step 2 — Write the first-order response equation.**

$$T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature difference (driving force).**

$$\Delta T = T_\infty - T_0 = 95 - 25 = 70^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\frac{t}{\tau} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponential term.**

$$e^{-1.25} \approx 0.2865$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the response equation.**

$$T_s(10) = 95 - (70 \times 0.2865)$$

$$T_s(10) = 95 - 20.055$$

$$T_s(10) = 74.945^\circ\text{C}$$

### Part (a) Final Answer
$$\boxed{T_s(10) \approx 74.95^\circ\text{C}}$$

### Part (b) — Time to reach $90\%$ of the final temperature

**Step 1 — Define "90% of final" mathematically.**

We require the sensor to reach $90\%$ of the *temperature change*:

$$T_s(t) - T_0 = 0.90 \times (T_\infty - T_0)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the general equation.**

$$T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau} - T_0 = 0.90 (T_\infty - T_0)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the left-hand side.**

$$(T_\infty - T_0) \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right] = 0.90 (T_\infty - T_0)$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $(T_\infty - T_0)$ from both sides** (since it is non-zero).

$$1 - e^{-t/\tau} = 0.90$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the exponential term.**

$$e^{-t/\tau} = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$

**Step 6 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.10)$$

$$\frac{t}{\tau} = -\ln(0.10) = 2.3026$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t$.**

$$t = 2.3026 \times \tau = 2.3026 \times 8$$

$$t = 18.42 \text{ s}$$

### Part (b) Final Answer
$$\boxed{t_{90\%} \approx 18.42 \text{ s}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Sensitivity & Linearity Error

### Problem Statement
A temperature sensor produces $V_{out} = 0.5 \text{ V}$ at $T = 20^\circ\text{C}$ and $V_{out} = 2.5 \text{ V}$ at $T = 70^\circ\text{C}$. The ideal (theoretical) line gives $V_{ideal} = 2.2 \text{ V}$ at $T = 50^\circ\text{C}$. If the full-scale output is $V_{FS} = 4.0 \text{ V}$, compute (a) the sensitivity, and (b) the linearity error at $50^\circ\text{C}$.

### Part (a) — Sensitivity

$$S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T} = \frac{V_{out,2} - V_{out,1}}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$S = \frac{2.5 - 0.5}{70 - 20} = \frac{2.0}{50}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.04 \text{ V}/^\circ\text{C} = 40 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}}$$

### Part (b) — Linearity Error

$$e_L = \frac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = \frac{\vert 2.2 - V_{actual} \vert}{4.0} \times 100\%$$

The actual output at $50^\circ\text{C}$ using the linear interpolation from the two calibration points:

$$V_{actual}(50) = V_{out,1} + S \times (50 - 20) = 0.5 + 0.04 \times 30 = 1.7 \text{ V}$$

Therefore:

$$e_L = \frac{\vert 1.7 - 2.2 \vert}{4.0} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = \frac{0.5}{4.0} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{e_L = 12.5\% \text{ FS}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection at a Glance

| Criterion | Thermocouple | RTD (Pt100) | Thermistor (NTC) | IC Sensor (LM35) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $-200^\circ\text{C}$ to $2300^\circ\text{C}$ | $-200^\circ\text{C}$ to $850^\circ\text{C}$ | $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $150^\circ\text{C}$ | $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $150^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Sensitivity** | $\sim 40 \text{ }\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ (low) | $\sim 0.385 \text{ }\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$ (medium) | $-4\% /^\circ\text{C}$ (very high) | $10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}$ (high) |
| **Linearity** | Moderate | Excellent | Poor (exponential) | Excellent |
| **Self-Powered?** | Yes (Seebeck) | No (needs excitation) | No (needs excitation) | No (needs excitation) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium–High | Low | Low |
| **Typical Use** | Furnaces, exhaust | Industrial labs | Inrush protection, HVAC | Microcontroller interfacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb:**
> - **High temperature + harsh environment** → Thermocouple.
> - **High accuracy + industrial process** → RTD (Pt100/Pt1000).
> - **High sensitivity + narrow range** → NTC Thermistor.
> - **Direct ADC interface + prototyping** → IC Sensor (LM35 / DS18B20).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Temperature Sensor Signal Chain in a CPS

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the signal flow from a physical temperature source to a digital display/actuator command in a Cyber-Physical System.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    physSource["PHYSICAL HEAT SOURCE"]
    sensElement["SENSOR ELEMENT RTD TC NTC IC"]
    condCircuit["CONDITIONING CIRCUIT AMPLIFIER FILTER BRIDGE"]
    adcNode["ADC MODULE MICROCONTROLLER"]
    procNode["PROCESSING UNIT ARDUINO ESP32 RASPBERRY PI"]
    displayNode["OUTPUT HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE ACTUATOR COMMAND"]
    fbNode["FEEDBACK LOOP TO CONTROL SYSTEM"]

    physSource -->|Thermal Energy| sensElement
    sensElement -->|Millivolt OR Resistance| condCircuit
    condCircuit -->|0 to 5 Volt Signal| adcNode
    adcNode -->|Digital Code 10 bit 12 bit| procNode
    procNode -->|Display OR PWM OR Decision| displayNode
    displayNode --> fbNode
    fbNode -->|Closed Loop Control Action| physSource
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Sensor Classification Logic for KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["TEMPERATURE INPUT"]
    qSelf{"IS IT SELF POWERED VOLTAGE OUTPUT"}
    qRes{"DOES RESISTANCE CHANGE LINEARLY WITH T"}
    qSemi{"IS IT A SEMICONDUCTOR BANDGAP DEVICE"}
    qRad{"DOES IT MEASURE EMITTED INFRARED RADIATION"}
    outTC["THERMOCOUPLE SEEBECK EFFECT"]
    outRTD["RTD METALLIC RESISTANCE PT100"]
    outTHERM["THERMISTOR NTC OR PTC"]
    outIC["IC SENSOR LM35 DS18B20 AD590"]
    outRAD["PYROMETER OR BOLOMETER"]

    startA --> qSelf
    qSelf -->|YES| outTC
    qSelf -->|NO| qRes
    qRes -->|YES POSITIVE COEFFICIENT| outRTD
    qRes -->|NO EXPONENTIAL RESPONSE| qSemi
    qSemi -->|YES BANDGAP JUNCTION| outIC
    qSemi -->|NO RESISTIVE CERAMIC| outTHERM
    qRes -->|NONE EM RADIATION| qRad
    qRad -->|YES| outRAD
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Thermal CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANT["PHYSICAL PLANT DOMAIN"]
        heater1["HEATER ELEMENT"]
        tank1["WATER TANK"]
    end

    subgraph SENSING["SENSING DOMAIN MODULE 2"]
        ts1["TEMPERATURE SENSOR PT100"]
        sig1["WHEATSTONE BRIDGE"]
        amp1["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
    end

    subgraph CYBER["CYBER DOMAIN"]
        mcu1["MCU WITH ADC AND PID ALGORITHM"]
        dac1["PWM OR DAC OUTPUT"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION["ACTUATION DOMAIN"]
        driver1["POWER DRIVER MOSFET OR SSR"]
        heaterActuator["HEATER COIL OR PELTIER MODULE"]
    end

    heater1 --> tank1
    tank1 -->|Heat Transfer| ts1
    ts1 -->|Resistance| sig1
    sig1 -->|Millivolt| amp1
    amp1 -->|0 to 5 Volt| mcu1
    mcu1 -->|Control Signal| dac1
    dac1 -->|Gate Drive| driver1
    driver1 -->|Electrical Power| heaterActuator
    heaterActuator -->|Thermal Output| heater1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the CPS Diagram:** The sensor is the *eyes* of the system, the microcontroller is the *brain*, and the actuator is the *muscle*. KTU Module 2 focuses almost entirely on the **Sensing Domain** block.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define temperature. Why is it considered an intensive thermodynamic property? List the four major classes of temperature sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Temperature is the physical quantity that quantifies the average kinetic energy of the microscopic particles of a thermodynamic system. It is classified as an **intensive property** because it does **not depend on the size or amount of matter** in the system; for example, two cups of water at $80^\circ\text{C}$ and a swimming pool at $80^\circ\text{C}$ share the same temperature, even though their thermal energies differ vastly.

The four major classes are:
1. **Thermoelectric sensors** — Thermocouple.
2. **Resistive sensors** — RTD (Pt100) and Thermistor.
3. **Semiconductor junction sensors** — LM35, AD590.
4. **Radiation / Pyroelectric sensors** — Pyrometer, Bolometer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write "transducer" instead of "sensor" and lose a mark. KTU strictly uses the term **sensor** in Module 2.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the static characteristics: Accuracy, Sensitivity, and Linearity. State their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** — The maximum deviation of the sensor output from the true (ideal) value. Mathematically: $\text{Accuracy} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V_{max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$.
- **Sensitivity** — The ratio of the change in output to the change in input. Mathematically: $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$, units: $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Linearity** — The closeness of the calibration curve to a straight line. The linearity error is: $e_L = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions offer an internal choice. The KTU pattern (Module 2) typically provides *one compulsory 7-mark part* and a *choice between two 7-mark sub-parts*. To best serve your preparation, the full $14$-mark version is provided below with a parallel alternative.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a closed-loop temperature control system used in a Cyber-Physical System. Identify the role of the **temperature sensor** in the loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A temperature sensor is initially at $T_0 = 30^\circ\text{C}$. It is placed in an oven at $T_\infty = 180^\circ\text{C}$. The sensor exhibits a first-order response with a thermal time constant $\tau = 12 \text{ s}$. Determine:
- (i) The sensor temperature after $t = 24 \text{ s}$.
- (ii) The time required to reach within $5^\circ\text{C}$ of the final temperature.
- (iii) Justify whether the sensor has reached $99.3\%$ of the final temperature at $t = 5\tau$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The closed-loop thermal CPS consists of **four domains**:

1. **Plant Domain** — Heater coil / furnace, where heat is generated.
2. **Sensing Domain** — Temperature sensor (e.g., Pt100 RTD) that converts thermal energy to a measurable electrical quantity.
3. **Cyber Domain** — Microcontroller / PLC that reads the ADC, executes the PID algorithm, and outputs a control signal.
4. **Actuation Domain** — Power driver (MOSFET / SSR) that modulates the heater power.

**Role of the temperature sensor:** It is the *perceptual front-end* of the loop. Without it, the cyber layer has no knowledge of the plant's thermal state, making **feedback control impossible**. The sensor *transduces* the physical temperature into a precise electrical signal (voltage / resistance) that is proportional, repeatable, and low in noise.

A labelled block diagram is provided in **Section 4.3** of these notes — refer to the *Functional Architecture – Closed-Loop Thermal CPS* figure.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $T_0 = 30^\circ\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 180^\circ\text{C}$, $\tau = 12 \text{ s}$.

**(i) Sensor temperature at $t = 24 \text{ s}$:**

The first-order response equation is:

$$T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$$

Substituting:

$$T_s(24) = 180 - (180 - 30) e^{-24/12}$$

$$T_s(24) = 180 - 150 \, e^{-2}$$

$$T_s(24) = 180 - 150 \times 0.1353$$

$$T_s(24) = 180 - 20.30$$

$$\boxed{T_s(24) \approx 159.70^\circ\text{C}}$$

**(ii) Time to reach within $5^\circ\text{C}$ of $T_\infty$:**

We require $T_s(t) \geq T_\infty - 5 = 175^\circ\text{C}$:

$$175 = 180 - 150 \, e^{-t/12}$$

$$150 \, e^{-t/12} = 5$$

$$e^{-t/12} = \frac{1}{30} = 0.03333$$

$$-\frac{t}{12} = \ln(0.03333) = -3.4012$$

$$t = 12 \times 3.4012$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 40.81 \text{ s}}$$

**(iii) Justification of $99.3\%$ at $t = 5\tau$:**

At $t = 5\tau$:

$$T_s(5\tau) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-5}$$

$$T_s(5\tau) = 180 - 150 \times 0.00674$$

$$T_s(5\tau) = 180 - 1.011 \approx 178.99^\circ\text{C}$$

The fraction of total change achieved:

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{T_s - T_0}{T_\infty - T_0} = \frac{178.99 - 30}{150} = \frac{148.99}{150} = 0.9933 = 99.33\%$$

> Therefore, at $t = 5\tau$, the sensor has indeed reached $99.3\%$ of the final temperature. This confirms the universal **KTU rule-of-thumb** that *five time constants* is the practical settling time for a first-order sensor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with reference to temperature sensors, and write the governing mathematical expression for each: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Thermal Time Constant
- (ii) Sensitivity
- (iii) Linearity Error

**(b)** A Pt100 RTD has a resistance of $138.5 \text{ }\Omega$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$. Given that the resistance at $0^\circ\text{C}$ is $R_0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$, determine:
- (i) The temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha$.
- (ii) The expected resistance of the same RTD at $T = 250^\circ\text{C}$ (assuming linear behaviour).
- (iii) The actual resistance at $250^\circ\text{C}$ using the more accurate Callendar-Van Dusen equation (first three terms). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

- **(i) Thermal Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time required for a first-order temperature sensor to reach $63.2\%$ of its final steady-state value when subjected to a step change in temperature. Mathematical expression: $T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **(ii) Sensitivity ($S$):** The ratio of the change in sensor output to the corresponding change in temperature. Mathematical expression: $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$, units $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- **(iii) Linearity Error ($e_L$):** The maximum deviation of the actual sensor output from the ideal (straight-line) output, expressed as a percentage of full-scale. Mathematical expression: $e_L = \dfrac{\vert V_{actual} - V_{ideal} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $R_0 = 100 \text{ }\Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$, $R_{100} = 138.5 \text{ }\Omega$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$.

**(i) Compute $\alpha$:**

The linear RTD equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]$$

Substituting $T = 100^\circ\text{C}$, $T_0 = 0^\circ\text{C}$:

$$138.5 = 100 \left[ 1 + \alpha (100 - 0) \right]$$

$$138.5 = 100 + 10000 \alpha$$

$$10000 \alpha = 38.5$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.00385 \text{ } \Omega/\Omega/^\circ\text{C} = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} /^\circ\text{C}}$$

> This is the standard nominal $\alpha$ for the European Curve (IEC 60751) Pt100 RTD, confirming the calibration.

**(ii) Linear estimate of $R$ at $250^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$R_{250} = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot 250 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.00385 \times 250 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.9625 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \times 1.9625$$

$$\boxed{R_{250,\,linear} = 196.25 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**(iii) Callendar-Van Dusen correction (first three terms):**

The Callendar-Van Dusen equation is:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 \right)$$

For $T \geq 0^\circ\text{C}$, the standard coefficients for Pt100 are:
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$

Substituting $T = 250$:

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(250) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(250)^2 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.977075 + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(62500) \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.977075 - 0.036094 \right]$$

$$R_{250} = 100 \times 1.940981$$

$$\boxed{R_{250,\,CVD} \approx 194.10 \text{ }\Omega}$$

**Comparison:** The linear estimate ($196.25 \text{ }\Omega$) differs from the accurate CVD prediction ($194.10 \text{ }\Omega$) by $2.15 \text{ }\Omega$, which is the *non-linearity* introduced at higher temperatures. This validates the necessity of the Callendar-Van Dusen correction in high-precision industrial RTDs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 2 (Introduction)**
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Always state units. Writing "$R = 100$" instead of "$R = 100 \text{ }\Omega$" costs a half-mark in KTU valuation.
> - **Kelvin vs Celsius in Exponentials:** Thermistor and CVD equations **require Kelvin**. Using Celsius in $e^{1/T}$ will produce a numerical answer that is wrong by a factor of hundreds, and the examiner will mark the entire step as zero.
> - **Skipping Intermediate Steps:** KTU expects $3$–$4$ visible steps in any $7$-mark derivation. Jumping directly to the final answer without substitution steps leads to **partial marking only**.
> - **Confusing Sensitivity with Linearity:** Sensitivity is the *slope*; Linearity is the *deviation from straightness*. Do not interchange them.
> - **Forgetting Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Thermocouples are a higher-order topic, but even in Module 2's introductory questions, students lose marks by not mentioning that the **reference junction must be compensated** to obtain an absolute temperature.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist – Module 2 Introduction**

- ☐ Temperature is an *intensive* thermodynamic property; we **infer** it via secondary effects.
- ☐ The three scales are Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin. Conversions use the $9/5$ ratio and offsets of $32$ and $273.15$.
- ☐ **Four pillars** of temperature sensing: Thermoelectric, Resistive, Semiconductor, and Radiative.
- ☐ **Static characteristics** to memorize: Range, Span, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Linearity, Resolution, Repeatability, Hysteresis.
- ☐ **First-order thermal response** $T_s(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}$.
- ☐ At $t = \tau$, the sensor has reached **$63.2\%$** of the total change.
- ☐ At $t = 5\tau$, the sensor is considered to have **practically settled** ($99.3\%$).
- ☐ The thermal time constant $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$, analogous to an electrical RC circuit.
- ☐ Pt100 RTD has a nominal $\alpha = 0.00385 /^\circ\text{C}$ (European Curve, IEC 60751).
- ☐ LM35 IC sensor outputs $10 \text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}$ with linear scaling in Celsius.
- ☐ **Always use Kelvin** in thermistor exponential equations.
- ☐ Sensitivity = slope = $\Delta V / \Delta T$, in units of $\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- ☐ Linearity error is expressed as a **% of full-scale (FS)** output.
- ☐ A temperature sensor is the *perceptual front-end* of any Cyber-Physical thermal control loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Basic Characteristics" of Sensors?

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme instrumentation (PCCPT205), the **Basic Characteristics** of a sensor are the set of quantitative descriptors that quantify *how well* a sensor converts a physical stimulus (the **measurand**) into a usable electrical (or digital) output signal. They are broadly classified into two families:

1. **Static Characteristics** — Performance evaluated when the input is *constant or slowly varying* (steady-state regime). The measurand does not change appreciably with time.
2. **Dynamic Characteristics** — Performance evaluated when the input is *time-varying* (transient or sinusoidal regime). The measurand changes faster than the sensor can track.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to *define, distinguish, and mathematically express* static and dynamic characteristics. The terms **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Sensitivity**, **Linearity**, **Hysteresis**, **Repeatability**, **Resolution**, **Span/Range**, **Time Constant**, and **Bandwidth** are the most frequently tested items.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Simple Weighing Scale

Imagine you step onto a **digital bathroom weighing scale** in your hostel room.

- **Accuracy** → After stepping on, the scale reads **exactly 68.4 kg**, the same value your college gym's calibrated scale shows. *(It is correct.)*
- **Precision** → You step on, get off, and step on again **five times**. Every reading is within ±0.1 kg of 68.4 kg. *(It is repeatable/consistent, but may still be wrong.)*
- **Sensitivity** → Drinking half a glass of water (~250 mL ≈ 0.25 kg) makes the displayed value increase from 68.4 → 68.65 kg. The ratio **ΔOutput / ΔInput ≈ 1 digit per 0.25 kg** is the sensitivity.
- **Linearity** → If 1 kg of mass always adds exactly 1.0 kg to the reading (10 kg → +10.0 kg, 20 kg → +20.0 kg), the scale is linear. If 10 kg adds 9.8 kg but 80 kg adds 79.0 kg, it is **non-linear**.
- **Hysteresis** → Step on, get off, place a **20 kg bag**, then step on again with the bag. The total reading may differ from the previous "20 kg alone + your weight" because the mechanical spring has memory. The maximum gap is **hysteresis error**.
- **Dead Band / Threshold** → If you place a 50 g coin on the scale, the display may stay at 0.0 kg. Below a certain input, the scale **does not respond**. That invisible zone is the **dead band**.
- **Drift** → Leave the scale on for 24 hours with no one on it. The display may slowly climb from 0.00 → 0.07 kg due to thermal expansion or aging. That is **zero drift**.
- **Dynamic Response** → When you **jump onto** the scale, the reading does not instantly jump to 68.4 kg. It rises, overshoots to 68.9 kg, oscillates, and settles. The **rise time**, **overshoot**, and **settling time** are dynamic characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static characteristics describe a sensor's behavior at steady state (DC view). Dynamic characteristics describe its behavior when the input changes with time (AC view).** Both are essential — a sensor that is accurate at steady state may still be useless for measuring fast-changing signals (e.g., engine crankshaft vibration).

---

## 1.3 Quick Definition Anchor Box

> [!NOTE]
> **FORMAL DEFINITIONS (as per KTU 2024 Scheme terminology):**
>
> - **Accuracy:** Closeness of the *measured value* to the *true value* of the measurand.
> - **Precision:** Closeness of *repeated measurements* of the same quantity to *each other*.
> - **Sensitivity (K):** Ratio of change in output to change in input, $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$.
> - **Span (Full-Scale Input, FSI):** The algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum input values the sensor is designed to measure.
> - **Full-Scale Output (FSO):** The algebraic difference between the output at maximum input and output at minimum input.
> - **Linearity:** Maximum deviation of the actual calibration curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of FSO.
> - **Hysteresis:** Maximum difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions (increasing vs. decreasing).
> - **Repeatability:** Ability of a sensor to reproduce the same output for the same input, *under identical conditions, repeated over a short period*.
> - **Reproducibility:** Ability to reproduce readings *after a long interval* or *under changed conditions* (different observer, environment, calibration).
> - **Resolution:** Smallest *detectable* change in input that produces a *perceptible* change in output.
> - **Threshold:** Smallest input magnitude that produces a detectable output at all.
> - **Dead Band:** The range of input over which the sensor produces no output (a special form of threshold).
> - **Drift:** Unwanted change of output with time when the input is held constant.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Cyber-Physical Systems

In a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, the physical world (sensors) and the digital world (controllers, AI) interact through these very characteristics. If the temperature sensor feeding a nuclear reactor controller has a **dead band of 5 °C**, a critical temperature rise of 3 °C will go *completely unnoticed* — a disaster. If an accelerometer in a self-driving car has poor **bandwidth**, it cannot detect a sudden pothole at 50 m/s. Hence, mastering these descriptors is non-negotiable for any cyber-physical engineer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Characteristics — Structured Logical Breakdown

### (a) Accuracy vs. Precision — The Two Pillars

- **Accuracy** is governed by **systematic errors** (calibration error, zero error, aging, loading error). It can be corrected by recalibration.
- **Precision** is governed by **random errors** (noise, friction, quantization, thermal noise). It can be improved by averaging but not by recalibration.
- A sensor can be **precise but inaccurate** (tight cluster around the wrong value) or **accurate but imprecise** (scattered around the right value) but rarely both unless expensive.

### (b) Span, Range, and Full-Scale Output

- **Range** = $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$ — the bounded input interval.
- **Span** = $x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ — the *width* of the input interval.
- **Full-Scale Output (FSO)** = $y(x_{\max}) - y(x_{\min})$ — the corresponding output width.
- **Static Sensitivity** = $\dfrac{\text{FSO}}{\text{Span}}$ — slope of the best-fit line.

### (c) Linearity (Three Definitions)

1. **Independent Linearity:** Maximum deviation of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line (chosen to minimize *maximum* deviation).
2. **Zero-Based Linearity:** Straight line drawn through $(x_{\min}, y_{\min})$ and $(x_{\max}, y_{\max})$.
3. **Terminal-Based Linearity:** Straight line connecting the *actual* end-points.

The KTU exam most commonly tests **independent linearity**, expressed as:

$$\text{Linearity Error } (\%) = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$$

where $\Delta y_{\max}$ is the maximum vertical deviation of the experimental curve from the reference straight line.

### (d) Hysteresis — A Memory Effect

Hysteresis arises from mechanical friction, magnetic remanence, elastic after-effect, or thermal lag. It is *direction-dependent*:

$$\text{Hysteresis Error } (\%) = \frac{\Delta y_H}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$$

where $\Delta y_H$ = output difference between the *up-scale* and *down-scale* readings at the *same input*.

### (e) Repeatability vs. Reproducibility

- **Repeatability:** Same input, *same* conditions, *same* operator, *short* time interval → spread of readings.
- **Reproducibility:** Same input, *different* conditions (operator, environment, instrument, day) → spread of readings. Reproducibility is *always worse* than repeatability.

$$\text{Repeatability Error } (\%) = \frac{\Delta y_R}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$$

### (f) Resolution, Threshold, and Dead Band

- **Threshold** = the *smallest input* that just produces a measurable output.
- **Dead Band** = the *zone of input values* where the output is *identically zero* (no response at all). Common in switches, relays, contact-type sensors.
- **Resolution** = smallest *change* in input that produces a *detectable change* in output (a digital instrument might have resolution = 1 LSB = 0.1 °C).

### (g) Drift — The Slow Enemy

$$\text{Drift} = \frac{\text{Change in output over time interval } \Delta t}{\Delta t}$$

Sub-categories:
- **Zero Drift:** Output change at *zero input*.
- **Sensitivity Drift:** Slope change over time.
- **Thermal Drift:** Output change per °C of ambient temperature change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the act of *comparing* a sensor's output against a *traceable standard* and adjusting (or mathematically compensating) to remove systematic errors. The KTU curriculum expects you to know **static calibration vs. dynamic calibration**.

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Structured Logical Breakdown

### (a) Why Dynamics Matter

When the measurand changes *faster* than the sensor can respond, the output lags behind. Two cases:

1. **Step Input Response** → characterized by **time constant**, **rise time**, **settling time**, **overshoot**, **delay time**.
2. **Sinusoidal Input Response** → characterized by **frequency response**, **bandwidth**, **phase lag**, **resonance peak**.

### (b) First-Order System Response (Most Common Sensor Model)

A vast majority of thermal, mechanical, and many chemical sensors behave as **first-order systems** governed by:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where:
- $\tau$ = **time constant** (seconds) — time to reach $63.2\%$ of the final value.
- $K$ = static sensitivity.
- $x(t)$ = input, $y(t)$ = output.

**Step Response (input jumps from 0 to $X$ at $t=0$, sensor initially at 0):**

$$y(t) = K X \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Key milestones:
- $t = \tau$ → $y = 0.632 \cdot y_\infty$ (**time constant**)
- $t = 2\tau$ → $y = 0.865 \cdot y_\infty$
- $t = 3\tau$ → $y = 0.950 \cdot y_\infty$
- $t = 5\tau$ → $y = 0.993 \cdot y_\infty$ (effectively settled)

**Sinusoidal Steady-State Response:**

$$y(t) = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1+(\omega \tau)^2}} \, X_m \sin(\omega t - \phi), \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega \tau)$$

- **Magnitude ratio** $M(\omega) = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+(\omega \tau)^2}}$
- **Phase lag** $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega \tau)$

The **$-3\text{ dB}$ bandwidth** of a first-order system is at $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$.

### (c) Second-Order System Response (Many Mechanical Sensors)

$$\frac{1}{\omega_n^2}\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_n}\frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot x$$

where:
- $\omega_n$ = **natural (undamped) frequency** (rad/s)
- $\zeta$ = **damping ratio** (dimensionless)

Depending on $\zeta$:
- $\zeta = 0$: undamped oscillation (never settles).
- $0 < \zeta < 1$: **underdamped** — overshoot, oscillation.
- $\zeta = 1$: **critically damped** — fastest non-oscillatory response.
- $\zeta > 1$: **overdamped** — sluggish, no overshoot.

**Key metrics:**
- **Overshoot** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$
- **Peak time** $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_d}$ where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- **Settling time** (2% criterion) $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$
- **Rise time** $t_r \approx \dfrac{\pi - \cos^{-1}\zeta}{\omega_d}$

### (d) Frequency Response and Bandwidth

- **Bandwidth** of a sensor is the range of input frequencies over which the output magnitude stays within $\pm 3\text{ dB}$ (or some specified tolerance) of the steady-state value.
- **Resonant peak** appears at $\omega_n$ for underdamped second-order systems — operation *near resonance* must be avoided because output magnitude is grossly exaggerated.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Characteristic | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output units / input units |
| 2 | Span (FSI) | $\text{Span} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Same as input |
| 3 | Full-Scale Output | $\text{FSO} = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | Same as output |
| 4 | Accuracy (as error) | $\%\text{Acc Error} = \dfrac{\vert y_m - y_t \vert_{\max}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$ | Compare to true value $y_t$ |
| 5 | Linearity Error | $\%\text{Linear} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Independent linearity preferred |
| 6 | Hysteresis Error | $\%\text{Hyst} = \dfrac{\Delta y_H}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Up-scale vs down-scale |
| 7 | Repeatability Error | $\%\text{Rep} = \dfrac{\Delta y_R}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Short term, same conditions |
| 8 | Reproducibility Error | $\%\text{Repro} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{Rep}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | Long term, varied conditions |
| 9 | Resolution | Smallest detectable $\Delta x$ | Often = 1 LSB in digital |
| 10 | Dead Band | Range of $x$ with $y \equiv 0$ | Same as input units |
| 11 | Drift Rate | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta t}$ at constant input | Output units / time |
| 12 | First-Order Step Response | $y(t) = y_\infty \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $\tau$ = time constant |
| 13 | First-Order Magnitude Ratio | $M(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(\omega \tau)^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | First-Order Phase | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega \tau)$ | Radians (lagging) |
| 15 | First-Order Bandwidth | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | rad/s |
| 16 | Second-Order Natural Freq. | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 17 | Second-Order Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 18 | Percent Overshoot | $M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Percentage |
| 19 | Settling Time (2%) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Seconds |
| 20 | Peak Time | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The above formulas allow a designer to (i) select a sensor for a given application, (ii) predict its behavior in feedback control loops, and (iii) decide whether an analog anti-aliasing filter is required *before* ADC sampling (Shannon-Nyquist theorem: sample at least $2\times$ the bandwidth).

---

## 2.4 Common Misconception — Static vs. Dynamic Sensitivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Sensitivity** is the slope of the *DC* calibration curve. **Dynamic Sensitivity** is the magnitude ratio $M(\omega)$ at a *specific input frequency*. For a first-order system, dynamic sensitivity *decreases* with frequency, while static sensitivity is frequency-independent. KTU examiners often test whether students realize these are *different numbers* for the *same* sensor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Step Response

**Statement:** A first-order sensor has governing equation

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

A *step input* $x(t) = X \cdot u(t)$ is applied at $t=0$, with initial condition $y(0)=0$. Derive the output $y(t)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the ODE for $t > 0$:**

For $t > 0$, $x(t) = X$ (constant), so:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K X$$

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a non-homogeneous linear first-order ODE with constant coefficients.**

The general solution is the sum of the *complementary function* (homogeneous solution) and the *particular integral* (steady-state solution):

$$y(t) = y_{\text{transient}}(t) + y_{\text{steady}}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Find the steady-state (particular) solution.**

At steady state ($t \to \infty$), $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0$, hence:

$$y_{\text{steady}} = K X$$

**Step 4 — Find the transient (homogeneous) solution.**

Setting the RHS to zero: $\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dy}{y} = -\dfrac{dt}{\tau}$

Integrating: $\ln y = -\dfrac{t}{\tau} + C_1 \Rightarrow y_{\text{transient}} = A e^{-t/\tau}$

**Step 5 — Combine and apply the initial condition.**

$$y(t) = A e^{-t/\tau} + K X$$

At $t = 0^+$, $y(0) = 0$:

$$0 = A + K X \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A = -K X$$

**Step 6 — Final step response expression.**

$$\boxed{\, y(t) = K X \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \,}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the time-constant interpretation.**

Set $t = \tau$:

$$y(\tau) = K X \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = K X (1 - 0.368) = 0.632 \cdot K X$$

Hence the **time constant $\tau$** is precisely the time required for the step response to reach **$63.2\%$** of its final value. This is the universal landmark used in instrumentation textbooks and KTU questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Constant Determination from Experimental Data

**Problem:** A thermistor-based temperature sensor is immersed in a water bath whose temperature is suddenly raised from 25 °C to 80 °C. The sensor output (in mV) is recorded:

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
|---------|---|---|----|----|----|----|----|
| $y$ (mV) | 200 | 320 | 405 | 466 | 510 | 541 | 563 |

The static sensitivity is $K = 10$ mV/°C. Determine the time constant $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Compute the steady-state output.**

Final value expected: $y_\infty = K \cdot (80 - 25) = 10 \times 55 = 550$ mV added to baseline.

Looking at the data, the readings asymptotically approach a value. Since at $t=30$ s, $y=563$ mV (within 2% of expected rise), we take $y_\infty \approx 600$ mV (or use the asymptotic formula).

**Step 2 — Use the rearranged step-response formula.**

$$y_\infty - y(t) = y_\infty e^{-t/\tau}$$

Take natural log:

$$\ln(y_\infty - y(t)) = \ln y_\infty - \frac{t}{\tau}$$

A plot of $\ln(y_\infty - y(t))$ vs $t$ should be linear with slope $-\dfrac{1}{\tau}$.

**Step 3 — Compute residual values** (using $y_\infty = 600$ mV):

| $t$ | $y_\infty - y$ | $\ln(y_\infty - y)$ |
|-----|---------------|--------------------|
| 0 | 400 | 5.991 |
| 5 | 280 | 5.635 |
| 10 | 195 | 5.273 |
| 15 | 134 | 4.898 |
| 20 | 90 | 4.500 |
| 25 | 59 | 4.078 |
| 30 | 37 | 3.611 |

**Step 4 — Linear regression slope:**

$$\text{slope} = \frac{\sum t \cdot \ln(\Delta y) - n \bar{t} \cdot \overline{\ln(\Delta y)}}{\sum t^2 - n \bar{t}^2}$$

Numerator: $(0)(5.991) + (5)(5.635) + (10)(5.273) + (15)(4.898) + (20)(4.500) + (25)(4.078) + (30)(3.611) - 7 \cdot (15)(4.855)$

$= 0 + 28.175 + 52.730 + 73.470 + 90.000 + 101.950 + 108.330 - 509.78$

$= 454.66 - 509.78 = -55.12$

Denominator: $(0+25+100+225+400+625+900) - 7 \times 225 = 2275 - 1575 = 700$

Slope $= -55.12 / 700 = -0.0787$ s$^{-1}$

**Step 5 — Compute time constant:**

$$\tau = -\frac{1}{\text{slope}} = -\frac{1}{-0.0787} = 12.7 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\, \tau \approx 12.7 \text{ s} \,}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

A time constant of 12.7 s means this thermistor is *slow* — it is suitable for HVAC monitoring but **not** for detecting a sudden fire-flash event (which needs $\tau < 0.1$ s).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Damping Ratio from Overshoot

**Problem:** A piezoelectric accelerometer exhibits **20% overshoot** when excited by a step input. Compute the damping ratio $\zeta$.

**Step 1 — Write the standard overshoot formula:**

$$M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Given $M_p = 0.20$, take natural log:

$$\ln(0.20) = -\frac{\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

$$-1.609 = -\frac{\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

$$\frac{\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = 1.609$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides:**

$$\frac{\pi^2 \zeta^2}{1-\zeta^2} = 2.589$$

$$\pi^2 \zeta^2 = 2.589 (1 - \zeta^2) = 2.589 - 2.589 \zeta^2$$

$$\zeta^2 (\pi^2 + 2.589) = 2.589$$

$$\zeta^2 (9.870 + 2.589) = 2.589$$

$$\zeta^2 = \frac{2.589}{12.459} = 0.2078$$

**Step 3 — Final answer:**

$$\zeta = \sqrt{0.2078} = 0.456$$

$$\boxed{\, \zeta \approx 0.456 \,}$$

Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped**, as expected for an accelerometer designed for vibration measurement.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Application | Critical Characteristic(s) | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Weighing scale in a lab | **Accuracy, Linearity, Hysteresis** | Static, low-frequency measurements |
| Engine vibration monitor | **Bandwidth, Natural Frequency, Damping Ratio** | Sinusoidal inputs up to kHz |
| Fire-alarm smoke sensor | **Response Time (Rise Time), Sensitivity** | Sudden step change in smoke density |
| Industrial process thermometer | **Time Constant, Repeatability, Drift** | Slow-varying temperature, 24/7 operation |
| ECG / heart-rate sensor | **Bandwidth (0.05–150 Hz), SNR, Resolution** | Low-frequency bioelectric signals |
| Inertial Navigation IMU | **Bias Stability (Drift), Bandwidth, Cross-axis Sensitivity** | Long-term DC accuracy + dynamic range |
