# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Accuracy and Precision in the context of a measuring instrument. Which one can be improved by recalibration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Definition | Closeness of measured value to the **true value** | Closeness of repeated measurements to **each other** |
| Error type | **Systematic** error | **Random** error |
| Effect of recalibration | **Improved** (systematic error removed) | **Not improved** (random error remains) |
| Example | A voltmeter reads 10.2 V when true value is 10.0 V (offset error) — corrected by calibration | Five readings: 9.8, 9.9, 10.0, 10.1, 10.2 V — precise but spread |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** *Accuracy* is improvable by recalibration; precision is improved only by reducing random noise sources.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Hysteresis (ii) Dead Band (iii) Drift.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Hysteresis (1 mark):** The maximum difference in output when the *same input* is approached from opposite directions. **Example:** A pressure gauge reads 50.2 kPa when pressure is increased from 0 to 50 kPa, but 49.6 kPa when the pressure is then decreased back to 50 kPa. The 0.6 kPa difference is hysteresis.

- **(ii) Dead Band (1 mark):** A range of input values over which the output remains *identically zero* (the sensor does not respond at all). **Example:** A mechanical thermostat may have a dead band of ±2 °C; small temperature fluctuations within this band do not switch the relay.

- **(iii) Drift (1 mark):** The unwanted change in output *with time* when the input is held constant. **Example:** A strain gauge on a bridge reads 100 µε at noon, but 105 µε at 6 PM under no-load conditions — 5 µε zero drift due to temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Define the following static characteristics of a sensor and give the formula for each: *(7 marks)*
   1. Accuracy
   2. Sensitivity
   3. Linearity
   4. Hysteresis
   5. Repeatability
   6. Resolution
   7. Span

**(b)** A pressure sensor has a nominal range of **0–200 kPa**. During calibration, the following data were obtained by increasing the input pressure from 0 to 200 kPa and then decreasing it back to 0. *(7 marks)*

| Input Pressure (kPa) | Output (mV) Increasing | Output (mV) Decreasing |
|-----------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 0  | 0.5  | 1.0  |
| 40 | 40.8 | 41.4 |
| 80 | 81.2 | 81.9 |
| 120 | 121.4 | 122.1 |
| 160 | 161.8 | 162.4 |
| 200 | 202.0 | 202.0 |

Compute the following:
   1. Static sensitivity (mV/kPa)
   2. Linearity error as a percentage of FSO
   3. Hysteresis error as a percentage of FSO
   4. Repeatability (qualitative comment)

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks, 1 mark each):

1. **Accuracy:** The degree to which the *measured value* conforms to the *true value* of the measurand. Expressed as **% of Full-Scale Output (FSO)** or **% of Reading**.

$$\%\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{|y_m - y_t|_{\max}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}}\right) \times 100$$

2. **Sensitivity ($K$):** The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input.

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{\text{FSO}}{\text{Span}}$$

3. **Linearity:** The maximum deviation of the *actual* calibration curve from an ideal straight line, expressed as a percentage of FSO.

$$\%\text{Linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$$

4. **Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in output for the *same input* when approached from increasing and decreasing directions.

$$\%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{\Delta y_H}{y_{\max} - y_{\min}} \times 100$$

5. **Repeatability:** The ability of a sensor to reproduce the same output for the same input under identical conditions over a short time interval.

$$\%\text{Repeatability} = \frac{\Delta y_R}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$$

6. **Resolution:** The smallest change in input that produces a perceptible change in output.

7. **Span:** The algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum input values. Span = $x_{\max} - x_{\min}$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Compute the average output at each input** (average of increasing and decreasing curves):

| $x$ (kPa) | $y_\text{avg}$ (mV) |
|-----------|---------------------|
| 0   | 0.75  |
| 40  | 41.10 |
| 80  | 81.55 |
| 120 | 121.75 |
| 160 | 162.10 |
| 200 | 202.00 |

**Step 2 — Compute static sensitivity:**

Using end-point method:

$$K = \frac{y_{200} - y_0}{x_{200} - x_0} = \frac{202.00 - 0.75}{200 - 0} = \frac{201.25}{200} = 1.006 \text{ mV/kPa}$$

Ideal output at each input: $y_{\text{ideal}} = 0.75 + 1.006 x$

| $x$ | $y_\text{avg}$ | $y_\text{ideal}$ | Deviation $\Delta y$ |
|-----|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 0   | 0.75  | 0.75  | 0.00  |
| 40  | 41.10 | 40.99 | +0.11 |
| 80  | 81.55 | 81.23 | +0.32 |
| 120 | 121.75 | 121.47 | +0.28 |
| 160 | 162.10 | 161.71 | +0.39 |
| 200 | 202.00 | 201.95 | +0.05 |

Maximum deviation $\Delta y_{\max} = 0.39$ mV (at 160 kPa).

**Step 3 — Linearity error:**

$$\%\text{Linearity} = \frac{0.39}{202.00 - 0.75} \times 100 = \frac{0.39}{201.25} \times 100 = 0.194\%$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis:**

For each input, take the absolute difference between increasing and decreasing output readings:

| $x$ | $\vert y_{\text{inc}} - y_{\text{dec}} \vert$ |
|-----|-----------------------------------------------|
| 0   | 0.5  |
| 40  | 0.6  |
| 80  | 0.7  |
| 120 | 0.7  |
| 160 | 0.6  |
| 200 | 0.0  |

Maximum hysteresis difference: $\Delta y_H = 0.7$ mV

$$\%\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{0.7}{201.25} \times 100 = 0.348\%$$

**Step 5 — Repeatability comment:**

The repeatability cannot be *numerically* calculated from this dataset because we have only *one* up-stroke and *one* down-stroke. Repeatability requires multiple readings under identical conditions. Qualitatively, the small (≤0.7 mV) variation between up and down strokes suggests good but not excellent repeatability.

**Summary of Results:**
- Sensitivity: $K = 1.006$ mV/kPa
- Linearity error: $0.194\%$ of FSO (excellent)
- Hysteresis error: $0.348\%$ of FSO (acceptable)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the step response of a first-order sensor governed by the equation $\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot x(t)$. Show that the output reaches $63.2\%$ of its final value at $t = \tau$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A temperature sensor exhibits a step response with a time constant $\tau = 4$ s and a static sensitivity $K = 0.5$ mV/°C. The temperature is suddenly changed from 30 °C to 100 °C. *(7 marks)*
   1. Write the expression for $y(t)$.
   2. Compute $y(2)$, $y(4)$, $y(8)$, and $y(20)$.
   3. Find the bandwidth of this sensor.
   4. What is the dynamic sensitivity at $f = 1$ Hz?

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Governing equation:**

For $t > 0$, with a step input $x(t) = X$ (a constant):

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K X$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear first-order ODE:**

The general solution is $y(t) = y_c + y_p$.

**Particular (steady-state) solution:** At $t \to \infty$, $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0$, so $y_p = KX$.

**Complementary (transient) solution:** Set RHS to zero:

$$\tau \frac{dy_c}{dt} + y_c = 0 \implies \frac{dy_c}{y_c} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

$$y_c = A e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Total solution:** $y(t) = A e^{-t/\tau} + KX$

**Step 3 — Apply initial condition** $y(0) = 0$:

$$0 = A + KX \implies A = -KX$$

**Step 4 — Final expression:**

$$y(t) = KX\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at $t = \tau$:**

$$y(\tau) = KX\left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = KX(1 - 0.3679) = 0.6321 \cdot KX$$

Hence the output reaches **$63.21\%$** of its final steady-state value at $t = \tau$, confirming the time-constant interpretation.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Expression for $y(t)$:**

Given $\Delta x = 100 - 30 = 70$ °C, $K = 0.5$ mV/°C, $\tau = 4$ s:

$$y(t) = K \cdot \Delta x \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 0.5 \times 70 \times \left(1 - e^{-t/4}\right)$$

$$y(t) = 35 \left(1 - e^{-t/4}\right) \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute outputs:**

- $y(2) = 35(1 - e^{-0.5}) = 35(1 - 0.6065) = 35 \times 0.3935 = 13.77$ mV
- $y(4) = 35(1 - e^{-1}) = 35 \times 0.6321 = 22.12$ mV
- $y(8) = 35(1 - e^{-2}) = 35 \times 0.8647 = 30.26$ mV
- $y(20) = 35(1 - e^{-5}) = 35 \times 0.9933 = 34.77$ mV

**Step 3 — Bandwidth:**

For a first-order sensor, the **$-3$ dB bandwidth** is:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{0.25}{2\pi} = 0.0398 \text{ Hz} \approx 39.8 \text{ mHz}$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic sensitivity at $f = 1$ Hz:**

At $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi$ rad/s $\approx 6.283$ rad/s.

Dynamic sensitivity (magnitude ratio):

$$M(\omega) = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^2}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi \times 4)^2}}$$

$$M = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{1 + 631.65}} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{632.65}} = \frac{0.5}{25.15} = 0.0199 \text{ mV/°C}$$

This is **25 times smaller** than the static sensitivity $K = 0.5$ mV/°C, because 1 Hz is far beyond the 39.8 mHz bandwidth. The sensor is essentially blind to such rapid temperature fluctuations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with precision** — A precise instrument can still be inaccurate. Always define both with an example.
> 2. **Forgetting the "% of FSO" basis** — Linearity, hysteresis, and repeatability are *percentages*, not absolute deviations. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the divisor (FSO) is missing.
> 3. **Using wrong $e^{-1}$ value** — $e^{-1} = 0.3679$, hence $1 - e^{-1} = 0.6321$ (not $0.65$ or $0.63$). This exact value is required for the time-constant landmark.
> 4. **Mixing up static and dynamic sensitivities** — They are *different* quantities. Static sensitivity is $K = \text{FSO}/\text{Span}$; dynamic sensitivity is $M(\omega) = K/\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}$.
> 5. **Ignoring the up-scale vs down-scale distinction in hysteresis** — Hysteresis is *direction-dependent*. Without two directions, you cannot compute hysteresis.
> 6. **Forgetting units in sensitivity** — A sensitivity of "1.006" without "mV/kPa" loses 0.5 mark.
> 7. **Writing overshoot formula as $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ but plugging in $M_p = 20$ instead of $0.20$** — Always remember: $M_p$ in the formula is the *fractional* overshoot, not the percentage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — "Basic Characteristics"**
>
> **Static Characteristics (DC regime):**
> - **Accuracy** → closeness to *true value*; improvable by recalibration.
> - **Precision** → closeness among *repeated readings*; not improvable by recalibration.
> - **Sensitivity $K$** → $\Delta y / \Delta x = \text{FSO} / \text{Span}$; units = output/input.
> - **Linearity** → max deviation from ideal straight line; **% of FSO**.
> - **Hysteresis** → max output difference for same input approached from opposite directions; **% of FSO**.
> - **Repeatability** → spread under identical short-term conditions; **% of FSO**.
> - **Reproducibility** → spread under varied long-term conditions; **% of FSO**.
> - **Resolution** → smallest detectable $\Delta x$; 1 LSB for digital sensors.
> - **Threshold** → smallest input producing any output.
> - **Dead Band** → range of $x$ where $y \equiv 0$.
> - **Drift** → unwanted change in $y$ with time at constant $x$.
> - **Span** → $x_{\max} - x_{\min}$.
> - **FSO** → $y_{\max} - y_{\min}$.
>
> **Dynamic Characteristics (AC/Transient regime):**
> - **First-order model** → $\tau \dot{y} + y = Kx$.
> - **Step response** → $y(t) = KX(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
> - **Time constant landmark** → at $t = \tau$, output = **63.2 %** of final.
> - **Settling time** → $\approx 5\tau$ for first-order (99.3 %).
> - **Bandwidth (first-order)** → $\omega_c = 1/\tau$, or $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
> - **Dynamic sensitivity (first-order)** → $M(\omega) = K/\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}$.
> - **Phase lag (first-order)** → $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$ (output lags input).
> - **Second-order model** → characterized by $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.
> - **Overshoot** → $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
> - **Damping regimes** → $\zeta = 0$ (undamped), $< 1$ (underdamped), $= 1$ (critical), $> 1$ (overdamped).
> - **Peak time** → $t_p = \pi/(\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$.
> - **Settling time (2 %)** → $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
>
> **Engineering Wisdom:**
> - A **fast sensor** has small $\tau$ and large bandwidth.
> - An **accurate sensor** has small systematic error (correctable).
> - A **precise sensor** has small random noise.
> - A **stable sensor** has low drift.
> - A **linear sensor** simplifies calibration but is rarely achievable; polynomial or look-up-table correction is common.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Photosensistors/Photo Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Photosensistors/Photo Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PHOTOSENSORS (PHOTODETECTORS)** are semiconductor or electronic devices that convert incident optical radiation (photons) into a measurable electrical signal (current, voltage, or change in resistance). They are the foundational transducers in any **optical sensing, imaging, communication, or cyber-physical feedback loop**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, a **Photosistor (Photosensitive Resistor)** is broadly defined as any solid-state device whose electrical characteristics—resistance, current, or voltage—exhibit a deterministic, measurable change in response to incident electromagnetic radiation in the **ultraviolet (UV), visible (VIS), or infrared (IR)** spectral bands.

The generalized transduction mechanism can be represented as:

$$\text{Photon Energy } (h\nu) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrical Output Signal } (V \text{ or } I)$$

where $h$ is the **Planck's constant** ($6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s) and $\nu$ is the **frequency of incident light**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Eye

Think of a photosensor exactly like the **human retina**:
- The **cornea and lens** of your eye gather and focus light → analogous to the **optical system (lens/fiber optic)** in front of a photodetector.
- The **photoreceptor cells (rods and cones)** in your retina convert photons into neural electrical impulses → this is precisely what a **photodiode, phototransistor, or CCD pixel** does.
- The **optic nerve** transmits the signal to the brain → analogous to the **amplifier and signal conditioning circuitry** that follows the photodetector.
- The **brain** interprets the signal → analogous to the **microcontroller / processor** in a cyber-physical system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Just as the human eye can detect intensity, color, and spatial information, advanced photosensors can detect **wavelength (color)**, **intensity (luminance)**, **spatial position (imaging)**, and **polarization** of light.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following are the **fundamental physical constants** universally used in photodetector characterization:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Planck's Constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Speed of Light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Electronic Charge | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| Wavelength-Frequency | $\lambda \nu$ | — | m·Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **threshold condition** for a photodetector to respond to a photon is governed by: $h\nu \geq E_g$, where $E_g$ is the **bandgap energy** of the semiconductor material. This defines the **cutoff wavelength** $\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$, beyond which the detector becomes blind.

## 1.4 The Three Fundamental Operating Mechanisms

Every photosensor falls into one (or a combination) of three quantum-mechanical processes:

1. **Photoelectric Effect (External)** — Photons eject electrons from a material surface (used in photomultiplier tubes).
2. **Photoconductive Effect (Internal)** — Photons generate electron-hole pairs inside a semiconductor, increasing its conductivity (used in LDRs, photodiodes).
3. **Photovoltaic Effect** — Photons generate a voltage across a PN junction (used in solar cells, photodiodes in zero-bias mode).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Photosensors

Photodetectors are systematically classified along **two orthogonal axes**: (a) the underlying physical mechanism, and (b) the device structure/geometry.

### Classification by Physical Mechanism

**A. Photon Effect Detectors (Quantum Detectors)**
- These rely on the **direct interaction of photons with bound electrons** in a semiconductor or vacuum.
- They exhibit **selective wavelength response** (cutoff wavelength is sharp).
- They are **fast** (response time in nanoseconds or faster).
- Subcategories:
  * **Photoemissive Detectors** — Vacuum phototubes, photomultiplier tubes (PMTs), image intensifiers.
  * **Photoconductive Detectors** — Light Dependent Resistors (LDR/CdS cell), photoconductive IR detectors (HgCdTe, PbS, InSb).
  * **Photovoltaic Detectors** — Solar cells, PIN photodiodes, avalanche photodiodes (APDs), phototransistors.

**B. Thermal Effect Detectors**
- These absorb photon energy and convert it to **heat**, which is then measured as a change in a physical property.
- They are **broadband** (wavelength-independent response) and **slower** (milliseconds to seconds).
- Subcategories:
  * **Bolometers** — Resistance changes with temperature (e.g., thermistor bolometer).
  * **Pyroelectric Detectors** — Spontaneous polarization changes with temperature (e.g., LiTaO₃, PZT).
  * **Thermopiles** — Series of thermocouples generating voltage from temperature gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Quantum detectors dominate in **telecommunications (fiber optics), LiDAR, barcode scanners, and high-speed imaging**. Thermal detectors dominate in **spectroscopy, thermal cameras (FLIR), motion detection (PIR sensors), and gas analysis**.

## 2.2 Detailed Operating Principles of Each Major Type

### 2.2.1 Photoresistor (LDR — Light Dependent Resistor)

- **Construction:** A photosensitive semiconductor (typically **Cadmium Sulphide (CdS)** or **Cadmium Selenide (CdSe)**) deposited as a thin zigzag track on a ceramic substrate with two metal electrodes.
- **Operation:** In darkness, the semiconductor has very few free carriers, so resistance is high (typically **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ**). When light falls on it, photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ liberate electron-hole pairs, drastically reducing the resistance (to as low as **few hundred ohms** in bright light).
- **Characteristic Curve:** The resistance follows an empirical relation: $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ where $L$ is the illuminance (lux) and $\gamma$ is typically 0.7–0.9 for CdS.

### 2.2.2 Photodiode (PN, PIN, and Avalanche)

- **PN Photodiode:** Operates in **reverse bias**. Photons absorbed in the depletion region create electron-hole pairs that are swept apart by the electric field, producing a photocurrent proportional to light intensity.
- **PIN Photodiode:** Inserts an **intrinsic (I) layer** between P and N regions. This widens the depletion region, improving quantum efficiency and response speed.
- **Avalanche Photodiode (APD):** Operates near the **breakdown voltage (avalanche region)**. The photo-generated carriers undergo impact ionization, producing internal gain (multiplication factor $M$ typically 10–100).

### 2.2.3 Phototransistor

- A **bipolar junction transistor (BJT)** whose base-collector junction is exposed to light. The light generates a base photocurrent $I_{ph}$, which is then amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$ (typically 100–1000). Thus the collector current is: $I_C = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph}$.
- **Advantage:** Built-in amplification. **Disadvantage:** Slower response than a photodiode.

### 2.2.4 Photothyristor (Light-Activated SCR — LASCR)

- A **thyristor (SCR)** triggered by light instead of a gate pulse. When sufficient light falls on the device, the leakage current increases until the latching condition is met and the device turns ON.

### 2.2.5 Photovoltaic Cell (Solar Cell)

- A large-area **PN junction** designed to deliver power (not just signal) when illuminated. Operates in the **photovoltaic mode (zero bias)** and delivers current to an external load.

### 2.2.6 Charge-Coupled Device (CCD)

- An array of **MOS capacitors** on a silicon substrate. Photogenerated charges are stored in potential wells and then sequentially transferred across the chip to a single output node. Used in **digital cameras, astronomy, and scientific imaging**.

### 2.2.7 CMOS Image Sensor (CIS)

- Each pixel contains its own **photodiode + amplifier + selection transistor** integrated on the same CMOS chip. Lower power than CCD, but historically lower uniformity.

### 2.2.8 Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)

- A vacuum tube with a **photocathode**, several **dynodes** (secondary emission electrodes), and an **anode**. A single photon can produce a measurable pulse (gain $10^6$–$10^7$). Used in **nuclear medicine, spectroscopy, and low-light detection**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J (or eV) | Energy of a single incident photon |
| 2 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$ | m | Maximum wavelength the detector can sense |
| 3 | Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = q \cdot \Phi \cdot \eta$ | A | $\Phi$ = photon flux (photons/s), $\eta$ = quantum efficiency |
| 4 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{N_{e^-}}{N_{photon}} = \frac{I_{ph}/q}{P/(h\nu)}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of incident photons that produce collected electrons |
| 5 | Responsivity | $\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{optical}} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W | Output current per unit incident optical power |
| 6 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = \frac{i_{noise}}{\mathcal{R}}$ | W/$\sqrt{Hz}$ | Minimum detectable power for SNR = 1 in 1 Hz bandwidth |
| 7 | Detectivity | $D = \frac{1}{NEP}$ | $\sqrt{Hz}$/W | Inverse of NEP — higher is better |
| 8 | Specific Detectivity | $D^* = D \cdot \sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}$ | cm·$\sqrt{Hz}$/W | Normalized figure of merit for comparing detectors |
| 9 | LDR Resistance | $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$ | Ω | Empirical; L = illuminance (lux), $\gamma \approx 0.7$–$0.9$ |
| 10 | Phototransistor Gain | $I_C = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph}$ | A | Internal current amplification |
| 11 | APD Photocurrent | $I_{ph,APD} = M \cdot \mathcal{R} \cdot P$ | A | $M$ = avalanche multiplication factor (10–100) |
| 12 | Photovoltaic Open-Ckt V | $V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$ | V | $I_0$ = reverse saturation current |
| 13 | Spectral Response Range | $\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(eV)}$ | $\mu m$ | Quick conversion (uses $hc = 1.24$ eV·µm) |
| 14 | Rise Time (PIN) | $t_r \approx \frac{w}{v_{sat}}$ | s | $w$ = depletion width, $v_{sat}$ = carrier saturation velocity |
| 15 | Dark Current | $I_{dark} \propto T^{3} e^{-E_g/2k_B T}$ | A | Temperature-dependent leakage in absence of light |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Check:** A photodetector with high $D^*$ and low NEP is suitable for **astronomical imaging and quantum communication**, while a detector with high $\mathcal{R}$ and high speed is suitable for **fiber-optic receivers and LiDAR**.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Responsivity from Quantum Efficiency

This is a **classic KTU derivation** that links two of the most important photodetector figures of merit.

**Given:** A photodetector with quantum efficiency $\eta$ is illuminated by monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ and incident optical power $P$.

**To Derive:** The expression for responsivity $\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P}$.

---

### Step 1 — Express the photon flux $\Phi$

If the incident optical power is $P$ (in watts = J/s), and the energy of a single photon is $E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$, then the **number of photons per second** striking the detector is:

$$\Phi = \frac{P}{E_{ph}} = \frac{P \lambda}{h c}$$

**Explanation:** Since power is energy per unit time, dividing the total optical power $P$ by the per-photon energy $E_{ph}$ gives the rate of photon arrival.

---

### Step 2 — Express the number of photo-generated electrons

By the definition of quantum efficiency $\eta$, only a fraction $\eta$ of the incident photons successfully liberate a collected electron. So the rate of photoelectron generation is:

$$\Phi_{e^-} = \eta \cdot \Phi = \frac{\eta P \lambda}{h c}$$

**Explanation:** Quantum efficiency is fundamentally a *probability* per photon. It accounts for reflection losses at the surface, absorption depth, recombination in the depletion region, and collection inefficiency.

---

### Step 3 — Convert electron rate to photocurrent

Each photoelectron carries a charge of magnitude $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C. The photocurrent is therefore the rate of charge flow:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \Phi_{e^-} = q \cdot \frac{\eta P \lambda}{h c}$$

**Explanation:** Current is defined as the rate of charge transport. So multiplying the electron count per second by the per-electron charge gives the current.

---

### Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain responsivity

By definition, $\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P}$. Substituting the result of Step 3:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c}$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{R} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c} \;\; \left[\frac{\text{A}}{\text{W}}\right]\;}$$

**Explanation:** Responsivity is **directly proportional to the wavelength $\lambda$** of light. This is a fundamental and counter-intuitive result — a photodetector is *more* sensitive to long-wavelength (red/IR) photons than to short-wavelength (UV) photons, *provided* $\lambda < \lambda_c$. This is because a long-wavelength photon carries less energy, so more photons arrive per joule, generating more electrons per joule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Photocurrent in a Photodiode under Reverse Bias

**Given:** A reverse-biased PN photodiode with depletion width $w$, reverse bias $V_R$, and incident light absorbed in the depletion region.

**To Derive:** The expression for the total current through the diode.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the three current components

The total current in a photodiode has three contributions:

$$I_{total} = I_{ph} - I_{dark} - I_{shunt}$$

where:
- $I_{ph}$ = photocurrent (signal current, generated by light).
- $I_{dark}$ = dark current (thermally generated leakage in absence of light, flows in the *opposite* direction to $I_{ph}$).
- $I_{shunt}$ = current through the shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ of the diode.

---

### Step 2 — Photocurrent magnitude

If the absorbed photon flux in the depletion region is $\Phi_{abs}$ (photons/s) and the internal quantum efficiency is $\eta_i$, then:

$$I_{ph} = q \eta_i \Phi_{abs}$$

**Explanation:** This is identical in form to the derivation in Section 3.1, but here the relevant flux is the *absorbed* flux, not the incident flux.

---

### Step 3 — Dark current from the Shockley diode equation

The dark (reverse saturation) current for a PN junction is:

$$I_{dark} = I_0 \left(e^{\frac{qV}{k_B T}} - 1\right)$$

Under strong reverse bias, the exponential term is negligible, so $I_{dark} \approx I_0$ (a constant).

---

### Step 4 — Total reverse-biased current

For a reverse-biased photodiode, the applied voltage $V = -V_R$ (negative). The total current becomes:

$$\boxed{\;I_{total} = I_{ph} + I_0 \approx I_{ph} \;\; \text{(when } I_{ph} \gg I_0\text{)}\;}$$

**Explanation:** In a well-designed photodiode, $I_{ph}$ dominates $I_0$, so the current is essentially the photocurrent alone, flowing in the reverse direction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Noise Equivalent Power (NEP) and Detectivity

**Given:** A photodetector with responsivity $\mathcal{R}$ and rms noise current $i_n$ in measurement bandwidth $\Delta f$.

**To Derive:** NEP and specific detectivity $D^*$.

---

### Step 1 — Define the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

$$SNR = \frac{I_{signal}}{i_{noise}} = \frac{\mathcal{R} \cdot P_{optical}}{i_n}$$

**Explanation:** The signal current is the responsivity times the optical power; noise current arises from Johnson noise, shot noise, and flicker (1/f) noise.

---

### Step 2 — NEP definition

NEP is defined as the optical power that produces SNR = 1 in a 1 Hz bandwidth. Setting SNR = 1:

$$1 = \frac{\mathcal{R} \cdot NEP}{i_n} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;NEP = \frac{i_n}{\mathcal{R}}\;}$$

**Explanation:** NEP is *inversely* proportional to responsivity — a more sensitive detector can detect weaker signals.

---

### Step 3 — Derive Specific Detectivity $D^*$

The raw detectivity is $D = 1/NEP$. However, NEP scales with the square root of the detector area $A$ and the bandwidth $\Delta f$. To compare detectors of different sizes, we normalize:

$$\boxed{\;D^* = \sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f} \cdot D = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}\;}$$

Units: cm·Hz$^{1/2}$·W$^{-1}$ (also called "Jones").

**Explanation:** $D^*$ allows a fair comparison between, say, a 1 mm² PIN photodiode and a 1 cm² HgCdTe IR detector, factoring out geometry and bandwidth.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of $\eta = 0.80$ at $\lambda = 900$ nm. The incident optical power is $P = 10$ µW. Calculate: (i) the photon flux $\Phi$, (ii) the photocurrent $I_{ph}$, and (iii) the responsivity $\mathcal{R}$.

**Given:** $\eta = 0.80$, $\lambda = 900$ nm $= 9 \times 10^{-7}$ m, $P = 10$ µW $= 10^{-5}$ W, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

### Solution

**(i) Photon flux:**

$$\Phi = \frac{P \lambda}{h c} = \frac{(10^{-5})(9 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}$$

$$\Phi = \frac{9 \times 10^{-12}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.527 \times 10^{13} \;\; \text{photons/s}$$

**(ii) Photocurrent:**

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.80)(4.527 \times 10^{13})$$

$$I_{ph} = 5.802 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{A} = 5.80 \;\; \mu\text{A}$$

**(iii) Responsivity:**

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.80)(9 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{1.1534 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.580 \;\; \text{A/W}$$

> **Verification using power:** $I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P = 0.580 \times 10^{-5} = 5.80$ µA ✓ (matches Step (ii))

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selecting the Right Photodetector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix in KTU viva voce or design questions. The right column is the **key tradeoff** in selection.

| Application | Recommended Detector | Why? |
|-------------|----------------------|------|
| Fiber-optic communication (1310, 1550 nm) | InGaAs PIN or APD | High speed, high sensitivity in IR |
| Ambient light sensing (auto-dim displays) | LDR (CdS) or Si photodiode | Cheap, mimics human eye response |
| Barcode scanner (650 nm laser) | Si PIN photodiode | Fast response, visible wavelength |
| Solar energy harvesting | c-Si or thin-film PV cell | High power conversion efficiency, large area |
| Thermal imaging (8–14 µm) | Microbolometer or HgCdTe | Sensitive to long-wave IR |
| Medical pulse oximeter | Si photodiode + LED (660/940 nm) | Matches Hb/HbO₂ absorption peaks |
| Motion detection (PIR) | Pyroelectric detector | Detects 10 µm IR from human body |
| Low-light / photon counting | Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) or Si PM | Internal gain $10^6$–$10^7$ |
| Digital camera | CMOS Image Sensor | Low power, integrated A/D |
| Astronomy / spectroscopy | CCD (back-illuminated, cooled) | Ultra-low noise, high $D^*$ |
| Industrial light curtain / safety | Phototransistor with IR LED | Built-in gain, robust |
