## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between photoconductive mode and photovoltaic mode of operation in photodiodes.**

### Model Answer (3 marks — 80–100 words):

| Aspect | Photoconductive Mode | Photovoltaic Mode |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Bias** | Reverse biased | Zero bias |
| **Speed** | Fast (ns) | Slower (µs) |
| **Linearity** | Highly linear | Slightly nonlinear |
| **Dark current** | Present ($I_0$) | Zero (no bias) |
| **Output** | Current $I_{ph}$ | Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ |
| **Noise** | Higher (shot noise from bias) | Lower |
| **Application** | High-speed optical communication | Solar cells, precision light meters |

> **Mnemonic for students:** "**PV** = **P**assive **V**oltage, **PC** = **P**owered **C**urrent."

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **Define the terms quantum efficiency and responsivity. How are they related?**

### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of the number of photo-generated electrons collected at the output terminal to the number of incident photons, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{N_{e^-}}{N_{photon}}$$

**Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** The ratio of the output photocurrent to the incident optical power, expressed in **A/W**.

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P}$$

**Relation:**

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{R} = \frac{q \lambda}{h c} \cdot \eta\;}$$

Responsivity is **directly proportional** to quantum efficiency, with the proportionality constant $\frac{q \lambda}{h c}$ being wavelength-dependent. Hence, at any fixed wavelength, doubling $\eta$ doubles $\mathcal{R}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2]`**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **PIN photodiode**. Compare its performance with a conventional **PN photodiode**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive the expressions for **responsivity** and **quantum efficiency** of a photodetector. A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 65% at 850 nm. Find its responsivity. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### (a) PIN Photodiode — Construction, Working & Comparison (7 marks)

**Construction:**
A PIN photodiode consists of three regions sandwiched between two ohmic metal contacts:
1. **P-region** — heavily doped p-type semiconductor (very thin, ~1 µm).
2. **Intrinsic (I) region** — undoped (or very lightly doped) semiconductor, typically 20–50 µm thick.
3. **N-region** — heavily doped n-type semiconductor.
4. The whole sandwich is mounted on a substrate with an **anti-reflection coating** on the illuminated face to minimize reflection losses.

**Working Principle:**

When reverse-biased, the entire intrinsic region becomes **fully depleted** (no free carriers), and a strong uniform electric field exists across it. Photons entering through the P-layer pass through the thin P-region (which is mostly transparent) and are absorbed primarily in the **wide intrinsic region**. The photon-generated electron-hole pairs are **instantly swept apart** by the strong field — electrons to the N-side, holes to the P-side — producing a photocurrent.

**Key advantages of the intrinsic layer:**
- Widens the depletion region → most photons are absorbed in the high-field region → **higher quantum efficiency**.
- Reduces the **junction capacitance** ($C_j \propto 1/w$, where $w$ is the depletion width) → **faster response time**.
- Lower noise due to lower bulk resistance.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PN Photodiode | PIN Photodiode |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Depletion width | Narrow (~1–3 µm) | Wide (20–50 µm, all of I-region) |
| Quantum efficiency | 30–60% | 60–90% |
| Response speed | Slower (µs) | Faster (ns to sub-ns) |
| Junction capacitance | Higher | Much lower |
| Sensitivity to low light | Lower | Higher |
| Typical application | General light sensing | High-speed fiber-optic receivers, CD players |

---

#### (b) Derivation of Responsivity and Quantum Efficiency (7 marks)

**Derivation of Quantum Efficiency:**

Let $N_{ph}$ be the number of photons incident on the detector per second and $N_e$ the number of photoelectrons collected per second. By definition:

$$\eta = \frac{N_e}{N_{ph}}$$

If each photon has energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ and the incident optical power is $P$:

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P}{h\nu} = \frac{P \lambda}{h c}, \quad N_e = \frac{I_{ph}}{q}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\;\eta = \frac{I_{ph} / q}{P \lambda / h c} = \frac{I_{ph} \cdot h c}{q \cdot P \cdot \lambda}\;}$$

**Derivation of Responsivity:**

Rearranging the above for $I_{ph}/P$:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c}$$

This is the **fundamental responsivity equation**. (Full derivation already shown in Section 3.1.)

**Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.65$, $\lambda = 850$ nm $= 8.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.65)(8.5 \times 10^{-7})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{8.852 \times 10^{-26}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{R} = 0.4453 \;\; \text{A/W} = 445.3 \;\; \text{mA/W}\;}$$

> **Exam tip:** A common trap is to forget to convert nm → m. Always do unit conversion **first**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]`**
> **(a)** Explain the construction and working of **phototransistors** and **photothyristors (LASCR)**. Mention their advantages and limitations. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the different types of photodetectors based on their **operating principles**, with suitable examples and applications. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

#### (a) Phototransistor and Photothyristor (7 marks)

**Phototransistor — Construction & Working:**

A phototransistor is essentially a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) — typically **NPN** — where the base region is made photosensitive and the base terminal is often left **floating** (no external electrical connection). The base-collector junction is exposed to incident light through a transparent window in the package.

**Working Principle:**
1. Light photons strike the **reverse-biased base-collector junction**.
2. Photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs in the BC depletion region.
3. The photo-generated holes drift into the **base region**, where they accumulate and forward-bias the **base-emitter junction**.
4. This forward bias causes a large injection of electrons from the emitter into the base.
5. The transistor's normal current amplification action (factor $\beta$) then **amplifies** the small photo-generated base current into a much larger collector current:

$$\boxed{\;I_C = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph}\;}$$

**Advantages:** Built-in gain of 100–1000; compact; no separate amplifier needed.
**Limitations:** Slower than photodiodes (µs); sensitive to temperature; nonlinear at high illumination.

**Photothyristor (LASCR) — Construction & Working:**

A Light-Activated Silicon Controlled Rectifier (LASCR) is a four-layer (PNPN) thyristor with a transparent window to admit light onto one of the reverse-biased junctions (typically J2).

**Working Principle:**
1. In the OFF state, the LASCR blocks the supply voltage $V_{AK}$.
2. Incident light on the J2 junction generates electron-hole pairs.
3. The resulting photocurrent acts like a **gate trigger current**.
4. When the photocurrent exceeds the **latching current** of the device, the regenerative PNPN action latches the LASCR into the ON state, and it remains ON even after the light is removed (until the anode current falls below the holding current).

**Advantages:** High current handling (amps to kiloamps); electrical isolation between control and power; ideal for AC mains switching from a light signal.
**Limitations:** Once latched, cannot be turned off by removing light; needs external commutation circuit.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Phototransistor | Photothyristor (LASCR) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| Layers | 3 (NPN) | 4 (PNPN) |
| Terminals | 3 (C, B, E) | 3 (A, K, G) — light acts as G |
| Output | Analog current | Latched ON/OFF |
| Typical current | mA range | A to kA range |
| Reset method | Remove light | Reduce $I_A$ below $I_H$ |
| Application | Optical encoders, object detection | Streetlight auto-switch, HV protection |

---

#### (b) Classification of Photodetectors by Operating Principle (7 marks)

Photodetectors are classified into three broad families based on the quantum-mechanical mechanism by which they convert photons into electrical signals:

**1. Photoemissive Detectors:**
Photons strike a photosensitive **cathode** and eject electrons via the external photoelectric effect. These electrons are collected at an anode (and may be multiplied by secondary emission at intermediate **dynodes**).
- *Examples:* Vacuum phototube, photomultiplier tube (PMT), image intensifier.
- *Applications:* Low-light astronomy, nuclear medicine, scintillation counting, photon counting.
- *Merits:* Extremely high gain ($10^6$–$10^7$), ultra-low noise.
- *Demerits:* Bulky, fragile, requires high voltage (1–2 kV).

**2. Photoconductive Detectors:**
Photon absorption increases the **electrical conductivity** of a semiconductor by generating free electron-hole pairs. The device is operated in a simple resistive circuit (e.g., LDR) or as a two-terminal photoresistor with an applied bias.
- *Examples:* CdS/CdSe LDR, PbS, PbSe, InSb, HgCdTe (MCT) cooled IR detectors.
- *Applications:* Camera light meters, flame detection, IR spectroscopy, thermal imaging.
- *Merits:* Wide spectral range (can be tuned via alloy composition), simple construction, low cost.
- *Demerits:* Requires cryogenic cooling for long-wave IR; slow response; nonlinear resistance-vs-light curve.

**3. Photovoltaic Detectors:**
A built-in electric field at a PN junction separates photon-generated carriers, producing either a **photocurrent** (under reverse bias) or a **photovoltage** (in open circuit).
- *Examples:* Si photodiode, PIN photodiode, avalanche photodiode (APD), phototransistor, solar cell, photothyristor.
- *Applications:* Optical communication, barcode scanners, solar power, imaging arrays.
- *Merits:* Fast, linear, low noise, can be made very small (integrated circuits).
- *Demerits:* Limited spectral range set by bandgap; silicon is blind beyond ~1.1 µm.

**Summary Table:**

| Mechanism | Photoemissive | Photoconductive | Photovoltaic |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|
| Physical effect | External photoelectric | Carrier generation | Junction field separation |
| Speed | Very fast (ns) | Slow to moderate (µs–ms) | Very fast (ps–ns) |
| Internal gain | Yes (dynodes) | No | Optional (APD) |
| Spectral selectivity | Sharp (cathode work function) | Tunable via $E_g$ | Tunable via $E_g$ |
| Typical materials | GaAs(Cs), bialkali | CdS, PbS, HgCdTe | Si, Ge, InGaAs, GaAs |
| Cooling required? | No | Often yes (for IR) | Usually no |
| Cost | High | Low–Medium | Low (Si) – High (InGaAs) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Disaster:** Forgetting to convert nm → m in the responsivity formula $\mathcal{R} = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$ leads to answers off by $10^9$. **Always convert first.**
> 2. **Confusing $D$ and $D^*$:** $D$ is just the inverse of NEP; $D^*$ is the *area-normalized* detectivity. Don't use them interchangeably.
> 3. **Forgetting $h\nu \geq E_g$:** If a question asks "can a Si photodiode ($E_g = 1.12$ eV) detect 1550 nm light?", you MUST compute $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$ first. The answer is **NO** (1550 nm > 1107 nm).
> 4. **LDR is NOT a photodiode:** A common KTU trap — LDR is *photoconductive*, not photovoltaic. Mark deduction: 1 mark.
> 5. **Phototransistor gain formula:** Must write $I_C = (\beta+1) I_{ph}$, not just $I_C = \beta I_{ph}$. The "+1" accounts for the leakage contribution.
> 6. **Diagrams matter:** In PIN photodiode and phototransistor answers, always draw the **depletion region and bias polarity**. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for the diagram alone.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY LAST-MINUTE REVISION — READ THIS BEFORE ENTERING THE EXAM HALL**

### Key Concepts to Memorize

- **Photosensor:** Converts photons → electrical signal. Foundational transducer.
- **Threshold equation:** $h\nu \geq E_g$ (a photon must have at least the bandgap energy to be detected).
- **Cutoff wavelength:** $\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ µm.
- **Quantum efficiency ($\eta$):** Probability that an incident photon produces a collected electron (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%).
- **Responsivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** Output current per unit optical power. Units: A/W. Formula: $\mathcal{R} = \frac{q\eta\lambda}{hc}$.
- **Photocurrent:** $I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi = \mathcal{R} \cdot P$.
- **NEP:** Minimum detectable power for SNR = 1 in 1 Hz bandwidth. $NEP = i_n / \mathcal{R}$.
- **Specific detectivity $D^*$:** Normalized figure of merit, units cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W (Jones). Higher is better.
- **Photoconductive mode:** Reverse-biased photodiode — fast, linear, but has dark current.
- **Photovoltaic mode:** Zero-biased photodiode — slower but zero dark current; used for solar cells.
- **PIN photodiode:** Adds intrinsic layer → wider depletion → higher efficiency, lower capacitance, faster.
- **APD:** Operates near breakdown → internal avalanche gain $M = 10$–$100$.
- **Phototransistor:** Photodiode + BJT in one package. $I_C = (\beta+1) I_{ph}$.
- **Photothyristor (LASCR):** Light triggers the latching of a PNPN structure; used in HV switching.
- **LDR (CdS):** Resistance drops from MΩ (dark) to few hundred ohms (light). $R \propto L^{-\gamma}$.
- **CCD:** Charge-coupled device — analog shift register for charge; high imaging quality.
- **CMOS Image Sensor:** Active pixel — each pixel has its own amplifier; lower power.
- **PMT:** Photocathode + dynodes + anode. Gain $10^6$–$10^7$. Used for single-photon detection.
- **Thermal detectors:** Bolometer, pyroelectric, thermopile. Wavelength-independent, slow.

### Comparative Snapshot (Most Important Table)

| Detector | Wavelength | Speed | Gain | Cost | Best For |
|----------|-----------|-------|------|------|----------|
| LDR (CdS) | VIS | Slow (ms) | None | Very Low | Toys, light meters |
| Si Photodiode | 400–1100 nm | Fast (ns) | None | Low | General purpose |
| PIN Photodiode | 400–1100 nm | Very fast (sub-ns) | None | Low | Fiber optics |
| APD | 400–1550 nm | Very fast | $M$ = 10–100 | Medium | Long-haul comms, LiDAR |
| Phototransistor | 400–1100 nm | Moderate (µs) | $\beta$ = 100–1000 | Low | Object detection, encoders |
| InGaAs PIN/APD | 900–1700 nm | Very fast | Optional | High | Telecom (1310/1550 nm) |
| PMT | UV–VIS | Ultra fast | $10^6$–$10^7$ | Very High | Photon counting |
| CCD | VIS–NIR | Moderate | None | High | Astronomy, scientific imaging |
| CMOS Image Sensor | VIS–NIR | Fast | Per-pixel amp | Low–Medium | Cameras, smartphones |
| Pyroelectric (PIR) | 8–14 µm | Slow | None | Very Low | Motion detection |
| Bolometer (micro) | 8–14 µm | Slow | None | High | Thermal cameras (FLIR) |
| Solar Cell (c-Si) | 400–1100 nm | N/A | None | Low | Power generation |

### Final Formulas (Memorize Verbatim)

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \quad \mu m$$

$$\eta = \frac{N_{e^-}}{N_{photon}} = \frac{I_{ph} \cdot h c}{q \cdot P \cdot \lambda}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P} = \frac{q \eta \lambda}{h c} \quad \left[\frac{A}{W}\right]$$

$$NEP = \frac{i_n}{\mathcal{R}} \quad [W / \sqrt{Hz}]$$

$$D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP} \quad [cm \cdot \sqrt{Hz} / W]$$

$$I_C = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph} \quad \text{(Phototransistor)}$$

$$V_{oc} = \frac{k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right) \quad \text{(Solar cell)}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-ray and Nuclear Radiation Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** X-ray and Nuclear Radiation Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 X-rays: The Formal Definition

**X-rays** are a form of **electromagnetic radiation** possessing wavelengths in the range of approximately **0.01 nm to 10 nm**, corresponding to photon energies between **100 eV and 100 keV**. They occupy the high-frequency portion of the electromagnetic spectrum, situated between ultraviolet light and gamma radiation. In engineering measurement systems, X-rays are classified as *ionizing radiation* because their photon energy exceeds the binding energy of valence electrons in atoms (typically > 10 eV), allowing them to eject orbital electrons upon interaction with matter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** X-rays are *NOT* particles; they are photons (quanta of electromagnetic energy). Confusing this with nuclear radiation (alpha/beta particles) is the single most common conceptual error in board exams.

### 1.2 Nuclear Radiation: The Formal Definition

**Nuclear radiation** refers to particles or electromagnetic waves emitted from unstable atomic nuclei during radioactive decay. The three principal types are:

- **Alpha particles (α):** Helium-4 nuclei ($^4_2\text{He}$) — heavy, +2 charge, low penetration (~few cm in air, stopped by paper).
- **Beta particles (β):** High-energy electrons ($e^-$) or positrons ($e^+$) — moderate penetration (~few mm in aluminum).
- **Gamma rays (γ):** High-energy electromagnetic photons (> 100 keV) — highly penetrating, requiring dense shielding (lead, concrete).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Ultrasonic Rangefinder of the Atomic World"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a bat using echolocation to "see" insects. X-rays and nuclear sensors work the same way — they *emit* a known signal, let it *interact* with a target, and then *measure* what comes back. If a bat sends a sound wave and the echo is weak, it knows the insect is small or far. Similarly, if an X-ray sensor sends a beam through a bone and the detector measures a shadow pattern, we can reconstruct the internal structure of the bone. The "sound waves" here are photons or particles; the "echo" is the attenuated/secondary radiation reaching the detector.

The fundamental sensing principle is identical to radar or sonar: **transmit → interact with target → detect return signal → infer material/dimensional properties**.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (Memorize These)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $3.00 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Electron charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | C |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | mol⁻¹ |
| Energy conversion | 1 eV | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | J |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** X-ray sensors dominate medical imaging (CT scanners, dental radiography), security screening (airport baggage scanners), industrial non-destructive testing (weld inspection), and material crystallography (XRD). Nuclear radiation sensors are vital in nuclear power plant monitoring, medical PET/SPECT imaging, smoke detectors, and geological dating.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Production of X-rays: The Two Mechanisms

X-rays are produced in an **X-ray tube** (a vacuum diode) when high-energy electrons strike a metal target (typically tungsten, molybdenum, or copper). Two distinct mechanisms generate the X-ray spectrum:

**Mechanism A — Bremsstrahlung ("Braking Radiation"):**
- When a high-speed electron passes near the nucleus of a target atom, the Coulomb field decelerates/deflects the electron.
- The kinetic energy lost is converted into an X-ray photon.
- This produces a **continuous spectrum** (white spectrum) with a well-defined minimum wavelength (Duane–Hunt limit).
- Maximum photon energy = full kinetic energy of the incident electron.

**Mechanism B — Characteristic Radiation (K-series, L-series):**
- The incident electron *ejects* an inner-shell electron (typically from the K-shell) of the target atom.
- An outer-shell electron drops down to fill the vacancy, releasing a photon with energy equal to the difference between the two shell energy levels.
- This produces **discrete sharp peaks** superimposed on the continuous background.
- The energy is *characteristic* of the target material, governed by **Moseley's Law**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of an X-ray Sensor System

1. **Electron emission:** A heated cathode (tungsten filament at ~2500 K) emits electrons via thermionic emission.
2. **Acceleration:** A high potential difference $V$ (typically 30–150 kV in medical X-rays) accelerates the electrons toward the anode.
3. **Target interaction:** Electrons strike the metal target. ~99% of energy becomes heat; ~1% becomes X-rays.
4. **Beam shaping/collimation:** X-rays exit through a beryllium window, then are collimated into a directed beam.
5. **Sample interaction:** The X-ray beam penetrates the sample, undergoing absorption, scattering, and transmission.
6. **Detection:** The transmitted/scattered/fluorescent X-ray is captured by a detector (film, scintillator, semiconductor, or Geiger counter).
7. **Signal processing:** The detector output is processed to yield image, spectrum, or quantitative measurement.

### 2.3 Nuclear Radiation Interaction with Matter

When nuclear radiation strikes matter, three primary interactions occur:

- **Photoelectric effect:** Photon transfers *all* its energy to a bound electron, ejecting it. Dominates at low energies and high-Z materials.
- **Compton scattering:** Photon scatters off a quasi-free electron, transferring *part* of its energy. Dominates at intermediate energies (~100 keV – 10 MeV).
- **Pair production:** High-energy photon (> 1.022 MeV) converts into an electron-positron pair in the Coulomb field of a nucleus. Dominates at high energies.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *frequently tested* in KTU board exams. Memorize the variables, the boundary conditions, and the units. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of $\vert$ for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $h$ = Planck const, $\nu$ = frequency, $\lambda$ = wavelength | eV, J, keV |
| 2 | $eV = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$ | Duane–Hunt limit (max X-ray energy) | $V$ = tube voltage, $\lambda_{\min}$ = min wavelength | V, m |
| 3 | $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1240}{V(\text{in V})}$ nm | Short-wavelength limit (numerical) | $V$ in volts | nm |
| 4 | $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$ | **Moseley's Law** (characteristic X-rays) | $Z$ = atomic number, $a, b$ = constants | Hz, dimensionless |
| 5 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | **Bragg's Law** (X-ray diffraction / XRD) | $d$ = plane spacing, $\theta$ = incidence angle, $n$ = order | nm, degrees |
| 6 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | **Beer–Lambert Attenuation Law** | $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient, $x$ = thickness | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mu = \mu_m \cdot \rho$ | Mass attenuation vs linear attenuation | $\mu_m$ = mass coeff, $\rho$ = density | cm⁻¹, cm²/g |
| 8 | $N = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | $N$ = remaining nuclei, $\lambda$ = decay constant | dimensionless |
| 9 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Half-life | $t_{1/2}$ in seconds | s, years |
| 10 | $A = \lambda N$ | Activity (decays per second) | $A$ in Becquerel (Bq) | s⁻¹, Ci |
| 11 | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Curie–Becquerel conversion | — | — |
| 12 | $R = r_0^2 Z^{1/3}$ | Nuclear radius (semi-empirical) | $r_0 \approx 1.2$ fm | fm |
| 13 | $E_{\gamma} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_\gamma}$ | Gamma photon energy | Same as #1 | keV, MeV |
| 14 | $C = \dfrac{dE}{dx}$ | Stopping power (energy loss rate) | — | MeV/cm |
| 15 | $N_e = n \cdot \mu \cdot I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} \cdot V$ | Number of ion pairs created (per unit time) | $n$ = efficiency, $V$ = volume | s⁻¹ |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility: Where This Matters

- **Medical diagnostics:** X-ray CT scanners exploit the Beer-Lambert law to reconstruct 3D anatomical images from 2D projections.
- **Security:** Luggage scanners use dual-energy X-ray imaging to distinguish organic from inorganic materials.
- **Materials science:** X-ray diffraction (XRD) uses Bragg's law to identify crystal structures and measure lattice parameters.
- **Nuclear industry:** Geiger-Müller counters with Moseley's law are used in spectroscopy to identify trace elements.
- **Space exploration:** Scintillation + semiconductor detectors measure cosmic ray flux in satellites.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Duane–Hunt Short-Wavelength Limit

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the minimum wavelength of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube operating at potential difference $V$.

**Starting Principle:** When a single electron of charge $e$ is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$, it acquires kinetic energy equal to $eV$. The maximum photon energy is produced when the *entire* kinetic energy of the electron is converted into a single X-ray photon (the most energetic photon possible from Bremsstrahlung).

**Step 1: Write the kinetic energy gained by the electron.**

$$E_k = eV$$

This equation says: the work done by the electric field on the electron equals the kinetic energy it gains.

**Step 2: Equate the electron's kinetic energy to the maximum photon energy.**

$$E_k = E_{\text{photon, max}}$$

The reasoning: the most energetic photon is created when 100% of the electron's energy is converted to a photon (the rarest event, but it sets the spectral upper bound).

**Step 3: Substitute the photon energy formula $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.**

$$eV = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

This expresses the conservation of energy between the electron and the emitted photon.

**Step 4: Solve algebraically for $\lambda_{\min}$.**

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values of the constants.**

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s})(3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C})(V)}$$

**Step 6: Simplify the numerical coefficient.**

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V}\,\text{m}$$

**Step 7: Express in nanometers for a tube voltage $V$ in volts.**

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1240}{V}\,\text{nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** $V > 0$ (must be accelerating, not decelerating). $V$ is the *peak* voltage, not RMS. In a 100 kV tube, the shortest X-ray wavelength is $0.0124$ nm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Beer–Lambert Attenuation Law from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** Show that a beam of monochromatic X-rays passing through a material of thickness $x$ follows $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.

**Step 1: Set up the differential equation.**

Consider a thin slice of material of thickness $dx$. The probability of a photon interacting in this slice is proportional to the thickness and to the local photon flux:

$$dI = -\mu \cdot I \cdot dx$$

The negative sign indicates intensity *decreases* with increasing path length. $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), a material property.

**Step 2: Separate variables to prepare for integration.**

$$\dfrac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides from the initial intensity $I_0$ to the final intensity $I$, and from $x = 0$ to $x$.**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \dfrac{dI'}{I'} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx'$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu x$$

**Step 5: Apply the logarithm identity $\ln(a) - \ln(b) = \ln(a/b)$.**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

**Step 6: Exponentiate both sides.**

$$\dfrac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 7: Solve for $I$.**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

This is the **Beer–Lambert Law**, valid for monochromatic X-rays. The quantity $\mu$ depends on photon energy and the material's atomic number $Z$ and density $\rho$.

> [!TIP]
> **Half-value layer (HVL):** Setting $I = I_0 / 2$ gives $x_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \mu = 0.693 / \mu$. This is the thickness needed to halve the X-ray intensity and is a standard engineering metric for shielding design.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Moseley's Law for Characteristic X-rays

**Problem Statement:** Derive the relationship between characteristic X-ray frequency and atomic number.

**Step 1: Bohr model energy for a hydrogen-like atom.**

For an electron in the $n$-th orbit of an atom with effective nuclear charge $(Z - \sigma)$ (where $\sigma$ is the screening constant):

$$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6\,\text{eV} \cdot (Z - \sigma)^2}{n^2}$$

**Step 2: Energy of the photon emitted in a $K_\alpha$ transition ($n = 2 \to n = 1$).**

$$E_{K_\alpha} = E_2 - E_1 = -13.6(Z - \sigma_K)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{4} - 1\right) = \dfrac{13.6 \cdot 3(Z - \sigma_K)^2}{4}$$

**Step 3: Convert energy to frequency using $E = h\nu$.**

$$\nu_{K_\alpha} = \dfrac{E_{K_\alpha}}{h} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 13.6 \cdot (Z - \sigma_K)^2}{4h}$$

**Step 4: Recognize that this is a square-law relationship.**

$$\nu_{K_\alpha} = a^2 (Z - b)^2$$

where $a$ and $b = \sigma_K$ are constants.

**Step 5: Take the square root of both sides.**

$$\sqrt{\nu_{K_\alpha}} = a(Z - b)$$

This is **Moseley's Law**. The graph of $\sqrt{\nu}$ versus $Z$ is a straight line, allowing experimental identification of elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:** Valid for $Z \geq 3$ (lithium and above, since $K$-shell transitions require at least K and L shells to exist). The constant $b \approx 1$ for $K_\alpha$ lines.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: X-ray vs. Nuclear Radiation Sensors

| Property | X-ray Sensors | Nuclear Radiation Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Source | X-ray tube (artificial) | Radioactive isotope (natural or artificial) |
| Detected entity | X-ray photons | α, β, γ particles + neutrons |
| Primary interaction | Photoelectric, Compton | Ionization, scintillation |
| Typical detectors | Film, scintillator, semiconductor, CCD | GM counter, ionization chamber, semiconductor |
| Energy range | 0.1 – 100 keV | 0.1 – 10 MeV |
| Shielding | Lead, concrete | Lead, borated polyethylene (for neutrons) |
| Engineering applications | Medical imaging, XRD, NDT, baggage scan | Nuclear plant monitoring, PET, smoke detectors |
| Formulae that dominate | Bragg's law, Beer–Lambert, Duane–Hunt | Decay law, Moseley's law, activity equation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: X-ray Production Tube (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 150 kV] --> B[Cathode Tungsten Filament heated to 2500 K]
    B --> C[Thermionic Emission of Electrons]
    C --> D[Electron Acceleration in Vacuum]
    D --> E[Focusing Cup and Anode Target Tungsten]
    E --> F[Bremsstrahlung Continuous Spectrum]
    E --> G[Characteristic X rays K alpha K beta Lines]
    F --> H[Beryllium Window Output]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Collimated X ray Beam]
    I --> J[Sample or Patient]
    J --> K[Detector Film Scintillator CCD]
    K --> L[Image or Spectrum Processing]
    L --> M[Display Image or Quantitative Result]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow moves from power input (top-left) to image output (bottom-right). Two parallel X-ray generation mechanisms (Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic) converge at the beryllium window. The detector branch is a *feedback path* — without the detector, the system is just an X-ray source.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Nuclear Radiation Sensor System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Radioactive Source Co-60 Cs-137 Am-241] --> B[Emission of Alpha Beta Gamma Neutron]
    B --> C[Interaction with Sample Material]
    C --> D[Type of Interaction Detection]
    D --> E[Photoelectric Effect in High Z Detector]
    D --> F[Compton Scattering in Medium Z Material]
    D --> G[Pair Production in High Energy Gamma]
    E --> H[Detector Selection Logic]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[GM Counter for High Sensitivity]
    H --> J[Ionization Chamber for Dose Measurement]
    H --> K[Scintillation Detector for Spectroscopy]
    H --> L[Semiconductor Detector Si Ge CdZnTe for High Resolution]
    I --> M[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Discriminator]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Multi Channel Analyzer MCA]
    N --> O[Energy Spectrum Output]
    M --> P[Count Rate Meter]
    P --> Q[Activity or Dose Rate Display]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic:** GM counters are simple, robust, but cannot distinguish energies. Ionization chambers measure dose accurately. Scintillation detectors offer moderate resolution and fast timing. Semiconductor detectors give the best energy resolution (~0.1%) but require cryogenic cooling for high-purity germanium.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: X-ray Interaction Pathways in Matter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident X ray Photon Energy E] --> B{Energy Range}
    B -->|Low E less than 50 keV| C[Photoelectric Dominant]
    B -->|Medium E 50 keV to 1 MeV| D[Compton Scattering Dominant]
    B -->|High E greater than 1.022 MeV| E[Pair Production Possible]
    C --> F[Electron Ejected with Energy E minus Binding]
    D --> G[Photon Scattered at Angle theta]
    G --> H[Recoil Electron with Reduced Energy]
    E --> I[Electron Positron Pair Created]
    F --> J[Detector Absorbs Energy]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Energy Deposited Converted to Signal]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to identify the dominant interaction in a given scenario, compare the photon energy to the binding energies of the material's electrons and to 1.022 MeV (the pair-production threshold). The K-edge energy of the detector material is also a critical parameter.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Operating Regions of Gas-Filled Radiation Detectors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Voltage Across Detector Tube] --> B{Region}
    B -->|Low V no collection| C[Recombination Region]
    B -->|Moderate V| D[Ionization Chamber Region Saturation Current]
    B -->|Higher V proportional| E[Proportional Counter Region Gas Multiplication]
    B -->|Even Higher V Geiger plateau| F[GM Counter Region Avalanche Discharge]
    B -->|Excessive V| G[Continuous Discharge Region Damage]
    D --> H[Measure Total Ionization Current]
    E --> I[Output Proportional to Energy of Incident Radiation]
    F --> J[Output Independent of Energy only count rate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** Operating a GM tube in the wrong voltage region (e.g., in the recombination or continuous-discharge region) leads to erroneous readings or detector damage. The "Geiger plateau" is the safe operating zone.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Bremsstrahlung radiation. Explain how it is produced in an X-ray tube.**

**Model Answer:**

Bremsstrahlung (German for "braking radiation") is the **continuous-spectrum electromagnetic radiation** produced when a high-speed charged particle — typically an electron — is *decelerated* or *deflected* by the Coulomb field of a target nucleus.

**Production mechanism:**
1. A high-energy electron (accelerated through potential $V$) approaches a target atom's nucleus.
2. The strong electrostatic attraction between the electron and the positively charged nucleus causes the electron to slow down and change direction.
3. The lost kinetic energy is emitted as an X-ray photon.
4. The energy of the emitted photon can range from 0 up to the full kinetic energy of the electron, giving a *continuous spectrum*.
5. The minimum wavelength (maximum energy) is given by the Duane–Hunt limit: $\lambda_{\min} = hc / (eV)$.

The maximum photon energy occurs when the electron loses *all* its kinetic energy in a single encounter. Most encounters, however, produce lower-energy photons, leading to the characteristic "continuous" shape of the spectrum.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between ionization chamber, proportional counter, and Geiger-Müller counter based on their operating voltage region and output characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ionization Chamber | Proportional Counter | Geiger-Müller Counter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating region | Moderate voltage (saturation) | Higher voltage (gas multiplication) | High voltage (Geiger plateau) |
| Output | Current proportional to total ionization | Pulse amplitude ∝ radiation energy | Pulse amplitude independent of energy |
| Measures | Total dose / exposure rate | Energy spectrum possible | Only count rate (yes/no radiation) |
| Gas multiplication | None (gain ≈ 1) | Moderate ($10^2$ – $10^4$) | Very high ($10^8$ – $10^{10}$) |
| Sensitivity | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Use case | Dosimetry, radiation monitoring | Spectroscopy of alpha/beta | Survey meters, contamination checks |

The three devices use the same basic gas-filled tube design, but differ in the applied voltage, which determines the behavior of the ion-electron pairs created by incident radiation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an X-ray tube. Derive the expression for the cut-off wavelength of continuous X-rays. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (Diagram Reference — See Section 4.1 Block Diagram):**

An X-ray tube is a high-vacuum diode with the following essential components:

1. **Cathode:** A tungsten filament heated to ~2500 K by a low-voltage AC supply (~10 V). It emits electrons via thermionic emission.
2. **Focusing cup:** A molybdenum cup that focuses the emitted electrons into a narrow beam aimed at the anode.
3. **Anode (target):** A high-$Z$, high-melting-point metal, typically tungsten ($Z = 74$, melting point 3422 °C), embedded in a copper block for heat dissipation.
4. **High-voltage source:** A step-up transformer providing 30–150 kV DC across cathode and anode.
5. **Beryllium window:** A thin (~0.5 mm) Be window that allows X-rays to exit the tube while maintaining vacuum and absorbing low-energy soft X-rays.
6. **Cooling system:** Water or oil circulation through the anode block to remove ~99% of the input power (which becomes heat, not X-rays).

**Working:**

- Electrons emitted from the heated cathode are accelerated toward the anode by the high potential difference $V$.
- On striking the tungsten target, the electrons undergo two processes:
  - **Bremsstrahlung** — sudden deceleration in the nuclear Coulomb field produces a *continuous* X-ray spectrum.
  - **Characteristic radiation** — ejection of inner-shell electrons followed by outer-shell electron transitions produces *discrete* characteristic X-ray lines.
- The X-rays exit through the beryllium window and are collimated into a directed beam for the application.

**Derivation of Cut-off Wavelength:**

The maximum photon energy is produced when the entire kinetic energy of an accelerated electron is converted into a single X-ray photon.

$$eV = E_{\text{photon, max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

Solving for $\lambda_{\min}$:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

Substituting constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1240}{V\,\text{(in volts)}}\,\text{nm}$$

For a 50 kV tube: $\lambda_{\min} = 1240 / 50000 = 0.0248$ nm.

---

**(b)** Explain the principle of a Geiger-Müller (GM) counter. Draw its characteristic curve and mark the different regions of operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**

A GM counter is a gas-filled (typically argon + halogen quench gas) cylindrical tube with a thin anode wire along the axis and the cylindrical wall as cathode. A high voltage (~500–1000 V) is applied between them, creating a strong radial electric field.

When ionizing radiation (β or γ) enters through a thin mica or beryllium window, it creates primary ion-electron pairs in the gas. These electrons are accelerated toward the central anode. Near the anode wire, where the field is very intense, secondary ionization (Townsend avalanche) occurs, producing a large number of additional ion pairs. The avalanche spreads along the entire length of the anode wire via UV photon emission, producing a uniform discharge.

This discharge produces a large, easily detectable output pulse (independent of the original radiation energy). The halogen quench gas absorbs UV photons and prevents secondary avalanches, allowing the tube to recover for the next event.

**Characteristic Curve (Diagram Reference — See Section 4.4 Block Diagram):**

The relationship between applied voltage and pulse count rate (for a constant radiation source) shows several distinct regions:

1. **Region I — Recombination (0 to ~threshold voltage $V_1$):** Field too weak; many ion pairs recombine before collection. Output is small and unstable.
2. **Region II — Ionization chamber region ($V_1$ to $V_2$):** All ion pairs are collected; output current is proportional to the rate of ionization. Plateau of saturation.
3. **Region III — Proportional region ($V_2$ to $V_3$):** Gas multiplication begins; output pulse amplitude is proportional to the original ionization energy. Used for spectroscopy.
4. **Region IV — Geiger plateau ($V_3$ to $V_4$):** Avalanche spreads along entire wire; output pulse is large but independent of radiation energy. The count rate is roughly constant (slope ~3–5% per 100 V). **This is the operating region.**
5. **Region V — Continuous discharge (above $V_4$):** Quench gas fails; continuous discharge destroys the tube.

The **operating voltage** is typically chosen at the center of the Geiger plateau for stable operation.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and explain Moseley's Law. How does it justify the reordering of certain pairs of elements in the periodic table? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Moseley's Law Statement:**

> The square root of the frequency of a particular characteristic X-ray line of an element is linearly proportional to its atomic number, after subtracting a screening constant.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$$

where $\nu$ is the frequency of the $K_\alpha$ (or $L_\alpha$) X-ray line, $Z$ is the atomic number, and $a, b$ are constants that depend on the particular X-ray series.

For the $K_\alpha$ series: $a \approx 2.47 \times 10^7\,\text{Hz}^{1/2}$ and $b \approx 1$.

**Explanation / Physical Origin:**

According to Bohr's atomic model, the energy of an electron in the $n$-th shell of an atom with effective nuclear charge $(Z - \sigma)$ is:

$$E_n = -\dfrac{R_\infty hc (Z - \sigma)^2}{n^2}$$

where $R_\infty$ is the Rydberg constant and $\sigma$ is the screening constant accounting for electron-electron repulsion. For an X-ray transition from level $n_2$ to $n_1$:

$$h\nu = R_\infty hc (Z - \sigma)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$$

Defining $a^2 = R_\infty c \left(\dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$ and $b = \sigma$:

$$\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$$

**Justification for Periodic Table Reordering:**

When Mendeleev arranged the periodic table by atomic mass, certain pairs of elements appeared out of order (e.g., Co–Ni, Ar–K, Te–I). Moseley's 1913–1914 experiments showed that:

- The plot of $\sqrt{\nu_{K_\alpha}}$ versus $Z$ is a *perfectly straight line* for all elements.
- The frequencies of the X-ray lines *increase monotonically* with $Z$.
- There are no gaps or reversals in the line.

This proved that the *atomic number* $Z$ — the count of protons in the nucleus — is the fundamental periodic property, not atomic mass. Elements with lower atomic mass but higher $Z$ (e.g., $\text{Co}$, $Z = 27$ vs. $\text{Ni}$, $Z = 28$) were correctly identified. Furthermore, Moseley's work led to the discovery of previously missing elements (e.g., Tc, Re, Pm) by identifying gaps in his linear plot.

---

**(b)** A radioactive sample has a half-life of 30 years. Calculate: (i) the decay constant, (ii) the time required for the activity to fall to 10% of its initial value, (iii) the fraction remaining after 100 years. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Half-life $t_{1/2} = 30$ years
- Initial activity $A_0$ (arbitrary reference)
- Final activity $A = 0.10 \, A_0$ (for part ii)
- Elapsed time $t = 100$ years (for part iii)

**Part (i): Decay constant**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{30\,\text{years}}$$

$$\lambda = 0.0231\,\text{year}^{-1}$$

**Part (ii): Time for activity to fall to 10%**

Using $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$0.10 \, A_0 = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\ln(0.10) = -\lambda t$$

$$t = \dfrac{-\ln(0.10)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2.3026}{0.0231\,\text{year}^{-1}}$$

$$t \approx 99.7\,\text{years}$$

**Verification using half-lives:** $\log_2(10) \approx 3.32$, so $t \approx 3.32 \times 30 \approx 99.6$ years ✓

**Part (iii): Fraction remaining after 100 years**

$$\dfrac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.0231)(100)} = e^{-2.31}$$

$$e^{-2.31} \approx 0.0993 \approx 9.93\%$$

So approximately **9.93% of the original sample remains** after 100 years, or equivalently, about **90.07% has decayed**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A very common error here is using $\log_{10}$ when the formula uses $\ln$ (natural log), or forgetting to convert units. If $\lambda$ is in year⁻¹, then $t$ must also be in years. Mixing units (e.g., $\lambda$ in s⁻¹ with $t$ in years) will cost you **2 of 7 marks** in the calculation sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

**Core Definitions:**
- X-rays: EM radiation, $\lambda = 0.01$ to $10$ nm, $E = 100$ eV to $100$ keV, *ionizing*.
- Nuclear radiation: α ($\,^4_2\text{He}$), β ($e^\pm$), γ (high-energy photon), neutron ($n^0$).
- Bremsstrahlung: continuous X-ray spectrum from electron deceleration.
- Characteristic X-ray: discrete lines from inner-shell electron transitions.
- Cut-off wavelength: $\lambda_{\min} = 1240/V$ nm (with $V$ in volts).

**Key Laws / Equations (memorize these 5 cold):**
1. **Duane–Hunt:** $\lambda_{\min} = hc/(eV)$ — the *spectral* upper-energy bound.
2. **Moseley:** $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$ — links X-ray frequency to atomic number.
3. **Bragg:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — governs X-ray diffraction and XRD.
4. **Beer–Lambert:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — attenuation of X-ray/γ beam through matter.
5. **Radioactive decay:** $N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$, $A = \lambda N$.

**Detector Types — remember the 4 main ones:**
- **Ionization chamber** → measures total dose (current mode).
- **Proportional counter** → measures energy (pulse mode, spectroscopy).
- **GM counter** → counts events only (Geiger plateau, large output).
- **Scintillation detector** → uses NaI(Tl) crystal + PMT, fast timing, moderate energy resolution.
- **Semiconductor detector** → best energy resolution, requires cooling (HPGe) or operates at room temperature (CdZnTe, Si).

**Interaction Mechanisms — match the energy:**
- Photoelectric → low $E$ (~keV range), high-$Z$ material.
- Compton → medium $E$ (~100 keV to 1 MeV), low-$Z$ material.
- Pair production → $E > 1.022$ MeV, needs nucleus (Coulomb field).

**Quick Numerical Conversions to Memorize:**
- $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J
- $1\,\text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq
- $hc = 1240$ eV·nm (extremely useful shortcut)
- $\ln 2 = 0.693$, $\ln 10 = 2.303$

**Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Confusing X-ray photon (no rest mass) with neutron (has rest mass).
- Using $E = mc^2$ for photons — that is for *rest* mass; photons use $E = h\nu$.
- Forgetting the $K$-edge energy in photoelectric effect calculations.
- Mixing up linear attenuation coefficient ($\mu$, cm⁻¹) with mass attenuation coefficient ($\mu_m$, cm²/g).
- Using RMS instead of peak voltage in the Duane–Hunt formula.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Study Strategy:** Practice deriving Moseley's law *and* the Duane–Hunt limit from scratch on paper. Solve at least three numerical problems involving radioactive decay, half-life, and activity. Memorize the Beer–Lambert derivation word-for-word — KTU examiners love it as a "complete-the-proof" question worth 5–7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fiber Optic Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fiber Optic Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Fiber Optic Sensor (FOS)** is a transducer that converts a physical, chemical, or biological measurand (such as temperature, strain, pressure, refractive index, magnetic field, or chemical concentration) into a modulated optical signal propagating through an optical fiber, where the modulation in one or more properties of light (intensity, phase, wavelength, polarization, or mode distribution) is correlated to the measurand and subsequently detected and demodulated at the receiver end.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course: PCCPT205 - Sensors and Actuators), Fiber Optic Sensors fall under the broad domain of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** because they provide a *digital-grade, EMI-immune, intrinsically safe*, and *remote-sensing capable* interface between the physical world and the cyber (computational) layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The emphasis is on understanding the **principle of total internal reflection (TIR)**, classification of FOS as **Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic**, and the four major modulation schemes — **Intensity, Phase, Wavelength (FBG), and Polarization** modulated sensors.

### 1.2 The Optical Fiber — Structural Anatomy

An optical fiber is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide consisting of three concentric regions:

| Layer | Material | Refractive Index | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Core** | SiO₂ doped with GeO₂ / P₂O₅ | n₁ (higher) | Guides the optical signal |
| **Cladding** | Pure SiO₂ or F-doped SiO₂ | n₂ (lower), n₂ < n₁ | Confines light via TIR |
| **Buffer / Jacket** | Acrylate, Polyimide, Metal | n₃ | Mechanical & environmental protection |

The *step-index* silica fiber has a typical core diameter of **8–10 µm** (single-mode, SMF) or **50–62.5 µm** (multi-mode, MMF), with cladding outer diameter standardized at **125 µm**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Pipe" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Garden Hose vs. A Fiber Optic Cable:**
> Imagine shining a flashlight into a curved, transparent acrylic rod. If the rod is straight, light leaks out the sides. But if you bend it, you notice something remarkable — *light still emerges at the other end*! This is because the light rays bounce off the inner walls of the rod at angles that prevent them from escaping. A fiber optic cable is essentially a *flexible, ultra-pure glass "light pipe"* that traps photons using a physical phenomenon called **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. The light "wants" to leave, but the boundary conditions (n₁ > n₂) forbid it. When an external stimulus (strain, heat, vibration) perturbs this light path, we measure the perturbation — and that measurement *is* the sensor output.

### 1.4 Principle of Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

When light travels from a **denser medium (n₁)** to a **rarer medium (n₂)** at an angle of incidence θ₁ greater than the **critical angle θ_c**, the refracted ray grazes along the interface (θ₂ = 90°), and beyond this, light is completely reflected back into the denser medium.

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition for TIR:** n₁ > n₂ **AND** θ₁ ≥ θ_c. This is the foundational physical law behind *all* light propagation in optical fibers.

### 1.5 Classification of Fiber Optic Sensors

**A) Based on Sensing Location:**
- **Intrinsic Sensor:** The fiber itself acts as the sensing element. The light is modulated *within* the fiber (e.g., FBG, evanescent wave sensors).
- **Extrinsic Sensor:** The fiber merely acts as a *light guide* to and from a separate sensing region (e.g., a Fabry-Pérot cavity at the fiber tip).

**B) Based on Modulation Principle:**
- Intensity-modulated
- Phase-modulated (Interferometric)
- Wavelength-modulated (FBG, LPFG)
- Polarization-modulated
- Distributed (Raman, Brillouin scattering)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Fiber Optic Sensor Works

The operation can be decomposed into **five sequential stages**:

1. **Optical Source Launching:** A coherent (laser) or broadband (LED, SLED) source injects light into the fiber core via a coupler/launcher.
2. **Guided Propagation:** Light propagates through the fiber via TIR, retaining the modulation state established at launch.
3. **Sensing Interaction:** The measurand (strain, temperature, pressure, chemical species) interacts with the guided light in the *sensitive region*, altering one or more optical properties.
4. **Modulation Encoding:** The interaction encodes the measurand information as a change in intensity (I), phase (φ), wavelength (λ), or polarization (ψ).
5. **Detection & Demodulation:** A photodetector (PIN, APD) converts the modulated optical signal into an electrical signal, which is then processed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fiber Optic Sensors are pivotal in Cyber-Physical Systems:** They are immune to **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)**, can operate in **explosive/hazardous environments** (no sparking), offer **multiplexing capability** (hundreds of sensors on a single fiber), and withstand **harsh temperatures up to ~1000 °C** with specialty fibers (sapphire, gold-coated).

### 2.2 Types of Fiber Optic Sensors — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Intensity-Modulated FOS
The simplest class. The measurand causes an **attenuation** in the guided light intensity, detected as I(x) = I₀ · T(x), where T(x) is a transmission function dependent on the measurand.
- *Examples:* Microbend sensor, evanescent-wave absorption sensor, fiber-displacement sensor.
- *Pros:* Low cost, simple demodulation.
- *Cons:* Susceptible to source power fluctuations and connector losses (requires reference channel).

#### 2.2.2 Phase-Modulated (Interferometric) FOS
The measurand changes the **optical path length (OPL = n·L)**, producing a phase shift:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot (n \Delta L + L \Delta n)$$

This is detected using interferometers:
- **Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI)** — most common for hydrophones, accelerometers.
- **Michelson Interferometer** — reflective configuration.
- **Sagnac Interferometer** — for rotation sensing (Fiber Optic Gyroscope, FOG).
- **Fabry-Pérot Interferometer (FPI)** — for point sensing of strain, temperature, ultrasound.

#### 2.2.3 Wavelength-Modulated FOS — Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)
A periodic modulation of the core refractive index (period Λ) reflects a specific Bragg wavelength and transmits all others.

$$\lambda_B = 2 \, n_{eff} \, \Lambda$$

where n_eff is the effective refractive index of the core mode. Strain and temperature shift λ_B:

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = (1 - p_e)\varepsilon + (\alpha_{fiber} + \xi)\Delta T$$

where p_e ≈ 0.22 is the effective photoelastic constant, α is the thermal expansion coefficient, and ξ is the thermo-optic coefficient (≈ 6.7 × 10⁻⁶ /°C for silica).

#### 2.2.4 Polarization-Modulated FOS
Uses the **Faraday Effect** (magnetic field rotates polarization) and **electro-optic Kerr / piezo-electric effects** to sense current, voltage, and electric fields.

#### 2.2.5 Distributed FOS
Uses **Rayleigh, Raman, or Brillouin back-scattering** along the fiber length. The Optical Time-Domain Reflectometer (OTDR) concept enables *thousands* of measurement points on a single fiber.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU ESE — they appear in nearly every question paper.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Snell's Law | n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Critical Angle | θ_c = sin⁻¹ (n₂ / n₁) | Radians / degrees; n₁ > n₂ |
| 3 | Numerical Aperture (NA) | NA = √(n₁² − n₂²) = n₀ sin θ_a | n₀ is the refractive index of the external medium (≈ 1 for air) |
| 4 | Acceptance Angle | θ_a = sin⁻¹ (NA / n₀) | Also called the "half-angle of the acceptance cone" |
| 5 | V-Number (Cutoff Parameter) | V = (2πa / λ) · NA | a = core radius; λ = operating wavelength |
| 6 | Number of Modes (Step-Index MMF) | N ≈ V² / 2 | For V > 2 (multimode regime) |
| 7 | Number of Modes (Graded-Index MMF) | N ≈ V² / 4 | Parabolic index profile |
| 8 | Single-Mode Condition | V < 2.405 (cutoff) | λ_c = (2πa · NA) / 2.405 |
| 9 | FBG Bragg Wavelength | λ_B = 2 n_eff Λ | Λ = grating period; n_eff ≈ 1.46 (silica) |
| 10 | FBG Strain Sensitivity | Δλ_B = λ_B (1 − p_e) ε | p_e ≈ 0.22 for germanosilicate |
| 11 | FBG Temperature Sensitivity | Δλ_B = λ_B (α + ξ) ΔT | α ≈ 0.55 × 10⁻⁶ /°C; ξ ≈ 6.7 × 10⁻⁶ /°C |
| 12 | Phase Shift (Interferometric) | Δφ = (2π / λ) (n ΔL + L Δn) | Used in MZI / Michelson / Sagnac |
| 13 | Faraday Rotation | θ_F = V · B · L | V ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ rad/A for silica at 1310 nm |
| 14 | Evanescent Field Penetration Depth | d_p = λ / (2π √(n₁² − n₂²)) | Used in chemical / bio-sensors |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Measurand | FOS Technology Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Aerospace structural health monitoring | Strain, vibration | FBG arrays |
| Oil & gas downhole sensing | Temperature, pressure, flow | Distributed DTS (Raman) / FBG |
| Power grid current sensing | Current (via B-field) | Faraday-effect polarimetric FOS |
| Civil engineering (bridges, dams) | Strain, displacement | FBG, BOTDA distributed sensing |
| Medical (MRI-compatible) | Pressure, temperature | Extrinsic Fabry-Pérot |
| Navigation / Inertial guidance | Angular velocity | Sagnac FOG (gyroscope) |
| Bio-chemical sensing | pH, glucose, gas | Evanescent-wave absorption |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Numerical Aperture and Acceptance Angle of a Step-Index Fiber

> **Problem Setup:** A step-index silica optical fiber has a core refractive index n₁ = 1.48 and a cladding refractive index n₂ = 1.46. The fiber is in air (n₀ = 1.00). Compute the numerical aperture (NA), the acceptance angle (θ_a) in air, and verify the critical angle condition for TIR.

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law at the core–cladding interface for the meridional ray at the critical angle.**

At the boundary between the core (n₁) and cladding (n₂), the ray strikes the interface at an angle φ₁ (measured from the *normal* to the interface). For total internal reflection to occur, the refracted angle in the cladding must be exactly 90°.

$$n_1 \sin \phi_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ = n_2$$

Therefore, the critical angle is:

$$\phi_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the critical angle.**

$$\phi_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1.46}{1.48}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) = 80.66^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Relate the core-cladding interface angle to the entry angle using Snell's Law at the fiber face.**

A meridional ray enters the fiber face at an external angle θ_a (in air, n₀ = 1) to the fiber axis. By the geometry of meridional rays in a step-index fiber, the angle θ_a and the interface angle φ are complementary (i.e., θ_a + φ = 90° at the entry face, but the angle the ray makes with the *axis* inside the fiber is θ = 90° − φ).

Applying Snell's law at the air-core entry face:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta = n_1 \cos \phi$$

For the *limiting* (critical) case, φ = φ_c, so cos φ_c = √(1 − sin²φ_c) = √(1 − (n₂/n₁)²).

Therefore:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Numerical Aperture (NA).**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute NA numerically.**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} = 0.2425$$

**Step 6 — Compute the acceptance angle θ_a.**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA} / n_0) = \sin^{-1}(0.2425 / 1.00) = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) = 14.03^\circ$$

**Step 7 — Verify the TIR condition.**

Since θ_c ≈ 80.66° < 90° and n₁ > n₂, the condition n₁ > n₂ **AND** θ ≥ θ_c is satisfied for all meridional rays entering within the acceptance cone. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The acceptance cone half-angle is **14.03°**, meaning light must be launched within a **full cone of 28.06°** about the fiber axis to be guided. A typical telecom SMF-28 fiber has NA ≈ 0.14, giving θ_a ≈ 8°, illustrating that *higher* NA corresponds to *larger* light-gathering capability (essential for sensor coupling efficiency).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — FBG Strain Response (How an FBG Encodes Strain into Wavelength Shift)

> **Problem Setup:** A uniform FBG with Bragg wavelength λ_B = 1550 nm is bonded to an aluminum cantilever beam. The photoelastic coefficient is p_e = 0.22, and the effective refractive index n_eff ≈ 1.456. Compute the Bragg wavelength shift for an applied axial strain ε = 1000 microstrain (µε = 10⁻³).

**Step 1 — Write the Bragg condition.**

$$\lambda_B = 2 \, n_{eff} \, \Lambda$$

**Step 2 — Take the total logarithmic differential to find fractional shift in λ_B.**

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = \frac{\Delta n_{eff}}{n_{eff}} + \frac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Express ΔΛ / Λ as the axial strain ε.**

By definition, axial strain is the fractional change in physical length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Relate Δn_eff / n_eff to strain via the photoelastic (elasto-optic) effect.**

The strain-induced refractive index change is:

$$\frac{\Delta n_{eff}}{n_{eff}} = -\frac{n_{eff}^2}{2}\left[p_{12} - \nu(p_{11} + p_{12})\right] \cdot \varepsilon \equiv -p_e \cdot \varepsilon$$

For germanosilicate fiber, p_e ≈ 0.22.

**Step 5 — Substitute into the Bragg equation.**

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = \varepsilon - p_e \, \varepsilon = (1 - p_e)\varepsilon$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerical shift.**

$$\Delta \lambda_B = \lambda_B (1 - p_e) \varepsilon = 1550 \times (1 - 0.22) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta \lambda_B = 1550 \times 0.78 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 1.209 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 7 — State the new Bragg wavelength.**

$$\lambda_B^{new} = 1550 + 1.209 = 1551.209 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The strain sensitivity is **~1.209 pm / µε** at 1550 nm. With an interrogator resolution of 1 pm, a sensor can resolve **sub-microstrain** levels — making FBG ideal for *structural health monitoring* in aircraft wings, bridges, and wind turbine blades.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — V-Number and Single-Mode Cutoff Wavelength

> **Problem Setup:** A step-index fiber has core radius a = 4.5 µm, n₁ = 1.465, n₂ = 1.460, operating at λ = 1310 nm. Determine the V-number and verify whether it is single-mode.

**Step 1 — Compute NA.**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.465)^2 - (1.460)^2} = \sqrt{0.014625} \approx 0.1209$$

**Step 2 — Compute V.**

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot \text{NA} = \frac{2\pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1310 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.1209$$

$$V = 21.59 \times 0.1209 \approx 2.61$$

**Step 3 — Compare to single-mode cutoff value.**

For a step-index fiber, the LP₁₁ mode cutoff occurs at V = 2.405. Since **V = 2.61 > 2.405**, the fiber is *just barely* multi-mode at 1310 nm.

**Step 4 — Compute the cutoff wavelength λ_c.**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{2\pi a \cdot \text{NA}}{2.405} = \frac{2\pi \times 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1209}{2.405} = 1.421 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For λ > 1421 nm, the fiber is *strictly* single-mode. This is why **1310 nm and 1550 nm telecommunications windows** are ideal — they sit comfortably above the SMF-28 cutoff wavelength.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of FOS Modulation Schemes

| Property | Intensity Modulated | Phase Modulated | Wavelength Modulated (FBG) | Polarization Modulated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensitivity** | Low to medium | Very high (~10⁻⁷ rad/√Hz) | High (~1 pm/µε) | Medium |
| **Demodulation Complexity** | Very low (photodiode) | High (interferometer) | Medium (spectrometer / tunable filter) | Medium (polarimeter) |
| **Reference Channel Needed?** | Yes | Optional | No (wavelength is absolute) | Yes |
| **Multiplexing Capability** | Limited | Difficult | Excellent (WDM of many FBGs) | Limited |
| **EMI Immunity** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **KTU Typical Question** | Define / explain | Derive phase shift | Compute λ_B shift | Faraday effect derivation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Generic Fiber Optic Sensor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OPTICAL SOURCE LASER OR LED"] --> B["COUPLER LAUNCHER"]
    B --> C["FIBER CORE GUIDED PROPAGATION"]
    C --> D["SENSING REGION INTRINSIC OR EXTRINSIC"]
    D --> E["MODULATED LIGHT INTENSITY PHASE WAVELENGTH POLARIZATION"]
    E --> F["PHOTODETECTOR PIN OR APD"]
    F --> G["SIGNAL CONDITIONING AMPLIFIER FILTER"]
    G --> H["DEMODULATION UNIT"]
    H --> I["DIGITAL OUTPUT TO CPS LAYER"]
    D -.-> J["MEASURAND PHYSICAL INPUT STRAIN TEMPERATURE PRESSURE FIELD"]
    J --> D
```

### 4.2 Cross-Section Block Architecture of a Step-Index Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F1["STEP INDEX OPTICAL FIBER CROSS SECTION"]
        direction TB
        node1["CORE DIAMETER 8 TO 62.5 MICRON n1 HIGH REFRACTIVE INDEX"]
        node2["CLADDING DIAMETER 125 MICRON n2 LOWER REFRACTIVE INDEX"]
        node3["BUFFER OR JACKET DIAMETER 250 MICRON MECHANICAL PROTECTION"]
        node4["COATING ACRYLATE OR POLYIMIDE ENVIRONMENTAL SHIELD"]
    end
    node4 --> node3 --> node2 --> node1
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Fiber Bragg Grating Sensor Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["BROADBAND SOURCE ASE OR SLED"] --> P2["CIRCULATOR OR COUPLER 3 PORT"]
    P2 --> P3["FBG PERIODIC REFRACTIVE INDEX MODULATION"]
    P3 --> P4["BRAGG REFLECTED WAVELENGTH LAMBDA B"]
    P3 --> P5["TRANSMITTED SPECTRUM LAMBDA NOT EQUAL LAMBDA B"]
    P4 --> P6["INTERROGATOR TUNABLE FABRY PEROT FILTER"]
    P5 --> P7["SPECTRUM ANALYZER OPTIONAL"]
    P6 --> P8["STRAIN OR TEMPERATURE DECODER EQUATION 10 AND 11"]
    P8 --> P9["CALIBRATED MEASUREMENT OUTPUT IN MICROSTRAIN OR DEGREE C"]
    M1["APPLIED STRAIN EPSILON"] -.-> P3
    M2["TEMPERATURE CHANGE DELTA T"] -.-> P3
```

### 4.4 Mach-Zehnder Interferometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["LASER SOURCE COHERENT"] --> S2["BEAM SPLITTER COUPLER 1"]
    S2 --> R1["REFERENCE ARM LENGTH L1"]
    S2 --> R2["SENSING ARM LENGTH L2 EXPOSED TO MEASURAND"]
    R1 --> S3["BEAM COMBINER COUPLER 2"]
    R2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4["OUTPUT PHOTODETECTOR"]
    S4 --> S5["PHASE UNWRAPPING AND DECODER"]
    M3["MEASURAND STRAIN TEMPERATURE PRESSURE"] -.-> R2
```
