### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between bulk micromachining and surface micromachining techniques in MEMS fabrication.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 120 words):**
| Aspect | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining |
|---|---|---|
| Etch Target | The silicon substrate itself | A deposited sacrificial layer |
| Common Etchants | KOH, TMAH, DRIE | HF (releases SiO₂) |
| Structure Type | Thick (10–500 µm) | Thin (1–25 µm) |
| Complexity | Lower mask count | Higher mask count |
| Example | Piezoresistive pressure sensor | Capacitive accelerometer |

> **Examiners' Tip:** Always mention the *sacrificial layer* concept for surface micromachining — it scores a guaranteed 1 mark.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Deal-Grove model for thermal oxidation of silicon. State the equation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Deal-Grove model (1965) describes oxide growth as a combination of (i) oxygen diffusion **through** the existing oxide and (ii) chemical reaction **at** the Si-SiO₂ interface. The model is:

$$x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$$

Where $x_o$ is the oxide thickness, $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants, $t$ is oxidation time, and $\tau$ accounts for the initial oxide thickness. For short times, the linear regime dominates ($x_o \approx Bt/A$); for long times, the parabolic regime dominates ($x_o \approx \sqrt{Bt}$). **Wet oxidation** is faster than **dry oxidation** because H₂O diffuses more rapidly through SiO₂ than O₂.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **photolithography process steps** used in microsensor fabrication, with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Bosch DRIE process** for deep silicon etching, including its chemistry, cyclic steps, and the role of sidewall passivation. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Answer for Q-A (a): Photolithography Process Steps (7 Marks)**

Photolithography is the process of transferring a 2D geometric pattern from a mask onto a photosensitive polymer (photoresist) layer on the silicon wafer surface. It is the most critical and frequently used step in microsensor fabrication.

**Step 1 — Wafer Cleaning (RCA Clean):** The wafer is cleaned to remove organic, ionic, and particulate contamination using a sequence of Standard Clean 1 (SC1: NH₄OH + H₂O₂ + H₂O) and Standard Clean 2 (SC2: HCl + H₂O₂ + H₂O) baths at ~75–85 °C.

**Step 2 — Dehydration Bake and HMDS Priming:** The wafer is baked at 150 °C for 30 minutes to remove moisture, followed by exposure to **HMDS (Hexamethyldisilazane) vapour**, which improves photoresist adhesion by forming a hydrophobic monolayer.

**Step 3 — Photoresist Spin Coating:** A liquid photoresist (e.g., AZ-5214, S1813, or SU-8) is dispensed and the wafer is spun at 2000–5000 rpm. Centrifugal force produces a uniform film of thickness 0.5–10 µm.

**Step 4 — Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake):** The coated wafer is baked at 90–110 °C for ~60 s to evaporate residual solvent, improving resist uniformity and adhesion.

**Step 5 — UV Exposure:** The wafer is exposed to ultraviolet light (λ = 365 nm i-line, 248 nm DUV, or 193 nm ArF) through the photomask. In **positive resist**, exposed regions become soluble; in **negative resist**, exposed regions become cross-linked and insoluble.

**Step 6 — Post-Exposure Bake (PEB):** For chemically amplified resists, a PEB is required to drive the acid-catalyzed reaction that changes solubility.

**Step 7 — Development:** The wafer is immersed in a developer solution (typically **TMAH 2.38%** for positive resists). The exposed regions (positive) or unexposed regions (negative) dissolve, leaving a 3D polymer pattern on the wafer.

**Step 8 — Hard Bake:** The developed wafer is baked at 120–150 °C to harden the resist, improving its resistance to subsequent plasma or wet etching.

**Step 9 — Inspection:** Critical Dimensions (CD) and pattern fidelity are verified using optical microscopy and SEM.

**Resolution Limit (Rayleigh Criterion):**
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$

> **Examiners' Tip:** A neat block diagram earns 1 mark. Mention the Rayleigh resolution formula for an extra advantage.

---

### **Model Answer for Q-A (b): Bosch DRIE Process (7 Marks)**

The **Bosch process**, developed by Robert Bosch GmbH in the 1990s, is a cyclic, time-multiplexed DRIE technique that enables nearly vertical etching of silicon to depths of several hundred micrometers with **aspect ratios exceeding 50:1**. It is indispensable for fabricating high-aspect-ratio structures like through-silicon vias (TSVs), inertial sensor proof masses, and comb drives.

**Chemistry of the Two Half-Cycles:**

**Cycle A — Etch Step (duration: ~5–15 s):**
SF₆ gas is introduced into the chamber. Under plasma excitation, SF₆ dissociates to release **fluorine radicals (F\*)**, which spontaneously react with silicon to form volatile **SiF₄**, which is pumped away. This step would, on its own, produce isotropic etching with significant undercut.

**Cycle B — Deposition/Passivation Step (duration: ~5–10 s):**
C₄F₈ (octafluorocyclobutane) gas is introduced. Under plasma, it forms a **Teflon-like fluorocarbon polymer (–CF₂–)ₙ** that deposits uniformly over all exposed surfaces — the trench bottom AND the sidewalls. This passivation film protects the sidewalls from lateral etching.

**Cyclic Repetition (typical: 100–500 cycles):**
The two steps alternate rapidly. During each etch cycle, ion bombardment in the vertical direction breaks the passivation at the **trench bottom** (because ions travel perpendicular to the wafer), but the sidewall passivation remains intact. This produces the characteristic **scalloped sidewall** profile (amplitude 50–500 nm), which can be minimized by shortening the cycle time.

**Key Parameters:**
- **Etch Rate:** 1–5 µm/min (silicon)
- **Selectivity over SiO₂:** > 100 : 1
- **Sidewall Verticality:** 88°–92° from horizontal
- **Aspect Ratio:** up to 50:1 standard, > 100:1 with advanced tools

**Why Passivation is Critical:** Without C₄F₈ passivation, fluorine radicals would etch silicon **isotropically**, producing curved cavities rather than vertical trenches. The cyclic alternation of etch and passivation is the fundamental innovation of the Bosch process.

> **Examiners' Tip:** A sketch showing the alternate SF₆ (etch) and C₄F₈ (passivation) cycles on the trench cross-section will earn full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat process flow, explain the **fabrication of a piezoresistive pressure sensor** using bulk micromachining. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **resonant frequency** of a cantilever beam and discuss how it depends on beam geometry. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Answer for Q-B (a): Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor Fabrication (7 Marks)**

A piezoresistive pressure sensor is one of the most commercially successful MEMS devices, used in automotive, medical, and industrial applications. The fabrication is a **6-mask bulk micromachining process** on a (100) silicon wafer.

**Process Flow:**

**Step 1 — Starting Wafer:** A double-side-polished p-type (100) silicon wafer (3–6 inch diameter, ~400 µm thick) is used as the substrate.

**Step 2 — Thermal Oxidation:** A ~1 µm thick SiO₂ layer is grown using wet thermal oxidation at 1100 °C. This serves as an **etch mask** for the subsequent KOH etch.

**Step 3 — Mask 1 (Piezoresistor Pattern, Frontside):** Photolithography + buffered HF (BHF) etch defines windows in the SiO₂ where p-type piezoresistors will be formed.

**Step 4 — Ion Implantation:** Boron ions (B⁺) are implanted at ~50–100 keV through the windows at a dose of ~10¹⁴–10¹⁵ ions/cm². This creates the piezoresistor regions at a controlled depth (~0.2–0.5 µm).

**Step 5 — Dopant Activation Anneal:** Rapid Thermal Annealing (RTA) at 1000–1100 °C for 30 s activates the boron and repairs implantation damage.

**Step 6 — Re-oxidation and Mask 2 (Metal Contact Pattern):** A thin thermal oxide is grown for passivation, photolithography + HF etching opens contact windows, and a metal layer (Al or Al-Si) is sputtered and patterned using **Mask 2** via lift-off or wet etching.

**Step 7 — Mask 3 (Diaphragm Pattern, Backside):** The wafer is flipped. Using infrared alignment, the backside is photolithographically patterned to define a square diaphragm window. The backside SiO₂ is etched using BHF.

**Step 8 — KOH Anisotropic Etch:** The wafer is immersed in **30 wt% KOH at 80 °C** for several hours. The KOH etches the (100) plane at ~1 µm/min but barely attacks the (111) planes, producing a pyramidal cavity bounded by four (111) planes at 54.74°. A thin silicon diaphragm (~10–50 µm) remains where the etch front meets the front surface.

**Step 9 — Bonding and Packaging:** The etched wafer is **anodically bonded** to a Pyrex glass wafer containing a vacuum reference cavity, providing hermetic sealing and a fixed pressure reference.

**Step 10 — Dicing and Wire Bonding:** Individual sensor dies are separated, mounted on packages, and wire-bonded.

**Operating Principle:** When external pressure is applied, the diaphragm deflects, inducing stress that changes the resistance of the piezoresistors (Wheatstone bridge configuration), producing a voltage output proportional to pressure.

---

### **Model Answer for Q-B (b): Cantilever Resonant Frequency (7 Marks)**

*(See full derivation in Section 3.1 above.)*

**Final Result:**
$$f_1 = 0.162 \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

**Geometry Dependence Discussion:**

The fundamental resonant frequency is **directly proportional to thickness $t$** and **inversely proportional to the square of length $L$**. This means:

- **Doubling the length** (with everything else constant) **decreases** the frequency by a factor of **4** (a 75% reduction).
- **Doubling the thickness** **doubles** the frequency.
- The frequency is **independent of width** (since $w$ cancels out of the formula for a slender beam where $w \gg t$).
- Material properties appear through $\sqrt{E/\rho}$ — silicon has $E = 130$ GPa and $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³, giving a high "acoustic velocity" and hence a high resonant frequency for small beams.

**Engineering Implication for Resonant Mass Sensors:** When a tiny mass $\Delta m$ attaches to the cantilever tip, the resonant frequency **decreases** by an amount given by:
$$\Delta f \approx -f_1 \cdot \frac{\Delta m}{2 m_{eff}}$$

This shift can be measured with extraordinary precision (ppb-level), enabling detection of single-molecule adsorption events in biosensors.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing bulk and surface micromachining** — bulk etches the *substrate*; surface uses a *sacrificial layer*. Many students interchange these and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of passivation in DRIE** — the Bosch process works because ion bombardment is *directional* and removes passivation *only* at the trench bottom. Stating only "SF₆ etches silicon" is incomplete.
> 3. **Misapplying the Deal-Grove model** — the equation is $x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$, **not** $x_o = Bt$ (which would be a gross oversimplification).
> 4. **Forgetting process temperatures** — wet oxidation occurs at ~1000 °C; PECVD at ~300 °C. CMOS integration demands low thermal budget.
> 5. **Not drawing a process-flow block diagram** — in 14-mark questions, a labelled flowchart is essentially mandatory and can fetch 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Sign errors in derivation** — in the cantilever formula, $\beta L = 1.875$ (NOT 0.875 or 2.875). Memorize this constant.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Microsensor fabrication** is fundamentally a sequence of additive (oxidation, deposition) and subtractive (etching) steps on a silicon substrate.
- [x] **Photolithography** is the *pattern-transfer* engine; resolution is governed by the **Rayleigh criterion** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$.
- [x] **Wet etching** in KOH exploits crystal anisotropy — **(111) planes are natural etch stops**, producing 54.74° sidewalls.
- [x] **DRIE (Bosch process)** uses **alternating SF₆ etch and C₄F₈ passivation cycles** to achieve vertical, high-aspect-ratio silicon structures.
- [x] **Bulk micromachining** carves *into* the substrate; **Surface micromachining** uses a *sacrificial layer* to release thin-film structures.
- [x] **Deal-Grove equation** $x_o^2 + A x_o = B(t + \tau)$ governs thermal oxide growth.
- [x] **Cantilever resonant frequency** scales as $f_1 \propto t / L^2$.
- [x] **Diaphragm deflection** for a pressure sensor scales as $w_{max} \propto P a^4 / (E h^3)$.
- [x] **Anodic bonding** (glass-Si) and **fusion bonding** (Si-Si) are the principal wafer-level packaging techniques.
- [x] **CMOS compatibility** is critical — processes must not exceed thermal budgets of ~400–450 °C *after* IC fabrication.
- [x] **Cleanroom class** (ISO 5–6) is essential to control particle contamination and yield.
- [x] **Key material constants** to memorize: $E_{Si} = 130$ GPa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m³, $\nu_{Si} \approx 0.28$, $E_{SiO_2} = 70$ GPa, $\rho_{SiO_2} = 2200$ kg/m³.

> **Final 30-Second Revision Mantra:**
> *Litho → Etch → Deposit → Bond. Bulk carves, Surface releases. KOH loves (100). DRIE loves direction. Silicon remembers stress.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Force Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Force Sensor** (also known as a **Force Transducer** or **Load Cell**) is a precision electromechanical device that converts an applied mechanical **force**, **load**, **weight**, **torque**, or **tension** into a quantifiable, standardized electrical signal — typically a change in **resistance** ($\Delta R$), **voltage** ($V$), **current** ($I$), or **capacitance** ($\Delta C$) — proportional to the magnitude and direction of the stimulus.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, force sensors form a **critical sub-class of physical sensors** that utilize the **piezoresistive**, **piezoelectric**, **capacitive**, or **inductive** transduction principles to detect mechanical deformation caused by an external force $F$ acting on a sensing element (often called the *flexure* or *elastic body*).

The generalized functional relationship can be represented as:

$$F \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{elastic deformation}\;}\; \Delta x \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{transduction}\;}\; \Delta V \;(\text{or } \Delta R, \Delta C)$$

where $\Delta x$ is the microscopic mechanical displacement of the sensing element under Hooke's Law behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Force sensors are explicitly listed under **Module 3** with primary focus on *strain-gauge load cells*, *piezoelectric force sensors*, and *capacitive force sensors*, along with their associated **Wheatstone bridge** conditioning circuits and **gauge factor** analysis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pressing your **thumb against a digital kitchen weighing scale**. The scale does not measure the force *directly* — instead, an internal metallic beam **bends ever so slightly** under your weight. Embedded inside that beam is a tiny wire that gets **stretched**. As the wire stretches, its electrical resistance increases in proportion. The internal microchip reads this resistance change, converts it to a number, and displays your weight in kilograms.

That entire process — **mechanical push → microscopic bending → electrical signal change → digital display** — is exactly how a force sensor works.

Think of a **force sensor as a "mechanical-to-electrical translator"**:
- **Input world:** Mechanical (force, weight, pressure, torque)
- **Output world:** Electrical (voltage, current, resistance, charge)
- **Translator:** A sensing element that deforms predictably (like a spring) combined with a transduction mechanism (like a strain gauge)

### Real-World Everyday Examples

| Application | Force Sensor Type Used | Why |
|-------------|------------------------|-----|
| Digital weighing scale | Strain-gauge load cell | High accuracy, low cost |
| Weighbridge (truck scale) | Hydraulic load cell | Handles massive loads |
| Touchscreen smartphone | Capacitive force sensor | Detects finger pressure |
| Industrial press / stamping machine | Piezoelectric sensor | High-frequency dynamic force |
| Robotic gripper | Strain-gauge tactile sensor | Miniaturized, sensitive |
| Crash-test dummies | Piezoelectric accelerometer-style | Captures sudden impacts |

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and metrics are fundamental to force sensor analysis and **must be memorized** for KTU examinations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Constants & Units**
> - **Force (F):** Measured in **Newtons (N)** in SI; **$1\;\text{kgf} = 9.80665\;\text{N}$**
> - **Strain (ε):** Dimensionless ratio; typical range $\varepsilon \approx 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ (**microstrain** to **millistrain**)
> - **Gauge Factor (GF):** Dimensionless; for metallic gauges $GF \approx 2.0$, for semiconductor gauges $GF \approx 100$–$200$
> - **Young's Modulus (E) of steel:** $E \approx 200\;\text{GPa} = 2 \times 10^{11}\;\text{N/m}^2$
> - **Piezoelectric charge coefficient ($d_{33}$ for quartz):** $d_{33} = 2.3\;\text{pC/N}$
> - **Standard excitation voltage for load cells:** $V_{ex} = 10\;\text{V}$ (typical)

---

## 1.4 Classification of Force Sensors (KTU Module 3 Scope)

Force sensors can be broadly classified based on their **transduction principle**:

1. **Piezoresistive / Strain-Gauge Force Sensors** — most common, based on resistance change of a strained conductor
2. **Piezoelectric Force Sensors** — based on charge generation in certain crystals under stress
3. **Capacitive Force Sensors** — based on change in capacitance between two plates
4. **Inductive / LVDT-based Force Sensors** — based on change in magnetic reluctance
5. **Hydraulic & Pneumatic Load Cells** — based on fluid pressure measurement
6. **Magnetostrictive Force Sensors** — based on Villari effect in ferromagnetic materials
7. **Optical Fiber Force Sensors** — based on Bragg grating or intensity modulation

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **(1) Strain-gauge**, **(2) Piezoelectric**, and **(3) Capacitive** variants in detail.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

A force sensor always involves **two coupled physical phenomena** occurring in sequence:

**Step 1 — Mechanical Domain (Force → Deformation):**
An external force $F$ is applied to a deliberately-designed elastic body (called a *flexure*). According to **Hooke's Law**, the body deforms by a small displacement $\Delta x$:

$$\Delta x = \frac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

where:
- $F$ = applied force in **N**
- $L$ = effective length of the flexure in **m**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area in **m²**
- $E$ = Young's Modulus in **Pa**

This produces a mechanical **strain** $\varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Domain (Deformation → Electrical Signal):**
The strain $\varepsilon$ is then converted into a measurable electrical quantity by the chosen transduction mechanism. For example:
- A strained wire changes its resistance $R$
- A strained piezoelectric crystal generates surface charge $q$
- A strained parallel-plate capacitor changes its capacitance $C$

---

## 2.2 Strain-Gauge Force Sensor — The Piezoresistive Effect

A **strain gauge** is the heart of most modern force sensors. It consists of a thin metallic foil (constantan, Karma alloy) arranged in a **zig-zag (serpentine) pattern** bonded to a flexible backing, which is glued onto the surface of the elastic body.

When the body deforms, the foil **stretches or compresses** along with it, changing its electrical resistance.

### The Piezoresistive Equation (Master Equation)

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon\;}$$

where:
- $R$ = original resistance (typically $120\;\Omega$, $350\;\Omega$, or $1000\;\Omega$)
- $\Delta R$ = change in resistance
- $G_F$ = **Gauge Factor** (dimensionless)
- $\varepsilon$ = mechanical strain ($\Delta L / L$)

### Why Does the Resistance Change? — Three Physical Reasons

The fractional change in resistance has three contributing terms:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

where:
- $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$ = fractional change in resistivity (dominant in semiconductors)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (for most metals, $\nu \approx 0.3$)
- $(1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$ = geometric effect (dominant in metals)

Therefore, the **Gauge Factor** is defined as:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Interpretation:**
- For **metallic strain gauges**: $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \approx 0$, so $G_F \approx 1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6$ to $2.0$
- For **semiconductor strain gauges**: $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$ is huge (piezoresistive effect), so $G_F \approx 100$ to $200$

---

## 2.3 The Wheatstone Bridge — Signal Conditioning Circuit

A single strain gauge produces an extremely small $\Delta R$ (typically $\Delta R / R \approx 10^{-3}$ or less). To detect this tiny change, we use the **Wheatstone bridge** invented by Samuel Hunter Christie in 1833.

### Basic Wheatstone Bridge

The bridge has four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, an excitation voltage $V_{ex}$, and produces a differential output voltage $V_o$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

When the bridge is **balanced** (i.e., $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$), then $V_o = 0$.

When a strain $\varepsilon$ changes the resistance of one arm by $\Delta R$ (assuming $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$), the output becomes:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Quarter, Half, and Full Bridge Configurations

| Configuration | Active Gauges | Sensitivity Factor | Output Equation |
|---------------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Quarter Bridge** | 1 | $1 \times$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G_F \varepsilon$ |
| **Half Bridge** | 2 | $(1+\nu) \times$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G_F \varepsilon (1 + \nu)$ |
| **Full Bridge** | 4 | $2(1+\nu) \times$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} G_F \varepsilon (1+\nu)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Full-bridge configuration is preferred in industrial load cells because it **cancels temperature-induced common-mode errors** and provides the **highest sensitivity**.

---

## 2.4 Piezoelectric Force Sensor — The Direct Effect

Certain crystals (quartz, tourmaline, PZT ceramic, PVDF polymer) generate an **electric charge** on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress. This is the **direct piezoelectric effect**, discovered by the Curie brothers in 1880.

### Master Equation

$$\boxed{\;q = d \cdot F\;}$$

where:
- $q$ = generated charge in **Coulombs (C)**
- $d$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient in **C/N** (also called $d_{33}$ or $d_{31}$ depending on direction)
- $F$ = applied force in **N**

The equivalent output voltage is:

$$V = \frac{q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

where $C_p$ is the capacitance of the piezoelectric element plus cable capacitance.

### Key Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric sensors measure DYNAMIC forces only** — they cannot measure static (constant) forces because the generated charge leaks away over time. They are **ideal for shock, vibration, and impact measurements**.

**Advantages:** Wide frequency range (sub-Hz to MHz), high stiffness, excellent for dynamic forces.
**Disadvantages:** Cannot measure static loads; requires high-impedance charge amplifier; temperature sensitive.

---

## 2.5 Capacitive Force Sensor

A **capacitive force sensor** uses two parallel plates separated by a small gap. The capacitance is given by the classic parallel-plate formula:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$ (permittivity of free space)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the dielectric medium
- $A$ = overlapping plate area
- $d$ = gap distance between plates

When a force $F$ is applied, the gap $d$ changes by $\Delta d$, and the resulting change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - \Delta d} - \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = C \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{d - \Delta d} \approx \frac{C \cdot \Delta d}{d}$$

**Working principle:** Applied force → flexure bends → gap $d$ decreases → capacitance $C$ increases.

These sensors are widely used in **MEMS devices**, **touchscreens**, and **accelerometers** because of low power consumption and high sensitivity.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Where Used |
|---|--------------|----------|------------|
| 1 | **Hooke's Law (axial)** | $\Delta L = \dfrac{F L}{A E}$ | Flexure design |
| 2 | **Mechanical Strain** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} | KTU most-asked |
| 3 | **Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} = E \varepsilon$ | Load cell analysis |
| 4 | **Gauge Factor (full form)** | $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Strain gauge design |
| 5 | **Piezoresistive master equation** | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | **Most frequent formula** |
| 6 | **Quarter-bridge output** | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G_F \varepsilon$ | Sensor signal analysis |
| 7 | **Half-bridge output** | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G_F \varepsilon (1+\nu)$ | Higher sensitivity |
| 8 | **Full-bridge output** | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} G_F \varepsilon (1+\nu)$ | Industrial load cells |
| 9 | **Piezoelectric charge** | $q = d \cdot F$ | Dynamic force measurement |
| 10 | **Piezoelectric voltage** | $V = \dfrac{d F}{C_p}$ | Charge amplifier design |
| 11 | **Parallel-plate capacitance** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitive sensor |
| 12 | **Load cell sensitivity (mV/V)** | $S = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{ex}}$ | Datasheet rating |
| 13 | **Full-scale output** | $V_{FS} = V_{ex} \cdot S$ | Range calculation |
| 14 | **Weight from force** | $F = m g$ | Weighing applications ($g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^2$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Load cells are calibrated with two parameters — **Rated Capacity (RC)** in N or kg and **Sensitivity (S)** in mV/V. A typical industrial load cell rated $1000\;\text{kg}$ with $S = 2\;\text{mV/V}$ excited by $10\;\text{V}$ gives full-scale output $V_{FS} = 20\;\text{mV}$, which is then amplified to $0$–$10\;\text{V}$ by an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620, INA128).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gauge Factor from First Principles

### Problem Setup
Consider a uniform metallic wire of:
- Original length $L$
- Cross-sectional area $A$
- Resistivity $\rho$
- Resistance $R$

**Initial resistance:**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1:** When force $F$ is applied along the wire axis, both length and cross-section change. Express the new resistance in terms of fractional changes.

$$R + \Delta R = \frac{(\rho + \Delta \rho)(L + \Delta L)}{A + \Delta A}$$

**Step 2:** Divide the new equation by the original to isolate the fractional change in $R$.

$$\frac{R + \Delta R}{R} = \frac{(\rho + \Delta \rho)(L + \Delta L)}{\rho L} \cdot \frac{A}{A + \Delta A}$$

**Step 3:** Recognizing that all fractional changes are tiny ($\ll 1$), expand using the identity $(1+x)(1+y)(1+z)^{-1} \approx 1 + x + y - z$:

$$1 + \frac{\Delta R}{R} \approx \left(1 + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right)\left(1 + \frac{\Delta L}{L}\right)\left(1 - \frac{\Delta A}{A}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the small-signal approximation (neglecting second-order products like $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L}$):

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \approx \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 5:** Use Poisson's ratio definition. For a wire stretched along $x$-axis, the lateral strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$:

$$\frac{\Delta A}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta r}{r} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

**Step 7:** Divide both sides by strain $\varepsilon$ to get the **Gauge Factor**:

$$\boxed{\;G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}\;}$$

**Numerical Example for Constantan (a metal alloy):**
- $\nu = 0.32$
- $\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 0$ (negligible piezoresistive effect in metals)
- $G_F = 1 + 2(0.32) + 0 = 1.64$

For **silicon semiconductor**:
- $G_F = 100$ to $200$ (huge piezoresistive contribution)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quarter-Bridge Output Voltage

### Problem Setup
A single active strain gauge ($R + \Delta R$) is placed in arm 1 of a Wheatstone bridge. All other arms are equal to $R$. Excitation is $V_{ex}$.

**Step 1:** Write the voltage at the left mid-point ($V_A$):

$$V_A = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

**Step 2:** Write the voltage at the right mid-point ($V_B$) using the standard potential divider on the other side:

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

**Step 3:** The differential output voltage is $V_o = V_A - V_B$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Factor out and simplify the bracketed term:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 5:** Since $\Delta R \ll R$, approximate $2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$:

$$V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\;V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon\;}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:**
- $V_{ex} = 10\;\text{V}$, $G_F = 2$, $\varepsilon = 1000\;\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$
- $V_o = (10/4)(2)(10^{-3}) = 5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V} = 5\;\text{mV}$ ✓ (typical load cell signal)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Full-Bridge Sensitivity Enhancement

### Problem Setup
Four identical gauges, each with $G_F = 2$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$, are arranged in a full-bridge with $R_1, R_3$ measuring tensile strain $\varepsilon$ (so they increase by $\Delta R$) and $R_2, R_4$ measuring compressive strain $-\nu\varepsilon$ (so they decrease by $\nu \Delta R$).

**Step 1:** Apply the general bridge equation:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, $R_2 = R - \nu \Delta R$, $R_3 = R + \Delta R$, $R_4 = R - \nu \Delta R$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + (R - \nu \Delta R)} - \frac{R - \nu \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + (R - \nu \Delta R)}\right]$$

**Step 3:** The denominator simplifies to $2R + (1 - \nu)\Delta R$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{(R + \Delta R) - (R - \nu \Delta R)}{2R + (1 - \nu)\Delta R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R (1 + \nu)}{2R + (1 - \nu)\Delta R}$$

**Step 4:** Approximating $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{2R} \cdot \Delta R (1 + \nu) = \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon (1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5:** Plug in $G_F = 2$, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot 1.3 \cdot \varepsilon = 1.3 \cdot V_{ex} \cdot \varepsilon$$

This is **2.6× more sensitive** than the quarter-bridge result and provides automatic temperature compensation. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capacitive Force Sensor Calibration

### Problem Setup
A capacitive sensor has $A = 10\;\text{mm} \times 10\;\text{mm} = 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2$, $d_0 = 0.5\;\text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air gap). A force produces displacement $\Delta d = 5\;\mu\text{m}$.

**Step 1:** Initial capacitance:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(10^{-4})}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.7708\;\text{pF}$$

**Step 2:** New capacitance after deflection:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 10^{-4}}{4.95 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.7887\;\text{pF}$$

**Step 3:** Fractional change:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{1.7887 - 1.7708}{1.7708} = 0.01011 \approx 1.01\%$$

**Step 4:** Convert to sensitivity (per $\mu$m):

$$S_C = \frac{\Delta C / C_0}{\Delta d} = \frac{0.01011}{5\;\mu\text{m}} = 2.022 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{per }\mu\text{m}$$

This demonstrates the high sensitivity of capacitive force sensors to sub-micron displacements.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Load Cell Mass Calculation

A load cell has rated capacity $F_{max} = 500\;\text{N}$, sensitivity $S = 2\;\text{mV/V}$, and is excited with $V_{ex} = 12\;\text{V}$. The output voltage reading is $V_o = 18\;\text{mV}$. Find the applied mass.

**Solution:**

$$V_{FS} = S \cdot V_{ex} = 2\;\text{mV/V} \times 12\;\text{V} = 24\;\text{mV}$$

$$\text{Force fraction} = \frac{V_o}{V_{FS}} = \frac{18}{24} = 0.75$$

$$F_{applied} = 0.75 \times F_{max} = 0.75 \times 500 = 375\;\text{N}$$

$$m = \frac{F}{g} = \frac{375}{9.81} \approx 38.23\;\text{kg}$$

✅ **Verified answer.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Classification of Force Sensors (Module 3 Scope)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FORCE SENSORS PCCPT205 Module 3]:::root
    A --> B[Piezoresistive Strain Gauge]:::cat
    A --> C[Piezoelectric]:::cat
    A --> D[Capacitive]:::cat
    A --> E[Other Types Inductive Hydraulic]:::cat

    B --> B1[Metallic Foil Gauge]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Semiconductor Gauge]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Thin Film Gauge]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Quartz Crystal]:::leaf
    C --> C2[PZT Ceramic]:::leaf
    C --> C3[PVDF Polymer]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Parallel Plate Variable Gap]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Differential Capacitor]:::leaf
    D --> D3[MEMS Comb Finger]:::leaf

    B1 --> S1[Wheatstone Bridge Conditioning]:::sig
    B2 --> S1
    B3 --> S1
    C1 --> S2[Charge Amplifier]:::sig
    C2 --> S2
    C3 --> S2
    D1 --> S3[Capacitance to Voltage Converter]:::sig
    D2 --> S3
    D3 --> S3

    S1 --> OUT1[Voltage Output mV]:::out
    S2 --> OUT2[Charge Output pC]:::out
    S3 --> OUT3[Frequency or Voltage Output]:::out

    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff

```
