# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reference

| Question Type | Marks | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | CO Mapping |
|---------------|-------|---------------------------|------------|
| Part A (2 questions) | 3 each | Remember / Understand | CO1, CO2 |
| Part B (1 of 2 choices) | 14 total | Apply / Analyze / Evaluate | CO2, CO3 |
| Total per module | 20 | — | — |

---

## 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for metallic and semiconductor strain gauges?**

**Model Answer:**

The Gauge Factor ($G_F$) of a strain gauge is defined as the **ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance** ($\Delta R / R$) to the **applied mechanical strain** ($\varepsilon$):

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

- **Typical value for metallic strain gauges:** $G_F \approx 1.8$ to $2.1$ (e.g., constantan: 2.0, Karma alloy: 2.0)
- **Typical value for semiconductor strain gauges:** $G_F \approx 100$ to $200$ (e.g., silicon: 120, germanium: 150)

The higher gauge factor of semiconductors arises from the dominant piezoresistive term $\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$, which is negligible in metals. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Distinguish between piezoelectric and piezoresistive force sensors. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive / Strain Gauge |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------------------|
| **Operating principle** | Generates electric charge when stressed (direct piezoelectric effect) | Resistance changes when strained |
| **Output quantity** | Charge ($q$) or Voltage | Resistance change ($\Delta R$) |
| **Frequency response** | Excellent for dynamic, high-frequency (kHz to MHz) | Good for static and low-frequency DC |
| **Static force measurement** | **Not possible** (charge leaks) | **Possible** |
| **Sensitivity** | Very high dynamic sensitivity | Moderate; requires bridge |
| **Material example** | Quartz, PZT, PVDF | Constantan, Karma alloy, Silicon |
| **Application** | Engine vibration monitoring, crash sensors, modal analysis | Weighing scales, weighbridges, load monitoring |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **ONE** of the following two questions. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit used in conjunction with a strain gauge for force measurement. Derive the expression for the output voltage when a single strain gauge undergoes strain $\varepsilon$. Assume all bridge arms are initially equal to $R$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction and Working Principle (4 marks):**

The Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a closed loop, with an excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ applied across opposite nodes (say, $C$ and $D$) and the output voltage $V_o$ measured across the other two nodes ($A$ and $B$).

When a strain gauge is mounted on an elastic flexure and subjected to force $F$, the resulting strain $\varepsilon$ causes its resistance to change by $\Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$. This unbalances the bridge, producing a differential output voltage $V_o$ proportional to the applied force.

**Node Voltages (2 marks):**

At Node $A$ (junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$):

$$V_A = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

At Node $B$ (junction of $R_3$ and $R_4$):

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Derivation of Output Voltage (1 mark):**

The differential output is $V_o = V_A - V_B$. With $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ (active gauge), $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R}\right] = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

Substituting $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{\;V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} G_F \varepsilon\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part A):**
> Students frequently **forget to apply the small-signal approximation** ($2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$) and leave the denominator unsimplified, losing 1 mark. Additionally, some omit the substitution of $\Delta R / R = G_F \varepsilon$ in the final step — both substitutions are required for full marks.

---

#### (b) A strain gauge with $G_F = 2.1$ and $R = 120\;\Omega$ is bonded to a steel cantilever of Young's modulus $E = 200\;\text{GPa}$. The cantilever dimensions are $L = 0.25\;\text{m}$, $b = 0.02\;\text{m}$ (width), $t = 0.003\;\text{m}$ (thickness). The bridge is excited with $V_{ex} = 10\;\text{V}$. Determine the bridge output voltage when a force $F = 50\;\text{N}$ is applied at the free end. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area**

$$A = b \times t = 0.02 \times 0.003 = 6 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the strain using Hooke's Law**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F}{A \cdot E} = \frac{50}{(6 \times 10^{-5})(200 \times 10^9)} = \frac{50}{1.2 \times 10^7} = 4.167 \times 10^{-6}$$

(Equivalent to $4.167\;\mu\varepsilon$)

**Step 3: Calculate the fractional resistance change**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-6} = 8.75 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the change in resistance**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 8.75 \times 10^{-6} = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}\;\Omega$$

**Step 5: Calculate the output voltage (quarter bridge)**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{10}{4} \times 8.75 \times 10^{-6} = 2.1875 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\;V_o \approx 21.88\;\mu\text{V}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> A common mistake is **omitting the bridge factor of 1/4** when the formula is applied. Also, many students confuse the **Young's modulus unit** — for steel, $E = 200 \times 10^9\;\text{N/m}^2$, not $200\;\text{N/m}^2$. This single error cascades into all subsequent values.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

#### (a) Explain the **piezoelectric effect** and describe how a piezoelectric force sensor works. Derive the relation between applied force and the generated charge. List any **three advantages** and **two limitations** of piezoelectric sensors. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Piezoelectric Effect (3 marks):**

The piezoelectric effect, discovered by **Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880**, is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (e.g., quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT ceramics, PVDF polymer) to generate an **electric charge** on their surfaces when subjected to **mechanical stress**.

There are two types:
- **Direct piezoelectric effect** (used in force sensors): Mechanical stress → electric charge
- **Converse piezoelectric effect** (used in actuators): Applied voltage → mechanical strain

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

A piezoelectric force sensor consists of a piezoelectric crystal disc sandwiched between two electrodes. When force $F$ is applied, the crystal experiences stress $\sigma = F/A$, which polarizes the crystal lattice and produces surface charges $+q$ and $-q$ on opposite faces. This charge is collected by the electrodes and converted to a voltage signal via a **charge amplifier**.

**Derivation of Force-Charge Relation (1 mark):**

The fundamental piezoelectric constitutive relation is:

$$\boxed{\;q = d \cdot F\;}$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N). For a quartz crystal, $d_{33} \approx 2.3\;\text{pC/N}$.

The output voltage across the crystal capacitance $C_p$ is:

$$V = \frac{q}{C_p} = \frac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$$

**Advantages (0.5 marks each):**
1. Wide frequency range (sub-Hz to MHz) — ideal for dynamic measurements
2. High stiffness and small size — minimal mechanical loading effect
3. Self-generating — no external power needed for the transducer

**Limitations (0.5 marks each):**
1. **Cannot measure static forces** — generated charge leaks away through impedance
2. Temperature sensitive and requires **high-impedance charge amplifier**

---

#### (b) A **capacitive force sensor** uses two parallel plates of area $A = 25\;\text{mm}^2$ separated by an air gap $d_0 = 0.4\;\text{mm}$. The sensor is connected to an oscillator of frequency $f = 100\;\text{kHz}$. A force is applied that reduces the gap by $\Delta d = 8\;\mu\text{m}$. Calculate: (i) the initial capacitance, (ii) the new capacitance, and (iii) the change in frequency of the oscillator if the capacitance is part of an LC tank circuit with $L = 10\;\text{mH}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 25\;\text{mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2$
- $d_0 = 0.4\;\text{mm} = 4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$
- $\Delta d = 8\;\mu\text{m} = 8 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}$
- $L = 10\;\text{mH} = 10 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{H}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{F/m}$

**Step (i): Initial Capacitance (2 marks)**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(25 \times 10^{-6})}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-16}}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.534 \times 10^{-13}\;\text{F}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_0 \approx 0.553\;\text{pF}\;}$$

**Step (ii): New Capacitance (2 marks)**

$$d_{new} = d_0 - \Delta d = 4 \times 10^{-4} - 8 \times 10^{-6} = 3.92 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$$

$$C_{new} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_{new}} = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-16}}{3.92 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.647 \times 10^{-13}\;\text{F}$$

$$\boxed{\;C_{new} \approx 0.565\;\text{pF}\;}$$

**Step (iii): Frequency Change (3 marks)**

Initial frequency (with $C_0$):

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_0}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(10 \times 10^{-3})(5.534 \times 10^{-13})}}$$

$$\sqrt{L C_0} = \sqrt{5.534 \times 10^{-15}} = 7.439 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 7.439 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.140 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Hz} = 2.14\;\text{MHz}$$

New frequency (with $C_{new}$):

$$f_{new} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_{new}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(10 \times 10^{-3})(5.647 \times 10^{-13})}}$$

$$\sqrt{L C_{new}} = \sqrt{5.647 \times 10^{-15}} = 7.515 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{s}$$

$$f_{new} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 7.515 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.118 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Hz} = 2.118\;\text{MHz}$$

**Frequency change:**

$$\Delta f = f_{new} - f_0 = 2.118 - 2.140 = -0.022\;\text{MHz}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta f \approx -22\;\text{kHz}\;}$$

The negative sign indicates frequency **decreased** as capacitance increased.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> 1. Many students confuse $\mu\text{m}$ with $\text{mm}$ — the gap $d_0 = 0.4\;\text{mm}$ should be converted to meters as $4 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$. 2. In the LC circuit formula, students often forget the **$2\pi$** factor. 3. The problem may ask for the **magnitude** of frequency change — clearly state $|\Delta f|$.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Force Sensors (PCCPT205 Module 3)**

- ✅ **Force sensor** = electromechanical device that converts mechanical force/load/weight into a quantifiable electrical signal ($\Delta R$, $V$, $q$, $\Delta C$).

- ✅ **Two-stage operation:** Force $\to$ Mechanical Deformation $\to$ Electrical Signal (always two coupled domains).

- ✅ **Hooke's Law:** $\Delta L = \dfrac{FL}{AE}$ ; Strain $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{F}{AE}$.

- ✅ **Master piezoresistive equation:** $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ — *single most important formula in this module*.

- ✅ **Gauge Factor definition:** $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$.

- ✅ **Gauge factor values to memorize:** Metal $G_F \approx 1.6$–$2.1$; Semiconductor $G_F \approx 100$–$200$.

- ✅ **Poisson's ratio $\nu$:** For most metals $\nu \approx 0.3$; used in half/full bridge formulas.

- ✅ **Wheatstone bridge output (quarter):** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G_F \varepsilon$.

- ✅ **Wheatstone bridge output (full):** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} G_F \varepsilon (1 + \nu)$ — **most sensitive configuration**.

- ✅ **Full-bridge is preferred in industry** — provides temperature compensation and 2.6× higher sensitivity than quarter bridge.

- ✅ **Piezoelectric effect (Curie brothers, 1880):** Stress $\to$ Charge, with $q = d \cdot F$.

- ✅ **Typical $d_{33}$ for quartz:** $2.3\;\text{pC/N}$; for PZT: $400$–$600\;\text{pC/N}$ (much higher).

- ✅ **Piezoelectric sensors are DYNAMIC ONLY** — they cannot measure static (constant) force because the generated charge leaks away.

- ✅ **Charge amplifier required** for piezoelectric sensors due to extremely high output impedance.

- ✅ **Capacitive force sensor equation:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — applied force $\to$ gap reduction $\to$ capacitance increase.

- ✅ **Load cell sensitivity unit:** $\text{mV/V}$ — full-scale output $V_{FS} = S \times V_{ex}$.

- ✅ **Strain gauge typical resistance values:** $120\;\Omega$, $350\;\Omega$, $1000\;\Omega$.

- ✅ **Standard excitation voltage:** $V_{ex} = 5\;\text{V}$ to $15\;\text{V}$ DC (regulated).

- ✅ **Young's modulus of steel:** $E = 200\;\text{GPa} = 2 \times 10^{11}\;\text{N/m}^2$ — memorize this value.

- ✅ **Strain range:** $\varepsilon \approx 10^{-6}$ (microstrain) to $10^{-3}$ (millistrain) for typical gauges.

- ✅ **Strain gauge bonding adhesive:** Epoxy or cyanoacrylate — must transfer strain faithfully.

- ✅ **Temperature effects:** Compensated using dummy gauge, self-temperature-compensated alloys (Karma), or full-bridge configuration.

- ✅ **Application areas:** Weighing scales, automotive (crash sensors, seat occupancy), industrial process control, robotics, aerospace, biomedical prosthetics, structural health monitoring.

- ✅ **KTU exam focus:** Derivation of gauge factor, derivation of bridge output, numerical problems on strain-to-voltage conversion, comparison of force sensor types, and signal conditioning circuits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pressure Sensors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Pressure Sensor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *pressure sensor* is a transducer that converts an applied mechanical pressure (force per unit area) into a measurable, standardized electrical signal (voltage, current, capacitance change, or frequency shift) proportional to the magnitude of the input pressure. It forms a critical sensing node in any Cyber-Physical System (CPS) where fluid or gas state variables must be observed and fed back to a digital controller. In SI units, pressure is defined as the normal force exerted per unit area: $$P = \frac{F}{A}$$ where $F$ is the applied force in Newtons (N) and $A$ is the surface area in square meters ($m^2$). The SI derived unit of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, which equals $1 \text{ N/m}^2$. ### 1.2 Pressure Reference Standards (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > Pressure is always measured *relative* to some reference. The four standard reference modes are listed below and are **frequently asked in KTU exams**: | Reference Mode | Zero Reference Point | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** | Perfect vacuum (0 Pa) | Altimeters, barometers, weather stations | | **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$)** | Local atmospheric pressure | Tire pressure, blood pressure, industrial tanks | | **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$)** | A second arbitrary pressure point | Flow measurement (orifice plates), filter monitoring | | **Vacuum / Negative Gauge** | Atmospheric (measured below it) | Vacuum chambers, suction systems | The mathematical relationship between them is: $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$ $$\Delta P = P_{high} - P_{low}$$ ### 1.3 Standard Pressure Units & Conversion Factors > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constant to memorize:** Standard atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 760 \text{ mmHg} = 14.696 \text{ psi}$. | Unit | Symbol | Equivalent in Pascals | |---|---|---| | Pascal (SI) | Pa | $1$ | | Kilopascal | kPa | $10^{3}$ | | Bar | bar | $1 \times 10^{5}$ | | Atmosphere | atm | $101325$ | | PSI (lb/in²) | psi | $6894.76$ | | mmHg (Torr) | mmHg | $133.322$ | | Inch of Water | inH₂O | $249.089$ | ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezed Balloon" Imagine pressing your thumb against a fully inflated balloon: - The harder you press (**more force $F$**), the more the balloon surface deforms. - The balloon's skin (a **diaphragm**) bends inward by a small amount — this bending is what every pressure sensor is built to detect. - Different sensors "read" this bending in different ways: some measure the **stretching of embedded resistors**, some measure the **change in distance** between two plates, and some generate a tiny **voltage charge** from the crystal itself. - The balloon's deformation $\rightarrow$ the sensor's output signal (electrical) $\rightarrow$ the controller's reading of "how hard is the press." This is exactly the operating philosophy of **every** modern pressure sensor: a mechanical deformation stage coupled to an electrical transduction stage. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 General Working Architecture of a Pressure Sensor Every pressure sensor — regardless of its transduction principle — follows the same three-stage functional chain: > [!NOTE] > **Stage 1 — Sensing Element (Mechanical):** An elastic structure (diaphragm, Bourdon tube, or bellows) deflects proportionally to applied pressure. > > **Stage 2 — Transduction Element (Electrical):** The mechanical deflection is converted into a change in electrical quantity (resistance, capacitance, charge, frequency). > > **Stage 3 — Signal Conditioning (Electronic):** Amplification, linearization, temperature compensation, and analog-to-digital conversion produce a clean, standardized output. ### 2.2 Classification of Pressure Sensors (KTU Taxonomy) #### A. Mechanical / Direct-Reading Pressure Sensors These sensors display pressure via mechanical motion of a pointer — no electricity involved historically, but their *principle* underlies many modern electrical sensors. **1. Manometer (U-Tube / Well-Type)** - A liquid column (mercury, water, or oil) rises or falls in proportion to the applied pressure. - The height difference $h$ of the liquid column is measured. $$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$ where $\rho$ is liquid density, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $h$ is the column height difference. - Mercury manometer: $P (\text{Pa}) = 133.322 \times h (\text{mmHg})$ - Water manometer: $P (\text{Pa}) = 9806.65 \times h (\text{mH}_2\text{O})$ **2. Bourdon Tube** - A curved, flattened metal tube straightens slightly when internal pressure rises. - The tip displacement is mechanically linked to a pointer. - Most common form: **C-type**, **helical**, and **spiral** Bourdon tubes. - Used for high-pressure industrial gauges (up to 700 MPa). **3. Diaphragm Sensor** - A thin circular or rectangular plate (metal or silicon) clamped at its edges. - Under uniform pressure $P$, the center deflection $y_{max}$ is given by: $$y_{max} = \frac{3 P (1 - \nu^2) a^4}{16 E t^3}$$ where $a$ is the effective radius, $t$ is diaphragm thickness, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. **4. Bellows Sensor** - A corrugated cylindrical element that axially elongates/contracts with pressure. - Provides much larger displacement than a flat diaphragm — useful for low-pressure measurement. #### B. Electrical Pressure Sensors (KTU Focal Area) **1. Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor (Strain-Gauge Based)** - Four piezoresistors are diffused onto a silicon diaphragm forming a **Wheatstone bridge**. - When pressure deflects the diaphragm, two resistors experience tensile strain ($\Delta R > 0$) and two experience compressive strain ($\Delta R < 0$). - The bridge output voltage is: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ where $V_{ex}$ is the bridge excitation voltage (typically 5 V or 10 V). - The fractional resistance change is related to strain $\varepsilon$ by the **Gauge Factor (GF)**: $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$ - For silicon piezoresistors, $GF \approx 100$ to $200$ (compared to $GF \approx 2$ for metal foil). - **Most widely manufactured pressure sensor type in the world** (used in automotive, medical, industrial). **2. Capacitive Pressure Sensor** - Pressure deflects a diaphragm that forms one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor. - The capacitance is: $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d \pm \Delta d}$$ where $d$ is the nominal gap, $\Delta d$ is the pressure-induced deflection, $A$ is the electrode area, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ is vacuum permittivity, and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the dielectric. - Sensitivity: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial P} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(d - y)^2} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dP}$ - **Advantages:** Low power, high sensitivity at low pressure, good temperature stability. - **Disadvantages:** Non-linear response, susceptible to EMI, requires complex readout circuitry. **3. Piezoelectric Pressure Sensor** - Uses the direct piezoelectric effect: certain crystals (quartz, PZT, PVDF) generate a charge $Q$ when mechanically stressed. - The generated charge is: $$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A$$ where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N). - **Crucial KTU point:** Piezoelectric sensors can only measure **dynamic pressure** (changing pressure). Static pressure slowly leaks away through the charge amplifier's input impedance — this is called **charge drift**. - Excellent for: shock waves, engine combustion pressure, blast pressure, ultrasonic transducers. **4. Optical Pressure Sensor (Fiber-Optic)** - Pressure changes the intensity, phase, or wavelength of light transmitted through an optical fiber. - A common architecture: pressure deflects a diaphragm that modulates the **Fabry–Pérot cavity length**. - The reflected wavelength $\lambda$ shifts according to: $$\lambda_m = \frac{2 n L}{m}$$ where $n$ is refractive index, $L$ is cavity length, and $m$ is the interference order. - **Advantages:** Immune to EMI, intrinsically safe (no sparking), works in explosive environments. - **Applications:** Down-hole oil well monitoring, medical catheters, high-voltage transformer tanks. **5. Resonant Pressure Sensor** - Pressure changes the tension in a vibrating wire or silicon resonator, altering its natural frequency $f$. - The frequency is given by: $$f = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$ where $L$ is wire length, $T$ is tension (changed by pressure), and $\mu$ is linear mass density. - The output is a **frequency**, which can be measured with extreme accuracy. - **Highest accuracy class of all pressure sensors** (used as primary pressure standards). **6. Ionization Pressure Sensor** - Used for **ultra-high vacuum (UHV)** measurement ($10^{-9}$ to $10^{-13}$ Pa). - Based on the ionization of residual gas molecules by a heated filament; the resulting ion current is proportional to gas density and therefore to pressure. - Two main types: **Hot-cathode** (Bayard-Alpert) and **Cold-cathode** (Penning, magnetron). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following equations — these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = F/A$ | Basic pressure definition | Pa | | 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Pressure reference conversion | Pa | | 3 | $P = \rho g h$ | Manometer (hydrostatic) | Pa | | 4 | $y_{max} = \dfrac{3P(1-\nu^2)a^4}{16 E t^3}$ | Diaphragm center deflection | m | | 5 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitive sensor capacitance | F | | 6 | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge factor (strain gauge) | dimensionless | | 7 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Wheatstone bridge output | V | | 8 | $Q = d \cdot P \cdot A$ | Piezoelectric charge output | C | | 9 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | Resonant wire frequency | Hz | | 10 | $\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y = \dfrac{3P a^2}{8 t^2}(1-\nu^2)$ | Diaphragm in-plane strain | dimensionless | ### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering - The **diaphragm deflection equation** lets MEMS designers choose thickness $t$ and radius $a$ to get a desired sensitivity for a target pressure range. - The **gauge factor equation** tells circuit designers how much $\Delta R$ to expect, helping them set the bridge excitation voltage and amplifier gain. - The **capacitance equation** with the $(d-y)^2$ in the denominator explains why capacitive sensors are **non-linear** — this drives the need for digital linearization. - The **resonant frequency equation** is the reason resonant sensors are the gold standard for precision barometry in meteorological and aerospace applications. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Center Deflection of a Circular Diaphragm > [!NOTE] > **Starting assumption:** A thin, circular, edge-clamped elastic diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $t$ is loaded with a uniform pressure $P$ on one side. The material is isotropic with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. **Step 1 — Set up the small-deflection plate equation.** For small deflections ($y \ll t$), classical Kirchhoff–Love thin plate theory gives the governing differential equation: $$D \nabla^4 w = q(x, y)$$ where $w$ is the transverse deflection, $q$ is the distributed load (here $q = P$), and $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate. **Step 2 — Express the flexural rigidity.** $$D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve in polar coordinates.** For axisymmetric loading on a circular plate, the biharmonic operator simplifies and the standard clamped-edge solution is: $$w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D}\left(a^2 - r^2\right)^2$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the maximum deflection at the center ($r = 0$).** Substitute $r = 0$: $$w_{max} = w(0) = \frac{P}{64 D} \cdot a^4$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the expression for $D$.** $$w_{max} = \frac{P}{64} \cdot \frac{12(1-\nu^2)}{E t^3} \cdot a^4$$ **Step 6 — Simplify the numerical constant.** $$w_{max} = \frac{12 \, P \, (1-\nu^2) \, a^4}{64 \, E \, t^3} = \frac{3 P (1-\nu^2) a^4}{16 E t^3}$$ This recovers the standard KTU formula. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering insight:** Doubling the radius $a$ increases $w_{max}$ by a factor of $2^4 = 16$. Doubling the thickness $t$ reduces $w_{max}$ by a factor of $2^3 = 8$. Therefore, **diaphragm geometry is the dominant design knob** for sensitivity tuning. ### 3.2 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output for Piezoresistive Sensor **Step 1 — Define the four arm resistances.** Let the four piezoresistors in the bridge be $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$. With no pressure, $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. **Step 2 — Apply the general bridge output equation.** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Introduce the strain-induced changes.** Design the layout so that $R_1$ and $R_3$ are in tension (resistance increases by $+\Delta R$) while $R_2$ and $R_4$ are in compression (resistance decreases by $-\Delta R$): $$R_1 = R + \Delta R, \quad R_2 = R - \Delta R, \quad R_3 = R + \Delta R, \quad R_4 = R - \Delta R$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the bridge equation.** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R - \Delta R}{2R} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Simplify both fractions.** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{2R} \right)$$ **Step 6 — Collect terms.** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ **Step 7 — Express $\Delta R / R$ via gauge factor and strain.** Since $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$, we have $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$. $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ **Step 8 — Relate strain to pressure using the diaphragm strain formula.** For a circular diaphragm, the radial strain at the edge is: $$\varepsilon = \frac{3 P a^2}{8 t^2}(1 - \nu^2)$$ **Step 9 — Final compact form of the sensor output.** $$V_{out} = \frac{3 V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot (1 - \nu^2) \cdot a^2}{8 t^2} \cdot P$$ $$V_{out} = K \cdot P$$ where the sensor constant $K$ depends only on geometry and material properties. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial KTU result:** The output is **linear in pressure** — this is one of the key reasons piezoresistive sensors dominate commercial pressure measurement. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Pressure Sensor Types | Sensor Type | Sensitivity | Linearity | Frequency Response | Temp. Stability | Best Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Piezoresistive** | High | Excellent (linear) | DC to ~kHz | Moderate (needs compensation) | General-purpose, automotive TPMS, medical | | **Capacitive** | Very High (low P) | Poor (non-linear) | DC to kHz | Excellent | Low-pressure, MEMS barometers, HVAC | | **Piezoelectric** | High | Good (above resonance) | kHz to MHz | Good | Dynamic / shock / vibration / combustion | | **Optical (Fiber)** | Moderate | Excellent | DC to MHz | Excellent | EMI environments, oil wells, HV | | **Resonant** | Very High | Excellent | DC (slow) | Excellent | Precision barometers, primary standards | | **Bourdon (mech.)** | Visual only | Good | Static only | Excellent | Local gauge reading, industrial dials | | **Manometer** | Visual only | Perfect (linear in h) | Static only | Depends on liquid | Calibration reference | | **Ionization** | N/A | Logarithmic | DC | Poor (filament aging) | Ultra-high vacuum | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Pressure Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PRESSURE INPUT P] --> B[ELASTIC SENSOR ELEMENT\nDiaphragm or Bourdon Tube] B --> C[MECHANICAL DEFLECTION y] C --> D[TRANSDUCTION STAGE\nPiezoresistive / Capacitive / Piezoelectric] D --> E[RAW ELECTRICAL SIGNAL\nmV / fF / pC] E --> F[SIGNAL CONDITIONING\nAmplifier and Filter] F --> G[TEMPERATURE COMPENSATION\nLook-up Table or Polynomial] G --> H[LINEARIZATION BLOCK\nCurve Fitting Algorithm] H --> I[ADC CONVERSION] I --> J[DIGITAL OUTPUT\nI2C / SPI / Analog Voltage] J --> K[MICROCONTROLLER / CPS NODE] K --> L[DECISION AND ACTUATION] ``` ### 4.2 Piezoresistive Sensor — Bridge Layout on Diaphragm ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CLAMPED SILICON DIAPHRAGM] --> B[EDGE REGION\nR1 and R3 in TENSION\nResistance Increases] A --> C[CENTER REGION\nR2 and R4 in COMPRESSION\nResistance Decreases] B --> D[BRIDGE NODE A] C --> E[BRIDGE NODE B] D --> F[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE NETWORK] E --> F F --> G[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT\nVout = Vex times DeltaR by R] ``` ### 4.3 Capacitive Pressure Sensor — Cross-Section Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FIXED GLASS SUBSTRATE\nBottom Electrode] --> B[AIR OR VACUUM GAP d] B --> C[PRESSURE SENSITIVE DIAPHRAGM\nTop Electrode] C --> D[APPLIED PRESSURE P] A --- E[CAPACITANCE C\nequals epsilon0 epsilonr A over d] B --- E C --- E ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flowchart for Pressure Sensor Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Identify Application] --> B{Is the Pressure\nStatic or Dynamic?} B -->|Static DC| C{Required Accuracy Class?} B -->|Dynamic AC| D[Piezoelectric Sensor\nRecommended] C -->|High Precision
Greater than 0.01 percent FS| E[Resonant or Optical Sensor] C -->|Industrial Standard| F{Environment has\nStrong EMI or HV?} F -->|Yes| G[Fiber Optic Pressure Sensor] F -->|No| H{Pressure Range} H -->|Low less than 10 kPa| I[Capacitive MEMS Sensor] H -->|Medium to High| J[Piezoresistive Silicon Sensor] H -->|Very High greater than 100 MPa| K[Bourdon Tube or Strain Gauge] ``` ### 4.5 Cyber-Physical System Integration Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSICAL PROCESS\nFluid or Gas] --> B[PRESSURE SENSOR NODE] B --> C[ADC and MICROCONTROLLER] C --> D[COMMUNICATION LAYER\nCAN / I2C / Wireless] D --> E[CLOUD OR EDGE COMPUTE] E --> F[CONTROL ALGORITHM] F --> G[ACTUATOR COMMAND] G --> H[ACTUATOR e.g. VALVE] H --> A E --> I[HMI DASHBOARD\nDisplay and Alerts] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the following terms with one example for each:* *(a) Absolute pressure* *(b) Gauge pressure* *(c) Differential pressure* **Model Answer:** > **(a) Absolute Pressure:** Pressure measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (zero reference). Example: Atmospheric pressure at sea level = 101.325 kPa (absolute). > > **(b) Gauge Pressure:** Pressure measured with respect to local atmospheric pressure. It can be positive (above atm) or negative (vacuum). Example: A tire pressure gauge reads 32 psi (gauge), meaning the absolute pressure inside the tire is 32 + 14.7 = 46.7 psi. > > **(c) Differential Pressure:** The difference in pressure between two points, irrespective of their absolute value. Example: The pressure drop across a pipe orifice plate used to measure flow rate. **Mathematical relation:** $$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any four different types of pressure sensors and state the underlying transduction principle for each.* **Model Answer:** | # | Sensor Type | Transduction Principle | |---|---|---| | 1 | Piezoresistive | Change in resistance due to mechanical strain in piezoresistors on a deflected diaphragm (Wheatstone bridge) | | 2 | Capacitive | Change in capacitance due to gap variation between a fixed plate and a pressure-deflected diaphragm | | 3 | Piezoelectric | Generation of electric charge ($Q = d \cdot F$) in crystals like quartz or PZT under stress | | 4 | Optical / Fiber-Optic | Modulation of light intensity, phase, or wavelength in a fiber due to diaphragm displacement | ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **piezoresistive pressure sensor**. Derive the expression for the output voltage of a Wheatstone bridge arrangement with four piezoresistors. (7 Marks) **(b)** A silicon diaphragm of radius $a = 2 \text{ mm}$, thickness $t = 0.2 \text{ mm}$, Young's modulus $E = 190 \text{ GPa}$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$ is loaded with a pressure of $P = 100 \text{ kPa}$. Calculate (i) the center deflection, and (ii) the maximum in-plane strain at the diaphragm edge. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) **Working Principle:** 1. Pressure $P$ is applied to one side of a thin silicon diaphragm that is rigidly clamped at its edges. 2. The diaphragm deflects, producing tensile strain at certain locations and compressive strain at others. 3. Four piezoresistors are ion-implanted at carefully chosen locations: two at the edge (tangential direction) where tensile strain is maximum, and two at the center (radial direction) where compressive strain is maximum. 4. The four resistors form a **Wheatstone bridge** excited by a stable voltage $V_{ex}$. 5. On deflection, the resistors in tension *increase* by $+\Delta R$ and those in compression *decrease* by $-\Delta R$. 6. The bridge becomes unbalanced, producing a differential output voltage $V_{out}$ that is linear in pressure. **Derivation of $V_{out}$:** Following Steps 1 to 7 of Section 3.2 above: $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ Using the diaphragm edge strain: $$\varepsilon = \frac{3Pa^2}{8t^2}(1-\nu^2)$$ Therefore: $$V_{out} = \frac{3 \cdot V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot (1-\nu^2) \cdot a^2}{8 t^2} \cdot P$$ The output is a **linear function of pressure** with sensitivity determined by $V_{ex}$, $GF$, and diaphragm geometry. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) **Given data:** - $a = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - $t = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$ - $E = 190 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$ - $\nu = 0.28$ - $P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 1 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$ **(i) Center Deflection $y_{max}$:** $$y_{max} = \frac{3 P (1 - \nu^2) a^4}{16 E t^3}$$ Calculate the geometric and material term: $$(1 - \nu^2) = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216$$ $$a^4 = (2 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 16 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$$ $$t^3 = (2 \times 10^{-4})^3 = 8 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3$$ Substitute: $$y_{max} = \frac{3 \times 1 \times 10^{5} \times 0.9216 \times 16 \times 10^{-12}}{16 \times 190 \times 10^{9} \times 8 \times 10^{-12}}$$ **Numerator:** $$3 \times 1 \times 10^{5} \times 0.9216 \times 16 \times 10^{-12} = 4.42 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Denominator:** $$16 \times 190 \times 10^{9} \times 8 \times 10^{-12} = 24.32 \times 10^{0} = 24.32$$ **Result:** $$y_{max} = \frac{4.42 \times 10^{-6}}{24.32} = 1.82 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.182 \text{ μm}$$ **(ii) Maximum In-Plane Strain:** $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 P a^2}{8 t^2}(1 - \nu^2)$$ $$a^2 = (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$ $$t^2 = (2 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$ $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \times 1 \times 10^{5} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}} \times 0.9216$$ $$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{0}}{3.2 \times 10^{-7}} \times 0.9216 = 3750 \times 0.9216 = 3456 \text{ μ-strain} = 3.456 \times 10^{-3}$$ > [!NOTE] > This very high strain (≈ 3456 microstrain) confirms why silicon piezoresistors with $GF \approx 150$ produce usable $\Delta R / R \approx 0.52$ — i.e., over 50% fractional change — explaining the excellent sensitivity of piezoresistive sensors. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **capacitive pressure sensor** with a neat sketch. State two advantages and two disadvantages. (7 Marks) **(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive pressure sensor has a diaphragm of area $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2$ and a nominal air gap of $d = 5 \text{ μm}$. When a pressure of $P = 50 \text{ kPa}$ is applied, the diaphragm deflects by $y = 0.8 \text{ μm}$ at the center. Calculate (i) the nominal capacitance and (ii) the fractional change in capacitance at the center. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) **Working Principle:** 1. The sensor consists of a **fixed bottom electrode** (on a glass or silicon substrate) and a **pressure-sensitive diaphragm** acting as the top electrode, separated by a small air gap $d$. 2. Together they form a **parallel-plate capacitor**. 3. When pressure is applied, the diaphragm bows toward the bottom plate, **reducing the local gap** to $(d - y)$ at the center. 4. This gap change **increases the local capacitance** since $C \propto 1/d$. 5. The change in capacitance is measured by a high-impedance readout circuit (often a switched-capacitor or charge amplifier). **Diagram (text representation):** ``` APPLIED PRESSURE P ↓ ↓ ↓ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ DIAPHRAGM (top plate) │ ← deflects by y ├──────────────────────────┤ │ AIR GAP d-y │ ← capacitance increases ├──────────────────────────┤ │ BOTTOM FIXED PLATE │ └──────────────────────────┘ ``` **Advantages:** 1. Very high sensitivity at low pressures (large $dC/dP$ at small $d$). 2. Low power consumption; compatible with CMOS/MEMS processes. 3. Excellent temperature stability and long-term drift performance. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Non-linear response** because $C \propto 1/d$ (inverse relationship). 2. **Susceptible to EMI** and parasitic capacitance; requires complex shielding and guard-ring design. 3. Output is small (fF range), demanding very low-noise readout electronics. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) **Given data:** - $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$ - $d = 5 \text{ μm} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ - $y = 0.8 \text{ μm} = 0.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ - $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$ (air) **(i) Nominal Capacitance $C_0$:** $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ $$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$C_0 = \frac{221.35 \times 10^{-18}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 44.27 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 44.27 \text{ pF}$$ **(ii) New Capacitance at Center $C_{new}$:** $$C_{new} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - y} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{(5 - 0.8) \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$C_{new} = \frac{221.35 \times 10^{-18}}{4.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 52.70 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 52.70 \text{ pF}$$ **Fractional Change:** $$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{C_{new} - C_0}{C_0} = \frac{52.70 - 44.27}{44.27} = \frac{8.43}{44.27} \approx 0.1904$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx 19.04\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > A ~19% capacitance change from a modest diaphragm deflection confirms why **capacitive pressure sensors are extremely sensitive** — but the readout IC must handle this large dynamic range with good linearity correction. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Pressure Sensor Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $(1 - \nu^2)$ term** in the diaphragm deflection or strain formula — this alone costs **1 full mark** in Part B numericals. > 2. **Confusing the gauge factor definition**: $GF$ is defined as $\frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$, **not** the other way around. Reversing the ratio = −1 mark. > 3. **Wrong reference for pressure**: Stating $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} + P_{atm}$ instead of the correct $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ is a frequently penalized error. > 4. **Missing units** in the final numerical answer. Always write the unit alongside the number (e.g., $1.82 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$ or $0.182 \text{ μm}$). > 5. **Writing "piezoelectric sensors can measure static pressure"** — this is **false** and will be marked wrong. Piezoelectric sensors are inherently AC-coupled due to charge leakage through the amplifier input impedance. > 6. **Not drawing the bridge circuit** in piezoresistive questions — even a hand-drawn sketch earns the **diagram mark** (typically 1–2 marks in KTU). > 7. **Unit conversion slip-ups**: Mixing mm with m without $10^{-3}$ conversion is the most common reason students score only 3/7 in numerical sub-parts. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pressure Sensors (Module 3)** - ✅ Pressure is force per unit area, $P = F/A$, measured in **Pascals (Pa)**. - ✅ $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$. - ✅ Four pressure reference modes: **Absolute, Gauge, Differential, Vacuum**. - ✅ **Piezoresistive sensors** are linear, low-cost, dominant in industry. Output: $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$. - ✅ **Capacitive sensors** have high sensitivity at low pressure but are non-linear ($C \propto 1/d$). - ✅ **Piezoelectric sensors** measure **dynamic pressure only**; $Q = d \cdot P \cdot A$. - ✅ **Optical / fiber-optic** sensors are immune to EMI, ideal for explosive or HV environments. - ✅ **Resonant sensors** offer the highest accuracy; output is a frequency directly related to wire tension. - ✅ **Diaphragm center deflection**: $y_{max} = \frac{3P(1-\nu^2)a^4}{16 E t^3}$. - ✅ **Gauge Factor**: $GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$; silicon $GF \approx 100$–$200$. - ✅ **Wheatstone bridge** with two tensile and two compressive resistors gives $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ directly. - ✅ **MEMS pressure sensors** are essentially miniaturized piezoresistive or capacitive devices. - ✅ **Ionization gauges** are required for ultra-high vacuum ($< 10^{-7}$ Pa). - ✅ Always convert units to SI (**Pascals and meters**) before plugging into any formula. - ✅ Always state the **pressure reference** (absolute or gauge) when reporting a pressure value.

topics.title: Strain Gauges and Practical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strain Gauges and Practical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Gauge (Strain Gage / Strain Transducer):** A **passive resistive transducer** that converts a mechanical quantity — specifically **mechanical strain (ε)** — into a proportional, measurable change in **electrical resistance (ΔR)**. The principle of operation is founded on the **piezoresistive effect** and the **geometric (dimensional) sensitivity** of a current-carrying conductor subjected to axial mechanical deformation.

The governing relationship is expressed through the dimensionless **Gauge Factor (GF)**, which quantifies the sensitivity of the gauge:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

where $\Delta R / R$ is the fractional change in resistance and $\varepsilon$ is the mechanical strain (dimensionless, often expressed in microstrain, $\mu\varepsilon$).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Stretchy Rubber Wire":** Imagine a thin elastic conducting wire glued tightly onto a steel ruler. When you bend the ruler, the wire stretches microscopically (or compresses). A stretched wire becomes **longer and thinner**, and as any first-year physics student knows, **longer + thinner = higher electrical resistance**. The strain gauge exploits precisely this "stretch-resistance" relationship. The change in resistance is tiny (often parts per million), so engineers cleverly embed the gauge in a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit to "amplify" this microscopic change into a measurable voltage.

In a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** context, the strain gauge acts as the **physical layer (PHY) sensing node** that translates a real-world mechanical event into an analog electrical domain variable, ready for ADC digitization and edge processing.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\nu$ | Poisson's Ratio (for constantan) | **0.3 to 0.33** |
| $\rho$ | Resistivity of conductor | **Material-dependent (Ω·m)** |
| $\varepsilon$ | Engineering strain | **Dimensionless** (typically $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| $1 \mu\varepsilon$ | Microstrain | $= 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m/m |
| Typical $GF$ (metal foil) | Gauge Factor | **2.0 to 2.2** |
| Typical $GF$ (semiconductor) | Gauge Factor | **100 to 200** |
| Standard gauge resistance | $R$ | **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 1000 Ω** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** Examiners love asking *"Why is the gauge factor of a semiconductor strain gauge much higher than that of a metallic foil gauge?"* The answer is **piezoresistivity dominates** in semiconductors (the $\Delta\rho/\rho$ term in $GF$ is huge), whereas in metals only the geometric term $(1+2\nu)$ contributes significantly.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Strain Gauges (Syllabus Must-Know)

- **Bonded Strain Gauges** — physically adhered to the test specimen.
  - *Wire type* (earliest form)
  - *Foil type* (photo-etched constantan grid — industry standard)
  - *Semiconductor type* (silicon piezoresistor — highest sensitivity)
- **Unbonded Strain Gauges** — suspended between fixed posts; movement stretches the wire.
- **Weldable / Bolt-on Gauges** — for harsh industrial environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind the Physics

A conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ has a baseline resistance:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

When subjected to an axial strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$, three physical phenomena occur simultaneously:

1. **Length change:** $L \rightarrow L + \Delta L$, contributing $+ \varepsilon$ to relative resistance change.
2. **Area change (Poisson effect):** Diameter contracts, area decreases, resistance increases, contributing $+ 2\nu \varepsilon$ (where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio).
3. **Resistivity change (Piezoresistive effect):** The atomic lattice deforms, altering carrier mobility. In metals, this term is negligible; in semiconductors, it is **dominant**.

---

### 2.2 Derivation of Gauge Factor (Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown)

**Step 1 — Start with the resistance expression:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Take the logarithmic differential (standard sensitivity analysis technique):**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Poisson's relation** — the lateral strain is $-\nu \cdot (dL/L)$, so the area strain is $2 \times (-\nu)(dL/L)$:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and define the Gauge Factor:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{GF = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

**Why each term matters:**
- $(1 + 2\nu)$ → **Geometric contribution** (dominant in metals, $\approx 1.6$ for constantan).
- $\frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$ → **Piezoresistive contribution** (negligible in metals, dominant in semiconductors, $\approx 100+$).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below — at least 2–3 of these equations will appear in every KTU End Semester Examination.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Ohm's law for a conductor | Base resistance |
| 2 | $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + (d\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ | Geometric + piezoresistive | Gauge sensitivity |
| 3 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change vs strain | Direct readout |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$ | Wheatstone bridge output | Generic bridge |
| 5 | $V_{out}/V_{ex} \approx GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ | Quarter-bridge approximation | 1 active gauge |
| 6 | $V_{out}/V_{ex} \approx GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$ | Half-bridge (adjacent arms) | 2 active gauges |
| 7 | $V_{out}/V_{ex} \approx GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge (4 active) | Maximum sensitivity |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \sigma / E = F/(A \cdot E)$ | Hooke's law link to stress | Load cell design |
| 9 | $\sigma_{max} = (6 \cdot M)/(b \cdot h^2)$ | Bending stress, rectangular beam | Cantilever analysis |
| 10 | $V_{bridge} \approx \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4R}$ (single arm) | Approximate small-ΔR balance | First-order analysis |

> **Units Reminder:** Strain is dimensionless, gauge factor is dimensionless, $V_{out}/V_{ex}$ is a dimensionless ratio (V/V), $\Delta R/R$ is dimensionless.

---

### 2.4 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations — Operational Logic

The **Wheatstone bridge** converts the tiny $\Delta R$ into a usable voltage. Its genius is the use of **differential measurement** — common-mode noise (temperature drift, lead resistance) cancels out, while the differential signal due to strain is amplified.

**A. Quarter Bridge (1 active gauge, 3 fixed)**
- Sensitivity: $V_{out}/V_{ex} = GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$
- **Use:** Low-cost, single-axis measurement; prone to temperature drift.

**B. Half Bridge (2 active gauges, 2 fixed)**
- Adjacent arms: doubles sensitivity to $GF \cdot \varepsilon / 2$.
- Opposite arms: cancels axial strain, doubles bending response.
- **Use:** Bending-beam load cells, temperature compensation.

**C. Full Bridge (4 active gauges)**
- Sensitivity: $V_{out}/V_{ex} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$
- **Use:** Industrial load cells, torque sensors, pressure diaphragms — best linearity, highest SNR.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Bridge structural-health monitoring, dam strain surveillance, building sway detection.
- **Aerospace:** Wing-flex monitoring, fuselage load telemetry.
- **Automotive:** Crash-test dummies, suspension load cells, tire pressure monitoring (TPMS).
- **Medical:** Catheter-tip force sensors, prosthetics, smart implants.
- **Industrial Weighing:** Load cells in platform scales (typically full-bridge).
- **Robotics/CPS:** Force-torque feedback in collaborative robot arms — **directly relevant to Cyber-Physical Systems** since the strain signal is fed back to the controller for compliant control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Quarter-Bridge Output Voltage

**Given:** A single strain gauge mounted on a beam. Gauge resistance $R_1 = R$ at zero strain. When strained by $\varepsilon$, the resistance becomes $R_1 = R + \Delta R$. The other three arms are fixed resistors $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. Excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across nodes A and C.

**Wheatstone Bridge Topology — Node Definitions:**
- Node A (top) — excitation $V_{ex}$
- Node C (bottom) — ground ($0$ V)
- Node B (left output, between $R_1$ and $R_2$)
- Node D (right output, between $R_3$ and $R_4$)

**Step 1 — Write the voltage divider at Node B:**

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + \Delta R + R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

**Step 2 — Write the voltage divider at Node D:**

$$V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the differential output voltage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_B - V_D \\
&= V_{ex} \left( \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right) \\
&= V_{ex} \left( \frac{2R - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \right) \\
&= V_{ex} \left( \frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-signal approximation** ($\Delta R \ll R$, so $\Delta R$ in the denominator is negligible):

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{-\Delta R}{4R} = -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the gauge factor definition** $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx -\frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}$$

The **negative sign** simply indicates the polarity convention — in practice, swapping the active and fixed arm positions inverts the sign, so most textbooks present the magnitude as:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (Board-Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A metal foil strain gauge with $R = 350 \ \Omega$ and $GF = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel cantilever beam. The beam experiences a strain of $1000 \ \mu\varepsilon$ (i.e., $\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6}$). The gauge is connected in a **quarter-bridge** circuit with $V_{ex} = 10$ V. Calculate:
> (a) The fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$
> (b) The absolute resistance change $\Delta R$ in $\Omega$
> (c) The output voltage $V_{out}$ in mV
> (d) The percentage error if we used the *un-approximated* equation.

**Solution:**

**(a) Fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.0021 \ \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

**(b) Absolute resistance change:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 350 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R = 0.735 \ \Omega}$$

**(c) Output voltage using approximation:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{10 \times 2.1 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 5.25 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V} = 5.25 \ \text{mV}}$$

**(d) Percentage error — exact vs approximate:**

The exact expression (from Step 3 of the derivation) is:

$$V_{out, exact} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = 10 \times \frac{0.735}{2(700 + 0.735)}$$

$$V_{out, exact} = 10 \times \frac{0.735}{1401.47} = 5.2453 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V}$$

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{V_{out, approx} - V_{out, exact}}{V_{out, exact}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{5.25 - 5.2453}{5.2453} \times 100 \approx -0.089 \ \%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Error} \approx 0.09 \ \% \ \text{(negligible — approximation is justified)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** When $\Delta R / R < 0.01$ (i.e., strain < $5000 \ \mu\varepsilon$ for metal gauges), the linear approximation holds with less than 0.5% error. **Always state this assumption in the exam!**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bridge Configurations

| Parameter | Quarter Bridge | Half Bridge | Full Bridge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Active gauges | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Sensitivity factor | $1/4$ | $1/2$ | $1$ |
| Output $V_{out}$ | $V_{ex} GF \varepsilon / 4$ | $V_{ex} GF \varepsilon / 2$ | $V_{ex} GF \varepsilon$ |
| Temperature compensation | Poor | Good (adjacent arms) | Excellent |
| Non-linearity | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Typical use | Lab demos, basic sensing | Bending beams, axial with temp comp | Industrial load cells, torque |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Strain-Gauge CPS

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **complete signal chain** from physical deformation to a digital CPS decision. This is the "Cyber-Physical" essence of the course — the physical deformation in the bridge is sensed, converted, and used for control.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Load: Force, Torque, Pressure] --> B[Elastic Element: Cantilever Beam, Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Strain Gauge Grid: Foil or Semiconductor]
    C --> D[Wheatstone Bridge Network]
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier: e.g. INA128]
    E --> F[ADC: Analog to Digital Converter]
    F --> G[Microcontroller: ARM Cortex / Arduino]
    G --> H{CPS Decision Logic}
    H --> I1[Display: LCD / HMI]
    H --> I2[Actuator Command: Motor / Valve]
    H --> I3[Wireless Tx: MQTT / LoRa]
    style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#990000
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
    style C fill:#d9f2d9,stroke:#006600
    style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style E fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#330099
    style F fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
    style G fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300
    style H fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300,color:#ffffff
    style I1 fill:#d0d0d0,stroke:#333333
    style I2 fill:#d0d0d0,stroke:#333333
    style I3 fill:#d0d0d0,stroke:#333333
```

---

### 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Circuit Schematic (Full-Bridge Topology)

> [!NOTE]
> A **full-bridge** configuration with four active strain gauges. All four gauges $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ experience strain when the beam bends — two in tension, two in compression. This produces the maximum output and inherently compensates for temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VexNode[V_ex: Excitation Voltage]:::supply
    GndNode[GND: 0 V Reference]:::supply
    A((Node A)):::top
    C((Node C)):::bottom
    B((Node B: V+ Output)):::outpos
    D((Node D: V- Output)):::outneg

    R1[R1: Gauge on TOP of beam]:::gauge
    R2[R2: Gauge on BOTTOM of beam]:::gauge
    R3[R3: Gauge on TOP of beam]:::gauge
    R4[R4: Gauge on BOTTOM of beam]:::gauge

    VexNode --- A
    C --- GndNode
    A --- R1
    R1 --- B
    B --- R2
    R2 --- C
    A --- R3
    R3 --- D
    D --- R4
    R4 --- C

    classDef supply fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#996600,color:#000000
    classDef top fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#990000
    classDef bottom fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#000099
    classDef outpos fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    classDef outneg fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    classDef gauge fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

---

### 4.3 Cantilever Beam with Bonded Strain Gauges — Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Fixed Wall: Rigid Clamp]:::fixed
    M[Tip Mass M: Applied Load F]:::load
    B[Elastic Cantilever Beam]:::beam
    G1[Gauge 1: TOP surface, Tension +epsilon]:::gaugeT
    G2[Gauge 2: BOTTOM surface, Compression -epsilon]:::gaugeC
    G3[Gauge 3: TOP, +epsilon]:::gaugeT
    G4[Gauge 4: BOTTOM, -epsilon]:::gaugeC

    W --- B
    B --- M
    B -. embeds .-> G1
    B -. embeds .-> G2
    B -. embeds .-> G3
    B -. embeds .-> G4
    G1 --- G2
    G3 --- G4

    classDef fixed fill:#a0a0a0,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef load fill:#ff8080,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef beam fill:#d9b380,stroke:#663300,color:#000000
    classDef gaugeT fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    classDef gaugeC fill:#ccccff,stroke:#0000cc,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Gauges 1 and 3** sit on the upper surface of the cantilever — they **stretch** (tension) when the beam bends down → $\varepsilon > 0$.
> - **Gauges 2 and 4** sit on the lower surface — they **compress** → $\varepsilon < 0$.
> - When wired into a full-bridge with alternating arms, both signals **add constructively** at the output, doubling the full-bridge sensitivity.

---

### 4.4 Signal-Conditioning Block Diagram (Practical CPS Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SG[Strain Gauge: 350 ohm] --> WB[Wheatstone Bridge]
    WB --> AMP[INA128 Instrumentation Amplifier: Gain 100]
    AMP --> LPF[2nd Order Low-Pass Filter: fc = 1 kHz]
    LPF --> ADC[16-bit ADC: ADS1115]
    ADC --> MCU[STM32 / ESP32 Microcontroller]
    MCU --> DISP[OLED Display: Strain in microstrain]
    MCU --> NET[Wi-Fi / MQTT Publisher]
    NET --> CLOUD[Cloud Dashboard: Grafana / AWS IoT]

    style SG fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#990000

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. Why is the gauge factor of a semiconductor strain gauge much higher than that of a metallic strain gauge?**

**Model Answer:**
The Gauge Factor ($GF$) of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of fractional change in electrical resistance to the mechanical strain producing it:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

In metallic gauges, only the **geometric term** $(1+2\nu)$ contributes, giving $GF \approx 2.0$.
In semiconductor gauges, the **piezoresistive term** $\frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ is dominant (because the band structure of silicon is highly sensitive to lattice deformation), giving $GF \approx 100$ to $200$ — roughly 50–100 times higher than metallic gauges.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three advantages of a full-bridge configuration over a quarter-bridge configuration in strain gauge measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Four times higher sensitivity** — output voltage is $V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (full) vs. $V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$ (quarter).
2. **Built-in temperature compensation** — all four gauges experience identical thermal expansion, cancelling thermal drift in the differential output.
3. **Inherent non-linearity cancellation** — symmetric bending beam arrangement produces $\pm \varepsilon$ pairs, doubling the effective output and linearizing the response.
4. (Bonus) **Higher signal-to-noise ratio** for the same excitation voltage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. There is an **internal choice** — students answer either **Question A or Question B** in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a strain gauge starting from the fundamental resistance equation $R = \rho L / A$. Explain each term physically.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit uses a single active gauge with $R = 120 \ \Omega$, $GF = 2.05$, and $V_{ex} = 5 \ \text{V}$. The gauge is bonded to a steel cantilever (Young's modulus $E = 200 \ \text{GPa}$) of dimensions $200 \ \text{mm} \times 20 \ \text{mm} \times 5 \ \text{mm}$. A tip load produces a maximum bending stress of $120 \ \text{MPa}$. Calculate:
(i) The strain at the surface where the gauge is bonded.
(ii) The fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$.
(iii) The output voltage of the bridge in mV.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation:** [Refer to Section 2.2 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation]

Starting from $R = \rho L / A$, take the logarithmic differential:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

By Poisson's ratio, $\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L}$. Substituting and dividing by strain $\varepsilon = dL/L$:

$$\boxed{GF = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{(d\rho/\rho)}{\varepsilon}}$$

- $1$ → from length elongation
- $2\nu$ → from area contraction (Poisson effect)
- $\frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$ → piezoresistive effect (atomic-level)

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Strain from stress (Hooke's Law):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 6 \times 10^{-4} = 600 \ \mu\varepsilon$$

**(ii) Fractional resistance change:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.05 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.23 \times 10^{-3}$$

**(iii) Quarter-bridge output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{5 \times 1.23 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.5375 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 1.54 \ \text{mV}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Wheatstone bridge** in strain gauge measurement. Compare quarter, half, and full-bridge configurations with respect to sensitivity and temperature compensation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A load cell uses four identical strain gauges ($R = 350 \ \Omega$, $GF = 2.0$) in a full-bridge configuration. The cell is designed to measure a maximum force of $50 \ \text{kN}$. The cylindrical load-sensing element has diameter $30 \ \text{mm}$ and Young's modulus $E = 210 \ \text{GPa}$. The excitation voltage is $10 \ \text{V}$. Determine:
(i) The maximum axial stress in the element.
(ii) The corresponding strain.
(iii) The full-bridge output voltage at full load.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a)** [Refer to Section 2.4 and Section 4.2 diagrams of these notes for the Wheatstone bridge explanation and configurations comparison table]

Key points expected:
- Diagram with four arms $R_1$ to $R_4$ and nodes A, B, C, D.
- The balance condition $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ and the output equation.
- Sensitivity comparison: Quarter $1/4$, Half $1/2$, Full $1$.
- Temperature compensation: Full-bridge best, half-bridge (adjacent arms) good, quarter-bridge poor.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum axial stress (using cross-sectional area):**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 70.736 \ \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Strain using Hooke's Law:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{70.736 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9} = 3.368 \times 10^{-4} \approx 337 \ \mu\varepsilon$$

**(iii) Full-bridge output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = 10 \times 2.0 \times 3.368 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 6.736 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V} \approx 6.74 \ \text{mV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks here!):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in stress calculation:** Students often forget to convert mm → m, leading to errors of 6 orders of magnitude. Always write units in **every step**.
> 2. **Confusing "fractional" vs "absolute" $\Delta R$:** $\Delta R / R$ is dimensionless, but $\Delta R$ is in Ohms. Examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 3. **Forgetting the small-signal approximation validity check:** When strain exceeds $5000 \ \mu\varepsilon$ for a metal gauge, the quarter-bridge approximation breaks down; you must use the exact expression. **Always state the assumption.**
> 4. **Sign convention confusion:** A compressive strain produces a *negative* $\Delta R$ and *negative* $V_{out}$. State the polarity explicitly.
> 5. **Gauge factor units:** $GF$ is **dimensionless** — never write "$\Omega/\Omega$" or "V/V" for gauge factor.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Strain gauge = passive resistive transducer** converting strain → $\Delta R$.
- [x] **Gauge Factor** is dimensionless; $GF = 1 + 2\nu + (d\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Metallic gauges:** $GF \approx 2$; **Semiconductor gauges:** $GF \approx 100$–$200$.
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge** is the standard signal conditioner for strain gauges.
- [x] **Quarter bridge:** $V_{out}/V_{ex} = GF\varepsilon/4$; **Half bridge:** $GF\varepsilon/2$; **Full bridge:** $GF\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Small-signal approximation** $\Delta R \ll R$ is valid for strain $< 5000 \ \mu\varepsilon$ in metal gauges.
- [x] **Hooke's Law** $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ is the bridge between mechanical and electrical domains.
- [x] **Full-bridge advantages:** maximum sensitivity, inherent temperature compensation, best linearity.
- [x] **Standard gauge resistance values:** **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 1000 Ω** — know these!
- [x] **Piezoresistive effect** dominates semiconductors; **geometric effect** dominates metals.
- [x] **Poisson's ratio** for constantan $\approx 0.3$–$0.33$.
- [x] **CPS relevance:** strain gauge → signal conditioning → ADC → controller → actuator feedback (closed-loop compliance).
- [x] **Common applications:** load cells, pressure sensors, torque sensors, structural-health monitoring, force-feedback robotics.
- [x] **Temperature compensation** is achieved by using a *dummy gauge* on a non-strained arm (quarter bridge) or by the differential bridge symmetry (half/full bridge).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When the question says "design a strain gauge circuit to measure…", **always**:
> 1. State the gauge material and the $GF$ you will use.
> 2. Choose the bridge configuration with justification (full > half > quarter for accuracy).
> 3. Compute $\Delta R / R$ using $GF \cdot \varepsilon$.
> 4. Compute $V_{out}$ using the correct bridge equation.
> 5. Mention that an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128) is used to amplify the millivolt signal for ADC input.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Principles of Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Principles of Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **actuator** is an electromechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, or photonic device that converts a control signal (electrical, fluid, or thermal energy) into a physical mechanical action — typically linear displacement, angular rotation, force, or torque — to produce motion or effect a change in a physical system. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, actuators serve as the physical output counterpart to sensors, executing the decisions made by the computational core on the real-world environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCPT205 – Module 4):**
> Actuators are energy-conversion devices that transform input energy (electrical, fluidic, thermal, chemical) into mechanical output. They form the *actuation layer* of cyber-physical systems, completing the **Sense → Compute → Actuate** triad.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Muscle of a Machine" Analogy**
> Imagine the human body. Your *senses* (eyes, ears, skin) are the **sensors** — they gather information about the world. Your *brain* is the **controller/computer** — it processes that information. Your *muscles* are the **actuators** — they receive the brain's electrical signals and actually *move your body*.
> In the same way, actuators are the "muscles" of every machine: a robotic arm that welds a car chassis, an aircraft flap that adjusts during flight, a valve that opens to release water — all of these are powered by actuators obeying commands from a controller.

---

### 1.3 Primary Classification of Actuators

Actuators are broadly classified by their **input energy domain**:

| Energy Domain | Actuator Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical | Solenoid, DC/Stepper/Servo motor, Voice coil | Robotics, CNC, valves |
| Hydraulic | Hydraulic cylinder, Hydraulic motor | Heavy machinery, presses |
| Pneumatic | Pneumatic cylinder, Air motor | Pick-and-place, packaging |
| Thermal | Shape Memory Alloy (SMA), Thermal bimetal | Smart structures, thermostats |
| Piezoelectric | PZT stack actuator, Piezo motor | Precision positioning, inkjet |
| Magnetic | Voice coil, Linear solenoid | Loudspeakers, haptics |
| Chemical/Rocket | Thrusters, Combustion engine | Aerospace, propulsion |

---

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Five" Actuator Performance Metrics every KTU student must memorize:**
> 1. **Stroke Length** (m or mm) — Maximum linear displacement.
> 2. **Force / Thrust Output** (N) — Mechanical force produced.
> 3. **Torque** (N·m) — Rotational capability.
> 4. **Response Time / Bandwidth** (Hz or ms) — Speed of actuation.
> 5. **Power Density** (W/kg or W/cm³) — Power delivered per unit mass/volume.

---

### 1.5 Energy Conversion Block Diagram (Conceptual)

```
[Input Energy] ───► [Conversion Mechanism] ───► [Mechanical Output]
(Electrical,         (Electromagnetic,              (Force, Torque,
Fluidic, Thermal,    Fluidic, Thermal               Linear/Rotary
Chemical)            Expansion, etc.)               Motion)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Actuators Matter in Cyber-Physical Systems:**
> Without actuators, the "physical" part of "Cyber-Physical" cannot be influenced. Actuators *close the loop* — they turn digital decisions into real-world consequences.
