## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principles — Actuator-by-Actuator Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Pneumatic Actuators
**Principle:** Compressed air pushes a piston inside a cylinder; the pressure differential across the piston face generates a net force.

**Working Steps (Logic Flow):**
1. Compressed air (typically **6 bar ≈ 600 kPa**) enters the cylinder chamber.
2. Pressure acts uniformly on the piston area.
3. The resulting force pushes the piston along the cylinder axis.
4. Air on the opposite side is exhausted through a valve.
5. Direction reversal is achieved by a **5/2-way or 5/3-way directional control valve**.

**Why this works:** Pascal's Law — pressure applied to an enclosed fluid (air) is transmitted undiminished in all directions.

---

#### 2.1.2 Hydraulic Actuators
**Principle:** An incompressible fluid (hydraulic oil) is pressurized by a pump; this pressurized fluid drives a piston or a hydraulic motor.

**Why hydraulic over pneumatic?**
- Oil is **incompressible** → higher stiffness and precision.
- Operating pressures of **70–350 bar (7–35 MPa)** → far higher force density.

**Working Steps:**
1. Electric motor drives a hydraulic pump.
2. Pump pressurizes the hydraulic fluid.
3. Pressurized fluid is routed through control valves to a cylinder/motor.
4. Force is generated on the piston/motor shaft.
5. Return fluid passes through a filter and cooler back to the reservoir.

---

#### 2.1.3 Electromagnetic Actuators (Solenoid)
**Principle:** Current through a coil produces a magnetic field that attracts or repels a ferromagnetic plunger.

**Why it works:** The magnetic force scales with the square of the flux density, which itself depends on the **magnetomotive force (MMF) = N·I**.

**Working Steps:**
1. Control circuit applies voltage across the solenoid coil.
2. Current builds up, producing axial magnetic flux.
3. The flux pulls the plunger toward the air gap, doing mechanical work.
4. When current is removed, a return spring restores the plunger.

---

#### 2.1.4 DC Motor / Servo Motor / Stepper Motor
**Principle (DC Motor):** A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$, producing torque on the rotor.

**Principle (Stepper Motor):** Electromagnetic phases are energized in sequence; each energization rotates the rotor by a fixed *step angle* (commonly **1.8°** for a 200-step motor).

**Principle (Servo Motor):** Closed-loop DC motor with encoder feedback; the controller adjusts voltage to track a desired position/velocity.

---

#### 2.1.5 Piezoelectric Actuators
**Principle:** Inverse piezoelectric effect — applying voltage across a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) causes mechanical strain.

**Working Steps:**
1. A control voltage (typically **0–200 V**, sometimes **±1000 V**) is applied across electrodes on the piezoelectric stack.
2. The crystal's lattice structure deforms along the poling axis.
3. Strain (ΔL/L) is generated — typically on the order of **0.1% to 0.2%**.
4. Stacked configurations amplify stroke (e.g., 100 layers of 1 mm = 100 mm × strain).

---

#### 2.1.6 Thermal Actuators (Shape Memory Alloy — SMA)
**Principle:** Certain alloys (e.g., **Nitinol** — Nickel Titanium) "remember" a parent shape and return to it upon heating above a transformation temperature.

**Working Steps:**
1. SMA wire is deformed at low temperature (martensite phase).
2. Current is passed through the wire → Joule heating raises temperature.
3. Upon crossing the *transformation temperature* (~**70 °C for Nitinol**), the crystal reverts to austenite phase.
4. Wire contracts, producing a tensile force (up to **~500 MPa** stress).

---

#### 2.1.7 Voice Coil Actuator
**Principle:** A coil suspended in a permanent magnetic field moves axially when current flows; Lorentz force drives it.
- This is the same principle as a loudspeaker.
- Used in hard-disk drive head positioning and precision linear stages.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **the single most high-yield reference** for KTU 2024 numerical problems on actuators.

| # | Actuator Type | Governing Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pneumatic Cylinder | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = Force (N), $P$ = Pressure (Pa), $A$ = Piston area (m²) |
| 2 | Hydraulic Cylinder | $F = P \times A \times \eta_m$ | $\eta_m$ = Mechanical efficiency |
| 3 | DC Motor Torque | $T = K_t \times I$ | $T$ = Torque (N·m), $K_t$ = Torque constant (N·m/A), $I$ = Current (A) |
| 4 | DC Motor Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \times \omega$ | $K_e$ = Back-EMF constant (V·s/rad), $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 5 | Solenoid Force (approx.) | $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | $B$ = Flux density (T), $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| 6 | Magnetomotive Force | $\text{MMF} = N \times I$ | $N$ = Turns, $I$ = Current (A), unit: Ampere-turns |
| 7 | Piezoelectric Strain | $\Delta L = d \times V \times n$ | $d$ = Piezo strain coeff. (m/V), $n$ = No. of layers |
| 8 | SMA Wire Contraction | $\Delta L = \alpha_{\text{eff}} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha_{\text{eff}}$ = effective SMA strain (typ. 0.04–0.08) |
| 9 | Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = Coefficient of linear expansion (1/K) |
| 10 | Power Density | $P_d = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{m}$ or $\dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{m}$ | $v$ = velocity (m/s), $m$ = mass (kg), $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 11 | Hydraulic Pump Power | $P = Q \times \Delta P$ | $Q$ = Flow rate (m³/s), $\Delta P$ = Pressure differential (Pa) |
| 12 | Stepper Motor Resolution | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s}$ | $N_s$ = Steps per revolution |
| 13 | Lorentz Force | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Used in voice coil / DC motor conductor force |
| 14 | Pneumatic Cylinder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $Q$ = Air flow rate (m³/s) |
| 15 | Spring Return Force | $F_s = k \cdot x$ | $k$ = Spring constant (N/m), $x$ = Deflection (m) |

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Where you will see these actuators in industry (often asked in KTU viva):**
> - **Pneumatic cylinders** — Food packaging lines, automotive assembly (welding robots).
> - **Hydraulic cylinders** — Excavators, aircraft landing gear, injection molding.
> - **Servo motors** — Robotic arms (e.g., KUKA, ABB), CNC machines.

> - **Voice coils** — HDD head positioning, linear stages.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Produced by a Pneumatic Cylinder

**Problem Statement:** A pneumatic cylinder has a piston diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ and is supplied with air at gauge pressure $P = 6 \text{ bar}$. Compute the theoretical (ideal) thrust force.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The piston area is calculated first:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Substituting $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{0.00785398}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

The thrust force (Pascal's Law applied to piston face):

$$
F = P \times A
$$

Convert pressure: $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, so $P = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

$$
F = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F = 1178.1 \text{ N}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{F \approx 1178 \text{ N} \approx 1.18 \text{ kN}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic check:** A 5-cm diameter piston at 6 bar ≈ **force of a moderately strong person pushing**. This is why pneumatic systems need *larger diameters* or *higher pressures* for heavy loads.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Cylinder Force With Load Pressure and Friction

**Problem Statement:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, rod diameter $d = 40 \text{ mm}$, supply pressure $P_s = 100 \text{ bar}$, back pressure $P_b = 5 \text{ bar}$, and mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$. Calculate the effective thrust force during extension.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Piston-side (full bore) area:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Rod-side (annular) area:

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080^2 - 0.040^2)}{4}
$$

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi \times (0.0064 - 0.0016)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0048}{4} = 3.7699 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Net pressure during extension:

$$
\Delta P = P_s - P_b = (100 - 5) \text{ bar} = 95 \text{ bar} = 9.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

Theoretical force:

$$
F_{\text{theo}} = \Delta P \times A_p = 9.5 \times 10^6 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{\text{theo}} = 47{,}751.75 \text{ N} \approx 47.75 \text{ kN}
$$

Effective force accounting for mechanical losses:

$$
F_{\text{eff}} = F_{\text{theo}} \times \eta_m = 47.75 \times 0.90
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{eff}} \approx 42.98 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: DC Motor Torque and Speed Characteristics

**Problem Statement:** A DC servomotor has torque constant $K_t = 0.05 \text{ N·m/A}$ and back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$. The armature resistance is $R_a = 1.0 \ \Omega$. With an applied voltage $V = 24 \text{ V}$, determine: (a) the no-load speed, (b) the stall torque.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Part (a): No-load speed**
At no load, armature current $I_a \to 0$, so back-EMF $E_b \approx V$.

From $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_0$:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{24}{0.05}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_0 = 480 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

Converting to RPM:

$$
N_0 = \omega_0 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 480 \times 9.5493
$$

$$
\boxed{N_0 \approx 4584 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**Part (b): Stall torque**
At stall, $\omega = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0$, so all voltage drops across $R_a$.

$$
I_{\text{stall}} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{24}{1.0} = 24 \text{ A}
$$

$$
T_{\text{stall}} = K_t \times I_{\text{stall}} = 0.05 \times 24
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{stall}} = 1.2 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Tangent line (linear speed-torque relationship):**

$$
T(\omega) = T_{\text{stall}} \left(1 - \frac{\omega}{\omega_0}\right)
$$

This is the classic linear "torque-speed curve" of a separately-excited or permanent-magnet DC motor — **a guaranteed 14-mark question every KTU exam cycle.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Piezoelectric Stack Actuator Stroke

**Problem Statement:** A PZT stack actuator has 200 layers, each of thickness $t = 100 \ \mu\text{m}$, with piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m/V}$. Driving voltage $V = 150 \text{ V}$. Compute the free stroke.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Strain per layer:

$$
\epsilon = d_{33} \times \frac{V}{t}
$$

Strain per layer in numbers:

$$
\epsilon = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times \frac{150}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\epsilon = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Stroke per layer:

$$
\Delta L_{\text{layer}} = \epsilon \cdot t = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 60 \text{ nm}
$$

Total stack stroke (200 layers in series mechanically):

$$
\Delta L_{\text{total}} = n \times d_{33} \times V
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{total}} = 200 \times 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 150
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{total}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L_{\text{total}} = 12 \ \mu\text{m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A single piezo layer moves only nanometres. Stacking *n* layers multiplies stroke linearly — this is the standard technique to achieve usable displacements (~tens of µm) for nanopositioning stages.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Stepper Motor Resolution and Pulse Frequency

**Problem Statement:** A hybrid stepper motor has 200 steps/revolution. It is driven by a controller with pulse frequency $f_p = 1000 \text{ Hz}$. Find: (a) step angle, (b) shaft speed in RPM.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step angle:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s} = \frac{360°}{200}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_s = 1.8°}
$$

Shaft speed (one step per pulse):

$$
n_{\text{rev/s}} = \frac{f_p}{N_s} = \frac{1000}{200} = 5 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:

$$
N_{\text{RPM}} = 5 \times 60
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{RPM}} = 300 \text{ RPM}}
$$

---

### 3.6 Comparative Performance Matrix (Master Comparison Table)

| Property | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Solenoid | DC/Servo | Piezo | SMA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stroke | High (mm–m) | High | Short (mm) | Continuous | Very short (µm) | Medium |
| Force | Medium | Very high | Low | Variable | High (block force) | High |
| Speed | Fast | Medium | Fast | Variable | Very fast | Slow (thermal lag) |
| Precision | Low | Medium | Low | High | Ultra-high | Low |
| Stiffness | Low | High | — | — | Very high | Low |
| Power density | Medium | High | Low | High | Very high | Medium |
| Control complexity | Simple | Medium | Simple | Medium–High | High | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Actuator in Cyber-Physical System Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Environment] --> B[Sensors]
    B --> C[Controller / Microprocessor]
    C --> D[Driver / Power Amplifier]
    D --> E[Actuator]
    E --> F[Mechanical Output Motion]
    F --> A
    G[User / Network Command] --> C
```

**Explanation of the loop:**
- The **physical environment** is sensed by the **sensor** block.
- The **controller** (PLC, microcontroller, or industrial PC) computes the required actuation.
- The **driver** converts the low-power logic signal into a high-power signal suitable for the actuator.
- The **actuator** produces **mechanical output** that physically changes the environment, closing the loop.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Pneumatic Cylinder with 5/2 DCV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressed Air Supply] --> B[Filter Regulator Lubricator FRL]
    B --> C[5 by 2 Directional Control Valve]
    C --> D[Pneumatic Cylinder Chamber A]
    C --> E[Pneumatic Cylinder Chamber B]
    D --> F[Piston and Rod]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Mechanical Load]
    H[Solenoid Coil of DCV] -.controls.-> C
    I[Controller PLC] --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Air is conditioned by the **FRL** block (Filter, Regulator, Lubricator).
- The **5/2 DCV** (Directional Control Valve) routes air to either side of the piston.
- Energizing/de-energizing the **solenoid coil** swaps the air paths, causing the piston to extend or retract.
- The **controller (PLC)** drives the solenoid based on logic.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — DC Servo Motor Position Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Position Command] --> B[Summing Junction]
    B --> C[PID Controller]
    C --> D[PWM Driver Power Amplifier]
    D --> E[DC Servo Motor]
    E --> F[Mechanical Load and Gearbox]
    F --> G[Output Position]
    G --> H[Encoder Feedback]
    H --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **summing junction** compares reference and feedback.
- **PID** computes the corrective control effort.
- The **PWM driver** modulates voltage to the motor.
- The **encoder** provides real-time position feedback — this *closes the loop*.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Piezoelectric Stack Drive Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Input Supply] --> B[HV DC DC Converter]
    B --> C[Opamp Driver Stage]
    C --> D[PZT Stack Actuator]
    D --> E[Nanopositioning Stage]
    E --> F[Capacitive Position Sensor]
    F --> G[Feedback to Opamp Driver]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- High-voltage supply (HV DC-DC) provides the actuation voltage.
- Opamp stage delivers bi-polar drive capability.
- The PZT stack deflects the nanopositioning stage.
- A **capacitive sensor** measures displacement and feeds back to the opamp.

---

### 4.5 Classification Hierarchy of Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actuators] --> B[Electrical]
    A --> C[Fluid Power]
    A --> D[Thermal]
    A --> E[Smart Material]
    B --> B1[Solenoid]
    B --> B2[DC Motor]
    B --> B3[Stepper Motor]
    B --> B4[Servo Motor]
    B --> B5[Voice Coil]
    C --> C1[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    C --> C2[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    C --> C3[Pneumatic Motor]
    C --> C4[Hydraulic Motor]
    D --> D1[SMA Wire]
    D --> D2[Thermal Bimetal]
    D --> D3[Thermal Expansion Actuator]
    E --> E1[Piezoelectric]
    E --> E2[Magnetostrictive]
    E --> E3[Electroactive Polymer EAP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a classification tree matters for KTU:** A common 7-mark question is *"Classify actuators with examples."* This tree gives you a complete, examiner-friendly answer.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define an actuator. List any four types of actuators with one application of each.**
*[KTU University Exam — June 2023 (Model)]*

**Model Answer:**
An actuator is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, fluidic, thermal, etc.) into a mechanical output — typically motion, force, or torque — to perform work on a physical system.

| Type | Application |
|---|---|
| DC Servo Motor | Robotic arm joint positioning |
| Pneumatic Cylinder | Pick-and-place in packaging lines |
| Piezoelectric Stack | Atomic Force Microscope nanopositioning |
| Hydraulic Cylinder | Excavator arm actuation |

*(3 marks)*

---

#### **Q2. State and explain Pascal's Law as applied to pneumatic actuators.**
*[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**Model Answer:**
**Pascal's Law:** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid (or gas) is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surface of the container."*

In a pneumatic cylinder, compressed air admitted into the chamber exerts uniform pressure on the piston face. The resulting thrust force is:

$$
F = P \times A
$$

where $P$ is the supply pressure and $A$ is the piston area. This principle enables pneumatic actuators to deliver linear force proportional to the regulated air pressure.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **hydraulic actuator** with a neat block diagram. Compare its performance with a pneumatic actuator on the basis of stroke, force, precision, and stiffness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100 \text{ mm}$, rod diameter $d = 50 \text{ mm}$, and is supplied at $P_s = 200 \text{ bar}$. The back pressure is $P_b = 10 \text{ bar}$ and the mechanical efficiency is $\eta_m = 0.85$. Compute the effective thrust force during extension and the retraction force. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Working Principle of a Hydraulic Actuator (5 marks for explanation + diagram):**

A hydraulic actuator converts pressurized hydraulic fluid (typically oil) into linear or rotary mechanical motion. The major components are:
1. **Reservoir** — stores hydraulic oil.
2. **Pump** — driven by an electric motor, pressurizes the fluid.
3. **Pressure regulator / relief valve** — sets system pressure.
4. **Directional control valve (DCV)** — routes fluid to actuator ports.
5. **Actuator (cylinder or motor)** — converts fluid energy to mechanical motion.
6. **Return line with filter and cooler** — completes the closed-loop circuit.

**Working Steps:**
- The electric motor drives the pump, drawing oil from the reservoir.
- The pump builds up high pressure (typically **70–350 bar**).
- The pressure-regulated oil is routed through a DCV.
- When oil enters the *blank-end* (full-bore) side of the cylinder, the piston extends.
- Return oil from the rod-end passes through a filter and cooler back to the tank.
- Direction reversal is achieved by shifting the DCV.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid | Compressible air | Incompressible oil |
| Operating pressure | 4–8 bar | 70–350 bar |
| Force density | Low | Very high |
| Precision | Low (air compressibility) | High (oil stiffness) |
| Stiffness | Low | High |
| Stroke | Long (up to several meters) | Long |
| Speed | Very high | Medium |
| Cost | Low | Higher |

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 (similar pneumatic diagram applies; replace air with oil and add pump reservoir).

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$, $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$, $P_s = 200 \text{ bar}$, $P_b = 10 \text{ bar}$, $\eta_m = 0.85$.

**Step 1: Compute piston and annular areas.**

Piston (full bore) area:

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Annular (rod-side) area:

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.100^2 - 0.050^2)}{4}
$$

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi \times (0.0100 - 0.0025)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0075}{4} = 5.890 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Extension thrust force.**

Net pressure during extension: $\Delta P_e = P_s - P_b = 190 \text{ bar} = 1.9 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$.

Theoretical extension force:

$$
F_{\text{ext,theo}} = \Delta P_e \times A_p = 1.9 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{\text{ext,theo}} = 149{,}226 \text{ N} \approx 149.23 \text{ kN}
$$

Effective extension force:

$$
F_{\text{ext}} = F_{\text{ext,theo}} \times \eta_m = 149.23 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{ext}} \approx 126.84 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3: Retraction force.**

During retraction, supply pressure acts on the rod-side (annular) area, and back pressure acts on the full-bore side.

Theoretical retraction force:

$$
F_{\text{ret,theo}} = P_s \times A_r - P_b \times A_p
$$

$$
F_{\text{ret,theo}} = 200 \times 10^5 \times 5.890 \times 10^{-3} - 10 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F_{\text{ret,theo}} = 117{,}810 - 7{,}854 = 109{,}956 \text{ N} \approx 109.96 \text{ kN}
$$

Effective retraction force:

$$
F_{\text{ret}} = 109.96 \times 0.85
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{ret}} \approx 93.46 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Extension force: $\approx 126.84 \text{ kN}$
- Retraction force: $\approx 93.46 \text{ kN}$

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model)]*

**(a)** Explain the **inverse piezoelectric effect** and describe the construction and working of a **piezoelectric stack actuator**. Mention two applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PZT stack has $n = 150$ layers, each of thickness $t = 80 \ \mu\text{m}$, with $d_{33} = 380 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m/V}$. The applied voltage is $V = 200 \text{ V}$. Compute (i) stroke per layer, (ii) total free stroke, and (iii) blocking force if the stack cross-section is $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2$ and Young's modulus $Y = 60 \text{ GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Inverse Piezoelectric Effect (3 marks):**
The *direct* piezoelectric effect, discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880, is the generation of electric charge in certain crystals when subjected to mechanical stress. The *inverse* (or *converse*) piezoelectric effect is the converse phenomenon: when an electric field is applied across a suitably poled piezoelectric crystal, the crystal undergoes a small mechanical deformation (strain) proportional to the applied field. Mathematically:

$$
\epsilon = d \cdot E = d \cdot \frac{V}{t}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the induced strain, $d$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (m/V), $E$ is the electric field (V/m), $V$ is voltage, and $t$ is layer thickness. Common piezoelectric materials: PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), quartz, PVDF.

**Construction of PZT Stack Actuator (2 marks):**
- A PZT stack consists of many thin piezoelectric disks/plates (typically 50–500 layers), each with electrodes on top and bottom.
- The layers are mechanically in series (stacked) and electrically in parallel.
- Each layer is polarized in the same direction during manufacturing.
- Outer electrodes are connected to a high-voltage driver.

**Working (1 mark):**
- A control voltage (typically 0–200 V, sometimes up to ±1000 V) is applied across the stack electrodes.
- Each layer contracts or expands slightly along the poling direction.
- Total displacement = $n \times$ displacement of a single layer.

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) nanopositioning stages.
2. Inkjet printer print-head nozzles.
3. Precision adaptive optics in telescopes.
4. Diesel fuel injection valves (Siemens / Bosch piezo injectors).

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $n = 150$, $t = 80 \ \mu\text{m} = 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $d_{33} = 380 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m/V}$, $V = 200 \text{ V}$, $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $Y = 60 \text{ GPa} = 60 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$.

**(i) Stroke per layer:**

$$
\Delta L_{\text{layer}} = d_{33} \times V = 380 \times 10^{-12} \times 200
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{layer}} = 7.6 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 76 \text{ nm}
$$

**(ii) Total free stroke:**

$$
\Delta L_{\text{total}} = n \times d_{33} \times V = 150 \times 380 \times 10^{-12} \times 200
$$

$$
\Delta L_{\text{total}} = 1.14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L_{\text{total}} = 11.4 \ \mu\text{m}}
$$

**(iii) Blocking force:**

The blocking force is the force required to suppress the entire free stroke (i.e., reduce $\Delta L$ to zero). For a stack constrained between two rigid surfaces:

$$
F_{\text{block}} = \frac{\Delta L_{\text{total}} \cdot A \cdot Y}{n \cdot t}
$$

Alternatively, since the free stroke $= n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ and the stiffness of the stack $= \dfrac{n \cdot A \cdot Y}{n \cdot t} \cdot n = \dfrac{n^2 A Y}{n \cdot t}$ — the standard compact form used in literature is:

$$
F_{\text{block}} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot V \cdot A \cdot Y}{t}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_{\text{block}} = \frac{380 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 60 \times 10^9}{80 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
380 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 = 7.6 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
7.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.9 \times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
1.9 \times 10^{-12} \times 60 \times 10^9 = 1.14 \times 10^{-1} = 0.114
$$

Dividing by $80 \times 10^{-6}$:

$$
F_{\text{block}} = \frac{0.114}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.114}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1425 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{block}} \approx 1425 \text{ N} \approx 1.43 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Actuator Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert **bar to Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$). Always write units in your substitution step.
> 2. **Piston vs Annular Area Confusion (–3 marks):** During *extension*, supply pressure acts on the **full bore**; during *retraction*, it acts on the **annular area**. Mixing these up gives wrong forces by tens of kN.
> 3. **Ignoring Mechanical Efficiency $\eta_m$ (–2 marks):** If the problem mentions efficiency, multiply by it. If no efficiency is mentioned, assume $\eta = 1$.
> 4. **Piezoelectric Layer Confusion:** Each layer moves by $d \cdot V$. Stacking **multiplies stroke linearly** ($n$ layers → $n \times$ stroke). Students often incorrectly square or divide.
> 5. **DC Motor Sign Error:** The back-EMF *opposes* the applied voltage ($V = E_b + I_a R_a$), not adds to it.
> 6. **Hydraulic vs Pneumatic Mix-up:** Hydraulic pressures are typically 100+ bar; pneumatic is **4–8 bar**. Force scales accordingly by 10–50×.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Working Principles of Actuators" — KTU Module 4**

- **Definition:** Actuator = energy conversion device that turns control signals into mechanical motion/force.
- **Classification:** Electrical, Fluid Power (Pneumatic/Hydraulic), Thermal (SMA/Bimetal), Smart Material (Piezo/Magnetostrictive).
- **Pneumatic Cylinder Force:** $F = P \times A$ (Pascal's Law). Pressure = 4–8 bar typical.
- **Hydraulic Cylinder Force:** Same formula but pressures 70–350 bar → ~10× force density.
- **DC Motor Torque:** $T = K_t \cdot I$; back-EMF: $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$; stall torque $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot (V/R_a)$; no-load speed $\omega_0 = V / K_e$.
- **Stepper Motor:** Step angle $\theta_s = 360° / N_s$ (1.8° for 200-step motor).
- **Piezoelectric Stroke:** $\Delta L = n \cdot d \cdot V$. Blocking force $F_b = d \cdot V \cdot A \cdot Y / t$.
- **SMA (Nitinol):** Heats to ~70 °C to recover parent shape; max stress ≈ 500 MPa; slow response due to thermal lag.
- **Voice Coil:** Lorentz force $F = BIL$; same principle as a speaker.
- **Solenoid:** MMF $= N \cdot I$; force $\approx B^2 A / (2 \mu_0)$.
- **Key Trade-offs:** Stroke vs Force, Precision vs Speed, Stiffness vs Compliance.
- **CPS Context:** Actuators complete the Sense → Compute → Actuate loop; without them, "Physical" in CPS cannot be acted upon.
- **Top 14-mark Question Types:** (i) Hydraulic cylinder force calculation with extension/retraction; (ii) DC motor torque-speed analysis; (iii) Piezo stack stroke calculation; (iv) Comparative essay on actuator classes.
- **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Always convert **bar → Pa**; always multiply by $\eta_m$ when efficiency is given; always identify the *effective pressure area* (full bore vs annular).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric and Piezoresistive Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric and Piezoresistive Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Piezoelectric Effect — Formal Definition

The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy (**direct piezoelectric effect**, discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880) and, conversely, to convert electrical energy into mechanical strain or displacement (**converse/indirect piezoelectric effect**, theorized by Gabriel Lippmann in 1881 and confirmed by the Curies in the same year).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Core Definition**
> A material is *piezoelectric* if its crystal lattice lacks a centre of symmetry, allowing the centres of positive and negative charge to displace relative to each other under mechanical stress, producing a net surface charge density (direct effect) — and conversely, to deform when subjected to an external electric field (converse effect).

Mathematically, the direct effect states that the generated electric displacement $D$ is proportional to the applied mechanical stress $T$:

$$D = d \cdot T$$

And the converse effect states that the induced mechanical strain $S$ is proportional to the applied electric field $E$:

$$S = d_t \cdot E$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (Coulombs/Newton or meters/Volt) and $d_t$ is its transpose.

### 1.2 Piezoresistive Effect — Formal Definition

The **piezoresistive effect** is the phenomenon in which the electrical resistivity of a material changes when mechanical stress is applied to it. Unlike the piezoelectric effect, it does **not** require non-centrosymmetric crystals; it arises from stress-induced changes in the band structure of semiconductors (most notably silicon) and from geometrical changes in conductors.

The fractional change in resistance can be expressed as:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio, $\varepsilon$ is the longitudinal strain, and $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is the fractional change in resistivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Piezoresistive is Often a Sensor Mechanism**
> Strictly speaking, the piezoresistive effect is primarily a *transduction* phenomenon used for **sensing** (e.g., MEMS pressure sensors, accelerometers, strain gauges). In a "piezoresistive actuator" context, the piezoresistive element is integrated into an actuator feedback loop or used to monitor the strain state of a host piezoelectric actuator. KTU groups these two effects in the actuator module because they form the foundational smart-material actuation/sensing pair.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Piezoelectric — The Gas Lighter Analogy:**
Think of a **disposable gas lighter** (the click-type used in kitchens). When you press the button, a small hammer strikes a piezoelectric crystal. The mechanical shock generates a high voltage across the crystal (several kilovolts) that creates a spark — this is the *direct effect*. Now imagine the reverse: apply a controlled voltage across a piezoelectric stack, and it expands by a few micrometres, moving a mirror in a microscope, ejecting ink in a printer, or positioning an AFM tip with **nanometre precision** — this is the *converse effect*.

**Piezoresistive — The Stretchy Wire Analogy:**
Imagine a thin metallic wire fixed at both ends. If you stretch it, two things happen: (1) it gets longer and thinner (geometrical effect, like a rubber band with a wire's stiffness), and (2) its intrinsic resistivity changes because the crystal lattice is being distorted. A **strain gauge** exploits this exact principle. In silicon, the resistivity change is **100× larger** than in metals, which is why silicon piezoresistors dominate modern MEMS.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid for the Board Exam**
> - **Piezo** = *Pressure → Electricity* AND *Electricity → Deformation* (two-way coupling)
> - **Piezoresistive** = *Stress → Resistance change* (one-way, used for sensing)
> - Key physical constants to remember: $\varepsilon_{33}$ for PZT ≈ **1700**, $d_{33}$ for PZT-5A ≈ **374 pC/N**, Gage factor for silicon ≈ **50–200**, Gage factor for metal ≈ **2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Piezoelectric Constitutive Equations (IEEE Standard)

The complete electromechanical behaviour of a piezoelectric material is described by **four coupled linear constitutive equations** (assuming small-signal, isothermal operation). The two most common forms are the **stress-charge (e-form)** and **strain-charge (g-form)** representations.

**Stress-Charge Form (e-form):**

$$S_i = s^E_{ij} \, T_j + d_{ik} \, E_k$$

$$D_m = d_{mj} \, T_j + \varepsilon^T_{mk} \, E_k$$

**Strain-Charge Form (g-form):**

$$T_i = c^E_{ij} \, S_j - e_{ik} \, E_k$$

$$D_m = e_{mj} \, S_j + \varepsilon^S_{mk} \, E_k$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = mechanical strain (tensor, Voigt notation)
- $T_j$ = mechanical stress (N/m²)
- $E_k$ = electric field (V/m)
- $D_m$ = electric displacement (C/m²)
- $s^E_{ij}$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N)
- $d_{ik}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N or m/V)
- $\varepsilon^T_{mk}$ = permittivity at constant stress (F/m)
- $c^E_{ij}$ = elastic stiffness at constant E (N/m²)
- $e_{ik}$ = piezoelectric stress coefficient (C/m²)
- $\varepsilon^S_{mk}$ = permittivity at constant strain (F/m)

### 2.2 Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Why two equations are needed:**
Mechanical strain depends on BOTH stress AND electric field (because the converse effect couples them). Similarly, electric displacement depends on BOTH stress (direct effect) AND electric field (capacitive response). One equation cannot capture the full coupling.

**Step 2 — Why Voigt notation (single index):**
Stress and strain are second-rank tensors. Using Voigt notation ($i, j \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$), the symmetric 3×3 tensor is reduced to a 6-element vector, simplifying matrix algebra for KTU-style problems.

**Step 3 — Why superscripts E and T:**
The constants are measured under specific boundary conditions: $s^E$ means compliance at *constant E* (free to deform electrically), and $\varepsilon^T$ means permittivity at *constant T* (mechanically free). These are practical measurability constraints.

**Step 4 — The coupling matrix $[d]$:**
The piezoelectric coefficient matrix is what makes the material "smart." For poled ceramics like PZT, it has the form:

$$[d] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & d_{15} & 0 & 0 \\ d_{31} & d_{31} & d_{33} & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N}$$

The $d_{33}$ component (strain in direction 3 from field in direction 3) is the **largest** and most exploited.

### 2.3 Piezoresistive Theory

**For isotropic conductors (metallic strain gauges):**
The resistance change is dominated by geometry. The **gage factor** $G$ is defined as:

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

For a metallic wire:
$$G \approx 1 + 2\nu \quad (\text{geometry only, typically} \approx 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0)$$

**For semiconductors (silicon piezoresistors):**
The piezoresistive coefficients $\pi_l$ (longitudinal) and $\pi_t$ (transverse) dominate. The longitudinal and transverse relative resistance changes are:

$$\frac{\Delta R_l}{R_l} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l + \pi_t \, \sigma_t$$

$$\frac{\Delta R_t}{R_t} = \pi_l \, \sigma_t + \pi_t \, \sigma_l$$

For p-type silicon, $\pi_l \approx 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$ and $\pi_t \approx -66.3 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$, giving effective gage factors of **50 to 200**, much higher than metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is silicon so sensitive?**
> Silicon's covalent bond energy depends strongly on the bond angle and length. Mechanical stress distorts the lattice, which shifts the conduction band minima (or valence band maxima) in k-space. This changes the effective mass tensor and carrier mobility anisotropy, producing a large $\Delta \rho / \rho$. Smith's 1954 paper established this for silicon and germanium.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Mechanism | Why It Is Used |
|---|---|---|
| AFM / STM nanopositioning | Piezoelectric stack | Sub-nm resolution, no backlash |
| Inkjet printer heads | Piezoelectric bimorph | Fast response (µs), precise droplet |
| MEMS pressure sensors | Piezoresistive bridge | High sensitivity, IC-compatible |
| Diesel fuel injection | Piezoelectric stack | High force, fast switching |
| Vibration energy harvesting | Piezoelectric cantilever | Direct conversion to electricity |
| MEMS accelerometers | Piezoresistive / capacitive | Compact, low power |
| Piezoelectric buzzer / speaker | Piezoelectric diaphragm | No magnetic parts, flat response |
| Active damping in smart structures | Piezoelectric patches | Distributed actuation |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = d \, T + \varepsilon^T \, E$ | Direct piezoelectric effect (linear) | C/m² |
| 2 | $S = s^E \, T + d_t \, E$ | Converse piezoelectric effect (linear) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $S = d_{33} \, E$ | Free strain from axial field (approx.) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta L = d_{33} \, V \, n$ | Stack actuator displacement ($n$ layers) | m |
| 5 | $k = d \sqrt{(c^E / \varepsilon^T)}$ | Electromechanical coupling factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $G = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gage factor definition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta R / R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | Piezoresistive change in semiconductors | dimensionless |
| 8 | $G \approx 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ | Full gage factor expression | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{block} = A \, c^E \, d_{33} \, E$ | Blocking force of stack actuator | N |
| 10 | $f_r = (1 / 2L)\sqrt{c^E / \rho}$ | Resonance frequency of bar actuator | Hz |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free Displacement of a Piezoelectric Stack Actuator

**Problem Setup:** A piezoelectric stack actuator consists of $n$ thin ceramic disks of thickness $t$ each, mechanically in series and electrically in parallel. The total height is $L = n t$. A voltage $V$ is applied across the electrodes. Find the free (unloaded) displacement $\Delta L$.

**Given:**
- Piezoelectric coefficient: $d_{33}$ (m/V)
- Number of layers: $n$
- Voltage per layer: $V$
- Layer thickness: $t$

**Step 1:** The electric field in each layer (assuming uniform field and parallel-plate geometry) is:

$$E = \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 2:** From the converse piezoelectric effect, the strain in each layer in the axial (3) direction is:

$$S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 3:** The displacement of a single layer is strain × thickness:

$$\Delta t = S_3 \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V$$

Notice that $t$ cancels — the displacement of one layer depends ONLY on the applied voltage and the $d_{33}$ coefficient, not on layer thickness. This is a famous and often counter-intuitive result in piezoelectric stack design.

**Step 4:** For $n$ layers mechanically in series, the total free displacement ADDS up:

$$\Delta L = n \cdot \Delta t = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**
- To maximize displacement, increase $n$ (more layers) or $V$ (higher drive voltage).
- Typical values: PZT-5A with $d_{33} = 374 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $V = 100$ V, $n = 100$ layers → $\Delta L = 374 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 100 = 3.74 \, \mu m$.
- The stack must be operated BELOW the depolarization field (typically $\approx 1$ kV/mm for PZT) and the coercive field to avoid depoling.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Blocking Force of a Piezoelectric Stack Actuator

**Problem Setup:** Find the maximum mechanical force a piezoelectric stack can generate when its ends are rigidly clamped (zero displacement, "blocked" condition).

**Step 1:** At the blocked condition, the total strain must be zero:
$$S_{total} = S_{free} + S_{mech} = 0$$

where $S_{free}$ is the free strain from the electric field and $S_{mech}$ is the strain produced by the externally applied (blocking) stress $T$.

**Step 2:** From the converse constitutive equation:
$$S_{total} = s^E \, T + d_{33} \, E = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the blocking stress $T_{block}$:
$$T_{block} = -\frac{d_{33} \, E}{s^E} = -d_{33} \, E \, c^E$$

(using $c^E = 1/s^E$, the elastic stiffness at constant E).

**Step 4:** The blocking force on a stack of cross-sectional area $A$ is:
$$F_{block} = A \cdot \vert T_{block} \vert = A \cdot d_{33} \cdot E \cdot c^E$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{F_{block} = A \, c^E \, d_{33} \, E}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**
- Blocking force scales with cross-sectional area $A$ — wider stacks push harder.
- Typical PZT-5A: $c^E \approx 5.6 \times 10^{10}$ N/m², $d_{33} = 374 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $E = 10^6$ V/m, $A = 10 \times 10 \, \text{mm}^2 = 10^{-4}$ m² → $F_{block} \approx 2.1$ kN.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Piezoresistive Bridge Output for a Pressure Sensor

**Problem Setup:** A silicon piezoresistor is mounted on a diaphragm. Under applied pressure, the diaphragm bends, producing a longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ on the piezoresistor. The resistor is connected in a Wheatstone bridge with three fixed resistors. Find the bridge output voltage $V_{out}$ as a function of $\sigma_l$.

**Step 1:** The piezoresistor's relative change:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l + \pi_t \, \sigma_t$$

For a diaphragm in equibiaxial stress, $\sigma_t = 0$, so:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l$$

**Step 2:** For a quarter-bridge (one active resistor), the differential output voltage is:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

where $V_{ex}$ is the bridge excitation voltage.

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \pi_l \, \sigma_l$$

**Step 4 — Membrane stress from pressure:**
For a square diaphragm of side $a$ and thickness $h$, with pressure $P$ on one side, the maximum stress at the clamped edge (centre of edge) is approximately:
$$\sigma_l \approx \frac{0.308 \, P \, a^2}{h^2}$$

(derived from plate bending theory with clamped edges).

**Step 5 — Final expression:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \pi_l \cdot \frac{0.308 \, P \, a^2}{h^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity calculation:**
For $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $P = 100$ kPa, $a = 1$ mm, $h = 25$ µm, $\pi_l = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$:
- $\sigma_l = 0.308 \times 10^5 \times (10^{-3})^2 / (25 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4.93 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $\Delta R / R = 71.8 \times 10^{-11} \times 4.93 \times 10^7 = 3.54 \times 10^{-3}$
- $V_{out} = 5 / 4 \times 3.54 \times 10^{-3} = 4.43$ mV

This is a realistic output for a clinical-grade pressure sensor.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resonance Frequency of a Piezoelectric Cantilever

**Problem Setup:** A piezoelectric cantilever of length $L$, density $\rho$, and elastic modulus $Y$ (≈ $1/s^E$ inverse compliance) is used as a resonant actuator/sensor. Derive the fundamental resonance frequency.

**Step 1:** The equation of motion for a uniform cantilever beam undergoing transverse vibration is:
$$\rho A \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} + Y I \frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} = 0$$

where $A$ is cross-section area and $I$ is the second moment of area.

**Step 2:** Assume harmonic solution $y(x,t) = Y(x) \cos(\omega t)$:
$$\omega^2 \rho A \, Y(x) = Y I \, \frac{d^4 Y}{dx^4}$$

**Step 3:** This is a 4th-order ODE with general solution $Y(x) = A \cosh(\beta x) + B \sinh(\beta x) + C \cos(\beta x) + D \sin(\beta x)$ where $\beta^4 = \omega^2 \rho A / (Y I)$.

**Step 4:** Apply clamped-free boundary conditions ($y(0)=0$, $y'(0)=0$, $y''(L)=0$, $y'''(L)=0$):
$$C = -A, \quad D = -B$$
$$A(\cosh\beta L + \cos\beta L) + B(\sinh\beta L + \sin\beta L) = 0$$
$$A(\cosh\beta L - \cos\beta L) + B(\sinh\beta L - \sin\beta L) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Non-trivial solution requires the determinant to vanish:
$$\cosh\beta L \cdot \cos\beta L = -1$$

The smallest positive root is $\beta L \approx 1.875$.

**Step 6:** Resonance frequency:
$$\omega_1 = (1.875)^2 \sqrt{\frac{Y I}{\rho A L^4}}$$

For a rectangular cross-section $b \times h$, $I = bh^3/12$ and $A = bh$:

$$\boxed{f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{h}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{Y}{12 \rho}}}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering implication:**
- Thinner, longer cantilevers have lower resonance — used for low-frequency energy harvesters (heartbeat, vibration).
- Shorter, thicker cantilevers resonate at kHz–MHz — used for ultrasonic transducers and atomizers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Piezoelectric Actuator System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow architecture of a piezoelectric actuator, from high-level controller down to mechanical output. It captures the multi-domain coupling (electrical → mechanical) and the typical feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Controller Host MCU DSP] --> B[High Voltage Amplifier 0 to 200 V]
    B --> C[Stack Actuator n x PZT Disks]
    C --> D[Mechanical Output Stage Flexure Hinge Lever]
    D --> E[Load / End Effector]
    F[Strain Gauge on Stack] --> G[Signal Conditioner]
    G --> H[ADC]
    H --> I[Feedback to Controller]
    I --> A
    C --> J[Direct Piezoelectric Effect Charge Generation]
    J --> K[Charge Amplifier]
    K --> H
    L[Oven Temperature Sensor] --> M[Thermal Stabilization Block]
    M --> A
    N[Creep Compensator Software] --> A
    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#bf6f24,stroke:#7a4310,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0f4d1a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#424242,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#ad1457,stroke:#560027,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#00838f,stroke:#002f36,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#3949ab,stroke:#1a237e,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#5d4037,stroke:#1b0f0a,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#00838f,stroke:#002f36,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#e65100,stroke:#9b3d00,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#e65100,stroke:#9b3d00,color:#ffffff
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Polarization P reorients in Dipoles]
```

> When asked to "draw and explain a piezoelectric actuator" in the ESE, use the operational logic flow above. It clearly shows: electrical input → field → dipole reorientation → strain → mechanical output. The two-branch "Free vs Loaded" endpoint is the key differentiator between positioning mode and force mode, and examiners award marks for showing both.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**Distinguish between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects. Give one practical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **direct piezoelectric effect** is the generation of an electric charge (or voltage) across the faces of a piezoelectric crystal when it is subjected to mechanical stress. The generated charge is given by $D = d \cdot T$. It is used in applications such as **piezoelectric gas lighters** (impact generates a spark), **quartz watches** (resonator), and **MEMS accelerometers** (impact mode).

The **converse piezoelectric effect** is the mechanical deformation (strain) of a piezoelectric crystal when an external electric field is applied across it. The induced strain is $S = d_t \cdot E$. It is used in applications such as **piezoelectric stack actuators** (nanopositioning stages, AFM), **piezo inkjet printer heads**, and **ultrasonic transducers**.

| Aspect | Direct Effect | Converse Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Mechanical stress | Electric field |
| Output | Electric charge/voltage | Mechanical strain |
| Symbol | $D = d \, T$ | $S = d_t \, E$ |
| Discovered | 1880 by J. & P. Curie | 1881 by Lippmann (confirmed by Curies) |

*(Award 1 mark for direct, 1 mark for converse, 1 mark for applications + comparison table.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`
**Define the piezoresistive effect. Why is silicon preferred over metals for piezoresistive sensors? Mention the typical gage factor values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **piezoresistive effect** is the change in electrical resistivity of a material when it is subjected to mechanical stress. It is quantified by the **gage factor** $G = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$.

**Why silicon is preferred:**
1. The piezoresistive coefficients of silicon ($\pi_l \approx 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$ for p-type) are **50–100× larger** than those of metals, which gives a much higher sensitivity.
2. Silicon is **CMOS-compatible**, allowing monolithic integration of the piezoresistor with readout circuitry on a single chip (MEMS + ASIC).
3. Silicon has excellent **mechanical properties** (high yield strength, no plastic deformation at low stress), giving reliable, repeatable performance.

**Typical gage factor values:**
- Metals (constantan, nichrome): $G \approx 1.6$ to **2.0**
- Silicon (p-type, longitudinal): $G \approx$ **100 to 200**
- Polysilicon thin films: $G \approx$ **20 to 40**

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for silicon advantages, 1 mark for gage factor values.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3.A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]` **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a piezoelectric stack actuator. Derive expressions for the **free displacement** and **blocking force** of a stack of $n$ layers. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A piezoelectric stack actuator is to be designed using PZT-5A ceramic. The relevant material properties are: $d_{33} = 374 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $c^E = 56 \times 10^9$ N/m², maximum safe field $E_{max} = 1 \times 10^6$ V/m, and each ceramic layer is $t = 100$ µm thick. The actuator must produce a free displacement of **5 µm** under a maximum voltage of **100 V**. Determine:
  - (i) the number of layers $n$,
  - (ii) the blocking force for a cross-section of $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle, Free Displacement & Blocking Force — 7 Marks**

A piezoelectric stack actuator consists of many thin PZT ceramic disks (each of thickness $t$, typically 50–200 µm) that are mechanically in series and electrically in parallel. Alternate electrodes connect to the top and bottom metallization. When a voltage $V$ is applied, each layer sees a field $E = V/t$, and the converse piezoelectric effect produces a strain $S_3 = d_{33} E$ in each layer.

The block diagram is:

```
[Controller/V source] → [High-voltage amplifier] → [Stack of n PZT disks] → [Mechanical output (flexure/lever)] → [Load]
```

**Free displacement derivation:**

$$\Delta L = n \cdot S_3 \cdot t = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t} \cdot t = n \, d_{33} \, V$$

**Blocking force derivation:**
At the blocked condition, total strain = 0:
$$s^E \, T_{block} + d_{33} \, E = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{block} = -d_{33} \, c^E \, E$$

$$F_{block} = A \cdot \vert T_{block} \vert = A \, c^E \, d_{33} \, E$$

*(Award 2 marks for working principle + block diagram, 2 marks for free displacement derivation, 2 marks for blocking force derivation, 1 mark for engineering interpretation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $d_{33} = 374 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V
- $c^E = 56 \times 10^9$ N/m²
- $E_{max} = 1 \times 10^6$ V/m
- $t = 100 \, \mu m = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Required: $\Delta L = 5 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $V_{max} = 100$ V
- $A = 10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m²

**(i) Number of layers $n$:**

From the free displacement formula:
$$\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$$
$$n = \frac{\Delta L}{d_{33} \cdot V} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{374 \times 10^{-12} \times 100}$$
$$n = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{3.74 \times 10^{-8}} = 133.7$$

**Round up to $n = 134$ layers** (you cannot have a fractional layer).

**Verification of safe field:**
$$E = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{100}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m} = E_{max}$$

Field is exactly at the limit, so the design is valid but operating at the edge of the safe range.

**Total stack height:**
$$L_{stack} = n \cdot t = 134 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 13.4 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Blocking force:**

$$F_{block} = A \cdot c^E \cdot d_{33} \cdot E$$
$$F_{block} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 56 \times 10^{9} \times 374 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{6}$$
$$F_{block} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 56 \times 10^{9} \times 374 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{6}$$

Compute step by step:
- $100 \times 10^{-6} \times 56 \times 10^{9} = 5.6 \times 10^{6}$ N/m (this is $A c^E$)
- $5.6 \times 10^{6} \times 374 \times 10^{-12} = 2.094 \times 10^{-3}$ (this is $A c^E d_{33}$)
- $2.094 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{6} = 2.094 \times 10^{3}$ N

**$\boxed{F_{block} \approx 2094 \text{ N} \approx 2.09 \text{ kN}}$**

*(Award 2 marks for $n$ calculation, 2 marks for $E$ verification, 1 mark for total stack height, 2 marks for blocking force with full working.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q3.B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]` **[14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **piezoresistive effect** in semiconductors. Derive an expression for the **gage factor** of a semiconductor strain gauge, clearly distinguishing the geometrical and resistivity contributions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A p-type silicon piezoresistor with longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_l = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$ is bonded on a steel cantilever beam. The beam has length $L = 50$ mm, width $b = 10$ mm, and thickness $h = 1$ mm. A force $F = 1$ N is applied at the free end, causing the beam to bend. The piezoresistor is placed at the clamped end (maximum strain location), aligned along the longitudinal axis. The resistor is connected as one arm of a **quarter Wheatstone bridge** with excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V. Young's modulus of steel $E_s = 200$ GPa. Determine:
  - (i) the longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ at the surface where the resistor is placed,
  - (ii) the fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$,
  - (iii) the bridge output voltage $V_{out}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Piezoresistive Effect and Gage Factor Derivation — 7 Marks**

The piezoresistive effect in semiconductors arises from the **stress-induced change in the energy band structure**, which alters the effective mass and mobility of charge carriers. Unlike metals where the geometric effect dominates, in semiconductors the **resistivity change** ($\Delta \rho / \rho$) is 50–100× larger.

The general expression for the fractional resistance change of a conductor under stress is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A} \approx \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

The **gage factor** is defined as:
$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} + (1 + 2\nu)$$

For a metal (where $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is small):
$$G_{metal} \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0 \quad \text{(geometrical only)}$$

For a semiconductor with $\Delta \rho / \rho = \pi_l \, \sigma_l = \pi_l \, E_s \, \varepsilon$:
$$G_{semi} = \pi_l \, E_s + (1 + 2\nu) \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$$

The two contributions are clearly:
- **Geometrical term** $(1 + 2\nu)$: dominant in metals.
- **Piezoresistive term** $\pi_l E_s$: dominant in semiconductors.

*(Award 2 marks for the effect explanation, 2 marks for the $\Delta R / R$ derivation, 2 marks for the $G$ derivation with the two contributions separated, 1 mark for numerical comparison.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $\pi_l = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$
- $L = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $b = 10$ mm $= 0.01$ m
- $h = 1$ mm $= 0.001$ m
- $F = 1$ N
- $V_{ex} = 5$ V
- $E_s = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa

**(i) Longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$:**

For a cantilever beam with an end load $F$, the bending moment at the clamped end is maximum:
$$M_{max} = F \cdot L = 1 \times 0.05 = 0.05 \text{ N·m}$$

The bending stress at the top/bottom surface is:
$$\sigma_l = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

where $c = h/2 = 0.0005$ m, and the second moment of area is:
$$I = \frac{b \, h^3}{12} = \frac{0.01 \times (0.001)^3}{12} = \frac{0.01 \times 10^{-9}}{12} = 8.33 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^4$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_l = \frac{0.05 \times 0.0005}{8.33 \times 10^{-13}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{8.33 \times 10^{-13}} = 3.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

**$\boxed{\sigma_l = 30 \text{ MPa}}$**

**(ii) Fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$:**

Assuming the transverse stress is negligible (resistor is at the surface, principal stress direction):
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \, \sigma_l = 71.8 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.0 \times 10^{7}$$
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 2.154 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2154\%$$

**$\boxed{\Delta R / R \approx 2.15 \times 10^{-3}}$**

**(iii) Bridge output voltage:**

For a quarter Wheatstone bridge:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.154 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times 2.154 \times 10^{-3} = 2.69 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$

**$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 2.69 \text{ mV}}$**

*(Award 2 marks for stress calculation, 2 marks for $\Delta R / R$, 2 marks for $V_{out}$, 1 mark for unit consistency throughout.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up $d$ and $e$ coefficients.** The $d$-coefficient (charge coefficient, C/N or m/V) is the most common. Using $e$ (stress coefficient, C/m²) without converting will lose **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $n$ in the stack formula.** Many students write $\Delta L = d_{33} V$ (single-layer result). KTU explicitly tests the $n$-layer stack formula — losing this is **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing piezoresistive with piezoelectric.** Piezoresistive = stress → resistance change. Piezoelectric = stress → charge. They are FUNDAMENTALLY different mechanisms. Examiners trap students with this every year.
> 4. **In piezoresistive numericals, omitting Poisson's effect or transverse stress.** For a beam in pure bending, $\sigma_t = 0$ along the principal direction; but for a diaphragm in equibiaxial stress, $\sigma_t = \sigma_l$ — and the full $\pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ expression must be used.
> 5. **Unit errors:** $d_{33}$ is in pm/V or C/N (these are equal dimensionally), never in m/V directly. Always convert $374 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Piezoelectric & Piezoresistive Actuators**

- **Piezoelectric effect** is a two-way coupling between mechanical and electrical domains; it exists only in **non-centrosymmetric crystals** (quartz, PZT, BaTiO₃, PVDF, LiNbO₃).
- **Direct effect** (Curie, 1880): $D = d \, T$. **Converse effect** (Lippmann, 1881): $S = d_t \, E$.
- The **constitutive equations** are four coupled linear relations; the most common form is the **stress-charge (e-form)** with $S = s^E T + d E$ and $D = d T + \varepsilon^T E$.
- The **stack actuator** free displacement is $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$; the blocking force is $F_{block} = A \, c^E \, d_{33} \, E$.
- The **bimorph actuator** consists of two piezoelectric layers (series-opposite or parallel-opposite poling) bonded together; it bends when a voltage is applied, producing much larger displacement than a stack of the same length but much smaller force.
- **Tube actuators** are hollow cylinders poled radially, used in STM and for fibre stretching.
- The **piezoresistive effect** is a one-way change in resistivity under stress; in silicon, the **gage factor** reaches **100–200**, dominated by the $\Delta \rho / \rho$ term.
- **Piezoresistive coefficients** $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are direction-dependent; for p-type silicon along $\langle 110 \rangle$, $\pi_l = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$ and $\pi_t = -66.3 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa$^{-1}$.
- **Wheatstone bridge** configurations: quarter-bridge (1 active), half-bridge (2 active), full-bridge (4 active). Full-bridge gives the highest sensitivity, 4× the output of a quarter-bridge.
- **Temperature compensation** is critical for both effects: PZT shows strong thermal expansion and depoling above the Curie temperature (~150–350 °C); silicon piezoresistors have a large temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) and piezoresistive coefficient (TCP).
- **Creep and hysteresis** are major non-idealities of piezoelectric actuators — must be compensated in closed-loop positioning.
- **Safety limit:** Drive field must stay below the **coercive field** ($E_c \approx 1$ kV/mm for PZT) to avoid depoling.
- **Real-world applications to remember:** AFM nanopositioning (stack), inkjet heads (bimorph), diesel injectors (stack), quartz watches (direct), pressure sensors (piezoresistive bridge), accelerometers (piezoresistive), buzzers/speakers (piezo diaphragm), energy harvesters (piezo cantilever).
- **Two KTU-favourite derivations:** (1) Stack free displacement and blocking force, (2) Piezoresistive bridge output for a pressure sensor — practice both until you can derive them cold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micropumps and Micro Actuators with Practical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Micropumps and Micro Actuators with Practical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Micro Actuators — Formal Definition

A **Micro Actuator** is a micro-scale electromechanical device that converts electrical, thermal, magnetic, or chemical energy into controlled mechanical motion, force, or displacement at the micro/nano scale. In the context of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and Cyber-Physical Systems, micro actuators are the "muscles" that provide physical actuation in response to signals from a controller (the "brain").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCPT205):**
> *"A micro actuator is a micro-fabricated device that produces a controlled mechanical output (displacement, force, vibration, or flow) in response to an external stimulus (voltage, current, heat, magnetic field, or chemical signal)."*

**Classification of Micro Actuators (KTU Taxonomy):**

| Family | Energy Domain | Typical Displacement | Typical Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrostatic | Electrical | Sub-µm – 50 µm | µN – mN |
| Piezoelectric | Electrical (with strain coupling) | nm – µm | mN – N |
| Thermal (Bimorph) | Thermal | 10 – 500 µm | mN |
| Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) | Thermal | 1 % – 8 % strain | N |
| Magnetic / Electromagnetic | Magnetic | 10 µm – mm | mN – N |
| Hydrogel / Chemical | Chemical / pH | 10 % – 50 % | µN – mN |

## 1.2 Micropumps — Formal Definition

A **Micropump** is a microfluidic device with characteristic dimensions typically below **10 mm** (often < 1 cm³ total volume) that can actively transport fluids (gases or liquids) at flow rates usually in the range of **µL/min to mL/min**. Micropumps are the "hearts" of microfluidic, biomedical, and lab-on-chip systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:**
> Micropumps are formally classified by their actuation principle into **two grand divisions**:
> 1. **Mechanical (Reciprocating) Micropumps** — Use a moving diaphragm or membrane to displace fluid.
> 2. **Non-Mechanical (Dynamic) Micropumps** — Use body forces (electric, magnetic, surface, acoustic) directly on the fluid.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### The Heart Analogy
Imagine a **medical syringe**: you push the plunger, fluid is forced out, then you release and fluid is sucked in through a check valve. A mechanical micropump works *exactly* like this, but built on a silicon chip:
- The **plunger** = a flexible silicon/polymer membrane.
- The **check valves** = micro-machined flap valves.
- The **pushing hand** = a piezoelectric disc, electrostatic comb, or thermal bimorph.

A **non-mechanical micropump**, on the other hand, behaves like a **river flowing downhill** — there is no plunger, but a continuous body force (electric field, magnetic field, or surface tension gradient) directly drags the fluid through a microchannel.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *Mechanical = "push-pull" (discrete strokes). Non-Mechanical = "drag-steer" (continuous body force).*

### Why Micropumps and Micro Actuators Matter (Cyber-Physical Context)
In a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) such as a **wearable insulin pump**, a **closed-loop drug delivery system**, or a **point-of-care diagnostic chip**, the **sensor** measures (e.g., blood glucose), the **controller** decides, and the **actuator + micropump** delivers the precise physical response (insulin dose). Without micro actuators and micropumps, the CPS cannot close the loop from the digital world to the physical body.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Micro Actuators — Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Electrostatic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Coulombic attraction between oppositely charged conductors produces a force. Used in comb drives, parallel-plate actuators, and scratch drives.

**Working Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Apply a voltage $V$ between a fixed electrode and a movable electrode.
2. Capacitance $C$ stores energy $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
3. The system tries to **minimize stored energy** by increasing $C$ — the movable electrode is pulled toward the fixed one.
4. Removing the voltage releases the actuator (with proper spring restoration).

**Governing Physics:**
- Capacitance of a parallel plate: $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$
- Stored energy: $U = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2d}$
- Electrostatic force: $F_e = -\dfrac{dU}{dx} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2d^2}$

### 2.1.2 Piezoelectric Actuators
**Operating Principle:** The **direct piezoelectric effect in reverse** (converse effect) — applying an electric field to a non-centrosymmetric crystal (PZT, quartz, PVDF) produces a mechanical strain $\varepsilon = d_{33} E$, where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient.

**Working Logic:**
1. Voltage $V$ applied across a piezo disc creates field $E = V/t$.
2. The crystal elongates by $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$ (in 33-mode).
3. This elongation pushes a diaphragm or stack element.
4. Reversing polarity produces contraction — enabling bidirectional pumping/actuation.

### 2.1.3 Thermal Bimorph Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Two materials with **different coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE)** bonded together. Heating causes differential expansion → bending.

**Working Logic:**
1. Current $I$ flows through a resistive heater strip.
2. Joule heating: $P = I^2 R$ → temperature rises.
3. Material with higher $\alpha$ expands more than material with lower $\alpha$.
4. Beam bends toward the low-CTE side.
5. Cooling restores flat state.

### 2.1.4 Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuators
**Operating Principle:** SMA (e.g., Nitinol = Nickel-Titanium) undergoes a **martensite-to-austenite phase transformation** at a critical temperature, "remembering" its original shape.

**Working Logic:**
1. Cold, deformed (martensitic) wire is plastically bent.
2. Heated above austenite finish temperature $A_f$ → wire snaps back to original (trained) shape.
3. Produces very high forces (> 100 MPa stress) but slow (cooling-limited).
4. Cooling returns wire to soft martensitic state for next cycle.

### 2.1.5 Magnetic / Electromagnetic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field: $F = B I L$.

**Working Logic:**
1. Permanent magnet provides background field $B$.
2. Current $I$ through micro-coil experiences force $F = BIL$.
3. Microstructure (cantilever, membrane) deflects.
4. Used in speakers, micro-mirrors, micropumps.

## 2.2 Micropumps — Detailed Working Principles

### 2.2.1 Piezoelectric Micropump (Mechanical, Reciprocating)
**Components:** Piezo disc + Si diaphragm + inlet & outlet check valves + pump chamber.

**Working Logic — Two-Phase Cycle:**

| Phase | Piezo Action | Diaphragm | Inlet Valve | Outlet Valve | Net Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Supply (Pumping)** | Displaces downward | Pushes fluid out | Closed (prevents backflow) | Open | Fluid ejected to outlet |
| **Suction (Refill)** | Returns upward | Creates vacuum | Open (draws fluid in) | Closed | Fluid drawn from inlet |

**Key Output Equation:**
- Theoretical displacement volume per stroke: $V_{stroke} = \Delta z_{piezo} \cdot A_{pump}$
- Flow rate: $Q = V_{stroke} \cdot f \cdot \eta_{vol}$, where $f$ is the actuation frequency and $\eta_{vol}$ is volumetric efficiency.

### 2.2.2 Electroosmotic Micropump (Non-Mechanical, Dynamic)
**Operating Principle:** When a microchannel wall carries a surface charge (e.g., glass → SiO⁻ in water), counter-ions form a thin **Electrical Double Layer (EDL)**. Applying a tangential electric field drags these ions, which viscously drag the bulk fluid (electroosmotic flow, EOF).

**Working Logic:**
1. Microchannel walls (glass, PDMS) become negatively charged in aqueous solution.
2. Cations (H⁺, Na⁺) form a thin EDL of thickness $\lambda_D$ (Debye length).
3. Tangential electric field $E$ exerts Coulomb force on EDL ions.
4. Ions drag adjacent fluid layers by viscosity → plug-like flow profile.

**Zeta Potential (ζ):** The potential at the slipping plane, typically **−25 mV to −150 mV** for glass/water.

### 2.2.3 Electromagnetic Micropump
**Operating Principle:** A Lorentz-force-driven conductive fluid (or a magnetically-active fluid/ferrofluid) moves through a channel under combined $E$ and $B$ fields.

**Working Logic (Conductive fluid in crossed fields):**
1. Electrodes inject current $J$ across the channel.
2. Permanent magnet provides $B$ perpendicular to both $J$ and flow direction.
3. Lorentz force on fluid: $F_L = J \times B$ per unit volume.
4. Pressure builds up: $\Delta P = J \cdot B \cdot L$ (where $L$ is channel length).
5. Flow rate: $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$, where $R_h$ is the hydraulic resistance.

### 2.2.4 Surface Tension (Capillary) Micropump
**Working Principle:** Exploits **Laplace pressure** difference across a curved meniscus: $\Delta P = \gamma \left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$.

**Working Logic:**
1. A heater locally elevates temperature, reducing surface tension $\gamma(T)$ on one side of the meniscus.
2. Asymmetric surface tension creates pressure gradient.
3. Marangoni flow carries fluid from hot to cold region.
4. Used in capillary-driven lab-on-chip devices (passive, no external power).

### 2.2.5 Ultrasonic / Acoustic Micropump
**Working Principle:** High-frequency (MHz) sound waves in a piezoelectric medium create **acoustic streaming** and standing-wave pressure nodes, propelling fluid.

### 2.2.6 Electrohydrodynamic (EHD) Micropump
**Operating Principle:** A non-uniform electric field induces motion in **dielectric fluids** via polarization forces. Useful for non-conductive liquids (oils, refrigerants).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **exam-critical equations** for Module 4. Memorize the units, the limiting assumptions, and the boundary conditions (small-gap, no fringing, Newtonian fluid, isothermal).

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel plates | Electrostatic actuator design |
| 2 | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2d^2}$ | Electrostatic attraction force | Comb-drive, pull-in analysis |
| 3 | $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$ | Piezo displacement (33-mode) | Piezo stack/diaphragm design |
| 4 | $F = B I L$ | Lorentz force on conductor | Electromagnetic actuator/pump |
| 5 | $\Delta P = J \cdot B \cdot L$ | EM pump pressure head | Conductive-fluid micropump |
| 6 | $u_{EOF} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta E}{\mu}$ | Helmholtz–Smoluchowski EOF velocity | Electroosmotic flow design |
| 7 | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r R T}{2 F^2 C_0}}$ | Debye length (EDL thickness) | Microchannel ionic design |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \gamma \left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$ | Laplace pressure (Young-Laplace) | Capillary/surface-tension pump |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$, $\ R_h = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow | Micropump flow rate |
| 10 | $Q_{avg} = V_{stroke} \cdot f \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | Mechanical pump throughput | Piezo/electrostatic micropump |
| 11 | $\varepsilon = d_{33} E$ | Piezoelectric strain law | Strain calculation for PZT |
| 12 | $S = \alpha \Delta T$ | Thermal strain (bimorph) | Bimorph tip deflection |
| 13 | $R_{hyd} = \dfrac{12 \mu L}{w h^3 \left(1 - \tfrac{h}{w}\right)^3}$ | Rectangular channel hydraulic resistance | Microfluidic network design |
| 14 | $P_{Joule} = I^2 R$ | Joule heating (bimorph/SMA) | Thermal actuator power budget |
| 15 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} < 1$ | Reynolds number criterion | Laminar flow validation in MEMS |

> [!TIP]
> **Units Discipline for KTU Exams:**
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ (vacuum permittivity)
> - $\gamma_{water} \approx 0.072\ \text{N/m}$ at 20 °C
> - $\mu_{water} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$ at 20 °C
> - Typical $d_{33}$ for PZT-5A: $\approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m/V}$
> - Typical $d_{33}$ for PVDF: $\approx 3.3 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m/V}$
