# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electrostatic Pull-In Force and "Pull-In" Instability

> **Problem:** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has plate area $A = 1\ \text{mm}^2$, gap $d = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, dielectric is vacuum. Find the attractive force at $V = 50\ \text{V}$ and identify the pull-in voltage.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of the parallel-plate structure.**

The capacitance depends on the instantaneous gap $x$:

$$
C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{x}
$$

**Step 2 — Electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor.**

$$
U(x) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 x}
$$

**Step 3 — Force on the movable plate.**

The force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to displacement. Because decreasing $x$ increases $C$ and the system seeks minimum energy, the force is **attractive**:

$$
F_e(x) = -\frac{dU}{dx} = -\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 x}\right) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 x^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m},\quad A = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2,\quad V = 50\ \text{V},\quad x = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
F_e = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(1 \times 10^{-6})(50)^2}{2 (10 \times 10^{-6})^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator and denominator separately.**

- Numerator: $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 2500 = 2.2135 \times 10^{-14}$
- Denominator: $2 \times (10^{-5})^2 = 2 \times 10^{-10}$

**Step 6 — Final force calculation.**

$$
F_e = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.107 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{N} \approx 110.7\ \mu\text{N}
$$

**Step 7 — Pull-in voltage derivation.**

The movable plate is held by a mechanical spring of stiffness $k$. Equilibrium condition: $F_e = kx$, i.e.

$$
\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 x^2} = k x \quad\Rightarrow\quad V^2 = \frac{2 k x^3}{\varepsilon_0 A}
$$

Stability requires $\dfrac{dF_e}{dx} < k$ (negative stiffness less than mechanical). This condition fails at:

$$
x_{PI} = \frac{d_0}{3}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

This is the famous **Pull-In Voltage** — beyond this, the plates snap together.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Electroosmotic Flow (EOF) Velocity — Helmholtz–Smoluchowski

> **Problem:** A glass microchannel of width $w = 100\ \mu\text{m}$ carries an aqueous KCl solution (10⁻⁴ M). The wall zeta potential is $\zeta = -75\ \text{mV}$. A potential $V = 1000\ \text{V}$ is applied across length $L = 5\ \text{cm}$. Compute the average flow velocity and volumetric flow rate.

**Step 1 — Electric field along the channel.**

$$
E = \frac{V}{L} = \frac{1000}{0.05} = 2 \times 10^{4}\ \text{V/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine Debye length $\lambda_D$.**

For a monovalent symmetric electrolyte ($z = 1$) at $T = 298\ \text{K}$:

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r R T}{2 F^2 C_0}}
$$

Use $\varepsilon_r = 78.5$ (water), $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$, $C_0 = 10^{-4} \times 10^3\ \text{mol/m}^3 = 0.1\ \text{mol/m}^3$.

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(78.5)(8.314)(298)}{2(96485)^2(0.1)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$
8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 78.5 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 1.7159 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**

$$
2 \times (96485)^2 \times 0.1 = 2 \times 9.31 \times 10^9 \times 0.1 = 1.862 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\lambda_D$.**

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{1.7159 \times 10^{-6}}{1.862 \times 10^{9}}} = \sqrt{9.21 \times 10^{-16}} = 3.04 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} = 30.4\ \text{nm}
$$

**Step 6 — Check the thin-EDL condition.**

Channel half-width: $h/2 = 50\ \mu\text{m}$. Ratio: $\lambda_D / (h/2) \approx 6 \times 10^{-4} \ll 1$. ✅ Thin EDL assumption valid.

**Step 7 — Apply Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation.**

$$
u_{EOF} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta E}{\mu}
$$

With $\mu_{water} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$:

$$
u_{EOF} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(78.5)(-0.075)(2 \times 10^{4})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute step by step.**

$$
= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 78.5 \times 0.075 \times 2 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 78.5 \times 0.075 \times 2 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.1775 \times 10^{8} \times 1.5
$$

$$
= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.766 \times 10^{8} = 1.564 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 9 — Magnitude of EOF velocity.**

$$
\boxed{\,u_{EOF} \approx 1.56\ \text{mm/s}\,}
$$

**Step 10 — Volumetric flow rate (for rectangular channel, $h = 100\ \mu\text{m}$):**

$$
Q = u_{EOF} \times w \times h = 1.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
Q = 1.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-8} = 1.56 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 15.6\ \text{nL/s}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow in a Micropump

> **Problem:** A cylindrical microchannel of radius $r = 50\ \mu\text{m}$, length $L = 10\ \text{mm}$, carries water ($\mu = 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$) under a pressure difference $\Delta P = 10\ \text{kPa}$. Find the flow rate and Reynolds number.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic resistance of a cylindrical tube.**

$$
R_h = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
R_h = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^4}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator and denominator.**

- Numerator: $8 \times 10^{-5}$
- Denominator: $\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-18} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-17}$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_h$.**

$$
R_h = \frac{8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-17}} = 4.074 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Pa·s/m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate.**

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R_h} = \frac{10\,000}{4.074 \times 10^{12}} = 2.454 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.45\ \mu\text{L/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Mean velocity.**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{\pi r^2} = \frac{2.454 \times 10^{-9}}{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.312\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Reynolds number.**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.312 \times (2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6})}{10^{-3}} = \frac{1000 \times 0.312 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 31.2
$$

**Step 8 — Regime check.**

$\text{Re} = 31.2 < 2300$ → **Laminar flow** ✅ (validates Poiseuille assumption).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Micropumps)

| Parameter | Piezoelectric | Electroosmotic | Electromagnetic | Capillary (Surface Tension) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Actuation type** | Mechanical reciprocating | Non-mechanical continuous | Non-mechanical continuous | Passive / non-mechanical |
| **Flow rate (typical)** | 0.1 – 10 mL/min | 0.01 – 10 µL/min | 0.1 – 100 µL/min | < 1 µL/min |
| **Pressure head (max)** | 100 kPa – 1 MPa | 10 – 100 kPa | 1 – 50 kPa | < 1 kPa |
| **Operating voltage** | 50 – 300 V | 100 V – 5 kV | 5 – 50 V | None (passive) |
| **Power consumption** | mW – W | mW | W (high current) | Zero |
| **Fluid compatibility** | Universal | Aqueous (conductivity matters) | Conductive / ferrofluid | Aqueous |
| **Backflow / valves** | Required | No | No | No |
| **Biocompatibility** | Good | Excellent (no moving parts) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Main drawback** | Diaphragm fatigue | High-voltage Joule heating | Heat dissipation, leakage | Very low pressure |
| **KTU Exam Favorite?** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Differentiate between mechanical and non-mechanical micropumps. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanical Micropump | Non-Mechanical Micropump |
|---|---|---|
| Actuation | Moving diaphragm/membrane | No moving parts; uses body force |
| Energy domain | Pressure-volume work | Electric / magnetic / surface field |
| Valves | Usually required (check valves) | Valveless in most designs |
| Examples | Piezoelectric, electrostatic diaphragm | Electroosmotic, electrohydrodynamic, ultrasonic |
| Backflow risk | Higher (depends on valve sealing) | Very low (continuous pumping) |

*Examples:* Mechanical → **Piezoelectric micropump, Thermopneumatic micropump.** Non-mechanical → **Electroosmotic micropump, Electromagnetic micropump.** *(1 mark for table/diagram clarity, 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]
**What is a Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) actuator? Briefly explain its working.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An SMA actuator is a thermal micro actuator made from alloys like Nitinol (Ni-Ti) that undergo a reversible solid-state phase transformation between **martensite** (low-temperature, soft) and **austenite** (high-temperature, hard) phases, "remembering" a pre-trained shape.
- **Working (2 marks):** When a deformed (martensitic) SMA wire is heated above its **austenite finish temperature $A_f$** (typically 60 – 100 °C for biomedical Nitinol), it reverts to its original trained shape, generating a large recovery stress (> 100 MPa). On cooling, it becomes soft again and can be re-deformed. The cycle produces mechanical work.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Shape Memory Effect** (one-way, heat-activated) with **superelasticity** (stress-induced, no heating). Examiners will deduct marks if the answer does not specify the *thermal* nature of the actuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU End-Semester Model, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **piezoelectric micropump**. Derive the expression for its average flow rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of operation of an **electroosmotic micropump**. Derive the Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation for the average electroosmotic velocity. State two advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a): Piezoelectric Micropump (7 marks)

**Sketch (2 marks):** Draw: PZT disc bonded on top of a Si diaphragm; pump chamber below; inlet and outlet check valves (flap or nozzle-diffuser type) on either side; inlet reservoir and outlet to load.

**Working description (3 marks):**
- A sinusoidal voltage (50 – 300 V) at frequency $f$ (typically 10 Hz – 10 kHz) is applied to the PZT disc.
- **Supply stroke:** PZT expands → diaphragm deflects down → chamber volume decreases → pressure rises → **outlet valve opens**, fluid ejected.
- **Suction stroke:** PZT contracts → diaphragm moves up → chamber volume increases → pressure drops → **inlet valve opens**, fluid drawn in.
- Net unidirectional flow is achieved by the asymmetry of the check valves.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

The diaphragm displacement imparted by the piezo at voltage $V$:

$$
\Delta z = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V
$$

where $n$ is the number of piezo layers (for a stack). Effective chamber volume change:

$$
V_{stroke} = \Delta z \cdot A_{pump} = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V \cdot A_{pump}
$$

Average volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q_{avg} = V_{stroke} \cdot f \cdot \eta_{vol} = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V \cdot A_{pump} \cdot f \cdot \eta_{vol}
$$

where $\eta_{vol}$ is the volumetric efficiency (typically 0.5 – 0.9, accounting for valve leakage and backflow).

---

#### Model Solution to A(b): Electroosmotic Micropump (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):** When a microchannel wall is charged (e.g., glass in water → SiO⁻), counter-ions in the solution form a thin Electrical Double Layer (EDL) of thickness $\lambda_D$. A tangential electric field $E$ exerts a Coulomb force on the EDL ions, which viscously drag the bulk fluid. This produces **electroosmotic flow (EOF)** with a near-plug velocity profile.

**Derivation of Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation (3 marks):**
- For thin EDL ($\lambda_D \ll$ channel dimension), the velocity is uniform across the channel.
- The EDL behaves as a charged sheet of surface charge density $\sigma_s$ at the wall.
- The Smoluchowski slip boundary condition: the fluid velocity at the slipping plane (potential = $\zeta$) is

$$
u_{slip} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta E}{\mu}
$$

- This slip velocity is transmitted across the entire cross-section → average velocity equals slip velocity.

$$
\boxed{\,u_{EOF} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta E}{\mu}\,}
$$

**Advantages (1 mark):** (i) No moving parts → high reliability, no wear, no backflow; (ii) plug-like velocity profile → minimal dispersion (ideal for separation and dosing); (iii) easy to integrate on a chip; (iv) precise flow control via applied voltage.

**Limitations (1 mark):** (i) Requires high voltage (kV range); (ii) Joule heating can damage biological samples; (iii) requires ionic fluid; (iv) limited flow rate (µL/min range).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU ESE Model]

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of an **electromagnetic micropump**. Derive the expression for the developed pressure head and volumetric flow rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of **electrostatic comb-drive actuators** and **piezoelectric bimorph actuators** with neat diagrams. Compare their displacement, force, and frequency response. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a): Electromagnetic Micropump (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** Channel with **two electrodes** on opposite walls (perpendicular to flow) and a **permanent magnet** providing a magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to both the current and the flow direction. A conductive fluid (e.g., saline, electrolyte) is the working medium.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Current density $J$ is driven through the fluid by the electrodes.
- Lorentz force per unit volume: $\vec{f} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$.
- Force is directed along the channel axis → fluid is propelled.
- $\Delta P$ is built up across length $L$ of the active region.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta P = \int_0^L f \, dx = J \cdot B \cdot L
$$

Volumetric flow rate through the channel of hydraulic resistance $R_h$:

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R_h} = \frac{J B L}{R_h}
$$

For a rectangular channel ($w \times h$):

$$
Q = \frac{J B L \cdot w h^3}{12 \mu L_{total}} = \frac{J B w h^3}{12 \mu}
$$

where $L_{total}$ is the total channel length (cancels out in the pressure-flow relationship).

**Significance (1 mark):** EM pumps are bidirectional by simply reversing the current, are valveless, and can handle conductive biofluids (e.g., blood, saline) in implants.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b): Comb-Drive vs. Piezo Bimorph (7 marks)

**Electrostatic Comb Drive (2 marks):**
- Interdigitated finger structures — one set fixed, one set movable.
- Applying voltage induces fringing-field-induced charges on the finger sidewalls.
- Sideways electrostatic force pulls fingers together → in-plane motion.
- Force $F = \dfrac{n \varepsilon_0 t V^2}{g}$, where $n$ = number of fingers, $t$ = thickness, $g$ = gap.
- Displacement up to ~50 µm, force ~µN, bandwidth up to MHz.

**Piezoelectric Bimorph (2 marks):**
- Two piezo layers (or piezo + passive layer) bonded together with opposite poling.
- Applied voltage causes one layer to expand, the other to contract → bending.
- Tip displacement: $\delta = \dfrac{3 d_{31} V L^2}{2 t^2}$.
- Displacement up to mm, force ~N, bandwidth kHz–MHz.

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Electrostatic Comb Drive | Piezoelectric Bimorph |
|---|---|---|
| Displacement | Small (sub-µm to 50 µm) | Large (mm possible) |
| Force | µN range | mN to N range |
| Drive voltage | Low (5 – 100 V) | Moderate (10 – 200 V) |
| Bandwidth | Very high (kHz – MHz) | Moderate (kHz) |
| Power at DC | Near zero | Near zero |
| Power at high freq | Capacitive losses | Dielectric losses |
| Non-linearity | Pull-in instability | Mild hysteresis |
| Biocompatibility | Good (coated) | Good (encapsulated) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Module 4):**
> 1. **Never omit the units** in a numerical answer. A correct value of $1.56\ \text{mm/s}$ with no unit gets zero credit on numerical sub-parts.
> 2. **Always state assumptions** in derivations (e.g., "thin EDL", "Newtonian fluid", "no fringing fields", "isothermal"). Examiners explicitly test this awareness.
> 3. **Sketch quality matters** — a 7-mark question typically reserves 1.5 – 2 marks for the diagram. Use **labeled** block diagrams, not vague boxes.
> 4. **Do not confuse SMA and bimorph.** SMA = phase-change memory effect (slow, large force). Bimorph = differential thermal expansion (fast, small force).
> 5. **Equation derivations** must include every algebraic step. Skipping "let $F = dU/dx$" is a half-mark deduction in KTU valuations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall.**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] A **micro actuator** converts a non-mechanical stimulus (electrical, thermal, magnetic, chemical) into mechanical motion at the micro scale.
- [ ] A **micropump** transports fluids (µL/min to mL/min) through microchannels in lab-on-chip and biomedical systems.
- [ ] **Mechanical micropumps** (piezo, electrostatic, thermopneumatic) → moving diaphragm + check valves → pulsatile flow.
- [ ] **Non-mechanical micropumps** (electroosmotic, EHD, electromagnetic, surface-tension) → no moving parts → continuous flow.

### Equations You Must Know Cold
- [ ] Electrostatic force: $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2 d^2}$
- [ ] Piezo displacement: $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$
- [ ] Lorentz force: $F = BIL$
- [ ] EOF velocity: $u_{EOF} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \zeta E}{\mu}$
- [ ] Debye length: $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r RT}{2 F^2 C_0}}$
- [ ] Laplace pressure: $\Delta P = \gamma \left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$
- [ ] Micropump flow rate: $Q = V_{stroke} \cdot f \cdot \eta_{vol}$
- [ ] Hagen–Poiseuille: $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$, $R_h = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ (cylinder)

### Key Physical Constants (Memorize!)
- [ ] $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- [ ] $F = 96\,485\ \text{C/mol}$ (Faraday)
- [ ] $\gamma_{water} \approx 0.072\ \text{N/m}$
- [ ] $\mu_{water} \approx 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$
- [ ] $\text{Re} < 2300$ → laminar; **almost always laminar in MEMS**

### Comparative Mnemonics
- [ ] "**E**lectrostatic = **E**asy voltage, **E**asy to integrate, but **E**asy pull-in."
- [ ] "**P**iezo = **P**recise nm displacement, **P**owerful force, but **P**olarity sensitive."
- [ ] "**S**MA = **S**uper strong, **S**low speed, needs **S**witching thermal cycle."
- [ ] "**E**lectroosmotic = **E**ven flow profile, **E**asy on chip, but **E**nergetic heating problem."

### Practical Applications to Mention in Answers
- [ ] Wearable insulin / drug delivery pumps → **piezo + SMA**
- [ ] Lab-on-chip diagnostics → **electroosmotic**
- [ ] CPU microchannel cooling → **electroosmotic / EHD**
- [ ] DLP projector micro-mirrors → **electrostatic**
- [ ] Micro-grippers / surgical tools → **SMA + piezo**
- [ ] PEM fuel cell H₂ recirculation → **electromagnetic diaphragm**

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the negative sign in EOF velocity (direction depends on ζ sign).
- [ ] ❌ Using parallel-plate capacitance formula for comb drives (use the comb-specific expression with $n$ fingers).
- [ ] ❌ Confusing Helmholtz–Smoluchowski (thin EDL) with EOF in nanochannels (thick EDL — different model).
- [ ] ❌ Treating SMA as a fast actuator (it is *not*; cycling is limited by cooling, ~Hz or sub-Hz).
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$ in mechanical pump flow-rate calculations.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Micropumps and Micro Actuators with Practical Applications.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic and Hydraulic Actuation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic and Hydraulic Actuation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pneumatic Actuation System?

A **Pneumatic Actuation System** is a type of fluid power system that uses a **compressible gaseous working medium** — most commonly **filtered, dried compressed air** — to generate, transmit, and control mechanical energy for performing useful work (linear motion, rotary motion, or gripping) at the actuator end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A pneumatic system converts the potential energy of compressed air into mechanical energy by acting on a piston inside a cylinder, where the pressure differential across the piston produces a unidirectional or bidirectional force output.*

**Core Working Principle:** The system takes in atmospheric air, compresses it using an external compressor, conditions it (filters moisture and dust), regulates the pressure, and routes it through directional control valves to a cylinder or motor, which converts the pneumatic pressure into mechanical motion.

**Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Pump Piston:**
Imagine pumping air into a bicycle tire. As you push the handle down, the compressed air inside the barrel pushes the piston rod outward with a force. This is essentially a single-stroke pneumatic actuator. Now scale this up: industrial pneumatic systems do exactly the same, but with **regulated, oil-free (or oil-lubricated) compressed air** at pressures typically between **4 bar to 8 bar (≈ 0.4 MPa to 0.8 MPa)**, and they do it hundreds of cycles per day with millisecond-precise directional control.

### 1.2 What is a Hydraulic Actuation System?

A **Hydraulic Actuation System** is a fluid power system that uses an **incompressible liquid working medium** — typically **hydraulic oil (ISO VG 32 or VG 46 mineral oil)** with a bulk modulus of elasticity around **1.4 GPa to 2.0 GPa** — to transmit power and produce very high force/torque output at the actuator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *A hydraulic system operates on Pascal's Law, where a pressurized incompressible fluid confined in a closed circuit exerts equal pressure in all directions, allowing force multiplication when transmitted from a small-area piston to a large-area piston.*

**Real-World Analogy — The Dentist's Chair Lift:**
Have you noticed how a dentist's chair can lift a full-grown human smoothly with just a finger-press on a lever? That is hydraulic actuation. A small force applied on a thin pump piston creates high pressure in the oil, which then acts on a larger-area piston underneath the chair, producing a multiplied lifting force. The incompressibility of the liquid means there is virtually no "springiness" — it feels **stiff, precise, and powerful**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction Highlighted by KTU Syllabus:**
> - **Pneumatic** = Gas (compressible, low stiffness, fast response, lower force).
> - **Hydraulic** = Liquid (incompressible, high stiffness, slower response, very high force).

### 1.3 Standard Units, Constants & Terminology

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| System Pressure | P | Pascal (Pa), Bar | 6 bar (Pneumatic), 100–250 bar (Hydraulic) |
| Force Output | F | Newton (N) | 500 N – 50,000 N |
| Piston Diameter | D | mm | 25 mm – 200 mm |
| Flow Rate | Q | L/min (lpm) | 50 lpm – 500 lpm |
| Atmospheric Pressure | P_atm | Pa | 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa (≈ 1.013 bar) |
| Bulk Modulus of Oil (β) | β | Pa | 1.5 × 10⁹ Pa (typical) |
| Density of Hydraulic Oil | ρ | kg/m³ | 870 kg/m³ |
| Speed of Sound in Air (20°C) | c | m/s | 343 m/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Governing Laws

#### A. Pascal's Law (The Heart of All Fluid Power)
> *"Pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts with equal force on equal areas."*

This single law is the **cornerstone** of every hydraulic system, and (with caveats about compressibility) also applies to pneumatic systems.

#### B. Boyle's Law (Pneumatic-Specific)
$$P_1 \cdot V_1 = P_2 \cdot V_2 \quad \text{(at constant temperature)}$$

Because air is compressible, the **volume change under load** is significant, which is why pneumatic systems have inherent "springiness".

#### C. Continuity Equation (For Both Systems)
$$Q = A \cdot v$$

Where $Q$ is volume flow rate, $A$ is cross-sectional area, and $v$ is fluid velocity.

### 2.2 Logical Operating Sequence of a Pneumatic System

1. **Air Intake** → Atmospheric air is drawn through an intake filter.
2. **Compression** → An air compressor (reciprocating or screw-type) compresses it to working pressure (typically 6–8 bar).
3. **Storage** → Pressurized air is stored in a **receiver tank** to buffer demand spikes.
4. **Conditioning (FRL Unit)** → The **F**ilter removes particulates, the **R**egulator maintains constant downstream pressure, and the **L**ubricator injects oil mist (in oil-fog systems) for cylinder lubrication.
5. **Distribution** → Air flows through pipes/tubing to directional control valves (DCVs).
6. **Directional Control** → A 3/2, 4/2, or 5/2 DCV routes air to either side of the cylinder.
7. **Actuation** → Pressure acts on the piston, producing linear force: $F = P \times A$.
8. **Exhaust** → Spent air is vented to atmosphere (often through a silencer/muffler).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Memorize the **"FRL" acronym** — Filter-Regulator-Lubricator — this is a guaranteed 2-mark short-answer question in most board exams.

### 2.3 Logical Operating Sequence of a Hydraulic System

1. **Reservoir (Tank)** → Holds hydraulic oil and allows air/contaminants to settle.
2. **Pump** → A **gear pump, vane pump, or piston pump** draws oil from the tank and pressurizes it (typically 100–350 bar for industrial systems).
3. **Pressure Relief Valve** → A safety valve that limits maximum system pressure.
4. **Directional Control Valve** → Routes oil to the cap-end or rod-end of the cylinder.
5. **Actuator** → Double-acting cylinder or hydraulic motor converts pressure into mechanical work.
6. **Return Line & Filter** → Oil returns to the tank, passing through a return-line filter.
7. **Heat Exchanger (Cooler)** → Hydraulic systems generate heat; an oil cooler is often essential.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | Cylinder thrust force | Both systems |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Full bore (cap-end) area | Both systems |
| 3 | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | Annular (rod-end) area | Both systems |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity | Both systems |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{L \cdot A}{Q}$ | Cylinder stroke time | Both systems |
| 6 | $P_{hyd} = F/A$ | Mechanical power (linear) | Both systems |
| 7 | $P_{out} = P \times Q$ | Hydraulic power (Watts) | Both systems |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | System efficiency | Both systems |
| 9 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Boyle's law (isothermal) | Pneumatic only |
| 10 | $p_{static} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Hydraulic only |
| 11 | $F_{out} = F_{in} \times \dfrac{A_{out}}{A_{in}}$ | Force multiplication | Hydraulic press |
| 12 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | Both systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Markdown Tables:** Never use the vertical bar symbol `\vert` (e.g., for absolute value $|x|$) inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, e.g., write $P \mid_{max}$ rather than $P \vert_{max}$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Pneumatic Systems**: Found in **automotive assembly lines** (robotic grippers, riveting tools, paint sprayers), **food & pharma packaging** (clean, oil-free air), **dentistry drills**, **air-bus landing gear systems**, and **SCADA/PLC-controlled valve actuators** in oil & gas.
- **Hydraulic Systems**: Found in **injection moulding machines**, **bulldozers and excavators**, **aircraft flight control surfaces** (Boeing 777 uses a fly-by-optics-hydraulic hybrid), **ship steering gear**, **forging presses**, and **high-force clamping systems** in CNC machining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Thrust Force from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter of $D = 50\text{ mm}$ and a rod diameter of $d = 20\text{ mm}$. The supply pressure is $P = 6\text{ bar}$. Calculate:
(a) The theoretical thrust force in the **extension** stroke.
(b) The theoretical thrust force in the **retraction** stroke.
(c) The piston velocity when the incoming flow rate is $Q = 10\text{ L/min}$.

**Given Data:**
$$D = 50 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}, \quad d = 20 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}$$
$$P = 6\text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^{5}\text{ Pa}, \quad Q = 10\text{ L/min} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{60}\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

#### Step 1 — Convert units to SI

We must convert all quantities to base SI units (meters, seconds, Pascals) to obtain force in Newtons directly.

**Step 1 explanation:** SI conversion is non-negotiable in KTU board problems. Bar to Pa conversion uses $1\text{ bar} = 10^5\text{ Pa}$.

#### Step 2 — Calculate Cap-End (Full Bore) Area

The cap-end is the blind end of the cylinder (no rod), so the full piston face is exposed to pressure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi (50 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \\
A_{cap} &= \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \\
A_{cap} &= \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \\
A_{cap} &= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 explanation:** The cap-end area is the full circular cross-section since the rod is on the opposite side.

#### Step 3 — Calculate Rod-End (Annular) Area

The rod-end has the rod passing through it, so the effective area is the **annulus** (full circle minus the rod circle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} \\
A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi ((50 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (20 \times 10^{-3})^2)}{4} \\
A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi (2.5 \times 10^{-3} - 0.4 \times 10^{-3})}{4} \\
A_{rod} &= \frac{\pi \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \\
A_{rod} &= \frac{6.5973 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \\
A_{rod} &= 1.6493 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 explanation:** Note the asymmetric areas — this is why the retraction force is always less than the extension force in a double-acting cylinder, and why retraction speed is correspondingly higher for the same flow rate.

#### Step 4 — (a) Calculate Extension Thrust Force

Pressure acts on the cap-end area to push the rod outward.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ext} &= P \times A_{cap} \\
F_{ext} &= 6 \times 10^{5} \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \\
F_{ext} &= 1178.1\text{ N} \approx 1.178\text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 explanation:** Direct application of $F = P \times A$. The kN unit is preferred for clarity.

#### Step 5 — (b) Calculate Retraction Thrust Force

Pressure acts on the annular rod-end area, but the rod's cross-section still contributes a frictional/adverse load in some analyses. For theoretical force, we use the annular area.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ret} &= P \times A_{rod} \\
F_{ret} &= 6 \times 10^{5} \times 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \\
F_{ret} &= 989.6\text{ N} \approx 0.990\text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 explanation:** The reduction from 1.178 kN to 0.990 kN is a **~16% loss** purely due to rod area. This is a classic KTU numerical.

#### Step 6 — (c) Calculate Extension Velocity

Apply the continuity equation $Q = A \times v$, solving for $v$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} \\
v_{ext} &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} / 60}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \\
v_{ext} &= \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \\
v_{ext} &= 0.0849\text{ m/s} \approx 84.9\text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 explanation:** To convert L/min to m³/s, divide by 60,000 (since 1 L = 10⁻³ m³ and 1 min = 60 s).

#### Step 7 — Calculate Retraction Velocity (Bonus)

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ret} &= \frac{Q}{A_{rod}} = \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6493 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1010\text{ m/s} \approx 101\text{ mm/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 explanation:** Retraction is faster because the same volumetric flow has a smaller area to fill.

#### Step 8 — Compute the Area Ratio and Force Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Area Ratio } \phi &= \frac{A_{cap}}{A_{rod}} = \frac{1.9635}{1.6493} = 1.190 \\
\text{Force Ratio } R_F &= \frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \phi = 1.190
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 explanation:** This ratio is a critical design parameter — selecting a standard rod diameter (e.g., $d = D/2$ gives $\phi = 1.333$) is a common KTU design problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Press Force Multiplication

**Problem Statement:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $d_1 = 25\text{ mm}$ and a large piston of diameter $d_2 = 150\text{ mm}$. A force of $F_1 = 200\text{ N}$ is applied to the small piston. Find the output force $F_2$ on the large piston, assuming no losses.

**Given Data:**
$$d_1 = 0.025\text{ m}, \quad d_2 = 0.150\text{ m}, \quad F_1 = 200\text{ N}$$

#### Step 1 — Compute Small Piston Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{\pi d_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4} \\
A_1 &= 4.9087 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Large Piston Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= \frac{\pi d_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0225}{4} \\
A_2 &= 1.7671 \times 10^{-2}\text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Pascal's Law (Pressure Equivalence)

Since the fluid is incompressible and the system is lossless, pressure generated by the small piston equals pressure experienced by the large piston.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_2 \\
\frac{F_1}{A_1} &= \frac{F_2}{A_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for Output Force

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= F_1 \times \frac{A_2}{A_1} \\
F_2 &= 200 \times \frac{1.7671 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} \\
F_2 &= 200 \times 36.0 \\
F_2 &= 7200\text{ N} = 7.2\text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 explanation:** The mechanical advantage equals the **area ratio** $= (d_2/d_1)^2 = (150/25)^2 = 6^2 = 36$. This single derivation is worth **7 marks** in a typical KTU Module-4 question.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Pressure Generated

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.074 \times 10^{5}\text{ Pa} \approx 4.07\text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 explanation:** A 4.07 bar pressure is trivial for a hydraulic system — this confirms the immense force-multiplying capability of even small fluid pressures.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Pneumatic vs. Hydraulic)

| Attribute | Pneumatic System | Hydraulic System |
|---|---|---|
| Working Fluid | Compressed air (compressible) | Hydraulic oil (incompressible) |
| Operating Pressure | 4 – 10 bar | 50 – 350 bar |
| Force Output | Low to Medium (≤ 25 kN typical) | Very High (up to several MN in presses) |
| Speed | Very fast (1 – 2 m/s) | Moderate (0.1 – 0.5 m/s) |
| Stiffness / Positioning Accuracy | Low (air springiness) | Very High (rigid positioning) |
| Leakage Consequence | Tolerable (air is safe) | Hazardous (fire risk, contamination) |
| Cleanliness | Excellent (no oil) | Requires sealed reservoir and filters |
| Compressibility Effect | Significant | Negligible (β_oil ≈ 1.5 GPa) |
| Cost | Lower (air is free) | Higher (oil, seals, filtration) |
| Noise | High (need silencers) | Low |
| Temperature Sensitivity | High | Moderate |
| Typical Application | Pick-and-place, packaging, tools | Presses, injection moulding, heavy machinery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pneumatic System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A2[Compressor Unit]
    A2 --> A3[Receiver Tank]
    A3 --> A4[FRL Unit: Filter]
    A4 --> A5[FRL Unit: Regulator]
    A5 --> A6[FRL Unit: Lubricator]
    A6 --> A7[Directional Control Valve DCV]
    A7 --> A8[Double Acting Cylinder]
    A8 --> A9[Silencer and Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    A7 -.-> A8
    A8 -.-> A7
```

**Block description (left to right):**
1. **Atmospheric Air Intake** — Suction through a dry-type air filter to keep dust out.
2. **Compressor Unit** — Reciprocating (piston) or rotary screw type; compresses air to ~7–8 bar.
3. **Receiver Tank** — Stores compressed air and dampens pulsations.
4. **FRL Unit — Filter** — Removes moisture and particulates down to 5–40 μm.
5. **FRL Unit — Regulator** — Maintains constant downstream pressure (e.g., 6 bar).
6. **FRL Unit — Lubricator** — Injects atomized oil for internal lubrication of moving parts.
7. **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — 5/2 or 4/2 valve (solenoid or pilot-actuated) routes air to either side of the cylinder.
8. **Double Acting Cylinder** — Produces bidirectional linear force.
9. **Silencer / Exhaust** — Vents spent air to atmosphere with noise reduction.

---

### 4.2 Hydraulic System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Hydraulic Reservoir Tank] --> B2[Suction Strainer]
    B2 --> B3[Hydraulic Pump Gear or Piston Type]
    B3 --> B4[Pressure Relief Valve]
    B4 --> B5[Pressure Gauge]
    B5 --> B6[Directional Control Valve DCV]
    B6 --> B7[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    B7 --> B8[Return Line Filter]
    B8 --> B9[Heat Exchanger Oil Cooler]
    B9 --> B1
    B4 -. Pressure Feedback .-> B3
    B6 -. Return Path .-> B8
```

**Block description:**
1. **Hydraulic Reservoir Tank** — Stores oil, allows air separation, and acts as a heat sink.
2. **Suction Strainer** — Coarse filter protecting the pump.
3. **Hydraulic Pump** — Gear, vane, or piston pump pressurizes oil to 100–250 bar.
4. **Pressure Relief Valve** — Safety valve, normally closed; opens at preset maximum.
5. **Pressure Gauge** — Real-time pressure monitoring for diagnostics.
6. **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — Routes high-pressure oil to cap-end or rod-end.
7. **Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder** — The actuator producing force.
8. **Return Line Filter** — Fine filtration (3–10 μm) of returning oil.
9. **Heat Exchanger** — Air-cooled or water-cooled oil cooler; closes the loop to the tank.

---

### 4.3 Cylinder Operation Sub-Diagram (Double-Acting, Both Systems)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Supply Pressure P_s] --> C2[4/2 or 5/2 DCV]
    C2 --> C3[Cap End Port A]
    C2 --> C4[Rod End Port B]
    C3 --> C5[Pushes Piston to Extend Rod]
    C4 --> C6[Pushes Piston to Retract Rod]
    C5 --> C7[Linear Force F_ext = P x A_cap]
    C6 --> C8[Linear Force F_ret = P x A_rod]
    C7 --> C9[Mechanical Work Output]
    C8 --> C9
```

**Key Insight:** In a double-acting cylinder, **the same supply pressure produces different forces in each direction** because the two effective piston areas are unequal (cap-end vs. rod-end). This asymmetry is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

---

### 4.4 Signal & Control Flow Diagram (Cyber-Physical Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[PLC or Microcontroller] --> S2[Solenoid Coil Driver]
    S2 --> S3[Solenoid Operated DCV]
    S3 --> S4[Flow to Pneumatic Cylinder or Hydraulic Cylinder]
    S4 --> S5[Position Sensor LVDT or Encoder]
    S5 --> S6[Feedback Signal 0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA]
    S6 --> S1
```

**Cyber-Physical Integration Note:** In a smart Industry 4.0 actuator system, the **PLC closes the loop** using position feedback from an LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) or magnetostrictive sensor, ensuring accurate position control — exactly the cyber-physical theme of your course **PCCPT205**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List and explain the functions of the three units in an FRL assembly used in a pneumatic system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) is the air preparation unit of every pneumatic circuit.

1. **Filter (1 mark):** Removes dust, moisture, and oil aerosols from the compressed air. Typical filtration grade is 5–40 μm. It protects downstream valves and cylinders from wear and corrosion.

2. **Regulator (1 mark):** Maintains a constant downstream pressure regardless of upstream fluctuations or downstream consumption. It is set to a specific working pressure (e.g., 6 bar) by adjusting the spring-loaded diaphragm.

3. **Lubricator (1 mark):** Injects a fine mist of lubricating oil into the airstream (proportional to flow rate) to reduce friction in moving parts of valves and cylinders, extending service life.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State Pascal's Law. Why is it the fundamental principle behind all hydraulic systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Statement (1.5 marks):** Pascal's Law states that *pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions, and acts with equal force on equal areas.*

**Significance (1.5 marks):** It is the fundamental principle because it enables **force multiplication** — a small input force on a small-area piston creates the same pressure throughout the fluid, which then produces a proportionally larger output force on a larger-area piston. This is the working basis of hydraulic presses, lifts, jacks, and brakes, giving hydraulics its characteristic high force output.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major components of a pneumatic system and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 30 mm. The supply pressure is 7 bar. If the cylinder is required to push a load of 1200 N against gravity, calculate:
  (i) The theoretical thrust force available during extension.
  (ii) The extension velocity when the incoming flow rate is 25 L/min. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Components of a Pneumatic System (7 marks):**

| # | Component | Function (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air Filter | Removes atmospheric dust at intake. |
| 2 | Compressor | Converts mechanical energy into pneumatic energy by compressing air (6–8 bar). |
| 3 | Receiver Tank | Stores compressed air and smooths pressure pulsations. |
| 4 | FRL Unit | Conditions air — filters, regulates pressure, lubricates. |
| 5 | Directional Control Valve (DCV) | Routes air to cap-end or rod-end of cylinder (e.g., 5/2 solenoid valve). |
| 6 | Double-Acting Cylinder | Converts pneumatic energy to linear mechanical work. |
| 7 | Silencer / Muffler | Reduces exhaust noise when air vents to atmosphere. |

*(Block diagram should accompany — refer to Section 4.1 above.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 80\text{ mm} = 0.080\text{ m}$, $d = 30\text{ mm} = 0.030\text{ m}$, $P = 7\text{ bar} = 7 \times 10^5\text{ Pa}$, $Q = 25\text{ L/min}$.

**Step 1 — Cap-end area:** $A_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}^2$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Extension thrust:** $F_{ext} = P \times A_{cap} = 7 \times 10^5 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 3518.6\text{ N} \approx 3.52\text{ kN}$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Load feasibility check:** Since $F_{ext} = 3.52\text{ kN} > 1.2\text{ kN}$ required, the cylinder can easily handle the load. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Convert flow rate:** $Q = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 4.1667 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Extension velocity:** $v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{cap}} = \dfrac{4.1667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829\text{ m/s} \approx 82.9\text{ mm/s}$ **(2 marks)**

**Final Answers:** $F_{ext} = 3.52\text{ kN}$, $v_{ext} = 82.9\text{ mm/s}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare pneumatic and hydraulic actuation systems on the basis of working fluid, operating pressure, force output, compressibility, cleanliness, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press is used in a forging operation. The small piston has a diameter of 30 mm and the large piston a diameter of 200 mm. An input force of 350 N is applied to the small piston.
  (i) Calculate the pressure developed in the fluid.
  (ii) Calculate the output force on the large piston.
  (iii) If the small piston moves down by 25 mm, find the displacement of the large piston. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** *(Refer to the Analytical Comparison Matrix in Section 3.3 above — students should reproduce at least 6 parameters with 1-line justification each. 1 mark for parameter + 0.5 mark for justification, summed to 7.)*

Key parameters expected: working fluid, operating pressure, force output, compressibility, cleanliness, applications.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d_1 = 30\text{ mm} = 0.030\text{ m}$, $d_2 = 200\text{ mm} = 0.200\text{ m}$, $F_1 = 350\text{ N}$, $s_1 = 25\text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Small piston area:** $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — (i) Pressure developed:** 
$$
P = \dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{350}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.95 \times 10^5\text{ Pa} \approx 4.95\text{ bar}
$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Large piston area:** $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (0.200)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2}\text{ m}^2$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — (ii) Output force:** 
$$
F_2 = P \times A_2 = 4.95 \times 10^5 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} = 15{,}551\text{ N} \approx 15.55\text{ kN}
$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — (iii) Displacement of large piston (incompressibility, conservation of volume):**
$$
A_1 \cdot s_1 = A_2 \cdot s_2 \implies s_2 = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} \times s_1 = \dfrac{1}{44.44} \times 25 = 0.5625\text{ mm}
$$ **(2 marks)**

**Final Answers:** $P = 4.95\text{ bar}$, $F_2 = 15.55\text{ kN}$, $s_2 = 0.56\text{ mm}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Examiner's Eye View (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion (–2 marks typical):** Writing $F = 6 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 0.01178$ N by forgetting the bar→Pa conversion ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$). **Always** write down the conversion in Step 1.
> 2. **Confusing cap-end vs. rod-end area (–2 marks):** Using $A_{cap}$ for the retraction stroke. Memorize: **Extension = full bore, Retraction = annular (full minus rod).**
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract rod area (–3 marks in 7-mark sub-question):** When asked for "extension force", some students incorrectly subtract the rod area.
> 4. **Not checking load feasibility (–1 mark):** When given a load, you should explicitly state whether the calculated force exceeds it.
> 5. **Mixing up $F = P \times A$ with $F = P/A$ (–3 marks):** This is a high-frequency mistake in board papers. **Pressure = Force ÷ Area**, so **Force = Pressure × Area**.
> 6. **Omitting the block diagram in 7-mark descriptive questions (–2 marks):** The KTU rubric explicitly awards marks for a "neat sketch/block diagram" — never skip it.
> 7. **Not specifying assumptions (–1 mark):** In derivations, always state "neglecting friction, leakage, and compressibility" before applying the formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pneumatic** systems use **compressed air** (compressible); **Hydraulic** systems use **incompressible oil**.
- Pascal's Law is the **governing principle** for both, but is more rigidly applicable in hydraulics.
- Standard industrial pressures: **Pneumatic ≈ 6 bar**, **Hydraulic ≈ 100–250 bar**.
- **FRL** = Filter-Regulator-Lubricator is the universal air preparation block.
- Core formulas: $F = P \times A$, $A = \pi D^2/4$, $A_{rod} = \pi (D^2 - d^2)/4$, $v = Q/A$, $t = L \cdot A / Q$.
- Force multiplication in hydraulic press: $F_2 / F_1 = A_2 / A_1 = (d_2/d_1)^2$.
- Volume conservation: $A_1 s_1 = A_2 s_2$ (incompressibility).
- **Double-acting cylinder** produces higher force in extension than in retraction (due to rod area).
- **Hydraulic power** (Watts) $= P \times Q$ — a key unit to remember.
- Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil $\beta \approx 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa makes it effectively incompressible.
- **Cyber-physical link:** PLC/microcontroller + solenoid valve + position sensor (LVDT/encoder) = closed-loop pneumatic or hydraulic actuator — a direct application of Industry 4.0 principles.
- Always **convert all units to SI** at the start of any numerical problem.
- **Answer-writing tip:** Begin every descriptive answer with a 1-line definition, follow with a block diagram, then a component-wise explanation, then a comparison table wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Actuation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ Module 4 — Actuation Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Actuation System?

An **Actuation System** is a sub-system of a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) that converts a control command — typically an electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal signal — into a **physical action** such as motion, force, torque, pressure, or displacement. In the closed-loop CPS architecture, the actuator sits at the **plant interface** and is the final energy-domain conversion element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *An actuator is a device that transforms an input control signal (electrical, electronic, pneumatic, hydraulic, etc.) into a physical output, generally a mechanical motion or force, in response to that signal.*

Mathematically, the actuator transfer function in the **frequency domain** can be represented as a mapping between input energy and output work:

$$
Y(s) = G_{act}(s) \cdot U(s)
$$

where $U(s)$ is the input command (voltage, current, pressure, or flow), $Y(s)$ is the physical output (position, force, velocity, torque), and $G_{act}(s)$ is the actuator transfer function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscle" of a Machine

Imagine a human body. Your **brain** decides to lift a cup. The **nerves** carry the signal, and your **bicep muscle** actually performs the lifting work by converting chemical energy (from the brain's signal) into mechanical movement. In a machine, the **controller** is the brain, the **wires/bus** are the nerves, and the **actuator** is the muscle. The sensor (in a closed loop) would be the eyes/feel that confirm the cup was lifted.

### 1.3 Classification of Actuation Systems (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

Actuators are classified by the **energy domain** they convert from and the **physical output** they produce.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Primary Categories (must know):**
> 1. **Hydraulic Actuators** — fluid-power (oil) → mechanical motion/force
> 2. **Pneumatic Actuators** — fluid-power (air) → mechanical motion/force
> 3. **Electromechanical Actuators** — electrical → mechanical (DC motors, stepper, servo, solenoids)
> 4. **Piezoelectric Actuators** — electrical voltage → nanometer-scale displacement
> 5. **Thermal / Shape-Memory Actuators** — thermal → mechanical (less common in industrial CPS)

### 1.4 Role in Cyber-Physical Systems

In a closed-loop CPS, the actuator is the **plant-side termination** of the controller. The generalized CPS block diagram flow is:

**Sensor → ADC → Controller (Decision Logic) → DAC → Power Amplifier → Actuator → Plant → Physical Output**

| CPS Stage | Energy Domain | Example Component |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | Physical → Electrical | Thermistor, LVDT |
| Computation | Electrical → Logical | Microcontroller, FPGA |
| Actuation | Electrical → Physical | DC Motor, Solenoid, Piezo stack |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics** you must memorize for KTU:
> - **Stroke length** (mm) — maximum linear displacement
> - **Force / Torque** (N / N·m) — output mechanical effort
> - **Bandwidth** (Hz) — speed of response
> - **Efficiency** (η) — ratio of output work to input energy
> - **Resolution** — smallest controllable step (especially for stepper / piezo)
> - **Power density** (W/kg) — power per unit mass

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydraulic Actuators

A hydraulic actuator uses an **incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil)** under high pressure to produce linear or rotary motion. The pressure is generated by a pump and regulated by control valves.

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
1. Electric motor drives a **hydraulic pump**, which pressurizes the fluid.
2. The high-pressure fluid is routed through a **directional control valve (DCV)**.
3. The pressurized fluid enters a **cylinder**, pushing a piston, which produces linear force.
4. A **relief valve** limits the maximum system pressure for safety.

### 2.1.2 Why Hydraulic?
- **High force/power density** (can produce forces in hundreds of kN).
- **Stiff and precise** under load — excellent for heavy machinery.
- Used in excavators, aircraft control surfaces, injection moulding, and CNC.

### 2.1.3 Governing Equation
The force developed by a hydraulic cylinder is:

$$
F = P \cdot A - P_{back} \cdot A_{piston-rod-side}
$$

For a **double-acting cylinder with equal-area piston** (rare in practice), this simplifies to:

$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

where:
- $F$ = output force in **Newtons (N)**
- $P$ = supply pressure in **Pascals (Pa)**
- $A$ = piston cross-sectional area in **m²**

For a cylinder with a piston rod (asymmetric), the effective areas are:

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2)
$$

where $D$ is the bore diameter and $d$ is the rod diameter.

The **flow rate → velocity** relationship is:

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate in m³/s.

## 2.2 Pneumatic Actuators

A pneumatic actuator works on the same principle as a hydraulic one, but uses **compressible air/gas** at lower pressures (typically 4–8 bar).

### 2.2.1 Key Differences vs. Hydraulic

| Property | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Pressure range | 50–700 bar | 2–10 bar |
| Force density | Very high | Moderate |
| Speed | Slower, smooth | Fast, with springiness |
| Cleanliness | Risk of oil leaks | Clean, suitable for food/pharma |
| Cost | Higher (pump, filtration) | Lower (compressor) |

### 2.2.2 Force Equation (Pneumatic Cylinder)

$$
F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is the mechanical efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95) accounting for friction and seal drag.

> [!NOTE]
> Because air is **compressible**, pneumatic systems are inherently **non-linear**. The isothermal relation $P \cdot V = \text{const}$ must be considered for accurate dynamic modelling.

## 2.3 Electromechanical Actuators — DC Motors

A **DC motor** converts DC electrical energy into rotational mechanical energy. It is the most common actuator in low-to-medium power CPS applications.

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
- Current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field experience a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$.
- The interaction produces a **torque** on the rotor, causing rotation.
- The commutator and brushes reverse current direction every half-turn to maintain continuous rotation (in brushed DC).

### 2.3.2 Governing Equations (Brushed DC Motor)

**Electrical side (armature circuit):**

$$
V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}
$$

**Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = K_e \cdot \omega
$$

**Motor torque:**

$$
T_m = K_t \cdot I_a
$$

**Mechanical side:**

$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega + T_L = T_m
$$

where:
- $V_a$ = applied armature voltage (V)
- $E_b$ = back-EMF (V)
- $I_a$ = armature current (A)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance (Ω)
- $L_a$ = armature inductance (H)
- $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $K_t$ = torque constant (N·m/A)
- $J$ = rotor moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $T_L$ = load torque (N·m)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Unit Consistency:** In SI units, $K_e = K_t$ numerically. This is a **very common KTU question** — students often confuse them. The back-EMF constant is in V·s/rad, the torque constant is in N·m/A, and they are dimensionally and numerically equal in SI.

### 2.3.3 Transfer Function of DC Motor

Taking the Laplace transform of the electrical and mechanical equations (with $T_L = 0$):

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_e K_t}
$$

The standard first-order approximation (ignoring $L_a$) gives:

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}
$$

with motor gain $K_m = K_t / (K_e K_t + B R_a)$ and mechanical time constant $\tau_m = J R_a / (K_e K_t + B R_a)$.

## 2.4 Stepper Motors

A **stepper motor** rotates in **discrete angular steps**, making it ideal for **open-loop position control** without needing a position sensor.

### 2.4.1 Step Angle Formula

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N}
$$

where $N$ is the number of steps per revolution.

Common values:
- **Full step** = 1.8° (200 steps/rev) — most common hybrid stepper
- **Half step** = 0.9° (400 steps/rev) — using dual-phase excitation
- **Microstep** = down to 0.0072° — sinusoidal current control

### 2.4.2 Holding Torque

The torque a stepper can hold against an external load without losing position is called **holding torque** $T_h$ (in N·m). At low speeds, output torque is near $T_h$; at high speeds, torque drops due to back-EMF and inductance.

## 2.5 Servo Motors (Closed-Loop DC/Brushless)

A **servo motor** combines a DC/BLDC motor with a **position sensor (encoder/potentiometer)** and a **closed-loop controller** for precise position, velocity, or torque control.

### 2.5.1 Control Block Diagram (Conceptual)

**Command Position → Error → PID Controller → PWM → H-Bridge → DC/BLDC Motor → Gear Train → Output Shaft → Encoder (Feedback)**

### 2.5.2 Standard Hobby Servo (PWM-driven)
Hobby servos use **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)** for command:
- **1.0 ms pulse** → 0° (or one extreme)
- **1.5 ms pulse** → 90° (neutral)
- **2.0 ms pulse** → 180° (other extreme)
- Frame period = 20 ms (50 Hz update rate)

The position command is:

$$
\theta_{cmd} = K_{pwm} \cdot (t_{pulse} - t_0)
$$

where $K_{pwm}$ is the pulse-to-angle conversion gain (typically 90°/ms or 100°/ms depending on servo).

## 2.6 Piezoelectric Actuators

A **piezoelectric actuator** uses the **inverse piezoelectric effect**: an applied voltage causes a crystalline material (PZT — lead zirconate titanate) to deform by a small amount.

### 2.6.1 Displacement Equation

$$
\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V
$$

where:
- $\Delta L$ = free displacement (m)
- $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric strain coefficient (m/V), typically $d_{33} \approx 300 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V for PZT
- $V$ = applied voltage (V)

For a stack of $n$ layers:

$$
\Delta L_{stack} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V
$$

### 2.6.2 Key Properties
- **Resolution** — sub-nanometer (effectively infinite for most CPS)
- **Bandwidth** — extremely high (kHz range)
- **Force** — can produce hundreds of N in a small package
- **Stroke** — very small (typically 0.1% of length), hence often used in **stack** configurations
- **Applications** — AFM probes, micro-positioning stages, fuel injectors, precision optics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Piezoelectric actuators are **inherently capacitive** — they draw very little current in steady state, but high inrush current when voltage changes rapidly. This is critical for selecting the **driver amplifier** in a KTU design question.

## 2.7 Solenoids (Linear Electromechanical)

A **solenoid** is a coil that produces linear motion of a ferromagnetic plunger when energized.

$$
F_{solenoid} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot I^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{dx}
$$

where $L$ is the coil inductance and $x$ is the plunger position. The force depends on the **gradient of inductance with position** — this is why solenoids have a strong "pull-in" force near the seat, and weaker holding force.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table summarizes **every formula you must memorize** for Module 4. Memorize it cold for the 14-mark questions.

| # | Actuator Type | Key Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Cylinder | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ (N), $P$ (Pa), $A$ (m²) |
| 2 | Pneumatic Cylinder | $F = P \cdot A \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = mech. efficiency (0.85–0.95) |
| 3 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = Q / A$ | $Q$ (m³/s), $v$ (m/s) |
| 4 | DC Motor Torque | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | $K_t$ (N·m/A), $I_a$ (A) |
| 5 | DC Motor Back-EMF | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ (V·s/rad) |
| 6 | Armature Voltage | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ | Steady-state: $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ |
| 7 | Motor Power | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | $P$ (W) |
| 8 | Stepper Step Angle | $\theta_s = 360° / N$ | $N$ = steps/rev |
| 9 | Servo PWM | $t_{pulse} \in [1.0, 2.0]$ ms, $T_{frame} = 20$ ms | 50 Hz update |
| 10 | Piezo Displacement | $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ | $d_{33}$ (m/V), $n$ = layer count |
| 11 | Solenoid Force | $F = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}$ | $L$ (H), $x$ (m) |
| 12 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = P \cdot Q$ | $P$ (Pa), $Q$ (m³/s), $P$ (W) |
| 13 | Motor Transfer Function | $\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_e K_t}$ | Standard 2nd order form |
| 14 | Motor Time Constant (mech.) | $\tau_m = J R_a / (K_e K_t + B R_a)$ | seconds (s) |
| 15 | Lorentz Force (motor) | $F = B I L$ | $B$ (T), $I$ (A), $L$ (m) |

## 2.9 Real-World Utility of Actuation Systems

| Domain | Application | Actuator Type |
|---|---|---|
| Robotics | Robotic arm joints | Servo / BLDC |
| Automotive | Fuel injection | Solenoid / Piezo |
| Aerospace | Flight control surfaces | Hydraulic / EHA |
| Manufacturing | CNC tool feed | Servo motor |
| Medical | Surgical robot / AFM | Piezo / Stepper |
| Industrial Automation | Valve control | Pneumatic |
| Mobile Robots | Wheel drive | DC gear motor |
| MEMS | Micro-mirror / micro-valve | Piezo / Electrostatic |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Output of a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

A double-acting cylinder has pressurized fluid on **both sides** of the piston. The piston rod occupies area on one side, so the effective areas are different.

**Given:** Bore diameter $D = 50$ mm, Rod diameter $d = 25$ mm, Supply pressure $P_s = 100$ bar, Back pressure $P_b = 5$ bar.

**Find:** (a) Effective area on cap side. (b) Effective area on rod side. (c) Extension force. (d) Retraction force.

### Step 1 — Convert units
Convert all lengths to metres and pressures to Pascals to keep SI consistency.

$$
D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}
$$

$$
P_s = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_b = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

### Step 2 — Effective area on the cap (full piston) side

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.050)^2
$$

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0025 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Effective area on the rod side

The rod side area is the full piston area minus the rod cross-section.

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.050^2 - 0.025^2)
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0025 - 0.000625) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.001875
$$

$$
A_{rod} = 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Extension force (fluid on cap side, rod side exhausted)
During extension, supply pressure acts on $A_{cap}$ and back pressure on $A_{rod}$ (opposing).

$$
F_{ext} = P_s \cdot A_{cap} - P_b \cdot A_{rod}
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (10 \times 10^6)(1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(1.4726 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 19635 - 736.3 = 18898.7 \text{ N} \approx 18.9 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 5 — Retraction force (fluid on rod side, cap side exhausted)

$$
F_{ret} = P_s \cdot A_{rod} - P_b \cdot A_{cap}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = (10 \times 10^6)(1.4726 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ret} = 14726 - 981.75 = 13744.25 \text{ N} \approx 13.7 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The retraction force is **lower** than the extension force because the rod-side effective area is smaller. This is a common KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Motor Steady-State Speed Equation

**Given:** $V_a = 24$ V, $R_a = 1.0$ Ω, $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, no load (neglect friction).

**Find:** No-load speed $\omega_{NL}$ and current.

### Step 1 — Apply the steady-state armature equation
At steady state, $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$, so $L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ drops out:

$$
V_a = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

### Step 2 — At no load, $I_a \approx 0$
Therefore $V_a \approx E_b$:

$$
E_b = V_a = 24 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 3 — Use the back-EMF equation
From $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$:

$$
\omega_{NL} = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{24}{0.05}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_{NL} = 480 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to RPM
$$
N_{NL} = \omega_{NL} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 480 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 4583.66 \text{ RPM}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for no-load speed, set $I_a \to 0$ and use $\omega = V_a / K_e$. When asked for **stall torque**, set $\omega = 0$ so $E_b = 0$, giving $I_{stall} = V_a / R_a$ and $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall}$. These two extremes define the motor's **torque-speed characteristic line**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stall Torque of a DC Motor

**Given:** Same motor as above, $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A.

**Find:** Stall torque $T_{stall}$ and starting current.

### Step 1 — At stall, rotor does not rotate
$\omega = 0$, hence $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega = 0$.

### Step 2 — Apply KVL to armature circuit
With $E_b = 0$:

$$
V_a = I_a R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{stall} = \frac{V_a}{R_a} = \frac{24}{1.0} = 24 \text{ A}
$$

### Step 3 — Stall torque
$$
T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.05 \cdot 24 = 1.2 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In a real motor, this inrush current can damage windings. This is why **soft starters** or **PWM current limiting** are used in CPS — a common KTU design question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stepper Motor Resolution for a CNC Application

**Given:** A CNC plotter requires linear resolution of 0.05 mm/step. The lead screw has a pitch of 5 mm/rev (one full turn = 5 mm linear travel). The motor is directly coupled (no gearbox).

**Find:** Required stepper motor step angle and microstepping factor.

### Step 1 — Find the number of steps per revolution needed
For 0.05 mm per step, the motor must step once per 0.05 mm. Since 1 revolution moves the load by 5 mm:

$$
N = \frac{5 \text{ mm/rev}}{0.05 \text{ mm/step}} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to step angle

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6° / \text{step}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine microstep factor
A standard 1.8° stepper has 200 steps/rev, which gives 5 mm / 200 = 0.025 mm/step (already finer than needed). However, to achieve smoother motion, the user may use **5× microstepping**:

$$
\text{Steps effective per rev} = 200 \times 5 = 1000
$$

$$
\text{Linear resolution} = \frac{5}{1000} = 0.005 \text{ mm/step} = 5 \, \mu\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical insight:** If a standard 1.8° motor is used at 5× microstepping, we get 1000 effective steps/rev, yielding 5 µm resolution — more than sufficient for the 0.05 mm requirement.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Piezo Stack Actuator Stroke

**Given:** A piezo stack actuator has $n = 100$ layers, each of PZT material with $d_{33} = 300 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. Maximum allowable voltage $V_{max} = 150$ V.

**Find:** Maximum free displacement $\Delta L_{max}$.

### Step 1 — Apply the stack displacement equation
$$
\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$
\Delta L_{max} = 100 \cdot (300 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 150
$$

$$
\Delta L_{max} = 100 \cdot 4.5 \times 10^{-8} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L_{max} = 4.5 \, \mu\text{m}}
$$

### Step 3 — Blocking Force
PZT stacks can also produce a **blocking force** (max force with zero displacement). For a PZT stack with cross-section $A$ and Young's modulus $Y$:

$$
F_{block} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot V \cdot A \cdot Y}{L_{stack}} = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V \cdot Y \cdot A / L_{stack}
$$

(For example, with $A = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m² and $Y = 60$ GPa, $F_{block} \approx$ several hundred newtons.)

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Actuator Selection (KTU Design Style)

This matrix summarizes the **decision criteria** you should use when a KTU question asks: *"Select an appropriate actuator for a given application."*

| Selection Criterion | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | DC Motor | Stepper | Servo | Piezo |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Force / Torque | **Very High** | Moderate | Medium | Medium | Medium | High (small stroke) |
| Speed | Slow–Med | **Fast** | Med–High | Low–Med | Med–High | Very High (BW) |
| Precision | High | Low | Med (with FB) | High (open loop) | **Very High** | **Highest** |
| Stroke | Long | Long | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous | **Very Short** |
| Cost | High | Low | Low | Low | Med | Very High |
| Power Density | High | Medium | High | Medium | High | **Very High** |
| Cleanliness | Poor (oil) | **Clean** | Clean | Clean | Clean | Clean |
| Open-Loop OK? | No | No | No | **Yes** | No | No (cap drift) |
| Typical CPS Use | Heavy plant | Valves | Toy bots | 3D printers | Robots | AFM, micro-positioning |

### Selection Decision Tree (Logic)

1. **Need high force (>10 kN)?** → Hydraulic
2. **Need clean, fast, low force?** → Pneumatic
3. **Need continuous rotation, variable speed?** → DC/BLDC motor
4. **Need open-loop position control?** → Stepper
5. **Need closed-loop precise position/torque?** → Servo
6. **Need nanometre precision or kHz bandwidth?** → Piezo

---

## 3.7 Comparison: Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic (Board-Exam Frequently Asked)

| Aspect | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Compressibility (β) | $\approx 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa | $\approx 1 \times 10^5$ Pa |
| Pressure | High (up to 700 bar) | Low (typically ≤ 10 bar) |
| Force per unit area | High | Low |
| Accuracy | High (stiff) | Low (springy) |
| Speed of response | Slower | Faster |
| Energy storage | Inherent (accumulator) | Inherent (tank) |
| Noise | Quiet | Noisy (exhaust) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Safety | Fire risk (oil) | Safer |
| Typical apps | Presses, lifts, mobile hydraulics | Assembly automation, pick-and-place |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Actuation System in a CPS

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete CPS loop** with a DC motor actuator, illustrating how the controller, power amplifier, and sensor feedback interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    setPoint[Setpoint Reference] --> summingJunction{Summing Junction}
    sensorFB[Position Sensor Feedback] --> summingJunction
    summingJunction -->|error signal| controller[PID Controller]
    controller -->|control voltage| dacUnit[DAC and PWM Generator]
    dacUnit -->|gate drive| powerAmp[H-Bridge Power Amplifier]
    powerAmp -->|armature voltage| dcMotor[DC Motor Actuator]
    dcMotor -->|rotary motion| gearTrain[Gear Train]
    gearTrain -->|reduced speed and increased torque| outputShaft[Output Shaft and Load]
    outputShaft -->|mechanical response| plantOut[Plant Physical Output]
    plantOut --> sensorFB
    dcMotor -.->|back EMF feedback| powerAmp
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **summing junction** computes the error between desired and actual position.
- The **PID controller** outputs a control voltage proportional to the error.
- The **PWM generator / DAC** translates this into a duty-cycled switching signal.
- The **H-bridge power amplifier** provides bidirectional current to the motor.
- The **gear train** reduces speed and increases torque.
- The **sensor** (encoder/potentiometer) closes the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Actuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elecMotor[Electric Motor] --> pump[Hydraulic Pump]
    pump -->|pressurized oil| reservoir[Oil Reservoir and Filter]
    reservoir -->|suction line| pump
    pump -->|high pressure flow| reliefValve[Pressure Relief Valve]
    reliefValve -->|excess oil| reservoir
    pump -->|regulated flow| dcv[Directional Control Valve DCV]
    dcv -->|forward or reverse flow| cylinder[Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    cylinder -->|linear force| pistonRod[Piston Rod and Load]
    pistonRod -->|physical output| mechanicalLoad[Mechanical Work Output]
    dcv -.->|spool position control| dcv
```

**Key elements:**
- The **relief valve** sets the **maximum system pressure** — critical safety element.
- The **DCV** is the actuator's "control valve" that routes fluid to either side of the piston.
- The **reservoir** holds fluid, allows cooling, and lets air escape.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pneumatic Actuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    atmAir[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> filterReg[Filter Regulator Lubricator FRL]
    filterReg -->|clean dry air at set pressure| compressor[Compressor and Receiver Tank]
    compressor -->|regulated supply| dcvP[3 by 2 Directional Control Valve]
    dcvP -->|forward stroke air| pCyl[Double-Acting Pneumatic Cylinder]
    pCyl -->|linear motion| pRod[Piston Rod and Load]
    pRod --> mechanicalWorkP[Mechanical Work]
    pCyl -.->|exhaust air| silencer[Silencer and Muffler]
    silencer --> atmAir
```

**Key elements:**
- The **FRL unit** removes moisture and contaminants and adds oil mist for lubrication.
- The **3/2 DCV** is a 3-port, 2-position valve — simplest form of pneumatic control.
- **Silencers** are required on exhaust ports to meet noise regulations.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Control with Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userCmd[User Command from Host] --> mcu[Microcontroller MCU]
    mcu -->|step pulses| driverIC[Stepper Driver IC e.g. A4988]
    driverIC -->|phase A and phase B| stepper[Hybrid Stepper Motor]
    stepper -->|rotary motion| leadScrew[Lead Screw and Linear Stage]
    leadScrew -->|linear position| linearAxis[Linear Axis Position]
    linearAxis -->|physical output| printBed[3D Printer Bed or CNC Tool]
    mcu -.->|enable and direction| driverIC
    psu[DC Power Supply] --> driverIC
```

**Key elements:**
- The **MCU** generates step pulses (one per step angle) and a direction signal.
- The **driver IC** handles current chopping and microstepping.
- The **lead screw** converts rotary motion to precise linear motion.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Actuator in Closed-Loop Micro-Positioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hostCtrl[Host Computer or DSP] --> dspCtrl[DSP Controller]
    dspCtrl -->|high voltage analog command| hvAmp[High-Voltage Piezo Amplifier]
    hvAmp -->|voltage 0 to 150 V| piezoStack[Piezo Stack Actuator]
    piezoStack -->|nanometer displacement| flexure[Flexure Guide Mechanism]
    flexure -->|amplified pure translation| stage[XY Nanopositioning Stage]
    stage -->|positioned sample| sample[Sample Under Test]
    sample -->|strain gauge signal| sgSensor[Strain Gauge Position Sensor]
    sgSensor -->|feedback voltage| dspCtrl
```

**Key elements:**
- The **high-voltage amplifier** is mandatory — piezo stacks require 100–200 V for full stroke.
- The **flexure mechanism** provides frictionless, hysteresis-free motion amplification.
- The **strain gauge** provides sub-nm position feedback for the closed loop.

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Servo Motor with PWM Command Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    joystick[Analog Joystick or PWM Source] --> pwmCmd[PWM Command Signal 50 Hz]
    pwmCmd --> servoCtrl[Internal Servo Controller Board]
    servoCtrl -->|error vs internal pot| hBridge[H-Bridge Motor Driver]
    hBridge -->|bidirectional current| dcMotorS[Small DC Motor]
    dcMotorS -->|high speed rotation| gearTrainS[Plastic Gear Train]
    gearTrainS -->|reduced speed high torque| outputHorn[Output Spline Horn]
    outputHorn -->|angular position 0 to 180 deg| mechanicalLink[Mechanical Linkage]
    mechanicalLink -->|feedback rotation| pot[Internal Potentiometer]
    pot -->|analog feedback| servoCtrl
```

**Key elements:**
- The internal **potentiometer** is the position sensor built into hobby servos.
- The **H-bridge** enables bidirectional rotation (unlike a single transistor driver).
- The **gear train** reduces high motor speed to usable output torque/speed.

---
