# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions are short-answer conceptual questions. They test definitions, principles, and quick numerical substitutions. Provide direct, 3-4 line answers.

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define an actuator. Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **actuator** is a device that converts a control command (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal) into a **physical action** such as motion, force, or torque.

**Difference:**

| Aspect | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of energy | Physical → Electrical (or other domain) | Electrical (or other) → Physical |
| Role in CPS | Measures the plant state | Drives the plant state |
| Example | Thermistor, LVDT | DC motor, solenoid, piezo stack |

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State and explain the principle of operation of a piezoelectric actuator. Mention one application.**

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoelectric actuator** operates on the **inverse piezoelectric effect** — when an electric voltage is applied across a piezo crystal (e.g., PZT), the crystal mechanically deforms.

The displacement is given by $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$, where $n$ is the layer count, $d_{33}$ is the strain coefficient, and $V$ is the applied voltage.

**Application:** Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) probe positioning, where sub-nanometer precision is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions in KTU ESE carry 14 marks. They have internal choice: **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each part-question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Solve both sub-parts in detail for full marks.

---

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **closed-loop DC motor position control system** using a PID controller. Derive the transfer function of the armature-controlled DC motor and discuss the effect of back-EMF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC servomotor has the following specifications: armature resistance $R_a = 2$ Ω, armature inductance $L_a = 0.5$ H, torque constant $K_t = 0.1$ N·m/A, back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.1$ V·s/rad, moment of inertia $J = 0.01$ kg·m², viscous friction $B = 0.001$ N·m·s/rad. The motor is supplied with $V_a = 50$ V. Calculate:
   (i) The no-load speed in RPM.
   (ii) The stall torque.
   (iii) The motor time constant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Block Diagram (described in words):**

The closed-loop position control system consists of: a **reference input** (desired position) → **summing junction** → **PID controller** → **PWM/H-bridge power amplifier** → **DC motor** (with load) → **output position** → **encoder/potentiometer** → **feedback to summing junction**.

**Transfer Function Derivation:**

**Armature electrical equation:**

$$
V_a = L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + R_a I_a + E_b
$$

**Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = K_e \omega
$$

**Mechanical equation:**

$$
J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega + T_L = T_m = K_t I_a
$$

Taking the Laplace transform of the electrical equation (assuming $T_L = 0$):

$$
V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_e \Omega(s)
$$

$$
I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_e \Omega(s)}{L_a s + R_a}
$$

Taking the Laplace transform of the mechanical equation:

$$
(J s + B) \Omega(s) = K_t I_a(s)
$$

$$
\Omega(s) = \frac{K_t I_a(s)}{J s + B}
$$

**Substitute $I_a(s)$ into the second equation:**

$$
\Omega(s) = \frac{K_t \left[ V_a(s) - K_e \Omega(s) \right]}{(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a)}
$$

$$
\Omega(s) (J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) = K_t V_a(s) - K_t K_e \Omega(s)
$$

$$
\Omega(s) \left[ (J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_t K_e \right] = K_t V_a(s)
$$

**Therefore the transfer function is:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(J s + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_t K_e}}
$$

**Effect of back-EMF:**
- The back-EMF $E_b = K_e \omega$ acts as a **negative feedback** within the motor itself.
- It **stabilizes the speed**: as speed increases, $E_b$ rises, reducing the net voltage across $R_a$ and $L_a$, hence reducing current and torque.
- Without back-EMF, the motor would accelerate uncontrollably.
- It also introduces a **damping term** $K_t K_e$ in the denominator, reducing oscillations.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given data:**
$R_a = 2$ Ω, $L_a = 0.5$ H, $K_t = 0.1$ N·m/A, $K_e = 0.1$ V·s/rad, $J = 0.01$ kg·m², $B = 0.001$ N·m·s/rad, $V_a = 50$ V.

**(i) No-load speed:**

At no load, $T_L = 0$ and $I_a \to 0$ (neglecting friction), so $E_b \approx V_a$:

$$
\omega_{NL} = \frac{V_a}{K_e} = \frac{50}{0.1} = 500 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to RPM:

$$
N_{NL} = 500 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 500 \times 9.5493 = 4774.65 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Answer: $\omega_{NL} \approx 4774.65$ RPM**

**(ii) Stall torque:**

At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$:

$$
I_{stall} = \frac{V_a}{R_a} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ A}
$$

$$
T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.1 \cdot 25 = 2.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Answer: $T_{stall} = 2.5$ N·m**

**(iii) Motor time constant:**

The mechanical time constant is:

$$
\tau_m = \frac{J R_a}{K_e K_t + B R_a}
$$

$$
\tau_m = \frac{0.01 \cdot 2}{(0.1)(0.1) + (0.001)(2)} = \frac{0.02}{0.01 + 0.002} = \frac{0.02}{0.012}
$$

$$
\tau_m = 1.667 \text{ s}
$$

The electrical time constant is:

$$
\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ s}
$$

**Answer: $\tau_m \approx 1.67$ s, $\tau_e = 0.25$ s**

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$, the electrical dynamics are much faster than the mechanical. This is why the **first-order approximation** $G(s) = K_m / (\tau_m s + 1)$ is valid for most DC motor design problems.

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **hydraulic and pneumatic actuation systems** in terms of working fluid, pressure range, force output, accuracy, cleanliness, cost, and typical applications. Explain the working of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** with a neat sketch, and derive the expression for its extension and retraction forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 40$ mm, and is supplied with $P_s = 150$ bar with back pressure $P_b = 10$ bar. Calculate:
   (i) The effective piston area on the cap side and rod side.
   (ii) The extension force.
   (iii) The retraction force.
   (iv) The piston velocity if the pump delivers $Q = 20$ L/min during extension. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Pressure | 50–700 bar | 2–10 bar |
| Force | Very high | Moderate |
| Accuracy | High (stiff) | Low (compressible) |
| Cleanliness | Poor (leakage) | Clean |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Applications | Excavators, presses, aircraft | Pick-and-place, valve control |

**Double-acting hydraulic cylinder — working:**

It has two ports: A (cap side) and B (rod side). Pressurized oil is directed via a DCV:
- **Extension:** Oil enters port A, pushes piston outward; oil on rod side exhausts through port B.
- **Retraction:** Oil enters port B, pushes piston inward; oil on cap side exhausts through port A.

**Force derivation (covered in detail in Section 3.1):**

The extension and retraction forces are:

$$
F_{ext} = P_s A_{cap} - P_b A_{rod}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P_s A_{rod} - P_b A_{cap}
$$

where

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2, \quad A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm = 0.080 m, $d = 40$ mm = 0.040 m, $P_s = 150$ bar = $150 \times 10^5$ Pa, $P_b = 10$ bar = $10 \times 10^5$ Pa, $Q = 20$ L/min.

**(i) Effective areas:**

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080^2 - 0.040^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0064 - 0.0016) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0048
$$

$$
A_{rod} = 3.7699 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**(ii) Extension force:**

$$
F_{ext} = P_s A_{cap} - P_b A_{rod}
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (150 \times 10^5)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) - (10 \times 10^5)(3.7699 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 75398 - 3769.9 = 71628 \text{ N} \approx 71.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Retraction force:**

$$
F_{ret} = P_s A_{rod} - P_b A_{cap}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = (150 \times 10^5)(3.7699 \times 10^{-3}) - (10 \times 10^5)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ret} = 56549 - 5026.5 = 51522 \text{ N} \approx 51.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iv) Piston velocity during extension:**

Convert $Q$: $20$ L/min $= 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/min $= \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s $= 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0663 \text{ m/s} = 66.3 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $A_{cap} = 5.03 \times 10^{-3}$ m², $A_{rod} = 3.77 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $F_{ext} = 71.6$ kN
- $F_{ret} = 51.5$ kN
- $v = 66.3$ mm/s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Most students lose 2–3 marks by forgetting to convert bar → Pa (multiply by $10^5$). Always show the conversion step.
> 2. **Asymmetric area confusion:** Students frequently use $A_{cap}$ for both extension and retraction, losing 1–2 marks. The rod side has **smaller** effective area.
> 3. **Confusing $K_e$ and $K_t$:** In SI units they are numerically equal, but they have **different units** ($K_e$ in V·s/rad, $K_t$ in N·m/A). Always state units.
> 4. **Back-EMF forgotten:** Many students write $V_a = I_a R_a$ even at non-zero speed. The correct steady-state equation is $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$.
> 5. **No-load vs. stall:** $T_{stall}$ uses $I_{stall} = V_a / R_a$ (not no-load current). $\omega_{NL}$ uses $E_b = V_a$ (not $E_b = 0$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Actuation Systems**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Actuator = **energy domain converter** (electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic/thermal → mechanical).
- ☐ In CPS, the actuator is the **final plant-side element** in the loop.
- ☐ Classification: Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electromechanical (DC, stepper, servo), Piezo, Thermal.

### Hydraulic Systems
- ☐ Working fluid: **oil** (incompressible). Pressure: up to 700 bar.
- ☐ Force: $F = P \cdot A$. Velocity: $v = Q / A$.
- ☐ Asymmetric cylinder: $A_{cap} > A_{rod}$ → extension force > retraction force.
- ☐ Components: pump, reservoir, DCV, relief valve, cylinder.

### Pneumatic Systems
- ☐ Working fluid: **air** (compressible). Pressure: 2–10 bar.
- ☐ Force: $F = P A \eta$. Lower force density but cleaner and faster.
- ☐ Always include FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator).

### DC Motors
- ☐ $T = K_t I_a$, $E_b = K_e \omega$. In SI: $K_e = K_t$ numerically.
- ☐ Steady state: $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$.
- ☐ No-load: $I_a \to 0$, $\omega_{NL} = V_a / K_e$.
- ☐ Stall: $\omega = 0$, $I_{stall} = V_a / R_a$, $T_{stall} = K_t I_{stall}$.
- ☐ Transfer function: $\Omega(s) / V_a(s) = K_t / [(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_t K_e]$.
- ☐ Back-EMF provides **inherent damping**.

### Stepper Motors
- ☐ Open-loop position control using discrete step angles.
- ☐ Step angle: $\theta_s = 360° / N$. Standard 1.8° (200 steps/rev).
- ☐ Holding torque: max static torque without losing position.
- ☐ Microstepping allows fractional steps for smoother motion.

### Servo Motors
- ☐ Closed-loop motor + encoder + controller.
- ☐ Hobby servo: PWM 1.0–2.0 ms pulse, 50 Hz frame.
- ☐ Industrial servos use fieldbus (EtherCAT, CAN, Modbus) for command.

### Piezoelectric Actuators
- ☐ Inverse piezoelectric effect. $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$.
- ☐ Sub-nanometer resolution, kHz bandwidth, very small stroke.
- ☐ Stack configuration for amplification. Requires **high-voltage driver**.
- ☐ Inherently capacitive load — driver must supply rapid current transients.

### Solenoids
- ☐ $F = \frac{1}{2} I^2 (dL/dx)$. Force depends on inductance gradient.
- ☐ Strong pull-in near seat; weak holding far from seat.

### KTU Exam Strategy
- ☐ Always state units. Convert bar → Pa, mm → m, RPM → rad/s.
- ☐ Draw the block diagram / sketch before any calculation.
- ☐ For 14-mark derivations, show all algebraic steps.
- ☐ For design questions, use the analytical matrix to justify actuator selection.
- ☐ Memorize all 15 formulas in the KTU Formula Sheet table.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic and Hydraulic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic and Hydraulic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Pneumatic Systems — Formal Definition

A **Pneumatic System** is an engineered energy-transmission and control system that uses a **compressible working fluid** (typically atmospheric air, but also nitrogen or other inert gases) confined within a closed network of pipes, valves, and actuators to perform mechanical work. The system converts the **potential energy of compressed gas** (stored as pressure) into **kinetic energy** of moving mechanical parts, primarily through linear motion devices (cylinders) or rotary motion devices (pneumatic motors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A pneumatic system is classified as a *fluid power system* where the fluid is a **gas**. Because gases are compressible, pneumatic systems are inherently compliant, making them ideal for low-to-medium force, high-speed, and inherently safe applications (e.g., food processing, mining, semiconductor manufacturing).

### 1.2 Hydraulic Systems — Formal Definition

A **Hydraulic System** is an engineered energy-transmission and control system that uses an **incompressible working fluid** (typically petroleum-based mineral oil, synthetic fluids, or water-glycol mixtures) within a sealed, pressurized circuit to transmit power from one location to another. The system obeys **Pascal's Law** and is used to generate large linear or rotary forces with high positional precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hydraulic systems are the *high-force cousins* of pneumatic systems. They dominate in heavy machinery (excavators, injection moulding, aircraft landing gear) because liquids cannot be compressed, allowing for rigid, precise motion control.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Bicycle Pump Analogy (Pneumatics):**
Think of a bicycle pump. When you push the handle, you compress air inside a tube. That compressed air, when released, can push a projectile, inflate a tyre, or power a small piston. Pneumatic systems are essentially **industrial-scale, controlled, continuous bicycle pumps**. The "springiness" of the air allows for safe, soft, cushioned motion — this is why dentists' drills and chocolate-moulding machines use pneumatics.

**The Hydraulic Press Analogy (Hydraulics):**
Imagine a salon chair that lifts a 100 kg person using just a finger's pressure. The chair has a tiny piston where you press and a large piston underneath the seat. A small force on the small piston creates a small pressure, but because pressure equals force divided by area, the *same pressure* acting on the *larger area* produces a multiplied force that lifts the person. **This is Pascal's Law in action** — the heart of every hydraulic system.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Distinction for Board Exams:**
> - **Pneumatic = Compressible Gas (Air) → Light Force, High Speed, Compliant, Inherently Safe.**
> - **Hydraulic = Incompressible Liquid (Oil) → Heavy Force, High Precision, Rigid, High Power Density.**

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** |
| Typical Pneumatic Working Pressure | $P_{pneu}$ | **4 to 8 bar (400 to 800 kPa)** |
| Typical Hydraulic Working Pressure | $P_{hyd}$ | **70 to 350 bar (7 to 35 MPa)** |
| Density of Mineral Hydraulic Oil | $\rho_{oil}$ | **≈ 870 kg/m³ at 40°C** |
| Bulk Modulus of Hydraulic Oil | $\beta$ | **≈ 1.5 to 2.0 GPa** |
| Bulk Modulus of Air (at 1 atm) | $\beta_{air}$ | **≈ 0.0001 GPa (highly compressible)** |
| Viscosity of ISO VG 46 Oil | $\nu$ | **46 cSt at 40°C** |
| Speed of Sound in Air | $c$ | **≈ 343 m/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **1000x difference in bulk modulus** between air and oil is the single most important physical reason why pneumatics are soft and hydraulics are rigid. Examiners love this comparison question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Fluid Power System

Every pneumatic and hydraulic system, regardless of complexity, contains the same five functional blocks. Understanding this **5-block model** is the foundation for all KTU board questions on this topic.

**Block 1 — Energy Source (Prime Mover + Pump/Compressor)**
Converts electrical/mechanical energy into fluid pressure energy. A pneumatic system uses a **compressor** (reciprocating, rotary screw, or centrifugal). A hydraulic system uses a **pump** (gear, vane, piston).

**Block 2 — Conditioning Unit (FRL / Reservoir + Filter)**
For pneumatics: an **FRL** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) removes contaminants, stabilizes pressure, and adds oil mist for lubrication. For hydraulics: a **reservoir** with strainer, breather, and sometimes a heat exchanger.

**Block 3 — Control Elements (Valves)**
Directional Control Valves (DCV) route fluid. Pressure Control Valves limit max pressure. Flow Control Valves regulate speed. Check Valves allow one-way flow.

**Block 4 — Actuation Elements (Cylinders / Motors)**
Convert fluid energy back into mechanical energy. **Linear actuators** (cylinders) or **rotary actuators** (motors).

**Block 5 — Load / Work Output**
The external mechanical work being done (lifting, pressing, rotating, gripping).

### 2.2 Pascal's Law — The Foundational Theorem

**Statement (Verbatim for Exam):** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished to every portion of the fluid and the walls of the containing vessel."*

**Why It Matters:**
Pascal's Law is what makes a small input force capable of producing a massive output force. It is the *energy conservation mechanism* of hydraulics. Pressure (intensive property) is the carrier; force changes only because the area changes.

### 2.3 Boyle's Law and the Ideal Gas Assumption in Pneumatics

For pneumatic systems, air is modeled as an **Ideal Gas** for analytical simplicity:
$$PV = nRT = mR_{specific}T$$

Where:
- $P$ = Absolute pressure in **Pa**
- $V$ = Volume in **m³**
- $n$ = Moles of gas
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (Universal gas constant)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **K**
- $R_{specific}$ = **287 J/(kg·K)** for air

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU problems, you will often see "a cylinder is loaded by a spring such that initial pressure is $P_1$ at volume $V_1$. As the piston advances, the gas expands isothermally to $V_2$. Find the final pressure." This requires **Boyle's Law**: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cylinder Output Force** | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ = thrust force, $P$ = gauge pressure, $A$ = piston area | F: N, P: Pa, A: m² |
| 2 | **Piston Area (circular bore)** | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ = piston bore diameter | m² |
| 3 | **Annulus Area (rod side)** | $A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | $D$ = bore, $d$ = rod diameter | m² |
| 4 | **Pascal's Law (force multiplier)** | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Force ratio = Area ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Boyle's Law (Isothermal)** | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | For pneumatic cylinder with constant $T$ | Pa·m³ |
| 6 | **Adiabatic Gas Law** | $P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$ | $\gamma$ = **1.4** for air (fast processes) | Pa·m³ |
| 7 | **Flow Rate (continuity)** | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate, $v$ = piston velocity | m³/s |
| 8 | **Cylinder Piston Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $Q$ = supply flow | m/s |
| 9 | **Hydraulic Power** | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | Power = Pressure drop × Flow | W |
| 10 | **Mechanical Power Output** | $P_{mech} = F \cdot v$ | Force × Velocity | W |
| 11 | **Time to Extend Cylinder** | $t = \dfrac{V_{cyl}}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | $L$ = stroke length | s |
| 12 | **Pump Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Ratio of actual to theoretical flow | dimensionless (0-1) |
| 13 | **Compressor Power (theoretical)** | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{n R T_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] \dot{m}$ | For adiabatic compression of air | W |
| 14 | **Bulk Modulus Relation** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\beta}$ | Volume change under pressure | m³ |
| 15 | **Reynolds Number (pipe flow)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (KTU 2024):** In the formulas above, $\Delta P$ means *Pressure Difference*. **NEVER** confuse gauge pressure ($P_{gauge}$) with absolute pressure ($P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$). Hydraulic calculations almost always use **gauge**; pneumatic gas-law calculations almost always use **absolute**. **This single mistake causes 30% of mark loss in numericals.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Pneumatic Application | Hydraulic Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Pick-and-place, clamping, robotic grippers | Presses, injection moulding, forging |
| **Automotive** | Brake systems (older), air suspension | Brake systems (modern), power steering, lifts |
| **Aerospace** | De-icing, landing-gear door actuation | Landing gear extension, flap actuators |
| **Civil** | Jackhammers, concrete sprayers | Excavators, cranes, bulldozers |
| **Medical** | Dental drills, ventilators | Hospital bed lifts, surgical tables |
| **Food** | Bottle filling, packaging | Chocolate moulding presses (some) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyber-Physical Context (KTU PCCPT205):** In modern CPS, both pneumatic and hydraulic systems are integrated with **electronic pressure sensors**, **solenoid valves** (controlled by microcontrollers/PLCs), and **feedback loops**. A CPS-enabled hydraulic press can detect material thickness via a force sensor and adjust pump flow in real time — a clear fusion of physical actuation and digital intelligence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Force Produced by a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Pattern):**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 80$ mm and a rod diameter $d = 45$ mm. The pump supplies oil at gauge pressure $P = 140$ bar. Calculate (a) the extension force, (b) the retraction force, and (c) the piston velocity if the supply flow rate is $Q = 30$ L/min.

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- $d = 45$ mm $= 0.045$ m
- $P = 140$ bar $= 140 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 1.4 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- $Q = 30$ L/min $= \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

#### Step 1 — Compute the Bore (Piston) Cross-Sectional Area

This is the area on the *blank side* (extension side) where full pump pressure acts on the entire piston face.

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Substitute $D = 0.080$ m:

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4}
$$

$$
A_1 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Annulus (Rod-side) Area

This is the area on the *rod side* (retraction side). The rod occupies part of the bore, so effective area is reduced.

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi [(0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2]}{4} = \frac{\pi [0.0064 - 0.002025]}{4}
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.004375}{4} = 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Extension Force (Blank-Side)

Use the fundamental fluid power equation: $F = P \times A$.

$$
F_{ext} = P \times A_1 = (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 70{,}371 \text{ N} \approx 70.37 \text{ kN}
$$

**Interpretation:** The cylinder pushes with ~70 kN on the extension stroke. This is equivalent to lifting a 7-tonne load.

#### Step 4 — Compute the Retraction Force (Rod-Side)

$$
F_{ret} = P \times A_2 = (1.4 \times 10^{7}) \times (3.4357 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ret} = 48{,}100 \text{ N} \approx 48.10 \text{ kN}
$$

**Note:** Retraction force is **~32% lower** than extension force because of the rod area. This is a *favourite* KTU exam observation question.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Piston Velocity During Extension

Apply the continuity equation $Q = A \cdot v$, so $v = Q/A$.

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v_{ext} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 9.95 \text{ cm/s}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute the Piston Velocity During Retraction

Note: the same flow $Q$ enters the smaller annulus area, so retraction is *faster*.

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4357 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1455 \text{ m/s} \approx 14.55 \text{ cm/s}
$$

#### Step 7 — Final Consolidated Numerical Answer Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bore Area | $A_1$ | $5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ | m² |
| Annulus Area | $A_2$ | $3.436 \times 10^{-3}$ | m² |
| Extension Force | $F_{ext}$ | **70.37** | kN |
| Retraction Force | $F_{ret}$ | **48.10** | kN |
| Extension Velocity | $v_{ext}$ | **0.0995** | m/s |
| Retraction Velocity | $v_{ret}$ | **0.1455** | m/s |
| Force Ratio | $F_{ext}/F_{ret}$ | **1.46** | dimensionless |

### 3.2 Derivation: Pneumatic Cylinder with Isothermal Expansion (Spring-Loaded)

**Problem Setup:**
A single-acting pneumatic cylinder has a piston of diameter $D = 100$ mm. The piston is pre-loaded by a spring with stiffness $k = 5000$ N/m and pre-compression $x_0 = 20$ mm. The cylinder is initially at $P_1 = 4$ bar (gauge), with the piston fully retracted. Air is admitted and the piston moves outward. The process is isothermal at $T = 300$ K. Find the *final pressure* $P_2$ when the piston has moved $L = 80$ mm.

**Given:**
- $D = 0.100$ m, so $A = \pi (0.100)^2 / 4 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $k = 5000$ N/m, $x_0 = 0.020$ m, $L = 0.080$ m
- $P_1 = 4 + 1.013 = 5.013$ bar (absolute) $= 5.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $V_1 = A \times x_0$ (initial gas volume — small clearance volume at bottom of stroke)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtlety the examiner will test:** When the piston is fully retracted, the gas occupies only the *clearance volume* below the piston. The clearance is given by the pre-compression $x_0 = 20$ mm. As the piston extends, gas volume increases.

#### Step 1 — Express Initial and Final Volumes

$$
V_1 = A \cdot x_0 = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.020 = 1.5708 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_2 = A \cdot (x_0 + L) = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.020 + 0.080)
$$

$$
V_2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Boyle's Law (Isothermal)

$$
P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 \implies P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2}
$$

$$
P_2 = (5.013 \times 10^5) \times \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
P_2 = (5.013 \times 10^5) \times (0.20) = 1.0026 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa (absolute)}
$$

#### Step 3 — Convert Back to Gauge Pressure for Reporting

$$
P_2 \text{ (gauge)} = 1.0026 \times 10^5 - 1.013 \times 10^5 \approx -0.01 \text{ bar (vacuum)}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The pressure dropped below atmospheric! This means the cylinder cannot complete the full 80 mm stroke under isothermal assumption with this spring — the gas would have to *pull* the piston, which is impossible. In reality, the piston stops when $P_2 = P_{atm}$ (or when force balance is re-established considering atmospheric back-pressure). **This is a classic KTU conceptual trap.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** This problem illustrates why pneumatic cylinders use **return springs** — without them, you'd have to actively vent or admit air to retract the rod, which requires a double-acting design. The "single-acting" cylinder uses spring force to do the return stroke.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pneumatic vs Hydraulic Comparison Table

This matrix is the **most-asked comparison question** in KTU Module 4. Memorize the column headers.

| Parameter | Pneumatic System | Hydraulic System |
|---|---|---|
| Working Fluid | Air (compressible gas) | Mineral oil (incompressible liquid) |
| Operating Pressure | **4 to 8 bar** (low-medium) | **70 to 350 bar** (high) |
| Force Output | Low to medium (up to ~25 kN typical) | Very high (hundreds of kN) |
| Speed | Very high (up to 1-2 m/s) | Medium (0.1 to 0.5 m/s typical) |
| Precision | Lower (compliance of air) | Higher (rigid, no compression) |
| Energy Storage | Receivers/tanks store compressed air | Accumulators store pressurized oil |
| Leakage | Less critical (air is safe) | Critical (oil leak = fire + slip) |
| Noise | High (~80-100 dB need silencers) | Low (~60-70 dB) |
| Temperature Sensitivity | High (gas density varies) | Low (oil density stable) |
| Cost | Low (compressors cheap) | High (pumps, filters, seals costly) |
| Maintenance | Simple, infrequent | Demanding, regular oil changes |
| Fire Hazard | None (air does not burn) | Moderate (mineral oil is flammable) |
| Reversibility | Easy (vent to atmosphere) | Requires return line and reservoir |
| CPS Integration | Solenoid valves, flow sensors | LVDT, pressure transducers, PLCs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Generic Pneumatic System — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC MOTOR] --> B1[COMPRESSOR]
    B1 --> C1[RECEIVER TANK]
    C1 --> D1[FRL UNIT]
    D1 --> D1a[AIR FILTER]
    D1 --> D1b[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    D1 --> D1c[LUBRICATOR]
    D1b --> E1[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE]
    E1 --> F1[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    F1 --> G1[LOAD]
    E1 -.VENT.-> H1[SILENCER]
    F1 -.EXHAUST.-> H1
    C1 -.SAFETY VALVE.-> I1[RELIEF TO ATMOSPHERE]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A1 → B1:** Electric motor drives the compressor (energy input).
- **B1 → C1 → D1:** Compressed air is stored in the receiver tank, then conditioned by the FRL.
- **D1 → E1:** Clean, regulated, lubricated air enters the directional control valve.
- **E1 → F1:** The valve routes air to either side of the cylinder piston.
- **F1 → G1:** The piston moves the external load.
- **Exhaust paths** go through a silencer to reduce noise (a key KTU safety point).

### 4.2 Generic Hydraulic System — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> B2[HYDRAULIC PUMP]
    B2 --> C2[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE]
    B2 --> D2[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE]
    D2 --> E2[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    E2 --> F2[LOAD]
    E2 -.RETURN.-> G2[RESERVOIR]
    G2 --> H2[STRAINER AND FILTER]
    H2 --> B2
    D2 -.TANK LINE.-> G2
    C2 -.OVERFLOW.-> G2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **A2 → B2 → D2 → E2 → F2:** This is the *high-pressure power delivery path*. Motor drives pump, pump pressurizes oil, valve routes to cylinder, cylinder moves load.
- **E2 → G2 → H2 → B2:** The *return path*. Oil returns to reservoir, gets filtered, and is recirculated by the pump.
- **C2 (relief valve):** A critical safety element that limits maximum system pressure.

### 4.3 Directional Control Valve (DCV) State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Neutral
    Neutral --> Extend: Solenoid A Energized
    Extend --> Neutral: Solenoid A De-energized
    Neutral --> Retract: Solenoid B Energized
    Retract --> Neutral: Solenoid B De-energized
    Neutral --> [*]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The DCV has **two stable states** (extend and retract) controlled by two solenoids.
- "Neutral" is the spring-centered, de-energized state — a hallmark of fail-safe pneumatic design.

### 4.4 Cylinder Force-Velocity Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Q: SUPPLY FLOW] --> DIVIDE{DIVIDE BY AREA}
    DIVIDE --> A1[A1: BORE AREA]
    DIVIDE --> A2[A2: ANNULUS AREA]
    A1 --> VEXT[VEXT: EXTENSION VELOCITY]
    A2 --> VRET[VRET: RETRACTION VELOCITY]
    P[P: SUPPLY PRESSURE] --> MULT{MULTIPLY BY AREA}
    MULT --> A1
    MULT --> A2
    A1 --> FEXT[FEXT: EXTENSION FORCE]
    A2 --> FRET[FRET: RETRACTION FORCE]
```

**Key Teaching Point:** This diagram visually shows the dual relationship — the same flow and pressure produce *different* velocities and *different* forces depending on which side of the cylinder they act on.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between a single-acting and a double-acting cylinder. State one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Air supply | To **one side** only | To **both sides** alternately |
| Return stroke | By **spring** or **external load** (gravity) | By **compressed air** |
| Force output | Only in **one direction** (extension) | In **both directions** |
| Control complexity | Simple (one DCV port) | Complex (3/4 or 5-port DCV) |
| Application | Door-closing on a paint shop line, simple clamping | Robotic arm extension-retraction, hydraulic press slide |

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for single-acting definition + example; 1 mark for double-acting definition + example; 1 mark for distinguishing line.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*State Pascal's Law. Explain how it is used in a hydraulic lift.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Pascal's Law states: *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions."*

In a hydraulic lift, a small force $F_1$ is applied on a small piston of area $A_1$, producing pressure $P = F_1/A_1$. This same pressure acts on a larger piston of area $A_2$ on the lift platform, producing an output force $F_2 = P \times A_2 = F_1 \times (A_2/A_1)$. Since $A_2 \gg A_1$, the force is multiplied, lifting a heavy load with a small input force. (Energy is conserved — the small piston must travel a proportionally longer distance.)

**Marking Scheme:** 1 mark statement; 1.5 marks hydraulic lift explanation; 0.5 mark for energy conservation note.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Mixing up *gauge* and *absolute* pressure — a 1.013 bar conversion error is *guaranteed* mark loss.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to subtract the rod area when calculating retraction force. **Always draw the cylinder cross-section first.**
> - **Pitfall 3:** Writing "force is multiplied in hydraulics" without explaining the **displacement trade-off** (energy conservation). Examiners deduct 2 marks for incomplete statements of Pascal's Law.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Confusing flow rate units (L/min vs m³/s). **1 L/min = 1.667 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s.** This conversion error ruins entire numericals.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of a hydraulic system. List the functions of (i) reservoir, (ii) pressure relief valve, (iii) directional control valve.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 100 mm and a rod diameter of 60 mm. The pump delivers oil at a flow rate of 60 L/min at a pressure of 100 bar. Calculate:
   (i) The extension and retraction forces.
   (ii) The extension and retraction velocities.
   (iii) The hydraulic power delivered to the cylinder during extension.

---

**Complete Model Solution for Q3(A):**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram and Functions [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Electric Motor → Hydraulic Pump → Pressure Relief Valve (parallel) →
Directional Control Valve → Double-Acting Cylinder → Load
                                          ↓
                                       Reservoir ← (Filter + Strainer)
```

**Functions (4 marks — 1.33 each, round to 1.5/1.5/1):**

(i) **Reservoir (1.5 marks):** Stores the hydraulic oil, allows air to escape, dissipates heat, and provides a mounting location for filters and the pump inlet. It acts as a buffer for thermal expansion and contamination settling.

(ii) **Pressure Relief Valve (1.5 marks):** A normally-closed valve set to a maximum safe pressure. If system pressure exceeds the set value, the valve opens and bypasses oil back to the reservoir, protecting the pump, actuators, and piping from overpressure damage.

(iii) **Directional Control Valve (1 mark):** Routes the pressurized oil to the appropriate port of the actuator (e.g., to the cap end for extension, to the rod end for retraction). It determines the *direction* of motion. Common types: 4/3, 5/2, 5/3 way valves.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m
- $Q = 60$ L/min $= \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1.0 \times 10^7$ Pa

**Step 1: Calculate Areas (1 mark)**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi [(0.100)^2 - (0.060)^2]}{4}
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.0100 - 0.0036)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0064)}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Forces (2 marks)**

$$
F_{ext} = P \times A_1 = (1.0 \times 10^7) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 78{,}540 \text{ N} \approx 78.54 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P \times A_2 = (1.0 \times 10^7) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) = 50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Velocities (2 marks)**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s} \approx 12.73 \text{ cm/s}
$$

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1990 \text{ m/s} \approx 19.90 \text{ cm/s}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Hydraulic Power During Extension (2 marks)**

$$
P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q = (1.0 \times 10^7) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}
$$

*(Equivalent check via mechanical: $P_{mech} = F_{ext} \times v_{ext} = 78{,}540 \times 0.1273 = 9999.8$ W ✓ — confirms energy balance.)*

**Final Answer Table (auto-completion):**

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{ext}$ | **78.54** | kN |
| $F_{ret}$ | **50.27** | kN |
| $v_{ext}$ | **0.127** | m/s |
| $v_{ret}$ | **0.199** | m/s |
| $P_{hyd}$ | **10.0** | kW |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Compare pneumatic and hydraulic systems on the basis of: (i) working fluid, (ii) pressure range, (iii) force capability, (iv) precision, (v) leakage concerns, (vi) fire hazard, (vii) typical application. Justify why hydraulics are preferred for an aircraft landing gear actuation system.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A single-acting pneumatic cylinder with a piston diameter of 150 mm is connected to a 6 bar (gauge) air supply through a 5/2 way DCV. The piston is pre-loaded by a spring (stiffness $k = 10$ kN/m, pre-compression = 30 mm). Assuming isothermal expansion, determine: (i) the maximum theoretical stroke length before pressure equals atmospheric, and (ii) the force on the load at this point.

---

**Complete Model Solution for Q3(B):**

**Part (a) — Comparison [7 Marks]**

Provide the comparison table from Section 3.3 (Analytical Matrix) with the 7 required parameters. Award 0.5 marks per parameter = 3.5 marks. Then 3.5 marks for the aircraft landing gear justification.

**Justification for Aircraft Landing Gear (3.5 marks):**
Aircraft landing gear requires:
- **Extremely high force density** to retract/extend 50-300 kN loads in a confined space — only hydraulics at 200-350 bar can deliver this.
- **Rigidity and precision** — pneumatic compression would cause uncontrolled motion and lag; hydraulics provide exact positional control.
- **Reliability under extreme conditions** — hydraulic seals and metallic components survive -50°C to +100°C; pneumatic systems suffer from moisture freezing.
- **Fire-resistant fluids** (e.g., Skydrol) are available for aviation hydraulics.
- **Compactness** — a 350 bar hydraulic actuator is ~10x smaller than a pneumatic equivalent for the same force.

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $D = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m, so $A = \pi (0.150)^2/4 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}$ m²
- $P_1 = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013$ bar absolute $= 7.013 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $k = 10{,}000$ N/m, $x_0 = 0.030$ m

**Step 1: Force Balance at Maximum Stroke (2 marks)**

At the maximum stroke, the gas pressure has dropped to atmospheric ($P_2 = P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa), and the spring force balances atmospheric pressure on the rod side (assume rod side is vented to atmosphere).

$$
F_{gas} = P_2 \cdot A = (1.013 \times 10^5) \times (1.767 \times 10^{-2}) = 1{,}790 \text{ N}
$$

Wait — re-examining the problem: the maximum stroke occurs when the *gas pressure times area equals the spring force plus atmospheric back-pressure* on the rod side. Assuming rod side atmospheric:

$$
P_2 \cdot A = k(x_0 + L_{max}) + P_{atm} \cdot A
$$

But $P_2 = P_{atm}$, so:

$$
P_{atm} \cdot A = k(x_0 + L_{max}) + P_{atm} \cdot A
$$

This gives $0 = k(x_0 + L_{max})$, which is impossible. **Reinterpretation:** The cylinder stops when the gas pressure drops to a value where it can no longer overcome the spring. So $P_2 \cdot A = k \cdot (x_0 + L_{max})$, with $P_2$ determined by Boyle's Law.

**Step 2: Apply Boyle's Law (2 marks)**

Initial volume: $V_1 = A \cdot x_0 = (1.767 \times 10^{-2}) \times 0.030 = 5.301 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
Final volume: $V_2 = A \cdot (x_0 + L_{max}) = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times (0.030 + L_{max})$

By Boyle: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$, so:

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2} = (7.013 \times 10^5) \cdot \frac{5.301 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times (0.030 + L_{max})}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Force Balance (2 marks)**

At maximum stroke: $P_2 \cdot A = k (x_0 + L_{max})$

Substituting $P_2$:

$$
\left[ (7.013 \times 10^5) \cdot \frac{5.301 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \cdot (0.030 + L_{max})} \right] \times (1.767 \times 10^{-2}) = 10{,}000 \times (0.030 + L_{max})
$$

Simplify the LHS:

$$
(7.013 \times 10^5) \times \frac{5.301 \times 10^{-4}}{(0.030 + L_{max})} = 10{,}000 \times (0.030 + L_{max})
$$

$$
\frac{371.76}{(0.030 + L_{max})} = 10{,}000 \times (0.030 + L_{max})
$$

Let $y = 0.030 + L_{max}$:

$$
371.76 = 10{,}000 \cdot y^2 \implies y^2 = 0.037176 \implies y = 0.1928 \text{ m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L_{max} = 0.1928 - 0.030 = 0.1628 \text{ m} \approx 162.8 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Force on Load (1 mark)**

$$
F_{load} = k \cdot y = 10{,}000 \times 0.1928 = 1{,}928 \text{ N} \approx 1.93 \text{ kN}
$$

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical concepts to memorize for KTU 2024 End-Semester Exam:**

- ✅ **Pneumatic = gas (compressible) → low force, fast, safe, cheap.** **Hydraulic = liquid (incompressible) → high force, precise, expensive, hazardous if leaks.**
- ✅ **5 Functional Blocks of Any Fluid Power System:** Source → Conditioning → Control → Actuation → Load.
- ✅ **Pascal's Law verbatim wording is mandatory** for full marks.
- ✅ **Master the formula $F = P \times A$** — this is used in 90% of numericals.
- ✅ **Remember the two areas:** Bore area $A_1 = \pi D^2/4$ and Annulus area $A_2 = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$.
- ✅ **Extension force > Retraction force** because $A_1 > A_2$.
- ✅ **Extension velocity < Retraction velocity** because $v = Q/A$ and $A_1 > A_2$.
- ✅ **Boyle's Law** ($P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$) is for *slow, isothermal* pneumatic processes; **Adiabatic law** ($P_1V_1^\gamma = P_2V_2^\gamma$, $\gamma = 1.4$) is for *fast* processes.
- ✅ **FRL** = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator — the universal pneumatic conditioning unit.
- ✅ **DCV notation:** "Ports/Ways" — e.g., 5/2 means *5 ports, 2 positions*.
- ✅ **Single-acting cylinder** uses a spring for return; **double-acting cylinder** uses air on both sides.
- ✅ **Pressure relief valve** is a *must* on every hydraulic system — open question for safety.
- ✅ **Always convert L/min to m³/s** by dividing by 60,000: $\dfrac{Q \text{ [L/min]}}{60000} = Q \text{ [m³/s]}$.
- ✅ **Gauge vs Absolute:** Hydraulics use gauge; Boyle's/Charles' Law use absolute.
- ✅ **CPS Context:** Solenoid valves + pressure sensors + PLC = modern electro-pneumatic / electro-hydraulic system.
- ✅ **Bulk modulus difference** (oil ≈ 1.5 GPa, air ≈ 0.0001 GPa) explains rigidity of hydraulics vs compliance of pneumatics.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Pneumatic and Hydraulic Systems**
> *Total study time recommended: 6-8 hours. Practice at least 3 numericals from the patterns above before the exam.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directional Control Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Directional Control Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** is a precision-engineered fluid power component whose primary function is to **direct the flow of compressed air (pneumatic) or hydraulic fluid to different paths within a system**, thereby controlling the direction of motion of actuators such as cylinders, rotary actuators, and motors. In the framework of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, the DCV acts as the critical *physical-layer interface* that translates *electrical control signals* (from PLCs, microcontrollers, or industrial IoT nodes) into *mechanical actuation paths*.

Mathematically, a DCV is characterized by two primary integers:
- **Number of Ports (Ways)**: Connection points (typically 2, 3, 4, or 5)
- **Number of Positions**: Discrete stable states of the spool (typically 2 or 3)

Therefore, a **4/3 valve** is read as *"4 ports, 3 positions"*, and a **5/2 valve** as *"5 ports, 2 positions"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Directional Control Valves form the *core of actuator command-and-control* in any pneumatic or hydraulic circuit. Mastery of valve symbols (per ISO 1219 / ISO 5599) is **non-negotiable** for KTU board exams, as graphical symbol questions carry **direct 3 to 7 marks** in every ESE cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Police of Fluid Power

Imagine a busy four-way intersection where vehicles (compressed air or hydraulic oil) must be routed to specific lanes. A **traffic police officer** stands at the center, raising one arm to allow east-bound flow and lowering the other to permit west-bound flow. The officer decides **where the fluid goes, when, and through which path**.

The **Directional Control Valve is exactly this traffic police officer for fluid power systems**:

| Traffic Police Analogy | Directional Control Valve Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Officer at the intersection | **Spool** (sliding element inside valve body) |
| Arms raised/lowered | **Spool positions** (2 or 3 stable states) |
| Roads entering the intersection | **Inlet/Outlet ports** (P, T, A, B) |
| Officer receiving wireless orders | **Solenoid coil** (electrical actuation signal) |
| Officer on duty / off duty | **Energized / De-energized** coil state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The DCV does **not regulate pressure or flow magnitude** — that is the job of pressure control valves and flow control valves respectively. The DCV's *exclusive* responsibility is **directional routing** of fluid energy.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Operating Pressure (Pneumatic):** **0.6 MPa to 1.0 MPa** (≈ **6 bar to 10 bar**)
- **Standard Operating Pressure (Hydraulic):** **7 MPa to 35 MPa** (≈ **70 bar to 350 bar**)
- **Standard Supply Voltage (Solenoid):** **24 V DC** (industry standard for CPS integration)
- **Standard Switching Time:** **15 ms to 50 ms** (typical for industrial-grade solenoids)
- **Standard Port Sizes (ISO 4397):** **G1/8, G1/4, G3/8, G1/2, G3/4, G1** (BSP threads)
- **Standard Mounting:** **ISO 5599 (Size 1, 2, 3)** for sub-base mounting; **CETOP / NFPA** patterns for hydraulic stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Directional Control Valves

DCVs are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1 — Number of Ports and Positions
- **2/2 Valve:** 2 ports, 2 positions (simple on/off — rarely used as a true DCV)
- **3/2 Valve:** 3 ports, 2 positions (poppet or spool — *most common for single-acting cylinders*)
- **4/2 Valve:** 4 ports, 2 positions (for double-acting cylinders)
- **4/3 Valve:** 4 ports, 3 positions (with distinct center condition)
- **5/2 Valve:** 5 ports, 2 positions (preferred for double-acting pneumatic cylinders)
- **5/3 Valve:** 5 ports, 3 positions (with center conditions like closed-center, tandem-center)

### Axis 2 — Type of Constructive Element
- **Poppet-type DCV:** Uses a ball or cone against a seat; *leak-tight*, *fast response*, *low flow*
- **Spool-type DCV:** Uses a sliding cylindrical spool with lands and grooves; *high flow capacity*, *most common*
- **Disc-seat / Diaphragm-type:** For ultra-clean pneumatic or low-pressure applications

### Axis 3 — Method of Actuation
- **Manual:** Push-button, lever, pedal, rotary hand-wheel
- **Mechanical:** Cam, roller, spring-return (limit-switch style)
- **Solenoid (Electro-magnetic):** Direct-acting or pilot-operated (the CPS workhorse)
- **Pilot-actuated (Pneumatic / Hydraulic):** A small pilot valve controls a larger main valve
- **Combined Actuation:** Solenoid-pilot + spring-return; double-solenoid detented

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** In PCCPT205, the **electro-pneumatic** and **electro-hydraulic** integration relies almost entirely on **solenoid-actuated pilot-operated DCVs**, where the *low-power digital signal* (24 V DC from microcontroller) controls *high-power fluid flow* (compressed air at 6 bar). This is the textbook **signal-to-power amplification** in cyber-physical systems.

## 2.2 Functional Anatomy of a 5/2 Solenoid-Actuated DCV

A 5/2 single-solenoid, spring-return valve has:

- **1 Pressure port (P)** — connected to supply (compressor or hydraulic pump)
- **2 Working ports (A, B)** — connected to actuator chambers
- **2 Exhaust ports (R, S)** — vented to atmosphere
- **1 Solenoid coil** (electrical input)
- **1 Return spring** (mechanical default position)
- **1 Spool** with 3 lands and 2 grooves (the moving heart of the valve)

In the **de-energized state** (spring side): Port P → A; Port B → R
In the **energized state** (solenoid side): Port P → B; Port A → S

## 2.3 Center Position Conditions (4/3 and 5/3 Valves)

For 3-position valves, the **center condition** is a critical design parameter:

| Center Type | ISO Symbol | Behavior | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed Center** | T-cross blocked | All ports blocked; actuator locked | Position-holding in vertical cylinders |
| **Tandem Center** | P, T connected | Pump unloads to tank; A, B blocked | Energy-saving circuits |
| **Float Center** | P, T, A, B all connected | Actuator free to move | Tramming circuits in mobile hydraulics |
| **Open Center** | P to T free flow | Pump flow returns directly to tank | Legacy systems |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are **frequently tested** in numerical questions. Memorize the variable meanings, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Flow Rate (ideal)** | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $A$ = piston area; $v$ = velocity | m³/s | Cylinder extension speed |
| 2 | **Cylinder Piston Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | $Q$ = flow; $A_p$ = piston area | m/s | Speed control via DCV |
| 3 | **Effective Piston Area** | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D^2 - d^2)$ | $D$ = bore; $d$ = rod diameter | m² | Double-rod extension vs. retraction |
| 4 | **Thrust Force (Pneumatic)** | $F_{th} = p \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_m$ | $p$ = pressure; $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (≈ 0.85–0.95) | N | Force exerted by actuator |
| 5 | **Valve Flow Coefficient** | $Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho}}$ | $C_v$ = valve coefficient; $\rho$ = fluid density | m³/s | DCV sizing |
| 6 | **Solenoid Force (Pilot)** | $F_{sol} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2\,g^2}$ | $N$ = turns; $I$ = current; $g$ = air gap | N | Solenoid design |
| 7 | **Spool Stroke Force Balance** | $F_{spring} + F_{pilot} = F_{friction} + F_{inertia}$ | Quasi-static spool motion | N | Pilot-operated DCV design |
| 8 | **Pressure Drop across DCV** | $\Delta p = p_{in} - p_{out}$ | Required for sizing | Pa | Energy loss in valve |
| 9 | **Power Dissipated in DCV** | $P_{loss} = \Delta p \cdot Q$ | Energy lost as heat | W | Thermal design |
| 10 | **Switching Time Constant** | $t_s = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_\infty - V_0}{V_\infty - V_{th}}\right)$ | $V$ = spool displacement; $V_{th}$ = threshold | s | Response time analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the **piston area $A_p$** for extension vs. retraction. For a single-rod cylinder:
> - **Extension (piston side):** $A_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
> - **Retraction (rod side):** $A_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$
> Therefore the **retraction force is always less** than the extension force for the same pressure.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Directional Control Valves are the **unseen workhorses** of modern automation:
- **Automotive assembly lines:** Robotic welding arms use 5/3 closed-center DCVs to lock tool positions.
- **Injection molding machines:** High-response 4/3 proportional DCVs control clamp motion.
- **Aircraft landing gear:** Hydraulic 4/3 tandem-center DCVs save energy during cruise.
- **Pneumatic logic circuits:** 3/2 valves cascade to build Boolean logic without electronics.
- **Agricultural tractors:** Load-sensing 4/3 float-center DCVs in hydraulic remotes operate implements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Piston Velocity as a Function of DCV Position

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder of bore $D = 50$ mm and rod diameter $d = 25$ mm is supplied with compressed air at $p_s = 6$ bar (gauge) through a 5/2 DCV. The load mass is $m = 50$ kg. The coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.15$. Compute the extension velocity of the piston.

### Step 1: Identify the physical regime
The piston extends under pressure $p_s$ acting on the full piston area. On the rod side, exhaust pressure $p_e$ is approximately atmospheric ($\approx 0$ bar gauge, since the DCV exhaust port vents directly to atmosphere). This is the **standard extension condition** for any 5/2 DCV with no back-pressure regulator.

### Step 2: Compute the effective piston area during extension

The effective area on the piston side (no rod) is:

$$
A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^2
$$

Substituting the numerical value $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{ext} &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.050)^2 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0025 \\
&= \frac{3.14159}{4} \times 0.0025 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.0025 \\
&= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard cross-sectional area of a 50 mm bore cylinder** — useful as a benchmark for all KTU numericals.

### Step 3: Compute the net thrust force on the piston

The net thrust is the difference between the driving force on the piston side and the resisting forces:

$$
F_{net} = p_s \cdot A_{ext} - p_e \cdot A_{ret} - F_{friction} - F_{weight}
$$

**Logical conversion:** In a horizontal mounting ($F_{weight} = 0$) with atmospheric exhaust ($p_e = 0$), the equation simplifies. The friction force is:

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g
$$

where $g = 9.81$ m/s². Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{friction} &= 0.15 \times 50 \times 9.81 \\
&= 0.15 \times 490.5 \\
&= 73.575 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The driving force from supply pressure is:

$$
F_{drive} = p_s \cdot A_{ext}
$$

where $p_s = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{drive} &= (6 \times 10^5) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= 6 \times 1.9635 \times 10^2 \\
&= 1178.1 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The net force available to accelerate the load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= F_{drive} - F_{friction} \\
&= 1178.1 - 73.575 \\
&= 1104.525 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law to find acceleration

$$
F_{net} = m \cdot a
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{F_{net}}{m} \\
&= \frac{1104.525}{50} \\
&= 22.09 \ \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Assume steady-state velocity reached after transient

In typical KTU problems, we assume the piston reaches a **steady-state velocity** governed by the flow rate $Q$ delivered by the DCV. If the supply flow rate is $Q = 0.0015$ m³/s, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_{ext}} \\
&= \frac{0.0015}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 0.7639 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **steady-state extension velocity** — the answer most KTU board questions seek.

### Step 6: Compute retraction velocity for completeness

The effective area on retraction is reduced by the rod cross-section:

$$
A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D^2 - d^2)
$$

Substituting $d = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{ret} &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,((0.050)^2 - (0.025)^2) \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4}\,(0.0025 - 0.000625) \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.001875 \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.001875 \\
&= 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The retraction velocity with the same supply flow:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{ret} &= \frac{Q}{A_{ret}} \\
&= \frac{0.0015}{1.4726 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 1.0186 \ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Retraction is **33% faster** than extension in this case because the same volumetric flow must fill a smaller annular volume. This asymmetry is a **frequently tested observation** in KTU viva voce.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Spool Valve Force Balance for Solenoid Sizing

**Problem Setup:** A 4/3 pilot-operated DCV has a main spool of diameter $D_s = 20$ mm, stroke $L = 12$ mm, and is shifted by a pilot pressure $p_{pilot} = 4$ bar. The spring rate is $k = 1.5$ N/mm. Compute the minimum solenoid pilot force required to shift the spool.

### Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the spool

The spool is acted upon by **four** primary forces:
- **Spring force** (resists motion)
- **Pilot pressure force** (drives motion)
- **Stiction (static friction) force** (resists motion)
- **Inertial force** (resists acceleration — assumed negligible at switching onset)

### Step 2: Quantify the spring force

The spring is pre-loaded to keep the spool in the default position. The force at the **end of stroke** is:

$$
F_{spring} = k \cdot L
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{spring} &= 1.5 \ \tfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}} \times 12 \ \text{mm} \\
&= 18 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Quantify the pilot pressure force

The pilot pressure acts on the pilot piston area $A_{pilot} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_s^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{pilot} &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 \\
&= 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The force generated by pilot pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{pilot} &= p_{pilot} \cdot A_{pilot} \\
&= (4 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-4}) \\
&= 314.16 \ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the force balance to find minimum required force

For the spool to just begin moving (overcoming static friction, $F_{fric} \approx 5$ N in this example):

$$
F_{pilot} \geq F_{spring} + F_{fric}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{pilot,\ min} = 18 + 5 = 23 \ \text{N}
$$

Since the available pilot force is **314.16 N**, the design is **safe by a factor of 13.6**. This is why **pilot-operated DCVs can switch much larger flows** than direct-acting solenoid valves.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of DCV Configurations

| Parameter | 3/2 DCV | 4/2 DCV | 5/2 DCV | 4/3 DCV (Closed) | 5/3 DCV (Tandem) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ports** | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| **Positions** | 2 | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| **Actuator Type** | Single-acting | Double-acting | Double-acting | Double-acting | Double-acting |
| **Exhaust Path** | Single | Single | Separate A & B | Single | Separate A & B |
| **Speed Control** | Possible | Limited | Excellent | Limited | Excellent |
| **Position Holding** | No | No | No | **Yes (center)** | **Yes (center)** |
| **Typical Use** | Air jets, simple clamping | Mobile hydraulics | Industrial pneumatics | Hydraulic press control | Energy-saving systems |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Directional Control Valves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Directional Control Valves] --> startB{By Ports and Positions}
    startB --> startC[2 by 2 Valve]
    startB --> startD[3 by 2 Valve]
    startB --> startE[4 by 2 Valve]
    startB --> startF[5 by 2 Valve]
    startB --> startG[4 by 3 Valve]
    startB --> startH[5 by 3 Valve]

    startA --> startI{By Actuation}
    startI --> startJ[Manual Lever]
    startI --> startK[Push Button]
    startI --> startL[Mechanical Cam]
    startI --> startM[Solenoid Direct]
    startI --> startN[Solenoid Pilot]
    startI --> startO[Pilot Pneumatic]
    startI --> startP[Pilot Hydraulic]

    startA --> startQ{By Spool Type}
    startQ --> startR[Poppet DCV]
    startQ --> startS[Spool DCV]
    startQ --> startT[Disc Seat DCV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The classification above is **directly aligned with the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus** mapping for PCCPT205. This structure appears in **ESE Part A questions** almost every semester.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a 5/2 Single-Solenoid, Spring-Return DCV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supplyA[Compressor Supply 6 bar] --> portA[Port P Pressure Inlet]
    cylA[Port A Cylinder Cap End] --> workOut[Work Output Linear Motion]
    cylB[Port B Cylinder Rod End] --> workOut
    tankR[Port R Exhaust 1] --> atmosphereA[Atmosphere]
    tankS[Port S Exhaust 2] --> atmosphereA

    coilX[Solenoid Coil 24 V DC] --> pilotX[Pilot Stage Amplifier]
    springX[Return Spring] --> pilotX
    pilotX --> spoolX[Spool Sliding Element]
    spoolX --> portA
    spoolX --> cylA
    spoolX --> cylB
    spoolX --> tankR
    spoolX --> tankS

    pLC[PLC or Microcontroller 24 V Signal] --> coilX
    sensorA[Limit Switch Feedback] --> pLC
```

## 4.3 State-Transition Diagram — 5/2 Solenoid DCV (Two Stable Positions)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Position1
    Position1: Position 1 De Energized
    Position1: P to A active
    Position1: B to R exhaust
    Position2: Position 2 Energized
    Position2: P to B active
    Position2: A to S exhaust
    Position1 --> Position2: Solenoid Energized 24V applied
    Position2 --> Position1: Solenoid De Energized Spring returns
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DCV in a Cyber-Physical Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPS[CYBER LAYER]
        ctrl1[PLC or Microcontroller]
        hmi1[HMI Operator Panel]
        sensor1[Position Sensors]
    end

    subgraph INTERFACE[SIGNAL INTERFACE LAYER]
        coil1[Solenoid Coil 24V DC]
        relay1[Driver Relay or MOSFET]
    end

    subgraph PHYSICAL[PHYSICAL FLUID POWER LAYER]
        dcv1[5 by 2 DCV]
        cyl1[Double Acting Cylinder]
        pmp1[Compressor and FRL Unit]
    end

    ctrl1 --> relay1
    relay1 --> coil1
    coil1 --> dcv1
    pmp1 --> dcv1
    dcv1 --> cyl1
    cyl1 --> workOut[Mechanical Work Output]
    cyl1 --> sensor1
    sensor1 --> ctrl1
    hmi1 --> ctrl1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Insight:** Notice the **closed feedback loop** in the architecture above. The PLC (cyber layer) commands the DCV (physical layer), the cylinder moves, the position sensor reports back, and the PLC makes the next decision. This is the **defining feature of a Cyber-Physical System** — the seamless integration of computation, communication, and physical actuation.
