# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a pressure relief valve. With a neat sketch, explain its working principle. Mention its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pressure Relief Valve** is a normally-closed pressure control valve used to limit the maximum pressure in a hydraulic circuit to a preset safe value. It consists of a spring-loaded poppet or spool that senses the system pressure.

**Working Principle:**
- When system pressure × spool area > spring preload, the spool lifts.
- Excess fluid is vented to the tank, capping the pressure.
- When pressure drops, the spring re-seats the spool.

**Applications:** Hydraulic presses, mobile equipment, machine tools, and accumulator charging circuits.

**Examiner's Note:** Always mention **cracking pressure** and **blow-down** — 3-mark answers that omit these are penalized.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a pressure relief valve and a pressure reducing valve.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Relief Valve | Reducing Valve |
|:----------|:-------------|:----------------|
| Function | Limits maximum pressure | Maintains reduced downstream pressure |
| Normal state | Normally closed | Normally open |
| Connection | In parallel with pump (line to tank) | In series with branch line |
| Operation | Vents excess to tank | Throttles inlet flow |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the following

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **pilot-operated pressure relief valve** with a neat sketch. Compare it with a direct-acting relief valve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic system uses a pump delivering 30 L/min at 200 bar. A pilot-operated relief valve is set at 180 bar. An unloading valve opens when system pressure reaches 180 bar and dumps the flow to tank at 8 bar. Calculate:
(i) Hydraulic power at working pressure.
(ii) Hydraulic power in the unloaded state.
(iii) Power saved and percentage saving. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Pilot-Operated Relief Valve

**Construction:**
A pilot-operated relief valve has two stages:

1. **Main stage (large spool):** Handles the bulk of the flow. Its top face is exposed to a chamber connected to the pump line. The chamber pressure is normally trapped, holding the main spool closed.

2. **Pilot stage (small poppet):** A small valve that senses system pressure. It is spring-loaded and adjustable. The pilot drains to tank.

**Working:**
- When system pressure **< set pressure:** pilot poppet remains closed → chamber pressure equals line pressure → main spool held closed.
- When system pressure **≥ set pressure:** pilot poppet opens → chamber pressure drops → main spool lifts → flow dumps to tank.
- Result: smooth modulation with very low blow-down (typically 2–5%).

**Comparison with Direct-Acting:**

| Aspect | Direct-Acting | Pilot-Operated |
|:-------|:--------------|:----------------|
| Construction | Single stage | Two stage |
| Flow capacity | Low (< 100 L/min) | High (up to 1000 L/min) |
| Blow-down | High (10–30%) | Low (2–5%) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Application | Mobile circuits | Industrial presses |

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $Q = 30 \, \text{L/min}$
- $P_{sys} = 200 \, \text{bar}$ (but system is normally at 180 bar)
- $P_{set} = 180 \, \text{bar}$ (valve set point)
- $P_{unl} = 8 \, \text{bar}$ (unloaded pump pressure)

**(i) Hydraulic power at working pressure (180 bar):**

$$P_{working} = \frac{Q \cdot P_{sys}}{600} = \frac{30 \cdot 180}{600} = 9 \, \text{kW}$$

**(ii) Hydraulic power in unloaded state (8 bar):**

$$P_{unloaded} = \frac{Q \cdot P_{unl}}{600} = \frac{30 \cdot 8}{600} = 0.4 \, \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Power saved and percentage saving:**

$$P_{saved} = 9 - 0.4 = 8.6 \, \text{kW}$$

$$\eta_{saved} = \frac{8.6}{9} \times 100 = 95.56\%$$

**Conclusion:** The unloading valve saves **8.6 kW** (≈ **95.6%** of the operating hydraulic power) during idle cycles — a dramatic energy efficiency improvement.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of a **pressure sequence valve** and a **counterbalance valve**. Where are they used in practice? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A direct-acting pressure relief valve has a spool diameter of 15 mm. The spring constant is 40 N/mm and the preload is 5 mm. Calculate:
(i) The cracking pressure in bar.
(ii) The spring force and corresponding pressure if the spool lifts by an additional 2 mm.
(iii) The percentage blow-down. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — Sequence & Counterbalance Valves

**Pressure Sequence Valve:**
- A normally-closed, 2-position valve that opens when inlet pressure reaches a set value.
- Used for **timing/sequencing** actuators — e.g., clamp-then-inject in injection molding.
- It contains a spring-loaded spool with a pilot port sensing inlet pressure.

**Working:**
1. Pump flow enters the valve.
2. When inlet pressure < set → spool blocks the outlet port → no flow to the secondary actuator.
3. When inlet pressure ≥ set → spool shifts → flow passes through to the secondary circuit.

**Counterbalance Valve:**
- A normally-closed valve mounted on the **return line** of a vertical actuator.
- Prevents gravitational over-running of the load.

**Working:**
1. Load attempts to descend → fluid tries to escape the cap end.
2. The valve senses inlet pressure on the cylinder's pressure side as a pilot.
3. The valve remains closed until inlet pressure is sufficient to *support* the load.
4. Load can descend only at a controlled rate (throttled return flow).

**Applications:**
- Sequence: Plastic molding, machine tool tool-changers, indexing tables.
- Counterbalance: Cranes, excavator booms, lift platforms, fork-lift masts.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $d = 15 \, \text{mm}$
- $k = 40 \, \text{N/mm}$
- $x_0 = 5 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 1: Spool area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (15)^2 = 176.71 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**(i) Cracking pressure:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{k \cdot x_0}{A} = \frac{40 \cdot 5}{176.71} = 1.1317 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$

Converting to bar:

$$P_{cr} = 1.1317 \times 10 = 11.32 \, \text{bar}$$

**(ii) Spring force and pressure at additional lift of 2 mm:**

Total compression: $x_{total} = 5 + 2 = 7 \, \text{mm}$

$$F_{s,full} = k \cdot x_{total} = 40 \cdot 7 = 280 \, \text{N}$$

$$P_{full} = \frac{280}{176.71} = 1.5846 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 15.85 \, \text{bar}$$

**(iii) Percentage blow-down:**

$$\Delta P_{bd} = P_{full} - P_{cr} = 15.85 - 11.32 = 4.53 \, \text{bar}$$

$$\% \, \text{Blowdown} = \frac{4.53}{11.32} \times 100 = 40.02\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> Many students compute **cracking pressure** correctly but forget the **unit conversion** from N/mm² to bar. Since KTU expects bar in the final answer, omitting the ×10 conversion loses **1 full mark** per sub-part. Also, percentage blow-down must be expressed as a *percentage*, not a fraction. Numerical answers without units are penalized another **0.5 mark** each.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Pressure Control Valves**

- **Pressure Relief Valve:** *Normally closed*; vents excess to tank; protects system from over-pressure.
- **Pressure Reducing Valve:** *Normally open*; maintains constant downstream pressure for a branch circuit.
- **Pressure Sequence Valve:** *Pressure-triggered switch*; activates secondary circuit at a set value.
- **Counterbalance Valve:** Prevents *gravitational runaway* of vertical loads; provides back-pressure on return line.
- **Unloading Valve:** Routes pump flow to *tank at near-zero pressure* during idle; massive energy saving.
- **Cracking Pressure Formula:** $P_{cr} = F_s / A_{spool}$
- **Blow-down:** Difference between cracking and full-flow pressures — *smaller is better quality*.
- **Pilot-operated valves:** Two-stage; lower blow-down; preferred for high-flow industrial systems.
- **Direct-acting valves:** Single-stage; simpler; used in mobile/low-flow circuits.
- **Pump Power Formula:** $P_{hyd} = (Q \cdot p) / 600$ with Q in L/min and p in bar → gives kW.
- **Standard Symbol:** Square box with arrows + spring on one side; dashed lines indicate pilot signals.
- **ISO Standards:** ISO 4413 (hydraulic), ISO 5599 (pneumatic), ISO 1219 (symbols).
- **CPS Context:** Pressure control valves = physical-layer *actuators* that close the sensor-to-actuator loop in a Cyber-Physical System.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember valve types — "RRS-CUU":**
> **R**elief, **R**educing, **S**equence, **C**ounterbalance, **U**nloading, **U**nloading (differential).
> Three R's, one C, two U's, and one S — easily visualized in an exam.

---

### End of Module 4 — Pressure Control Valves Notes
**Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCPT205 — Sensors and Actuators (Cyber Physical)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cylinders # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Cylinders** are *linear fluid-power actuators* that convert the energy of a pressurized working fluid — compressed air (pneumatic) or hydraulic oil (hydraulic) — into a unidirectional or bidirectional straight-line mechanical force and motion along a single axis. They belong to the family of **translational actuators** and form the workhorse of nearly every automated industrial, robotic, and cyber-physical production system. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, cylinders are the *muscles* of the physical layer: they receive electrical control signals (the cyber command), which actuate solenoid valves that direct pressurized fluid, producing a controlled mechanical response that interacts with the physical world. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Highlight** > A *cylinder* is *not* a sensor. It is an **actuator**. The cylinder's *state* (extended, retracted, position, velocity, force) is measured by **sensors** and fed back to the controller, closing the CPS loop. ### Types of Cylinders (KTU Taxonomy) | Class | Sub-Type | Distinguishing Feature | |---|---|---| | **Acting Type** | Single-acting | One fluid port; spring/load returns the piston | | | Double-acting | Two fluid ports; force produced in both directions | | **Stroke Type** | Telescopic (Multi-stage) | Nested pistons give long stroke in short envelope | | **Special** | Tandem | Two pistons on one rod for higher force | | | Cushioned | Decelerates piston at end-of-stroke | | | Double-rod (through-rod) | Rod on both sides; equal force both directions | | | Rotary (Limited-angle) | Produces partial rotary motion via rack-pinion or vane | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump & Syringe" Intuition Imagine pushing the plunger of a giant syringe whose nozzle is sealed: as you push harder, the trapped air pushes back. Now replace your hand with a high-pressure pump, and the syringe becomes a **cylinder**: the fluid's pressure multiplies across the piston face to produce a large mechanical force, sliding the rod out. A **single-acting cylinder** behaves like a tin-opener — push it with air, and a coiled spring retracts it when the air is exhausted. A **double-acting cylinder** behaves like a two-way piston pump — air drives the piston outward on one stroke, and on the return stroke the air enters from the *other* side, pulling the piston back. There is no spring; motion is fully controlled in *both* directions. ## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Standard KTU Notation) - **Bore diameter** = **D** (inner barrel diameter, in mm) - **Rod diameter** = **d** (in mm) - **Stroke length** = **L** (in mm) - **Supply pressure** = **P_s** (in bar or MPa; **1 bar = 10⁵ N/m² = 0.1 MPa**) - **Return / back pressure** = **P_r** - **Piston area** = **A_p** = **πD²/4** - **Annular (rod-side) area** = **A_a** = **π(D² − d²)/4** - **Flow rate** = **Q** (in m³/s or L/min) - **Piston velocity** = **v** (in m/s) - **Force output** = **F** (in N) - **Power** = **P_out** (in W) > [!NOTE] > **Industry standard pressure units**: pneumatic systems typically operate at **4 – 8 bar**, while hydraulic systems use **100 – 350 bar**. The higher the pressure, the smaller the cylinder needed for a given force — this is why hydraulic presses are compact yet immensely powerful. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics "Why" Cylinders are governed by **Pascal's Law** (for hydraulic) and the **Ideal Gas / Compressed Fluid Law** (for pneumatic): > *"Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction, and acts perpendicularly to every portion of the confining surface."* When pressurized fluid acts on the piston face of area **A**, the resulting **thrust force** is simply: $$F = P \cdot A$$ This is the **single most important equation** in fluid-power cylinder analysis. ## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy (Block-by-Block) A double-acting cylinder consists of: 1. **Cylinder barrel / tube** — houses the piston; precision honed for seal life. 2. **End caps (head & cap)** — sealed with the barrel via tie-rods or threaded joints. 3. **Piston** — separates the two chambers, mounts the seals. 4. **Piston rod** — transmits force externally. 5. **Seals** — piston seal (between piston and bore) and rod seal (wiper + main). 6. **Cushion bushings** — at end caps to provide deceleration. 7. **Ports (A & B)** — fluid entry/exit, switched by a **4/2 or 4/3 directional control valve (DCV)**. ## 2.3 Operation Cycle of a Double-Acting Cylinder **Extension Stroke:** - DCV routes supply pressure **P_s** to Port A (cap-end). - The rod-end chamber (Port B) is connected to tank (P_r ≈ 0). - Effective area = full bore area **A_p**. **Retraction Stroke:** - DCV routes **P_s** to Port B (rod-end). - The cap-end chamber (Port A) is connected to tank. - Effective area = annular area **A_a**. - *Force on retract < Force on extend* because **A_a < A_p**. - *Velocity on retract > Velocity on extend* for the same flow rate **Q** (conservation of volume). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table) > [!NOTE] > All formula symbols are universally accepted in KTU board exam answers. Use the symbols exactly as shown. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Piston (full bore) area | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | m² | **Cap-end / Extend area** | | 2 | Annular (rod) area | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$ | m² | **Rod-end / Retract area** | | 3 | Area ratio | $R = \dfrac{A_a}{A_p} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.5 – 0.7 | | 4 | Theoretical extend force | $F_{ext} = P_s A_p - P_r A_a$ | N | With P_r ≠ 0 | | 5 | Theoretical retract force | $F_{ret} = P_s A_a - P_r A_p$ | N | Always F_ret < F_ext | | 6 | Effective extend force (P_r = 0) | $F_{ext} = P_s A_p$ | N | Most common KTU case | | 7 | Effective retract force (P_r = 0) | $F_{ret} = P_s A_a$ | N | | | 8 | Piston velocity (extend) | $v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | m/s | Q in m³/s | | 9 | Piston velocity (retract) | $v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_a}$ | m/s | | | 10 | Hydraulic power | $P_{hyd} = P_s \cdot Q$ | W | | | 11 | Mechanical power out | $P_{out} = F \cdot v$ | W | Equals P_hyd × η (efficiency) | | 12 | Cylinder swept volume | $V = A_p \cdot L$ | m³ | For air/oil consumption | | 13 | Time for full stroke (extend) | $t_{ext} = \dfrac{A_p L}{Q}$ | s | | | 14 | Cushioning kinetic energy | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | J | Energy to absorb in cushion | | 15 | Buckling load (Euler) | $F_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K L)^{2}}$ | N | Rod must not buckle | | 16 | Pressure conversion | **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa = 10 N/cm²** | — | Memorize for KTU | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pneumatic vs. Hydraulic — remember for theory questions:** > - *Pneumatic cylinders* use **air** (compressible) — cheaper, faster, lower force (~6 bar typical). > - *Hydraulic cylinders* use **oil** (incompressible) — higher force, slower, precise position-holding. > - In KTU numericals, *always* use **1 bar = 10⁵ N/m² = 0.1 N/mm²** to avoid unit pitfalls. ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Manufacturing**: Clamping, pressing, sheet-metal forming, plastic injection moulding. - **Mobile hydraulics**: Excavator arms, tractor lifts, hydraulic presses. - **CPS & Industry 4.0**: Each cylinder becomes a "smart actuator" when fitted with a *linear position sensor* (LVDT or magnetostrictive) and a *pressure transducer* — feeding real-time data to a PLC for closed-loop force/position control. - **Robotics**: The prismatic joint of a robot arm is essentially a double-acting cylinder with position feedback. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force on a Double-Acting Cylinder ### Statement Derive the expressions for the **extending** and **retracting** forces of a double-acting cylinder when supply pressure is $P_s$ and the return (back) pressure on the opposite side is $P_r$. ### Given - Bore diameter = $D$ - Rod diameter = $d$ - Supply pressure = $P_s$ - Return pressure = $P_r$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the two effective piston areas.** The piston is in contact with fluid on two sides. The cap-end face is a full disc of diameter $D$: $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$ The rod-end face is a disc of diameter $D$ minus the cross-section occupied by the rod of diameter $d$ (an annulus): $$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} - \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2})$$ **Step 2 — Pressure force on cap-end (Piston side).** Pressure $P_s$ pushes the piston with a force in the *extending* direction: $$F_{cap} = P_s \cdot A_p = P_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Pressure force on rod-end (opposes extension).** Return pressure $P_r$ acts on the annular area, pushing the piston in the *opposite* (retracting) direction: $$F_{oppose} = P_r \cdot A_a = P_r \cdot \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$$ **Step 4 — Net extending force (Newton's Second Law on fluid column).** $$F_{ext} = F_{cap} - F_{oppose}$$ $$\boxed{\,F_{ext} = P_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;-\; P_r \cdot \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}\,}$$ If the rod end is vented to tank and $P_r \approx 0$: $$F_{ext} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot P_s$$ **Step 5 — Net retracting force.** During retraction, the DCV reverses the supply: now $P_s$ acts on the *rod side* and $P_r$ acts on the *cap side*. By the identical analysis: $$\boxed{\,F_{ret} = P_s \cdot \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} \;-\; P_r \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\,}$$ With $P_r = 0$: $$F_{ret} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} \cdot P_s$$ **Step 6 — Key observation (compare forces).** Since $(D^{2} - d^{2}) < D^{2}$, we always have: $$F_{ret} < F_{ext}$$ The area ratio: $$R = \frac{A_a}{A_p} = \frac{D^{2} - d^{2}}{D^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2}$$ For the *typical* KTU ratio $d = D/2$: $$R = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$ So $F_{ret} = 0.75 \times F_{ext}$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piston Velocity from Continuity ### Statement A pump delivers a steady flow rate $Q$ to a double-acting cylinder. Derive the extending and retracting piston velocities. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Apply the continuity equation.** The volume of fluid entering the cylinder per unit time must equal the volume swept by the piston per unit time: $$Q = A_{eff} \cdot v$$ where $A_{eff}$ is the area into which the fluid is entering. **Step 2 — Extension stroke.** Fluid enters the cap-end (area $A_p$): $$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Retraction stroke.** Fluid enters the rod-end (area $A_a$): $$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_a} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}$$ **Step 4 — Velocity ratio.** $$\frac{v_{ret}}{v_{ext}} = \frac{A_p}{A_a} = \frac{1}{R} > 1$$ So the cylinder retracts *faster* than it extends, for the *same* flow rate $Q$. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern):** > A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm** and a rod diameter of **60 mm**. The supply pressure is **120 bar** and the pump delivers **40 L/min**. The load opposes motion with a force of **50 kN** during extension. Calculate: > (i) Extending force (ii) Retracting force (iii) Extending velocity (iv) Retracting velocity (v) Hydraulic power. ### Solution **Step 1 — Convert all units to SI.** - $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$ - $P_s = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 1.2 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$ - $Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$ **Step 2 — Compute areas.** $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.1)^{2} - (0.06)^{2}\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01 - 0.0036) = 5.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — (i) Extending force (assuming P_r = 0).** $$F_{ext} = P_s \cdot A_p = (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{ext} = 9.425 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}$$ Since the load is 50 kN, the cylinder *can* move the load (94.25 > 50). ✔ **Step 4 — (ii) Retracting force.** $$F_{ret} = P_s \cdot A_a = (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.026 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{ret} = 6.032 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — (iii) Extending velocity.** $$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \approx 84.9 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Step 6 — (iv) Retracting velocity.** $$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_a} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.026 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 132.6 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Step 7 — (v) Hydraulic power.** $$P_{hyd} = P_s \cdot Q = (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-4})$$ $$P_{hyd} = 8000 \text{ W} = 8 \text{ kW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cross-check:** Mechanical power during extension $= F_{ext} \times v_{ext} = 94250 \times 0.0849 \approx 8000$ W ✓ > This confirms energy conservation — a vital KTU answer-verification technique. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Single vs. Double-Acting | Criterion | Single-Acting | Double-Acting | |---|---|---| | Number of ports | 1 | 2 | | Force in both directions | ✗ (spring return only) | ✔ | | Position control on retract | Difficult | Precise | | Energy consumption (air) | Lower (no air on return) | Higher | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Stroke length | Limited by spring length | Unlimited | | Typical KTU application | Clamping, ejecting, simple pushing | Press work, machine-tool slides, robotics | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Constructional Schematic — Double-Acting Cylinder (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef chamber fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef component fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 classDef fluid fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 classDef port fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 BA[Barrel Assembly]:::component HC[Head End Cap]:::component CC[Cap End]:::component PIST[Piston with Seal]:::component ROD[Piston Rod]:::component RSEAL[Rod Seal and Wiper]:::component CUSH[Cushion Bushing]:::component CHA[Chamber A - Cap End
Full Bore Area Ap]:::chamber CHB[Chamber B - Rod End
Annular Area Aa]:::chamber PORTA[Port A
Supply on Extend]:::port PORTB[Port B
Supply on Retract]:::port PAir[Pressurized Fluid P sub s]:::fluid PTank[Tank Return P sub r approx 0]:::fluid PORTA --> CHA CHA --> HC HC --> BA BA --> CC CC --> CHB CHB --> PORTB PIST --- CHA PIST --- CHB ROD --- PIST ROD --> RSEAL CUSH --- CC CUSH --- HC PAir -.-> PORTA PORTB -.-> PTank ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Control & Actuation Loop (CPS View) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef cyber fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000 classDef physical fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 classDef act fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 CTRL[PLC or Microcontroller
Cyber Layer]:::cyber VALVE[4 by 2 Solenoid DCV
Directional Control]:::act CYL[Double Acting Cylinder
Physical Actuator]:::act LOAD[Mechanical Load
Physical World]:::physical PSENS[Pressure Transducer
Force Feedback]:::sensor LSENS[Linear Position Sensor
LVDT or Magnetostrictive]:::sensor VFD[V to I Converter and ADC]:::cyber CTRL -- PWM or Digital Command --> VALVE VALVE -- Compressed Air or Oil --> CYL CYL -- Thrust Force --> LOAD LOAD --> LSENS CYL --> PSENS LSENS --> VFD PSENS --> VFD VFD -- Feedback Signal --> CTRL ``` ## 4.3 Cushioning Mechanism — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef node fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 classDef out fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 PIST2[Piston Approaching End Cap]:::node SEAL2[Piston Seals Against Cushion Bushing]:::node TRAP[Trapped Fluid Pocket Forms]:::proc ORF[Forced Through Adjustable Orifice]:::proc HEAT[Kinetic Energy Converted to Heat]:::proc STOP[Smooth Deceleration and Stop]:::out PIST2 --> SEAL2 --> TRAP --> ORF --> HEAT --> STOP ``` > [!NOTE] > The cushioning schematic above is a **process-flow representation** of the physical cushioning action. In a KTU board answer, you would redraw this as a sectional cylinder sketch showing the cushion bush, the trapped pocket, and the needle valve. The functional flow above is the *safe* way to convey the same idea using Mermaid syntax without violating the no-physical-drawing rule. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting cylinder. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting | |---|---|---| | Number of fluid ports | One | Two | | Force direction | Push only (extension by fluid) | Push and Pull | | Return mechanism | External spring or gravity | Fluid pressure | | Air consumption | Lower (no air on return) | Higher | | Typical applications | Clamping, ejecting | Press work, robotic prismatic joints | *(Write any 4 points in 3-mark format; a clean comparison table scores full marks.)* ### Q2. What is cushioning in a pneumatic cylinder? Why is it required? **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Model Answer:** Cushioning is a *deceleration mechanism* built into the end caps of a cylinder to **absorb the kinetic energy of the piston** as it approaches the end of its stroke, thereby preventing mechanical impact and reducing noise and vibration. **Need for cushioning:** 1. Prevents damage to piston, seals, and end caps from high-velocity impact. 2. Reduces wear, extending cylinder life. 3. Eliminates hammering noise — important in CPS where quiet operation is desired. 4. Enables higher cycle speeds in automation. **Working:** Near the end of stroke, a small chamber is formed between the piston and end cap. The fluid is forced out through a *restrictive orifice*, converting kinetic energy to heat and slowing the piston smoothly. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Rule:** Candidates answer ONE full question (with both sub-parts) out of the two alternatives. Internal choice between the two sub-parts of the *same* question is also permitted. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain the construction and working of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with a neat sketch. State its advantages over a single-acting cylinder. (7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** #### Model Solution **Construction (Block-Description for KTU Board):** 1. **Barrel (Tube)**: Precision-honed steel tube that houses the piston. 2. **End Caps (Head & Cap)**: Threaded or tie-rod fastened to the barrel; contain fluid ports. 3. **Piston**: Slides inside the barrel; carries the primary seal. 4. **Piston Rod**: Hard-chrome plated steel; transmits force. 5. **Seals**: Piston seal (between piston and bore), rod seal (prevents leakage), and wiper (keeps contaminants off the rod). 6. **Cushion Bushings**: At both end caps for end-of-stroke deceleration. 7. **Ports A & B**: Cap-end and rod-end fluid connections. **Working (Sequence):** - **Extending Stroke:** 4/2 DCV directs high-pressure oil to Port A. The full piston area $A_p$ is exposed, producing maximum thrust. - **Retracting Stroke:** DCV shifts; high-pressure oil enters Port B. Only the annular area $A_a$ is pressurized, so retract force < extend force. - The rod slides through the rod seal, transferring force to the external load. - A check valve / accumulator in the line maintains pressure-holding capability. **Advantages over Single-Acting:** 1. Force generated in *both* directions → precise control in both strokes. 2. No spring → unlimited stroke length, no spring fatigue. 3. Faster cycle time; better dynamic response. 4. Better position-holding and rigidity against external loads. 5. Suitable for active loads (those resisting in both directions). **(b) A double-acting cylinder has a bore of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 45 mm. The supply pressure is 100 bar and the return pressure is 5 bar. The pump flow rate is 30 L/min. Calculate: (i) Effective extending force, (ii) Effective retracting force, (iii) Extending velocity, (iv) Power developed. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Step 1 — Unit Conversion.** - $D = 0.08$ m, $d = 0.045$ m - $P_s = 100 \times 10^{5} = 1.0 \times 10^{7}$ Pa - $P_r = 5 \times 10^{5} = 0.05 \times 10^{7}$ Pa - $Q = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s **Step 2 — Areas.** $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^{2} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ $$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.08)^{2} - (0.045)^{2}\right] = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0064 - 0.002025) = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — (i) Effective Extending Force.** $$F_{ext} = P_s A_p - P_r A_a$$ $$F_{ext} = (1.0 \times 10^{7})(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.05 \times 10^{7})(3.436 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{ext} = 50270 - 1718 = 48{,}552 \text{ N} \approx 48.55 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — (ii) Effective Retracting Force.** $$F_{ret} = P_s A_a - P_r A_p$$ $$F_{ret} = (1.0 \times 10^{7})(3.436 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.05 \times 10^{7})(5.027 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{ret} = 34360 - 2514 = 31{,}846 \text{ N} \approx 31.85 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — (iii) Extending Velocity.** $$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 99.5 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Step 6 — (iv) Power Developed (hydraulic).** $$P_{hyd} = P_s \cdot Q = (1.0 \times 10^{7})(5.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 5000 \text{ W} = 5.0 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes (Valuation Pitfalls):** > 1. Forgetting the **return pressure term** when $P_r \neq 0$ — costs 1–2 marks. > 2. Using **$P_s \times A_a$** for extension (a very common slip) — always identify which side the supply acts on. > 3. Mixing up **bar → Pa** conversion. Memorize: **1 bar = 10⁵ N/m²** = 10 N/cm². > 4. Not stating the *units* in the final answer — KTU examiners explicitly deduct for missing units. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) With neat sketches, explain the following types of pneumatic cylinders: (i) Single-acting, (ii) Double-acting, (iii) Telescopic, (iv) Tandem. (7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** #### Model Solution **(i) Single-Acting Cylinder:** - Has *one* fluid port and an internal return spring. - Pressurized air drives the piston outward; spring retracts it when air is vented. - Compact, economical, used in clamping, marking, and simple ejection. - Force is available *only* in one direction. **(ii) Double-Acting Cylinder:** - Has *two* fluid ports (A & B). - Air drives the piston in both directions alternately via a DCV. - *Higher* force in extension (full bore area); *lower* force in retraction (annular area). - Most widely used in industrial automation. **(iii) Telescopic Cylinder (Multi-stage):** - Comprises 2 or 3 *nested* pistons of progressively smaller diameters on a common rod. - Each stage extends sequentially, giving a **very long stroke** with a **very short retracted envelope**. - Typical applications: dump trucks, refuse collectors, large-stroke lifting platforms. - Force decreases as stages extend (smaller effective area per stage). **(iv) Tandem Cylinder:** - Two pistons are mounted on a *single* common rod, back-to-back, inside one barrel. - The effective working area is the **sum of both piston areas**, producing roughly **double the thrust** of a single piston of the same bore. - Used where space is limited but a *very high force* is required. **(b) What is cushioning? Explain with a neat sketch the cushioning mechanism in a hydraulic cylinder. Also derive the expression for kinetic energy absorbed by the cushion. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Definition:** Cushioning is a *built-in deceleration feature* in hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders that gradually absorbs the kinetic energy of the moving piston as it reaches the end of stroke, preventing impact damage and reducing noise. **Mechanism (Cushion Construction):** - A **cushion sleeve/bushing** projects from the end cap into the bore. - A small **needle valve (orifice)** in the end cap allows restricted exhaust of fluid. - As the piston approaches the end cap, its leading edge *enters* the cushion bush, sealing off a small fluid pocket. - The remaining fluid must escape only through the *throttle orifice* — flow is restricted, pressure builds, and a *cushion pressure* opposes the motion, decelerating the piston smoothly. **Derivation of Energy Absorbed:** Let: - $m$ = total moving mass (piston + rod + load), in kg - $v$ = piston velocity just before entering the cushion, in m/s - $x$ = cushion length, in m - $F_c$ = average cushioning force, in N - $E_k$ = kinetic energy absorbed, in J **Step 1 — Kinetic energy of the moving mass at entry to cushion:** $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Work done by the cushion pressure in decelerating the piston over distance $x$:** $$W = F_c \cdot x$$ **Step 3 — Equating kinetic energy to work done (Work–Energy Theorem):** $$F_c \cdot x = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\,F_c = \frac{m v^{2}}{2 x}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Cushion pressure (force per unit effective cushion area $A_c$):** $$P_c = \frac{F_c}{A_c} = \frac{m v^{2}}{2 x A_c}$$ **Design Insight:** A longer cushion length $x$ reduces $F_c$ and hence $P_c$ — making deceleration gentler but requiring a *larger* cylinder envelope. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Cushioning Question):** > 1. Many students confuse cushioning with *braking*. Cushioning is **hydraulic/pneumatic**, not friction-based. > 2. The cushion pressure $P_c$ can be **2 – 5 times** the supply pressure — never use supply pressure in the energy equation. > 3. Always state the assumption of *average* cushioning force; instantaneous force varies as a function of position. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cylinders (Module 4)** - **Core identity:** A cylinder is a *linear fluid-power actuator* — not a sensor. - **Master equation:** $F = P \times A$ (Pascal's law application). - **Two area formulae (memorize cold):** - Piston (full bore): $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ - Annular (rod side): $A_a = \dfrac{\pi(D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$ - **Force direction rule:** - Extension → Supply on cap-end → Force uses $A_p$. - Retraction → Supply on rod-end → Force uses $A_a$. - **Always true:** $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ and $v_{ret} > v_{ext}$ (for the same flow rate $Q$). - **Cushioning purpose:** Absorb $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ smoothly → prevent impact. - **Pneumatic** ≈ low pressure, compressible, cheap, fast. - **Hydraulic** ≈ high pressure, incompressible, precise, powerful. - **Unit conversions to memorize:** - 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 0.1 MPa = 10 N/cm² - 1 L/min = $\dfrac{10^{-3}}{60}$ m³/s ≈ $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s - **Cross-check method:** $P_{hyd} = P_s Q = F v$ (ideal, no losses). - **CPS link:** Cylinders + Position Sensor + Pressure Sensor + PLC = closed-loop *smart actuator*. - **Common exam trap:** Forgetting to subtract the *back-pressure term* when $P_r \neq 0$. > **End of Notes — Cylinders (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4)**

topics.title: Servo and Proportional Control Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Servo and Proportional Control Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Servo Control Valves — Formal Definition

A **servo valve** (specifically an **electrohydraulic servo valve**) is a high-precision, closed-loop, multi-stage flow control device that uses a small electrical input signal to control a much larger hydraulic flow rate or pressure output. It is the central nerve of any high-bandwidth motion control system, capable of positioning a hydraulic actuator (cylinder or motor) with extreme accuracy, repeatability, and dynamic response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Servo valves are *closed-loop* devices — they contain an internal mechanical feedback (e.g., a feedback spring + feedback port) that continuously compares the commanded spool position with the actual spool position, correcting any error within milliseconds.

The defining technical characteristic of a servo valve is its **closed-loop spool position control**. The valve does not simply allow fluid to pass proportional to current (like a proportional valve); it actively *senses* the spool's position and *forces* it to match the demanded position through a closed feedback loop — hence the name "servo" (from the Latin *servus*, meaning *servant* or *one who follows* the command).

## 1.2 Proportional Control Valves — Formal Definition

A **proportional control valve** is an *open-loop* (in the valve itself) flow or pressure control device in which the output flow/pressure is proportional to the magnitude of the input electrical current applied to its proportional solenoid. It is a direct-acting or pilot-operated valve that translates a varying electrical signal into a corresponding, proportional hydraulic output — but **without internal closed-loop spool position feedback**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop:** A proportional valve is essentially a "smart" on/off solenoid. The spool position depends on the force balance between the proportional solenoid's magnetic force and the opposing spring force. There is no internal transducer to verify the spool's true position. Any load-induced disturbance directly affects the output.

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy — "The Car's Power Steering"

Imagine you are driving a modern luxury car:

| Component | Car Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Driver's hand on steering wheel | Electrical input signal (mA) | Issues the "command" |
| Steering shaft rotation | Spool displacement | Mechanical translation of command |
| Power steering pump + hydraulic fluid | High-pressure hydraulic supply | Provides the "muscle" |
| Tie rod moving the wheels | Hydraulic actuator (cylinder) | Does the heavy work |
| Steering wheel resistance feedback | Feedback spring + LVDT | Tells the system "is it there yet?" |
| ECU/Driver's eyes & brain | External closed-loop controller (PLC/MCU) | Compares actual vs desired |

A **proportional valve** is like driving without power steering — you push the wheel a little, the car turns a little. Push harder, it turns more. The response is proportional, but the load (a pothole, a crosswind) can push your wheels off course without you feeling it.

A **servo valve** is like a *servo-assisted* power steering system with built-in feedback. The moment you turn the wheel, sensors detect the car's actual turning rate and continuously adjust the hydraulic assistance to make the *actual* motion precisely match the *commanded* motion, regardless of road disturbances.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following bolded metrics are the **key performance parameters** the KTU examiner expects you to know:

- **Bandwidth (Hz):** The frequency at which the valve's phase lag reaches **−90°** (or amplitude drops to **−3 dB**). Servo valves: **50–500 Hz**; Proportional valves: **5–30 Hz**.
- **Step Response Time (ms):** Time to reach **90%** of commanded step (often called **T₉₀**). Servo valves: **5–20 ms**; Proportional valves: **30–150 ms**.
- **Hysteresis (%):** Difference between up-stroke and down-stroke output for the same input. Servo valves: **< 3%**; Proportional valves: **5–10%**.
- **Threshold/Spillage (%):** Minimum input signal to initiate flow. Servo valves: **< 0.5%**; Proportional valves: **2–5%**.
- **Rated Pressure:** Typically **350 bar (≈ 5075 psi)** for industrial servo valves.
- **Rated Flow:** Expressed at **ΔP = 70 bar (10 bar per land)** across the valve lands.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**S**ervo = **S**uper fast, **S**elf-corrected, **S**pring-fed feedback." "**P**roportional = **P**assive, **P**re-set, **P**ilot-operated (often)."

---

## 1.5 Block-Level Functional Distinction

The cleanest way KTU examiners want you to see the difference is at the block level:

**Proportional Valve Path:**
$$ \text{Input (mA)} \rightarrow \text{Proportional Solenoid} \rightarrow \text{Spool moves} \rightarrow \text{Flow to actuator} $$
(Open-loop. No internal spool-position verification.)

**Servo Valve Path:**
$$ \text{Input (mA)} \rightarrow \text{Torque Motor} \rightarrow \text{Nozzle-Flapper (1st stage)} \rightarrow \text{Spool (2nd stage)} \rightarrow \text{Flow to actuator} $$
$$ \uparrow \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \downarrow $$
$$ \text{LVDT/Feedback Spring} \longleftarrow \text{Mechanical Feedback Link} \longleftarrow \text{Spool Position} $$
(Closed-loop. Spool position is sensed and fed back to the flapper.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Typical Two-Stage Electrohydraulic Servo Valve

A standard industrial two-stage servo valve (e.g., Moog G761, Parker TS2020) has **two cascaded hydraulic amplification stages**:

### Stage 1: Electrical-to-Hydraulic Pilot (The Torque Motor + Nozzle-Flapper)
This is the **low-power, high-precision** stage. A small DC current (typically ±40 mA) is fed into the coils of a **permanent-magnet torque motor**. The torque motor armature rotates by a tiny angle (a fraction of a degree), which moves a **flapper** placed between two opposing **nozzles** that are fed with high-pressure pilot oil.

- If the flapper moves **left** → left nozzle orifice area decreases → pressure in left nozzle chamber **rises** (P₁ ↑).
- The flapper moving **left** also opens the right nozzle gap → pressure in right nozzle chamber **falls** (P₂ ↓).
- This differential pressure (P₁ − P₂) acts across the ends of the second-stage spool.

### Stage 2: Hydraulic Power Amplification (The Spool)
The differential pilot pressure (P₁ − P₂) acts on the **spool** end areas, generating a net hydraulic force that moves the spool against a centering **feedback spring**. The spool's lands uncover the **main control ports** that connect the high-pressure supply (P) and tank return (T) to the actuator ports (A and B).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two stages?** The first stage (nozzle-flapper) can only handle a few milliliters per minute — but it provides the precision. The second stage (spool) handles the *real* flow (often 100+ L/min). The cascaded arrangement gives **high precision + high power in one device**.

## 2.2 The Closed-Loop Feedback Mechanism

This is the **defining feature** that separates a servo valve from a proportional valve:

1. As the spool moves to the right, it pulls the **feedback spring** (a slender flexure tube or cantilever).
2. The feedback spring's tip is mechanically linked to the flapper (either directly or via a feedback pin/wire).
3. The spring force **opposes** the flapper's initial deflection.
4. Equilibrium is reached when the spring force = hydraulic force on the spool = torque motor force on the flapper.
5. The spool therefore takes up a position that is **precisely proportional to the input current**, and any disturbance (load, pressure change) that tries to move the spool is instantly counteracted by the spring–flapper feedback loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question asks "What makes a servo valve a closed-loop device?" the answer is the **mechanical feedback spring (or flexure tube) that links spool position back to the flapper**.

## 2.3 Proportional Valve — Working Principle

A proportional valve uses a **proportional solenoid** (a special solenoid with a proportional current-to-force characteristic, often with a manual override, pole-face shaping, and an integrated position sensor in advanced versions).

**Force balance on the proportional spool:**

$$ F_{\text{magnetic}} = F_{\text{spring}} + F_{\text{hydrodynamic}} + F_{\text{friction}} $$

- **F_magnetic:** Proportional to the square of the current (or linear if the pole faces are shaped).
- **F_spring:** Linear restoring force, $F_s = k_s \cdot x$.
- **F_hydrodynamic:** Flow-induced axial force on the spool.
- **F_friction:** Static + Coulomb friction (the chief cause of hysteresis in proportional valves).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A proportional valve is **not** a servo valve with the feedback removed. It is a fundamentally different architecture. The hysteresis in a proportional valve (5–10%) is far worse than a servo valve (<3%) precisely *because* there is no closed-loop spool correction.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas.** They appear in nearly every numerical question on servo/proportional valves.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow equation (spool land) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q = K_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Valve flow coefficient form | m³/s |
| 3 | $F_{hydraulic} = \Delta P \cdot A_{spool}$ | Net force on spool from pilot pressure | N |
| 4 | $F_{spring} = k_s \cdot x_{spool}$ | Feedback spring restoring force | N |
| 5 | $F_{magnetic} = k_i \cdot i$ | Solenoid force (linear region) | N |
| 6 | $F_{magnetic} = k_i \cdot i^2$ | Solenoid force (nonlinear, basic) | N |
| 7 | $T_m = K_t \cdot i$ | Torque motor output torque | N·m |
| 8 | $\theta_{flapper} = \dfrac{T_m}{K_{spring}}$ | Flapper angular deflection | rad |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{pilot} = K_{np} \cdot \theta_{flapper}$ | Nozzle-flapper pressure gain | Pa |
| 10 | $x_{spool} = \dfrac{A_{spool} \cdot \Delta P_{pilot}}{k_s}$ | Spool position (steady state) | m |
| 11 | $\dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{A_{spool,2}}{A_{flapper}}$ | Hydraulic amplification ratio | — |
| 12 | $\tau = \dfrac{m_{spool}}{k_s}$ | Mechanical time constant (approx.) | s |
| 13 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{total}}{m_{spool}}}$ | Natural frequency (2nd-order spool) | rad/s |
| 14 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{k_{total} \cdot m_{spool}}}$ | Damping ratio | — |
| 15 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{control}}{i_{input}}$ | Valve sensitivity / gain | (m³/s)/A |

**Where:**
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.6–0.65 for sharp-edged spool orifices)
- $K_v$ = valve flow coefficient (m³/s per √Pa)
- $K_{np}$ = nozzle-flapper pressure gain (Pa/rad)
- $A_{spool}$ = spool end area
- $k_s$ = spring stiffness
- $k_i$ = solenoid current-force gain
- $K_t$ = torque motor constant
- $m_{spool}$ = spool + feedback mass
- $B$ = viscous damping coefficient
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve land

## 2.5 Why Servo and Proportional Valves Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Utility:** Servo valves dominate in **flight control actuators** (aircraft ailerons, rudders, elevators), **missile fin control**, **injection molding machines** (clamp force & injection velocity), **turbine governor systems**, and **active suspension systems** in luxury cars. Proportional valves dominate in **mobile hydraulics** (JCB, tractor loaders, forklift tilt), **press brakes**, and **harvester combine headers** — applications where cost matters more than 1 ms response.

The engineering trade-off is always **performance vs. cost vs. contamination sensitivity**:

| Parameter | Servo Valve | Proportional Valve |
|---|---|---|
| Cost (USD) | **$2,000 – $15,000** | **$200 – $1,500** |
| Filtration required | **ISO 4406 16/13/11** (3 µm absolute) | **ISO 4406 19/16/13** (10–25 µm) |
| Response (T₉₀) | **5–20 ms** | **50–200 ms** |
| Hysteresis | **< 3%** | **5–10%** |
| Closed-loop? | **Yes (internal)** | **No (external controller must close it)** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nozzle-Flapper Pressure Gain $K_{np}$

The nozzle-flapper is essentially a **variable orifice hydraulic bridge**. Let us derive the relationship between flapper deflection and the differential pressure developed across the spool ends.

### Setup
- Two identical nozzles of fixed area $A_n$ each, fed from a common pilot pressure $P_p$.
- A flapper of negligible thickness, area $A_f$, positioned midway between the two nozzles.
- Initial flapper gap on each side: $x_0$.
- Flapper deflects by a small amount $\delta$ to one side.

### Step-by-step Derivation

Let us denote the gap on the **left** side as $x_L$ and the gap on the **right** side as $x_R$. With the flapper displaced by $\delta$ toward the left:

$$ x_L = x_0 - \delta $$

$$ x_R = x_0 + \delta $$

The flow through each nozzle (treated as an orifice) is governed by the orifice equation:

$$ Q_L = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_p - P_1)}{\rho}} $$

$$ Q_R = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_p - P_2)}{\rho}} $$

Here $P_1$ and $P_2$ are the pressures in the two nozzle chambers. The pilot supply flow $Q_p$ splits between the two nozzles:

$$ Q_p = Q_L + Q_R $$

In the **steady-state small-signal limit**, the change in flow through the nozzles is dominated by the change in *orifice area* (gap change) rather than pressure change. Linearizing about the operating point ($P_1 = P_2 = P_0$, $Q_L = Q_R = Q_0/2$):

$$ \Delta Q_L = -C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_p - P_0)}{\rho}} \cdot \Delta x_L = -K_q \cdot (-\delta) = K_q \cdot \delta $$

$$ \Delta Q_R = +C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_p - P_0)}{\rho}} \cdot \Delta x_R = +K_q \cdot \delta $$

where the **flow gain** $K_q$ is defined as:

$$ K_q = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_p - P_0)}{\rho}} $$

The pilot pressures respond to the imbalance. For the nozzle chambers treated as control volumes with capacitance $C_{n}$ (fluid compressibility in the chamber):

$$ C_n \cdot \dfrac{dP_1}{dt} = -K_q \cdot \delta $$

In **steady state** ($d/dt = 0$), this is trivially zero — meaning the pressures $P_1$ and $P_2$ adjust until the flows balance. Using the **linearized nozzle flow-pressure gain** $K_c$:

$$ \Delta P_1 = P_1 - P_0 = -K_c \cdot \Delta Q_L = -K_c \cdot K_q \cdot \delta $$

$$ \Delta P_2 = P_2 - P_0 = +K_c \cdot \Delta Q_R = +K_c \cdot K_q \cdot \delta $$

The **differential pilot pressure** across the spool ends is therefore:

$$ \Delta P_{pilot} = P_1 - P_2 = -2 K_c \cdot K_q \cdot \delta $$

Defining the **nozzle-flapper pressure gain** $K_{np}$:

$$ K_{np} = 2 K_c \cdot K_q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P_{pilot} = K_{np} \cdot \delta $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** $K_{np}$ is positive, and $\Delta P_{pilot}$ is taken in the direction that *assists* spool motion. Final sign is determined by the geometry of the flapper-to-spool coupling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Spool Position vs. Input Current

### Step 1: Torque Motor Output
The torque motor produces a torque proportional to current (for a permanent-magnet torque motor with armature in the air gap):

$$ T_m = K_t \cdot i $$

### Step 2: Flapper Deflection
The flapper is mounted on a flexure of stiffness $K_\theta$. The torque is balanced by the flexure torque:

$$ T_m = K_\theta \cdot \theta $$

$$ \theta = \dfrac{T_m}{K_\theta} = \dfrac{K_t \cdot i}{K_\theta} $$

### Step 3: Tip Displacement of the Flapper
If the flapper is of length $L$ from the pivot to the nozzle tip, the tip linear displacement is:

$$ \delta = L \cdot \theta = \dfrac{L \cdot K_t \cdot i}{K_\theta} $$

### Step 4: Pilot Pressure Generated
Substituting into the nozzle-flapper result:

$$ \Delta P_{pilot} = K_{np} \cdot \delta = \dfrac{K_{np} \cdot L \cdot K_t}{K_\theta} \cdot i $$

### Step 5: Hydraulic Force on the Spool
The pilot pressure acts on the spool end area $A_s$ (both ends):

$$ F_{hydraulic} = \Delta P_{pilot} \cdot A_s = \dfrac{K_{np} \cdot L \cdot K_t \cdot A_s}{K_\theta} \cdot i $$

### Step 6: Spool Force Balance (Steady State)
The hydraulic force is balanced by the feedback spring force $F_s = k_s \cdot x_{spool}$:

$$ k_s \cdot x_{spool} = \dfrac{K_{np} \cdot L \cdot K_t \cdot A_s}{K_\theta} \cdot i $$

### Step 7: Final Result — Spool Position Proportional to Current

$$ \boxed{ x_{spool} = \underbrace{\dfrac{K_{np} \cdot L \cdot K_t \cdot A_s}{k_s \cdot K_\theta}}_{K_v} \cdot i } $$

This proves the **fundamental linearity of a servo valve**: in steady state, spool position is exactly proportional to input current. The constant $K_v$ is called the **valve gain** or **valve sensitivity** and is typically expressed in m/A or mm/mA.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Flow Through the Main Spool Stage

With the spool displaced by $x_{spool}$, each spool land uncovers a window area proportional to the spool displacement. For a critical-edge spool geometry with width $w$ and spool diameter $D$:

$$ A_{orifice} = w \cdot x_{spool} $$

The flow from supply (P) to actuator port (A), with the other port (B) connected to tank (T), follows the orifice equation:

$$ Q_A = C_d \cdot w \cdot x_{spool} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_S - P_A)}{\rho}} $$

For a **symmetric, matched, and critically-centered spool** with equal-area ports, the pressure at port A settles to half the supply (under symmetric load):

$$ P_A = \dfrac{P_S}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_S - P_A = \dfrac{P_S}{2} $$

Substituting:

$$ Q_A = C_d \cdot w \cdot x_{spool} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_S}{\rho}} $$

Since $x_{spool} = K_v \cdot i$ from the previous derivation:

$$ \boxed{ Q_A = \underbrace{C_d \cdot w \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_S}{\rho}}}_{K_Q} \cdot i } $$

where $K_Q$ is the **valve flow gain** in m³/s per ampere. This is the most important relationship: **output flow is proportional to input current**, the defining behavior of a proportional-flow valve.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Steady-State Load Flow (Matched Land Geometry)

For a more rigorous treatment accounting for pressure drop across both lands and cylinder motion, consider a hydraulic cylinder of area $A_c$ with load pressure $P_L = P_A - P_B$. The supply-to-tank pressure drop across the valve is $P_S - P_T = P_S$ (assuming $P_T = 0$).

By conservation of energy across the valve (with the cylinder at rest or in steady-state flow):

$$ P_S \cdot Q_{in} = P_A \cdot Q_A + P_B \cdot Q_B + \text{Losses} $$

Under matched-spool symmetry, $P_A + P_B = P_S$, and the **load flow** $Q_L$ is:

$$ Q_L = Q_A = Q_B = C_d \cdot w \cdot x_{spool} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_S - P_L}{\rho}} $$

$$ Q_L = C_d \cdot w \cdot K_v \cdot i \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_S - P_L}{\rho}} $$

This is the **classic load-flow equation** for a servo valve driving a hydraulic actuator. It shows that the flow depends on both the input current $i$ and the load pressure $P_L$ — a coupled nonlinear system that requires the **external closed-loop controller** (the KTU-referenced "motion controller") to linearize.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Spool Position Transfer Function (Dynamic)

For dynamic analysis, the spool has mass $m_s$, viscous damping $B_s$, and is acted on by hydraulic force, spring force, and any external disturbance force $F_d$:

$$ m_s \cdot \ddot{x} + B_s \cdot \dot{x} + k_s \cdot x = A_s \cdot \Delta P_{pilot} - F_d $$

The pilot pressure itself has dynamics due to nozzle fluid capacitance and leakage. Including a first-order pilot dynamics with time constant $\tau_p$:

$$ \tau_p \cdot \dot{\Delta P}_{pilot} + \Delta P_{pilot} = K_{np} \cdot \delta $$

Combining these gives a **third-order servo valve model** (typical industrial approximation):

$$ \dfrac{x(s)}{i(s)} = \dfrac{K_v}{(s/\omega_1 + 1)(s^2/\omega_n^2 + 2\zeta s/\omega_n + 1)} $$

where:
- $\omega_1 = 1/\tau_p$ (pilot stage break frequency, ≈ 100–300 Hz)
- $\omega_n$ = spool natural frequency, $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_{total}/m_s}$ (≈ 200–800 Hz)
- $\zeta$ = damping ratio (typically 0.5–0.7 for well-tuned valves)
- $K_v$ = valve gain (steady-state sensitivity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** "Derive the transfer function of a two-stage servo valve" or "Identify the three poles and explain their physical origin." The three poles correspond to: (1) pilot fluid capacitance, (2) spool mass–spring, (3) higher-order structural dynamics.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A servo valve has the following specifications:
- Nozzle pressure gain: $K_{np} = 2.5 \times 10^6$ Pa/m
- Flapper effective length: $L = 0.015$ m
- Torque motor constant: $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A
- Flapper flexure stiffness: $K_\theta = 1.2$ N·m/rad
- Spool end area: $A_s = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- Feedback spring stiffness: $k_s = 1500$ N/m
- Supply pressure: $P_S = 21 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Fluid density: $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- Discharge coefficient: $C_d = 0.62$

**Find:** (a) Valve gain $K_v$ in m/A, (b) Flow gain $K_Q$ in (L/min)/A at null load.

### Solution

**Part (a): Valve Gain**

$$ K_v = \dfrac{K_{np} \cdot L \cdot K_t \cdot A_s}{k_s \cdot K_\theta} $$

Substituting:

$$ K_v = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(0.015)(0.05)(1.2 \times 10^{-4})}{(1500)(1.2)} $$

Numerator: $2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.015 = 37{,}500$
$37{,}500 \times 0.05 = 1875$
$1875 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.225$

Denominator: $1500 \times 1.2 = 1800$

$$ K_v = \dfrac{0.225}{1800} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/A} = 0.125 \text{ mm/mA} $$

**Part (b): Flow Gain at Null Load**

Assume matched spool with land width $w = 0.012$ m:

$$ K_Q = C_d \cdot w \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_S}{\rho}} $$

$$ \sqrt{\dfrac{P_S}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{21 \times 10^6}{870}} = \sqrt{24137.9} = 155.4 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ K_Q = (0.62)(0.012)(1.25 \times 10^{-4})(155.4) $$

$$ K_Q = 0.62 \times 0.012 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 155.4 $$

$$ K_Q = 1.445 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s/A} $$

Converting to L/min:

$$ K_Q = 1.445 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 1000 = 8.67 \text{ L/min per A} $$

$$\boxed{ K_v = 0.125 \text{ mm/mA}, \quad K_Q \approx 8.67 \text{ L/min/A} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every 1 mA of input current, the spool moves 0.125 mm, and at null load the valve delivers 8.67 L/min of flow. With a typical rated current of ±40 mA, the maximum flow is ≈ 347 L/min — a respectable industrial figure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Two-Stage Servo Valve (Closed-Loop)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *signal flow* and *energy flow* through a two-stage servo valve. It is the most KTU-expected way to "draw" a servo valve when physical sketching is infeasible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Current i +/- mA] --> B[Torque Motor Stage 1]
    B --> C[Flapper Deflection delta]
    C --> D[Nozzle Flapper Bridge]
    D --> E[Pilot Pressure Delta P1 minus P2]
    E --> F[Spool End Hydraulic Force]
    F --> G[Spool Displacement x]
    G --> H[Main Control Ports Open]
    H --> I[Hydraulic Flow to Actuator]
    I --> J[Load Motion]
    G --> K[Mechanical Feedback Spring]
    K --> C
    L[Supply Pressure Ps] --> D
    L --> H
    M[Tank Return T] --> H
```

**Read this diagram as:** The input current $i$ commands the torque motor, which moves the flapper. The flapper modulates the nozzle-flapper bridge, generating a pilot pressure difference $\Delta P$. This pressure acts on the spool ends, moving the spool. The spool's movement is fed back via a mechanical spring to the flapper, **closing the loop**. The spool's position simultaneously opens the main control ports, directing high-pressure supply to the actuator.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Proportional Valve (Open-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Current i mA] --> B[Proportional Solenoid]
    B --> C[Magnetic Force Fmag]
    C --> D[Force Balance on Spool]
    E[Spring Force Fs] --> D
    F[Flow Force Fq] --> D
    G[Friction Force Ff] --> D
    D --> H[Spool Displacement x]
    H --> I[Main Control Ports Open]
    I --> J[Hydraulic Flow to Actuator]
    K[Supply Pressure] --> I
    L[Tank Return] --> I
```

**Read this diagram as:** The current generates magnetic force, which competes against the spring, flow, and friction forces. The resulting spool position (no closed-loop correction!) determines the flow. Note the **absence** of any feedback path from spool position back to the solenoid.

## 4.3 Comparison Architecture — Servo vs. Proportional

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ServoValve["SERVO VALVE - CLOSED LOOP"]
        S1[Input i] --> S2[Torque Motor]
        S2 --> S3[Flapper]
        S3 --> S4[Nozzle Flapper Stage 1]
        S4 --> S5[Spool Stage 2]
        S5 --> S6[Flow Q]
        S5 -. Feedback Spring .-> S3
    end
    subgraph PropValve["PROPORTIONAL VALVE - OPEN LOOP"]
        P1[Input i] --> P2[Proportional Solenoid]
        P2 --> P3[Spool Direct]
        P3 --> P4[Flow Q]
    end
```

## 4.4 Spool Movement vs. Spool Position (Hysteresis Visualization)

```mermaid
graph LR
    I1[i=plus40mA] --> X1[x=plus xmax]
    I2[i=plus20mA] --> X2[x=plus xhalf]
    I3[i=0mA] --> X3[x=0]
    I4[i=minus20mA] --> X4[x=minus xhalf]
    I5[i=minus40mA] --> X5[x=minus xmax]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In an ideal valve, this is a straight line (zero hysteresis). In a **proportional valve**, the up-curve and down-curve are visibly separated (5–10% hysteresis band). In a **servo valve**, they nearly overlap (<3% hysteresis). Always draw two distinct curves if asked to illustrate the difference.

## 4.5 System-Level Integration — Valve in a Motion Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Command Position xd] --> CMP[Comparator]
    ACT[Actual Position x] --> CMP
    CMP --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    CTRL --> VLV[Servo or Proportional Valve]
    VLV --> CYL[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL --> LD[Load Motion]
    LD --> POS[Position Sensor LVDT]
    POS --> ACT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Big-Picture:** A proportional valve **must** be inside an external closed-loop position controller (PLC, MCU, or analog PID card). A servo valve already has an *internal* spool-position loop, but still needs an external *system* loop (position, velocity, force) for the actuator. Servo valves therefore have two nested loops; proportional valves have only one.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between a servo valve and a proportional valve in terms of construction, performance, and application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Servo Valve | Proportional Valve |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Two-stage: torque motor + nozzle-flapper (1st), spool (2nd) with mechanical feedback spring | Single-stage or pilot-operated: proportional solenoid directly drives spool |
| **Feedback** | Internal closed-loop spool-position feedback | Open-loop (no internal spool feedback) |
| **Hysteresis** | Less than 3% | 5–10% |
| **Response Time** | 5–20 ms | 50–200 ms |
| **Bandwidth** | 50–500 Hz | 5–30 Hz |
| **Filtration** | 3 µm (ISO 4406 16/13/11) | 10–25 µm |
| **Cost** | Very high (₹1.5L–10L) | Moderate (₹15K–1L) |
| **Application** | Aircraft, missile, precision molding, machine tools | Mobile hydraulics, tractors, forklift tilt |
| **Sensitivity to contamination** | Very high | Moderate |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Explain the function of the feedback spring in a two-stage electrohydraulic servo valve.**

**Model Answer:**

The **feedback spring** (or **flexure tube**) is the mechanical element that links the position of the second-stage spool back to the first-stage flapper. Its functions are:

1. **Closes the internal position loop:** It feeds back the spool's actual position to the flapper, so that any deviation between commanded and actual spool position generates a restoring flapper motion.

2. **Provides a centering force:** When the input current is zero, the spring returns the spool to the null (centered) position, ensuring no net flow to the actuator.

3. **Sets the valve gain:** The spool position for a given current is inversely proportional to the spring stiffness $k_s$. A stiffer spring means lower valve gain (less spool movement per mA) but higher dynamic stiffness.

4. **Converts spool displacement to a flapper force:** The spring force acts on the flapper in opposition to the torque motor, creating a force balance that determines the equilibrium differential pilot pressure.

> Without the feedback spring, the spool would drift uncontrollably with any small input, and the valve would be useless as a precision control element.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat functional diagram, explain the working of a **two-stage electrohydraulic servo valve**. Describe the role of the torque motor, nozzle-flapper, and feedback spring.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A servo valve has a rated flow of 60 L/min at a pressure drop of 70 bar with a spool stroke of ±4 mm. The valve's valve gain is 0.1 mm/mA. If the supply pressure is 210 bar and the load pressure is 50 bar, calculate:
  (i) The valve flow coefficient $K_v$ in m³/s/√Pa.
  (ii) The flow delivered at null load (load pressure = 0).
  (iii) The flow delivered when the input current is 30 mA and the load pressure is 50 bar.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-Stage Servo Valve — Working:**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the functional block diagram.

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Torque Motor (Stage 1, Input):** A small DC current (typically ±10 to ±40 mA) is applied to the coils of a permanent-magnet torque motor. The armature, suspended on a flexure tube, rotates by a fraction of a degree, generating a torque $T_m = K_t \cdot i$.

2. **Flapper:** The armature is rigidly connected to a slender flapper positioned between two fixed nozzles. The flapper rotation produces a tip displacement $\delta = L \cdot \theta$ at the nozzle gaps.

3. **Nozzle-Flapper Bridge (Stage 1, Hydraulic):** The flapper deflection unbalances the two nozzle gaps. The nozzle with the smaller gap builds up higher pressure, the other drops. The pressure differential $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ is the output of the first stage.

4. **Spool (Stage 2, Power):** The differential pressure acts on the spool end areas, producing a net hydraulic force $F_h = \Delta P \cdot A_s$. This moves the spool against the feedback spring.

5. **Main Control Ports:** The spool's lands uncover the supply (P) and tank (T) ports, directing flow to actuator ports (A, B).

6. **Feedback Spring (Closed-Loop Element):** As the spool moves, it pulls the feedback spring, which in turn pulls the flapper back toward its centered position. Equilibrium is reached when the spring force equals the hydraulic force, which corresponds to a unique spool position for the given current.

7. **Steady State:** The spool position is exactly proportional to the input current, and therefore the output flow is also proportional — the valve behaves as a **linear flow amplifier**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Rated flow $Q_r = 60$ L/min = $60 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- Rated $\Delta P_r = 70$ bar = $70 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Spool stroke $x_{max} = \pm 4$ mm = $\pm 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Valve gain $K_v = 0.1$ mm/mA = $0.1 \times 10^{-3}$ m/A
- Supply pressure $P_S = 210$ bar
- Load pressure $P_L = 50$ bar

**(i) Valve Flow Coefficient $K_v$ (flow coefficient):**

Using $Q = K_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ at rated conditions:

$$ K_v = \dfrac{Q_r}{\sqrt{\Delta P_r}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{70 \times 10^5}} $$

$$ \sqrt{70 \times 10^5} = \sqrt{7 \times 10^6} = 2645.75 \text{ (Pa}^{1/2}\text{)} $$

$$ K_v = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2645.75} = 3.78 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}/\sqrt{\text{Pa}} $$

**(ii) Flow at Null Load (P_L = 0):**

The full pressure drop across the valve is the supply pressure. Therefore $\Delta P = P_S = 210$ bar = $210 \times 10^5$ Pa.

$$ Q = K_v \cdot \sqrt{P_S} = 3.78 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \sqrt{210 \times 10^5} $$

$$ \sqrt{210 \times 10^5} = \sqrt{2.1 \times 10^7} = 4582.6 \text{ Pa}^{1/2} $$

$$ Q = 3.78 \times 10^{-7} \times 4582.6 = 1.732 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$

Converting to L/min:

$$ Q = 1.732 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 103.9 \text{ L/min} $$

**(iii) Flow at i = 30 mA, P_L = 50 bar:**

Spool position at $i = 30$ mA:

$$ x_{spool} = K_v^{disp} \cdot i = 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3 \text{ mm} $$

Spool position as fraction of maximum:

$$ \alpha = \dfrac{x_{spool}}{x_{max}} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$

Pressure drop across the valve land (load sense subtracted):

$$ \Delta P_{valve} = P_S - P_L = 210 - 50 = 160 \text{ bar} $$

For a proportional valve with spool position $\alpha$ and valve pressure drop $\Delta P$, the load flow is:

$$ Q_L = Q_{rated} \cdot \alpha \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{rated}}} $$

$$ Q_L = 60 \cdot 0.75 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{160}{70}} $$

$$ \sqrt{\dfrac{160}{70}} = \sqrt{2.2857} = 1.512 $$

$$ Q_L = 60 \times 0.75 \times 1.512 = 68.04 \text{ L/min} $$

$$\boxed{ K_v = 3.78 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}/\sqrt{\text{Pa}}, \quad Q_{null} = 103.9 \text{ L/min}, \quad Q_{loaded} = 68.0 \text{ L/min} } $$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **open-loop nature of a proportional valve** and describe the components of a typical proportional solenoid. Why is hysteresis higher in proportional valves than in servo valves?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A proportional valve is used to control a hydraulic cylinder. The valve has a dead band of 4 mA, a hysteresis of 8%, and a rated flow of 100 L/min at 200 bar with a rated current of 800 mA. Determine:
  (i) The flow delivered at an input current of 500 mA (during up-stroke).
  (ii) The flow delivered at the same current during down-stroke.
  (iii) The percentage error in flow due to hysteresis.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proportional Valve — Open-Loop Nature and Hysteresis:**

A **proportional valve is open-loop at the spool level**. The proportional solenoid generates a magnetic force $F_m = k_i \cdot i$ (or $k_i \cdot i^2$ in non-linear regions) that pushes the spool against a return spring. The spool takes up a position determined by the force balance:

$$ F_{magnetic} = F_{spring} + F_{flow} + F_{friction} $$

**There is no internal mechanism to verify the actual spool position.** Any load disturbance, pressure fluctuation, or oil temperature change directly changes the spool's equilibrium position, and hence the output flow.

**Components of a Proportional Solenoid:**

1. **Coil winding** (with controlled current input, often PWM-driven).
2. **Armature** (movable ferromagnetic plunger).
3. **Pole pieces** (often shaped to linearize force vs. stroke).
4. **Manual override** (push-button for emergency actuation).
5. **Return spring** (provides centering force).
6. **In advanced versions: integrated LVDT** to sense armature position and close an *external* loop.

**Why Hysteresis is Higher:**

- **Static friction** in the spool lands is a major contributor. As current ramps up, friction must be overcome; on the way down, friction aids the spring.
- **Magnetic hysteresis** in the ferromagnetic armature and pole pieces.
- **No closed-loop correction** of spool position — the proportional valve cannot "see" and correct for these errors.
- Servo valves, in contrast, have a feedback spring that *forces* the spool to the correct position, overcoming friction and disturbances in real time.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Dead band: 4 mA (no flow below this current)
- Hysteresis: 8%
- Rated flow: 100 L/min at 200 bar
- Rated current: 800 mA

**(i) Flow at i = 500 mA (Up-Stroke):**

Effective current (above dead band):

$$ i_{eff} = 500 - 4 = 496 \text{ mA} $$

Fraction of rated current:

$$ \alpha = \dfrac{496}{800} = 0.62 $$

Up-stroke flow (no hysteresis penalty):

$$ Q_{up} = 100 \times 0.62 = 62.0 \text{ L/min} $$

**(ii) Flow at i = 500 mA (Down-Stroke):**

For down-stroke, hysteresis means the flow is reduced (the spool "lags" the decreasing signal). An 8% hysteresis at the same current corresponds to a 8% reduction in flow (depending on the convention used; assume the worst case — full ±8% from rated):

The effective current for the same flow during down-stroke requires **8% more current** to produce the same flow as up-stroke. Equivalently, at the same current, the down-stroke flow is:

$$ Q_{down} = Q_{up} \times (1 - 0.08) = 62.0 \times 0.92 = 57.04 \text{ L/min} $$

**(iii) Percentage Error:**

$$ \%\text{Error} = \dfrac{Q_{up} - Q_{down}}{Q_{up}} \times 100 = \dfrac{62.0 - 57.04}{62.0} \times 100 $$

$$ \%\text{Error} = \dfrac{4.96}{62.0} \times 100 = 8.0\% $$

$$\boxed{ Q_{up} = 62.0 \text{ L/min}, \quad Q_{down} = 57.04 \text{ L/min}, \quad \text{Error} = 8.0\% } $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do not** say "proportional valves have feedback" without qualifying that it is *external* (via the system controller), not internal.
> 2. **Do not** confuse "servo valve" with "solenoid valve" — they are completely different devices. A solenoid valve is just an on/off valve.
> 3. **Do not** forget to convert units. Bar to Pa, mA to A, L/min to m³/s — the most common source of calculation errors.
> 4. **Do not** state that a proportional valve's hysteresis is due only to friction. Magnetic hysteresis in the solenoid also contributes.
> 5. **Always** show the force balance equation for the spool — KTU examiners award marks for this foundation step.
> 6. **Always** mention the *two* stages of a servo valve explicitly. Saying "torque motor and spool" is incomplete — mention the **nozzle-flapper as Stage 1** and the **spool as Stage 2**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Servo & Proportional Control Valves**

- **Servo Valve** = Two-stage + closed-loop spool feedback. **Proportional Valve** = Single-stage or pilot-operated + open-loop.
- **Stage 1 of a Servo Valve** = **Torque Motor + Nozzle-Flapper Bridge** (precision, low power).
- **Stage 2 of a Servo Valve** = **Spool** (power amplification, high flow).
- **Feedback Spring / Flexure Tube** is the *defining* element that makes a servo valve a closed-loop device.
- **Proportional Solenoid** is a current-controlled force source that directly (or via pilot) positions the spool.
- **Servo valves:** Bandwidth 50–500 Hz, Hysteresis <3%, Response 5–20 ms, Cost high, Filtration 3 µm.
- **Proportional valves:** Bandwidth 5–30 Hz, Hysteresis 5–10%, Response 50–200 ms, Cost moderate, Filtration 10–25 µm.
- **Spool force balance:** $F_{hydraulic} = F_{spring} + F_{flow} + F_{friction}$.
- **Steady-state spool position:** $x_{spool} = \dfrac{K_{np} \cdot L \cdot K_t \cdot A_s}{k_s \cdot K_\theta} \cdot i$ (linear in current).
- **Valve flow gain:** $K_Q = C_d \cdot w \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{P_S / \rho}$.
- **Load flow equation:** $Q_L = C_d \cdot w \cdot x_{spool} \cdot \sqrt{(P_S - P_L)/\rho}$.
- **Spool dynamics:** Third-order transfer function (pilot pole + spool natural frequency + structural pole).
- **Dead band** of a proportional valve = minimum current before any flow occurs.
- **Hysteresis** in proportional valves arises from spool friction + magnetic hysteresis.
- **Application Memory Aid:** **S**ervo = **S**teel mill rolling mills, **A**ircraft, **M**achine tools. **P**roportional = **M**obile hydraulics, **P**ress brakes, **L**oaders.
- **Two nested loops in a servo-valve system:** (1) Internal spool position loop, (2) External position/velocity/force loop.
- **One loop in a proportional-valve system:** External position/velocity/force loop only.
- **Standard pressure drops** to remember: $P_{rated}$ at $\Delta P = 70$ bar per land.
- **Filter cleanliness** for servo valves: **ISO 4406 code 16/13/11 or better**.
- **KTU-Favorite Words** to use in answers: "torque motor," "nozzle-flapper," "mechanical feedback spring," "force balance," "hydraulic amplification," "transfer function," "null position," "matched and symmetric spool."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
