<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Process Control Valve (PCV)** is the **final control element** in a closed-loop industrial process control system. It is a power-operated device used to modulate (vary) the rate of flow of a process fluid — liquid, gas, steam, slurry, or two-phase mixture — in response to a control signal received from an electronic/pneumatic controller. The control signal typically arrives as a standardized **pneumatic pressure signal (3–15 psig or 0.2–1.0 bar)**, an **analog electrical current (4–20 mA)**, or a **digital fieldbus command (HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus PA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Wording):** "A Process Control Valve is a continuously variable throttling device that converts a controller output signal into a corresponding change in the volumetric or mass flow rate of a process medium, thereby enabling automatic regulation of critical process variables such as pressure, temperature, level, and flow."

In the language of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, the PCV is the **physical actuation node** that bridges the digital control domain (controller, SCADA, DCS) with the physical process domain (pipe, fluid, plant).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Faucet"

Imagine a kitchen faucet connected to a smart thermostat. The faucet (valve) can be twisted anywhere from fully closed to fully open. Now suppose an electronic brain (controller) decides, 50 times per second, exactly how much the faucet should be open to maintain the perfect water temperature. The Process Control Valve is exactly that — a **continuously variable, remotely commanded, precisely metered "faucet"** for industrial chemicals, oil, steam, or water, operating with **hundreds of times more precision and under far more hostile conditions** than any household tap.

The **actuator** is the muscle that physically turns/raises/lowers the valve's internal plug. The **valve body** is the plumbing. The **positioner** is the local "mini-controller" that ensures the muscle is in the exact commanded position, correcting for friction and fluid forces.

## 1.3 Role in a Typical Control Loop

A process control valve is the **last stage** of a cascade:

$$ \text{Setpoint} \rightarrow \text{Controller (PID)} \rightarrow \text{I/P Converter} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Valve Trim} \rightarrow \text{Process Flow} $$

It accepts a low-energy command and delivers a high-energy physical change in fluid flow — making it the **"hand" of the control system**.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard ISA Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical ISA-75 / IEC 60534 Performance Parameters:**
> - **Cv (Valve Flow Coefficient):** Gallons per minute of water at 60 °F that flows through a fully open valve with a 1 psi pressure drop. **Unit: US GPM/√psi** (imperial) or **Kv** in metric (m³/hr with 1 bar drop).
> - **Rangeability (R):** Ratio of maximum controllable flow to minimum controllable flow, typically **30:1 to 50:1** for globe valves.
> - **Turndown:** Same as rangeability in industry parlance.
> - **Travel:** Linear or rotary displacement of the plug; usually **½" to 6"** linear.
> - **Characteristic Curve:** Plot of flow vs. valve stem position (linear, equal percentage, quick opening).
> - **Hysteresis:** Maximum difference in flow at the same stem position when approached from opposite directions. Industry standard: **< 3–5%** of full travel.
> - **Deadband:** Smallest change in input signal that produces a measurable change in output. Typically **< 0.5%** of full span.
> - **Stroke Speed / Time Constant:** Time for valve to move 0–100% — typically **5 to 60 seconds** for pneumatic, **2–10 seconds** for electric.

These parameters are the **"spec sheet" of a control valve** and are directly tested in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Process Control Valve

A complete PCV consists of **four functional sub-assemblies**:

### (a) Actuator — The "Muscle"
Provides the force/torque to move the stem. Three principal families:

| Actuator Type | Energy Source | Typical Output | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pneumatic (Diaphragm)** | Compressed air 3–15 psi | 0.2–1.0 bar to stem thrust | Simple, safe in hazardous areas, cheap, fast | Limited thrust, needs air supply |
| **Pneumatic (Piston)** | Compressed air 20–100 psi | High thrust, long stroke | Powerful, longer stroke | Needs higher air pressure, positioner mandatory |
| **Hydraulic** | Hydraulic oil 100–3000 psi | Very high thrust | Highest thrust density, precise | Pump, reservoir, leak risk |
| **Electric (Motorized)** | 24 V DC / 110/230 V AC | Rotary or linear motion via gear/screw | No air/oil needed, precise position hold | Slower, needs gearbox, fail-position tricky |
| **Electrohydraulic (EHA)** | Electric pump + hydraulic cylinder | High thrust, all-electric bus | Ideal for aerospace | Expensive |

### (b) Valve Body — The "Plumbing"
Houses the trim and connects to the pipeline. Major body types:

- **Globe Valve** — Most common in throttling. Plug moves perpendicular to seat. Excellent control, high pressure drop capability.
- **Ball Valve** — Rotary ball with a bored hole. Best for on/off or coarse throttling; modified V-port balls for finer control.
- **Butterfly Valve** — Disc rotates 90°. Cheap, light, large diameters (up to 10 m), poor low-flow resolution.
- **Gate Valve** — Linear wedge; for isolation only, not for modulation.
- **Diaphragm Valve** — Flexible elastomer weir; ideal for corrosive, sanitary, slurry service.
- **Plug Valve** — Tapered cylindrical plug with rectangular port.

### (c) Trim — The "Throat"
Internals: plug, seat, stem, cage, retainer. Trim is the **flow-shaping element** that imparts the characteristic curve.

### (d) Positioner — The "Local Brain"
A closed-loop feedback device that reads stem position (via a cam/lever) and modulates the actuator pressure to make stem position exactly match controller command. **Reduces hysteresis from 5–10% to < 0.5%.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** A positioner is **mandatory** for piston actuators, and is **strongly recommended** for diaphragm actuators on high-pressure-drop services. Without a positioner, the inherent hysteresis of packing friction makes accurate throttling impossible.

## 2.2 The Three Inherent Flow Characteristics

This is the **most heavily tested sub-topic** in KTU Module 4.

### (A) Linear Characteristic
Flow is **directly proportional** to stem travel. Equal increments of travel produce equal increments of flow.

$$ \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{L}{L_{\max}} $$

where $L$ is stem travel, $Q$ is flow, and subscripts denote maximum values.

- **Use case:** When the process gain $\left(\dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial L}\right)$ is roughly constant — i.e., system pressure drop across the valve is much larger than drop in the rest of the system. Best for **level control** and **flow control loops** (because flow vs. valve position in a turbulent pipe is roughly linear).

### (B) Equal Percentage (Logarithmic) Characteristic
Equal increments of travel produce equal **percentage** changes in flow. The slope grows exponentially with travel.

$$ \frac{d(Q/Q_{\max})}{d(L/L_{\max})} = n \cdot \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} $$

Integrating:

$$ \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{\left(\frac{L}{L_{\max}} - 1\right)} $$

where $R$ is the **rangeability** (often 30 or 50) and $n = \ln R$.

- **Use case:** When the process gain varies widely with load — typical of **pressure control** and **temperature control**, where small flows at low loads must be regulated precisely.

### (C) Quick-Opening Characteristic
Most of the flow change occurs in the first 20–30% of travel. The flow rises sharply with small stem lift.

$$ \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{L}{L_{\max}}\right)^m $$

where $m$ is a constant (typically 1.5 to 3).

- **Use case:** On/off or two-position service, **safety relief**, or applications needing snap action. **Not** used for fine modulation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Numerical Problems in KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N \sqrt{\Delta P / \text{SG}}}$ | Liquid sizing (SI: $C_v$ in GPM units, $Q$ in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi) | dimensionless × GPM/√psi |
| 2 | $C_v = \dfrac{W}{N \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot \gamma_1}}$ | Sizing for compressible fluids (mass basis) | GPM units |
| 3 | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \dfrac{L}{L_{\max}}$ | Linear characteristic | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{(L/L_{\max} - 1)}$ | Equal percentage characteristic | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = 1 - (1 - L/L_{\max})^m$ | Quick-opening characteristic | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{Q_{\max,\text{controllable}}}{Q_{\min,\text{controllable}}}$ | Rangeability / turndown ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_R = 1 + \dfrac{0.5\,\nu}{\sqrt{C_v}}$ | Viscosity correction (Reynolds correction factor) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Re}_v = \dfrac{12{,}700 \cdot Q}{\nu \sqrt{C_v}}$ | Valve Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta P_v = \Delta P_{\text{system}} \cdot f(x^2)$ | Installed characteristic, $x = \Delta P_v/\Delta P_{\text{system}}$ | psi or bar |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{\text{Stem Thrust}}{\text{Actuator Thrust Capacity}}$ | Sizing check (should be $\leq 0.7$ for safety) | dimensionless |

> **Legend:** $Q$ = flow rate; $\Delta P$ = pressure drop; SG = specific gravity; $W$ = mass flow; $\gamma_1$ = specific weight at inlet; $L$ = lift/travel; $R$ = rangeability; $m, n$ = curve-shape constants; $F_R$ = Reynolds correction factor; $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity in cSt.

## 2.4 Installed vs. Inherent Characteristic

A valve's *inherent* characteristic is measured in a test bench with **constant differential pressure**. In a real plant, the differential pressure across the valve **decreases as the valve opens** (because more flow means more pressure drop in the pipe). This distortion converts:
- Linear → becomes **convex** (quick-opening in the field).
- Equal percentage → becomes more **linear** in the field (which is why it is preferred for variable-load systems).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Students often plot and quote the *inherent* characteristic. Always clarify in the answer whether the question asks for **inherent** (test-bench) or **installed** (field) behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Liquid Valve Sizing Equation (Cv)

The starting point is the **orifice equation** for an incompressible fluid in steady, turbulent, fully-choked-free flow through a restriction:

$$ Q = C_d \cdot A_e \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho}} $$

- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient of the valve trim (typically 0.6–0.98)
- $A_e$ = effective flow area at travel position $L$ (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve (Pa)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)

By definition, the **valve flow coefficient $C_v$** is the flow rate of water (SG = 1) in **US GPM** that passes through a fully open valve with a **1 psi** pressure drop at 60 °F. We therefore normalize:

Step 1: Substitute the numerical values of water at standard conditions into the orifice equation.
- $\rho_{\text{water}} = 999.4 \text{ kg/m}^3$ ≈ 62.4 lbm/ft³
- $A_e$ replaced by an equivalent orifice area $A_o$ such that at $L = L_{\max}$, $C_v$ has its rated value.
- Apply the unit conversion $1 \text{ psi} = 6894.76 \text{ Pa}$, $1 \text{ GPM} = 6.309 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

Step 2: Combining constants into a single empirical constant. After substituting and reducing, the engineering industry-standard empirical formula is:

$$ C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N_1 \sqrt{\Delta P \, / \, \text{SG}}} $$

where the constant $N_1$ captures units (a different $N_i$ exists for each fluid family, e.g., $N_4$ for saturated steam, $N_5$ for gases, $N_6$ for viscous liquids).

Step 3: For the **viscous correction case**, the Reynolds number inside the valve is:

$$ \text{Re}_v = \dfrac{12{,}700 \cdot Q}{\nu \sqrt{C_v}} $$

If $\text{Re}_v < 10^4$, the flow is in the laminar/transitional regime and the discharge coefficient drops. The corrected $C_v$ is:

$$ C_{v,\text{corrected}} = F_R \cdot C_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{0.5\,\nu}{\sqrt{C_v}}\right) \cdot C_v $$

where $\nu$ is in **centistokes (cSt)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
> A 2-inch globe valve must pass 80 GPM of water (SG = 1) with a 9 psi pressure drop. Calculate the required $C_v$.
>
> $$ C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N_1}\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SG}}{\Delta P}} = \dfrac{80}{1}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{9}} = 80 \times \dfrac{1}{3} = 26.67 $$
>
> Therefore, a valve rated $C_v = 30$ (next standard size) is selected.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Equal-Percentage Curve from the Slope Definition

Starting from the defining differential equation of an equal-percentage trim:

$$ \dfrac{d(Q/Q_{\max})}{d(L/L_{\max})} = n \cdot \dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} $$

where $n = \ln R$ is the natural logarithm of the rangeability.

Step 1: Separate variables. Let $u = Q/Q_{\max}$ and $v = L/L_{\max}$:

$$ \dfrac{du}{u} = n \, dv $$

Step 2: Integrate both sides from the closed-valve reference (where $v = 0$, $u = 1/R$) to the open-valve reference (where $v = 1$, $u = 1$):

$$ \int_{1/R}^{u} \dfrac{du'}{u'} = n \int_{0}^{v} dv' $$

Step 3: Evaluate the integrals:

$$ \ln u - \ln(1/R) = n \cdot v $$

$$ \ln(uR) = n \cdot v $$

Step 4: Exponentiate:

$$ uR = e^{nv} \quad\Rightarrow\quad u = \dfrac{e^{nv}}{R} $$

Step 5: Apply the boundary condition $u = 1$ at $v = 1$, giving $n = \ln R$:

$$ \boxed{\dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{\,(L/L_{\max} - 1)} } $$

This is the canonical equal-percentage equation used in every control-valve textbook.

## 3.3 Installed-Characteristic Approximation

When the valve is installed in a piping system with finite pressure drop, only a fraction $x$ of the total system drop $\Delta P_s$ appears across the valve at full open:

$$ x = \dfrac{\Delta P_{v,\text{full open}}}{\Delta P_s} \quad (0 < x \leq 1) $$

For any intermediate flow, the installed flow is:

$$ Q_{\text{installed}} = Q_{\text{inherent}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1 - x}{x^2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_{\text{inherent}}}{Q_{\max}}\right)^2}} $$

> This is the famous **"valve authority"** equation. A rule of thumb in KTU board exams: choose $x \geq 0.5$ for good control; below $x = 0.3$ the valve authority is too weak to control.

## 3.4 Pneumatic Actuator Thrust Derivation

For a **diaphragm actuator**, the thrust $T$ on the stem is:

$$ T = A_e \cdot p - A_s \cdot p_0 - F_s $$

- $A_e = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ = effective diaphragm area
- $p$ = signal air pressure (typically 3–15 psig)
- $A_s$ = stem cross-section
- $p_0$ = atmospheric pressure (back side)
- $F_s$ = stem packing friction + spring preload

The **stem position** vs. pressure relationship is:

$$ L = L_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{p - 3}{15 - 3} = L_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{p - 3}{12} $$

because a 3–15 psig signal (12 psi span) moves the stem 0–100%. This linearity is why pneumatic signals are still the industrial standard.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Process Control Valve in a CPS Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL[Controller PID in DCS] -- 4-20 mA or HART --> IPCONV[I/P Converter]
    IPCONV -- 3-15 psig air --> POS[Valve Positioner]
    ACT[Actuator Diaphragm Piston Hydraulic Electric]
    POS -- Feedback Loop --> ACT
    ACT -- Linear or Rotary Motion --> TRIM[Valve Trim Plug and Seat]
    TRIM -- Modulates --> PROC[Process Pipeline Flow]
    PROC -- Disturbance and Dynamics --> SENSE[Sensor PT TT LT FT]
    SENSE -- 4-20 mA --> CTRL
    PROC -. Process Variable .-> SENSE
    classDef ctrl fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef field fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class CTRL,IPCONV ctrl
    class POS,ACT,TRIM,PROC,SENSE field
```

**Reading the diagram:** Blue nodes = digital/control domain (the "cyber" side). Orange nodes = physical/field domain (the "physical" side). The valve positioner forms a **local sub-loop** that mimics the master control loop — a beautiful example of hierarchical control in CPS.

## 4.2 Internal Block Architecture of a Pneumatic Globe Valve Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AIR[Compressed Air Supply 20 psig] --> REL[Air Filter Regulator Lubricator FRL]
    REL --> POS[Positioner Smart or Pneumatic]
    SIG[Controller Signal 4-20 mA or 3-15 psig] --> POS
    POS --> DIAP[Diaphragm Chamber]
    DIAP --> SPRG[Range Spring]
    DIAP --> STEM[Valve Stem Vertical Motion]
    STEM --> PLUG[Valve Plug]
    PLUG <--> SEAT[Valve Seat]
    SEAT --> BODY[Globe Valve Body]
    BODY --> PIPE[Process Pipe Flanged]
    STEM -. Feedback via Cam Lever .-> POS
    classDef sig fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mech fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#CC4125,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class AIR,REL,POS,SIG sig
    class DIAP,SPRG,STEM,PLUG,SEAT,BODY,PIPE mech
```

**Reading the diagram:** Green = signal/command path. Orange = mechanical/power path. The dashed feedback line from the stem to the positioner is the **closed-loop correction path** that makes pneumatic valves competitive with electric actuators in precision.

## 4.3 Valve Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Need a Control Valve] --> B{Service Type}
    B -- Throttling and Modulating --> C{Pressure Drop}
    B -- On Off Isolation --> D[Gate Valve or Ball Valve]
    C -- High Delta P above 50 psi --> E[Globe Valve with Multi Stage Trim]
    C -- Low Delta P below 10 psi --> F{Pipe Diameter}
    F -- Below 6 inches --> G[Globe Valve or Segmented Ball]
    F -- Above 6 inches --> H[Butterfly Valve High Performance]
    B -- Slurry or Corrosive --> I[Diaphragm Valve or Lined Globe]
    B -- Cryogenic or Cleanroom --> J[SANITARY DIAPHRAGM]
    E --> K{Actuator Energy Available}
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    K -- Plant Air 3-15 psig --> L[Pneumatic Diaphragm with Positioner]
    K -- Hydraulic Power Pack --> M[Hydraulic Piston Actuator]
    K -- No Compressed Air --> N[Electric Motorized Actuator]
    K -- Hazardous Area Zone 1 --> O[Intrinsically Safe Electric or Pneumatic]
    classDef step fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O step
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **canonical ISA-75 selection tree**. In KTU viva, walking through this decision tree with a sample service description (e.g., "throttling 4-inch line, 25 psi drop, plant air available") and ending at "Globe valve, pneumatic diaphragm with positioner" earns full marks.

## 4.4 Inherent Flow Characteristic Curves — Visual Comparison

The following block diagram conceptually represents the three curves over a 0–100% stem travel X-axis and 0–100% flow Y-axis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Linear Curve Straight Diagonal from 0,0 to 100,100]
    L2[Equal Percentage Curve Exponential Concave Upward Starts Flat then Rises Sharply]
    L3[Quick Opening Curve Concave Downward Rises Sharply then Flattens]
    Q[Stem Travel percent 0 to 100] --- L1

    Q --- L3
```

| Stem Travel % | Linear Q % | Equal % Q (R=50) % | Quick-Open Q (m=2) % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 2.0 | 0 |
| 10 | 10 | 2.45 | 19 |
| 25 | 25 | 3.76 | 44 |
| 50 | 50 | 7.07 | 75 |
| 75 | 75 | 13.30 | 94 |
| 100 | 100 | 100 | 100 |

> [!NOTE]
> The equal-percentage curve gives **2% flow at 0% travel** when $R = 50$, and rises to 100% at full travel — exactly the behaviour needed for precise low-flow modulation. This is why equal-percentage trim is the **default choice** for modulating process control valves.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **valve flow coefficient (Cv)**. State its units and explain its physical significance in process valve sizing.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The valve flow coefficient $C_v$ is defined as the number of US gallons per minute of water (at 60 °F) that will flow through a fully open valve when the pressure drop across it is **1 psi**. Its unit is **GPM/√psi** (US customary) or **Kv** in m³/hr per 1 bar in metric. Physical significance: $C_v$ is a direct measure of the valve's **flow capacity** — a higher $C_v$ means the valve can pass a larger flow at the same pressure drop. It encapsulates the effective area, discharge coefficient, and trim geometry in a single number used for valve sizing. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **inherent** and **installed** flow characteristics of a control valve. Why does this distinction matter in practice?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **inherent characteristic** is the relationship between flow and stem travel measured on a test bench with **constant differential pressure** across the valve — a pure property of the trim. The **installed characteristic** is the actual flow-vs-travel relationship observed in the field, where the differential pressure across the valve **decreases as it opens** (because more flow causes more pressure drop in the piping). The distinction matters because: (i) a linear inherent characteristic appears **quick-opening** in the field at low valve authority; (ii) an equal-percentage inherent characteristic appears **more linear** in the field — which is precisely why it is preferred for pressure and temperature loops where the system pressure drop is shared between valve and pipe. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note on KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. A genuine module-internal choice is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **pneumatic diaphragm-actuated globe valve** used as a process control valve. Discuss the role of the **valve positioner** in such an assembly. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **equal-percentage inherent flow characteristic** from its defining differential equation and show that $Q/Q_{\max} = R^{(L/L_{\max} - 1)}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A pneumatic diaphragm-actuated globe valve consists of:

1. **Actuator housing** (upper part) — contains a flexible elastomer/fabric diaphragm clamped between two halves. The lower chamber is open to atmosphere; the upper chamber receives the **3–15 psig** control signal. A **range spring** (helical compression spring) provides the opposing force.
2. **Stem** — A polished metal rod screwed to the diaphragm, transmitting vertical motion downward through a packing gland into the valve body.
3. **Valve body** — A cast/ forged globe-shaped body with a horizontal seat ring and vertical plug guide. Fluid enters at one port, is forced to make a tortuous S-shaped path through the seat-plug annulus, and exits at the other port.
4. **Trim** — The plug (could be contoured parabolic, V-port, or characterized cage) and seat ring form the flow restriction.
5. **Positioner** — Mounted on the side of the actuator yoke. A **lever/cam** mechanically linked to the stem feeds back actual stem position to a pneumatic relay inside the positioner. The relay compares this to the input signal and vents/supplies air to the diaphragm until the position matches the command.

**Working:** When controller output rises from 3 to 15 psig, air pressure on the diaphragm pushes the stem down against the spring. The plug descends into the seat, **reducing** the flow area (air-to-close action) — or the opposite for air-to-open construction. **With a positioner**, any deviation between the commanded position and actual stem position (caused by packing friction, process fluid forces, or spring nonlinearity) is automatically corrected by the positioner's internal pneumatic amplifier. This reduces **hysteresis from 5–10% down to < 0.5%** and dramatically improves dynamic response. (7 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.2** above. The defining differential equation of the equal-percentage curve is:

$$ \dfrac{d(Q/Q_{\max})}{d(L/L_{\max})} = n \cdot \dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} $$

Separating variables: $\dfrac{du}{u} = n\,dv$ where $u = Q/Q_{\max}$ and $v = L/L_{\max}$.

Integrating from $(v=0, u=1/R)$ to general $(v, u)$:

$$ \int_{1/R}^{u} \dfrac{du'}{u'} = n \int_{0}^{v} dv' $$

$$ \ln u + \ln R = nv \quad\Rightarrow\quad uR = e^{nv} $$

$$ u = \dfrac{e^{nv}}{R} = e^{n(v-1)} = R^{(v - 1)} $$

Substituting back:

$$ \boxed{\dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{\,(L/L_{\max} - 1)} } $$

With $R = 50$ (standard): $Q/Q_{\max} = 50^{(L/L_{\max} - 1)}$, which gives 2% at 0% travel and 100% at 100% travel, with smooth exponential resolution throughout. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **pneumatic, hydraulic, and electric actuators** for process control valves. State at least two application-specific advantages and disadvantages of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A control valve is required to pass 200 GPM of water (specific gravity = 1) at a pressure drop of 16 psi. Calculate the required valve flow coefficient $C_v$. If the same valve is to pass oil of specific gravity 0.85 and viscosity 25 cSt, determine the corrected $C_v$ using the Reynolds correction factor. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Criterion | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** | Compressed air 3–15 psi or 20–100 psi | Hydraulic oil 100–3000 psi | 24 V DC / 110–230 V AC |
| **Thrust/force** | Low to medium | **Highest** (10× pneumatic) | Medium |
| **Speed of response** | Fast (sub-second to 5 s) | Fast (0.5–3 s) | Slow (5–30 s with gear) |
| **Position holding** | Needs positioner or air-hold | Excellent (incompressible fluid) | Excellent (self-locking gear) |
| **Hazardous area** | **Inherently safe** (no sparks) | Safe (no sparks) | Needs explosion-proof enclosure |
| **Maintenance** | Low (air is clean) | High (oil leaks, filters) | Medium (motor brushes) |
| **Cost** | Low | High (pump, reservoir) | Medium |
| **Best application** | General process industries, oil & gas, chemical | High-pressure, large valves, steel mills | Remote sites, no air supply, HVAC |

**Specific advantages/disadvantages:**

- *Pneumatic:* **Pro** — intrinsically safe for Zone 1 hazardous areas; **Con** — needs reliable instrument air supply.
- *Hydraulic:* **Pro** — highest thrust density, can move large valves against high pressure drops; **Con** — leakage risk, fire hazard with petroleum oils.
- *Electric:* **Pro** — no air/oil utility required, holds position on power loss; **Con** — slower dynamic response, complex fail-safe logic. (7 marks)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Water (incompressible) sizing:**

$$ C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N_1}\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{SG}}{\Delta P}} = \dfrac{200}{1}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{16}} = \dfrac{200}{4} = 50 $$

So $C_v$ (water) = 50. Select a valve with rated $C_v$ = 50 or 60.

**Step 2 — Oil (viscous fluid) correction:**

Given: $Q = 200$ GPM, $\Delta P = 16$ psi, SG = 0.85, $\nu = 25$ cSt.

$$ C_{v,\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{200}{1}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.85}{16}} = 200 \times \dfrac{0.922}{4} = 46.10 $$

**Step 3 — Compute the valve Reynolds number:**

$$ \text{Re}_v = \dfrac{12{,}700 \cdot Q}{\nu \sqrt{C_{v,\text{ideal}}}} = \dfrac{12{,}700 \times 200}{25 \times \sqrt{46.10}} = \dfrac{2{,}540{,}000}{25 \times 6.79} = \dfrac{2{,}540{,}000}{169.75} \approx 14{,}960 $$

Since $\text{Re}_v > 10^4$, the flow is still turbulent. However, to be conservative we apply the viscosity correction factor:

$$ F_R = 1 + \dfrac{0.5 \times 25}{\sqrt{46.10}} = 1 + \dfrac{12.5}{6.79} = 1 + 1.841 = 2.841 $$

$$ C_{v,\text{corrected}} = F_R \cdot C_{v,\text{ideal}} = 2.841 \times 46.10 \approx 131.0 $$

**Conclusion:** Although the basic $C_v$ for oil is **46.1**, the viscosity effect raises the corrected $C_v$ to **131**, meaning a much larger valve (or a different trim design) is needed. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing $C_v$ (US) with $K_v$ (metric):** $K_v = 0.865 \cdot C_v$. Writing the wrong constant in the formula leads to full-marks loss.
> 2. **Forgetting the "next standard size up":** If the calculation gives $C_v = 26.7$, the *correct* answer is to select the next available size (e.g., $C_v = 30$), not to invent a non-standard size.
> 3. **Mixing inherent and installed characteristics:** A linear-inherent becomes quick-opening in the field. Always declare which you are plotting.
> 4. **Skipping the Reynolds correction step:** Many KTU numericals (especially with oil or glycerol) test this. Forgetting $F_R$ costs 3 of 7 marks in part (b).
> 5. **Drawing the positioner as a passive block:** A positioner is an **active closed-loop feedback device**, not a passive indicator. Markers look for the "feedback arrow" from the stem back to the positioner.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Process Control Valves (Module 4)**

- ☐ A Process Control Valve is the **final control element** in a closed-loop process; it modulates flow in response to a 3–15 psig, 4–20 mA, or fieldbus command.
- ☐ The four functional blocks are: **Actuator + Body + Trim + Positioner**.
- ☐ **Cv** is the GPM of water through a fully open valve at 1 psi drop; the canonical sizing equation is $C_v = Q / \sqrt{\Delta P / \text{SG}}$.
- ☐ Three **inherent flow characteristics**: Linear, Equal Percentage, Quick-Opening.
- ☐ **Linear** is used for flow and level control; **Equal Percentage** for pressure and temperature control (variable process gain); **Quick-Opening** for on/off or two-position service.
- ☐ Equal-percentage equation: $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{(L/L_{\max} - 1)}$ — derived from $d(Q/Q_{\max})/d(L/L_{\max}) = n \cdot Q/Q_{\max}$.
- ☐ **Rangeability** $R$ = max controllable flow ÷ min controllable flow; typically 30–50 for globe valves.
- ☐ **Installed characteristic** differs from inherent because pressure drop across the valve drops as it opens; the **valve authority** $x = \Delta P_v / \Delta P_s$ should be ≥ 0.5.
- ☐ **Pneumatic diaphragm actuators** use 3–15 psig air and a range spring; thrust equation: $T = A_e \cdot p - A_s \cdot p_0 - F_s$.
- ☐ A **valve positioner** is a local PID-like device that drives stem position to match the input signal; reduces hysteresis from 5–10% to < 0.5%.
- ☐ **Pneumatic** = safe in hazardous areas, needs instrument air. **Hydraulic** = highest thrust, for large valves. **Electric** = no air/oil needed, slower response.
- ☐ **Globe valves** are the workhorse of throttling; **ball/butterfly** for large diameters or isolation; **diaphragm** for corrosive/sanitary/slurry.
- ☐ Viscosity correction $F_R = 1 + 0.5\nu/\sqrt{C_v}$ must be applied when $\nu > 1$ cSt; valve Reynolds number $\text{Re}_v = 12{,}700 Q / (\nu \sqrt{C_v})$.
- ☐ In a CPS architecture, the PCV is the **physical actuation node** that bridges the digital controller (DCS/PLC) with the physical process; the positioner forms a **hierarchical sub-control loop** mirroring the master PID loop.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS (CYBER PHYSICAL)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotary Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotary Actuator:** A rotary actuator is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a source of energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal) into **angular displacement (rotation)** about a fixed axis, producing a controlled **rotary motion** characterized by torque, angular velocity, and angular position. Unlike linear actuators that produce straight-line motion, rotary actuators deliver continuous or stepped rotation through an output shaft.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, rotary actuators serve as the final control element that physically actuates the digital command received from the controller, producing real-world rotational movement in robotic joints, conveyor belts, CNC spindles, drones, and automotive throttle systems.

### 1.2 Key Performance Parameters

The following standard metrics define the operational envelope of any rotary actuator:

- **Torque (T):** The rotational force produced, measured in **N·m**
- **Angular Speed (ω):** The rotational velocity, measured in **rad/s** or **RPM**
- **Angular Position (θ):** The absolute or relative shaft angle, measured in **radians** or **degrees**
- **Power (P):** The rate of doing work, **P = T·ω**, measured in **Watts (W)**
- **Efficiency (η):** Ratio of output mechanical power to input electrical/fluid power
- **Holding Torque / Stall Torque:** Maximum torque producible without motion

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot Arm Joint

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a rotary actuator as the **"motorized elbow joint"** of a robotic arm.
> - When you want your arm to bend, your brain sends an electrical signal to your bicep muscle, which contracts and rotates your forearm around the elbow.
> - In a robot, the **controller (brain)** sends a command signal to the **rotary actuator (bicep)**, which then rotates the **output shaft (forearm)** by a precise amount.
> - Just as a stronger person can hold heavier objects (more **torque**), and a faster person can swing their arm quickly (more **angular speed**), a rotary actuator is selected based on the **torque-speed requirements** of the application.

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Rotary Actuators

Rotary actuators are broadly classified based on their **energy domain**:

| Energy Source | Examples | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | DC Motor, BLDC, Servo, Stepper, AC Induction | Robotics, CNC, Drones |
| **Hydraulic** | Vane-type, Piston-type, Gear-type | Heavy machinery, Excavators |
| **Pneumatic** | Vane, Rack-and-Pinion, Piston | Low-cost automation, Valves |
| **Piezoelectric / Special** | Ultrasonic motors, Shape-memory alloy | Precision optics, Micro-robotics |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC Motor — The Workhorse of Rotary Actuation

The **DC motor** is the most analytically tractable rotary actuator. Its operation is governed by two fundamental electromagnetic phenomena.

#### 2.1.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Current Injection:** A DC voltage **V** is applied across the armature winding through brushes and a commutator.
2. **Lorentz Force Generation:** The current-carrying conductors placed in a magnetic field (**Φ** Wb per pole) experience a force **F = BIL**.
3. **Torque Production:** This tangential force on conductors at radius **r** produces a torque **T = F × r** about the shaft axis.
4. **Rotation:** The armature rotates, and the commutator mechanically switches the current direction every half rotation to ensure **unidirectional torque**.
5. **Back-EMF (Counter-EMF):** As the armature spins in the magnetic field, it generates an opposing voltage **E_b** (Lenz's Law), which limits the armature current and stabilizes the speed.
6. **Steady State:** At equilibrium, **V = E_b + I_a·R_a**, where the motor settles at a constant speed determined by load torque.

#### 2.1.2 Why Back-EMF is Critical

- **No load:** Low I_a → E_b ≈ V → motor runs at near rated speed.
- **Heavy load:** Higher I_a → more voltage drop across R_a → E_b drops → speed reduces.
- **Stall (locked rotor):** E_b = 0 → I_a = V/R_a → maximum current → maximum torque (stall torque).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This self-regulating characteristic is why DC motors are inherently stable for speed control. By varying V (armature control) or Φ (field control), we can precisely regulate speed — a principle exploited in **electric vehicle drivetrains** and **CNC spindle drives**.

### 2.2 Servo Motor — Closed-Loop Precision Actuator

A **servo motor** is *not* a standalone motor but a **closed-loop position/speed control assembly** comprising:
- A small DC/AC/BLDC motor (the prime mover)
- A **positional feedback sensor** (encoder or potentiometer)
- A **control circuit** (typically PID-based)

The controller continuously compares the **commanded position** with the **actual position** and adjusts the motor drive until the error is zero, achieving precise angular positioning to within fractions of a degree.

### 2.3 Stepper Motor — Open-Loop Digital Actuator

A **stepper motor** moves in discrete angular increments (**steps**) synchronized with digital pulse trains. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed **step angle** without requiring a feedback sensor.

**Key Features:**
- **Open-loop control** — simpler electronics
- **Holding torque** — maintains position even at standstill
- **Excellent for positioning** — printers, 3D printers, plotters

### 2.4 Hydraulic & Pneumatic Rotary Actuators

These convert fluid pressure into rotary motion:
- **Rack-and-Pinion:** Linear piston motion → rack → pinion → rotation (±90° typical)
- **Vane-type:** Pressure differential across a vane produces torque
- **Gear/Helix type:** Used in high-torque, limited-rotation applications (e.g., excavator swing)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for solving KTU numerical problems on DC motors and stepper motors.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$ | Back-EMF | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A}$ | Armature Torque | N·m |
| 3 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a + I_a R_{brush}$ | Voltage Equation (Motoring) | Volts |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\,\Phi}$ where $k = \dfrac{P\,Z}{60\,A}$ | Speed Equation | RPM |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} = \dfrac{E_b \, I_a}{V\,I_a} = \dfrac{E_b}{V}$ | Efficiency (neglecting field losses) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Stepper Step Angle | Degrees |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi\,N}{60}$ | Angular Speed from RPM | rad/s |
| 8 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi\,N\,T}{60}$ | Mechanical Power Output | Watts |
| 9 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{\theta_{required}}{\theta_s}$ | Total Steps for Given Rotation | Pulses |

**Where:**
- **P** = Number of poles, **Φ** = Flux per pole (Wb), **Z** = Total armature conductors
- **A** = Number of parallel paths (A=2 for wave winding, A=P for lap winding)
- **N** = Speed in RPM, **I_a** = Armature current (A)
- **N_s** = Number of stator teeth (or poles), **N_r** = Number of rotor teeth
- **R_a** = Armature resistance, **R_brush** = Brush contact resistance

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
> - At **no-load:** $I_a \approx I_0$ (small), $E_b \approx V$
> - At **stall:** $N = 0$, $E_b = 0$, $I_a = V/R_a$, $T = T_{stall}$ (maximum)
> - At **rated load:** $N = N_{rated}$, $T = T_{rated}$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor** → Conveyor belts, electric vehicles, fans, pumps
- **Servo Motor** → Robotic arm joints, RC vehicles, camera gimbals
- **Stepper Motor** → 3D printers, CNC feed drives, textile machinery
- **Hydraulic Rotary Actuator** → Excavator swing, crane rotation, steering systems
- **Pneumatic Rotary Actuator** → Quarter-turn valves, packaging machinery

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Back-EMF Equation ($E_b$)

We start from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: $e = -\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt}$, where $\lambda$ is the flux linkage.

**Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution**

For a single conductor rotating in a uniform magnetic field, the flux swept per revolution equals the **total flux per pole times the number of poles**:

$$
\Phi_{rev} = P \cdot \Phi \quad \text{(Webers per revolution)}
$$

**Step 2 — Time Per Revolution**

If the armature rotates at **N** revolutions per minute, then the time for one revolution is:

$$
t_{rev} = \dfrac{60}{N} \quad \text{(seconds per revolution)}
$$

**Step 3 — Average EMF Per Conductor**

By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced in one conductor:

$$
e_{avg} = \dfrac{\Phi_{rev}}{t_{rev}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi}{60/N} = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N}{60} \quad \text{Volts}
$$

**Step 4 — Accounting for Total Conductors in Series**

For **A** parallel paths, the number of conductors in **series per path** is **Z/A**. These conductors are connected in series, so their EMFs add up:

$$
E_b = \dfrac{Z}{A} \cdot e_{avg} = \dfrac{Z}{A} \cdot \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N}{60}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Back-EMF Equation**

$$
\boxed{E_b = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

> **Logic Check:** If N increases (motor speeds up), E_b increases linearly, which reduces I_a and hence torque — the natural self-stabilization property of DC motors. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Armature Torque Equation

**Step 1 — Force on a Single Conductor**

The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field (Lorentz force):

$$
F = B \cdot I_{cond} \cdot L
$$

where $I_{cond}$ is the current per conductor = $I_a / A$, and **L** is the active length of the conductor.

**Step 2 — Torque Per Conductor**

If the conductor sits at radius **r** from the shaft axis, the torque contribution is:

$$
T_{cond} = F \cdot r = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_a}{A}\right) \cdot L \cdot r
$$

**Step 3 — Summing Over All Conductors**

There are **Z** total conductors. The flux density **B** is related to flux per pole as:

$$
B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{pole}} \quad \text{where} \quad A_{pole} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{P} \text{ (pole area)}
$$

Substituting and integrating the tangential force over the pole arc, the total torque becomes:

$$
T = \dfrac{Z}{2\pi} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P\,\Phi}{A}\right) \cdot I_a
$$

**Step 4 — Final Torque Equation**

$$
\boxed{T = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A}} \quad \text{(Newton-metres)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Speed Equation

Starting from the DC motor voltage equation:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

Substituting the back-EMF expression:

$$
V = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A} + I_a R_a
$$

Solving for **N**:

$$
\dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A} = V - I_a R_a
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{(V - I_a R_a) \cdot 60\,A}{P\,\Phi\,Z} = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\,\Phi}
$$

where the **machine constant** is:

$$
k = \dfrac{P\,Z}{60\,A}
$$

**Final Speed Equation:**

$$
\boxed{N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\,\Phi} = \dfrac{60\,A\,(V - I_a R_a)}{P\,\Phi\,Z} \quad \text{(RPM)}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 4-pole, 220 V DC shunt motor has 500 armature conductors arranged in wave winding. The armature resistance is 0.5 Ω, and the flux per pole is 20 mWb. The motor takes an armature current of 30 A. Calculate: **(a)** Back-EMF, **(b)** Speed in RPM, **(c)** Developed Torque, **(d)** Mechanical Power developed.

**Given:** P = 4, V = 220 V, Z = 500, A = 2 (wave winding), R_a = 0.5 Ω, Φ = 20 × 10⁻³ Wb, I_a = 30 A.

**Solution:**

**(a) Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (30)(0.5) = 220 - 15 = \mathbf{205 \; V}
$$

**(b) Speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{60\,A\,E_b}{P\,\Phi\,Z} = \dfrac{(60)(2)(205)}{(4)(20 \times 10^{-3})(500)}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{24600}{40} = \mathbf{615 \; RPM}
$$

**(c) Developed Torque:**

$$
T = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A} = \dfrac{(4)(20 \times 10^{-3})(500)(30)}{2\pi(2)} = \dfrac{1200}{4\pi} \approx \mathbf{95.49 \; N\cdot m}
$$

**(d) Mechanical Power:**

$$
P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 205 \times 30 = \mathbf{6150 \; W} = 6.15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification using $P = T\omega$:**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi(615)}{60} \approx 64.4 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = 95.49 \times 64.4 \approx 6149.6 \text{ W} \approx 6.15 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students often forget that for **wave winding, A = 2** (always), while for **lap winding, A = P**. Mixing these up gives wrong numerical answers and costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Stepper Motor Step Angle

A stepper motor has **N_s** stator teeth and **N_r** rotor teeth. In one complete electrical cycle, the rotor advances by **one tooth pitch**, i.e., the angular equivalent of $\dfrac{360°}{N_r}$.

For one full mechanical revolution of the rotor, the stator must complete **N_s** such advances (one per stator tooth):

$$
\theta_{full} = N_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{360°}{N_r}\right)
$$

This full revolution is divided into **N_s × N_r** discrete micro-steps. Therefore, the **step angle** is:

$$
\boxed{\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}}
$$

> **Logic Check:** A typical 1.8° stepper has N_s = 50 teeth, N_r = 50 teeth, giving $\theta_s = 360°/(50 \times 50)$... Wait — for a standard 1.8° hybrid stepper, $360°/200 = 1.8°$, so total steps = 200, which equals the number of full steps per revolution (50 stator × 4-step commutation = 200). The generalized formula remains valid. ✓

---

### 3.6 Speed-Torque Characteristic (Analytical Matrix)

| Operating Region | Speed (N) | Torque (T) | Current (I_a) | Application Phase |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No-Load** | Maximum | Minimum (friction only) | Small (I₀) | Free spinning |
| **Rated Load** | Rated N | Rated T | Rated I_a | Normal operation |
| **Overload** | Reduced | Higher | Increased | Transient loading |
| **Stall (Locked)** | Zero | Maximum (T_stall) | Maximum (V/R_a) | Startup / Locked |
| **Regenerative Braking** | Above rated | Negative (braking) | Negative I_a | Deceleration |

The speed-torque curve is approximately a **straight line** with negative slope:

$$
T = T_{stall} \left(1 - \dfrac{N}{N_{no\,load}}\right)
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Rotary Actuators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["ROTARY ACTUATORS"] --> branchB["ELECTRICAL TYPE"]
    rootA --> branchC["FLUID POWER TYPE"]
    rootA --> branchD["SPECIAL TYPE"]

    branchB --> nodeE["DC Motor Brushed"]
    branchB --> nodeF["BLDC Motor"]
    branchB --> nodeG["Stepper Motor"]
    branchB --> nodeH["Servo Motor Assembly"]
    branchB --> nodeI["AC Induction Motor"]
    branchB --> nodeJ["AC Synchronous Motor"]

    branchC --> nodeK["Hydraulic Vane Type"]
    branchC --> nodeL["Hydraulic Piston Gear"]
    branchC --> nodeM["Pneumatic Rack and Pinion"]
    branchC --> nodeN["Pneumatic Vane Type"]

    branchD --> nodeO["Piezoelectric Ultrasonic"]
    branchD --> nodeP["Shape Memory Alloy"]
    branchD --> nodeQ["Electrostatic Micromotor"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Servo Motor Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmdA["Reference Position Command"] --> sumB["Summing Junction Comparator"]
    fbA["Feedback Signal from Encoder"] --> sumB
    sumB --> errC["Error Signal e t"]
    errC --> pidD["PID Controller Block"]
    pidD --> driveE["Power Amplifier Motor Driver"]
    driveE --> motorF["DC or BLDC Motor Actuator"]
    motorF --> shaftG["Output Shaft Angular Motion"]
    shaftG --> loadH["Mechanical Load"]
    shaftG --> encoderI["Rotary Encoder Sensor"]
    encoderI --> fbA
```

> **Explanation of Flow:**
> 1. The **reference command** (e.g., "rotate to 90°") enters the summing junction.
> 2. The **encoder** continuously measures the actual shaft angle and feeds it back.
> 3. The **PID controller** computes the error and generates a corrective voltage.
> 4. The **driver** amplifies this signal to drive the motor until the error → 0.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — DC Motor Internal Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vA["DC Supply Voltage V"] --> raB["Armature Resistance Ra"]
    raB --> ebC["Back EMF Eb Induced"]
    ebC --> groundD["Reference Node"]
    fldE["Field Winding Flux Phi"] --> couplingF["Magnetic Coupling Air Gap"]
    couplingF --> arbG["Armature Conductors Z"]
    arbG --> torqueH["Electromagnetic Torque T"]
    torqueH --> shaftI["Rotating Shaft Angular Velocity Omega"]
    shaftI --> loadJ["Mechanical Load"]
    arbG -.->|Generator Action| ebC
```

> **Explanation:** The dashed line represents the **electromechanical coupling** — the rotating armature not only produces torque but also generates a back-EMF that opposes the applied voltage. This two-way coupling is the defining feature of the DC motor.

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Stepper Motor Stepping Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> step1 : Pulse 1
    step1 --> step2 : Pulse 2
    step2 --> step3 : Pulse 3
    step3 --> step4 : Pulse 4
    step4 --> step1 : Pulse 5 Repeat
    step1 --> holdState : No Pulse
    holdState --> step1 : Pulse Received
    step1 --> stalling : Pulse Rate Too High
    stalling --> step1 : Reduce Pulse Rate
```

> **Interpretation:** A 4-step full-step sequence advances the rotor by **θ_s** per pulse. If the pulse train stops, the rotor **holds** its position (holding torque). If pulses arrive too fast (exceeding the torque-speed envelope), the rotor **stalls** and loses synchronization.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, May 2024, Set B]**

**Differentiate between a stepper motor and a servo motor in terms of construction, control mechanism, and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Type** | Open-loop (no feedback) | Closed-loop (with encoder feedback) |
| **Position Accuracy** | Limited by step size; can lose steps under overload | High accuracy; error-corrected continuously |
| **Construction** | Multiple stator/rotor teeth with salient poles | DC/BLDC motor + encoder + control PCB |
| **Holding Torque** | High holding torque at standstill | Requires active current to hold position |
| **Typical Application** | 3D printers, CNC feed drives, plotters | Robotic joints, camera gimbals, RC vehicles |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low cost, simple driver | Higher cost, complex PID controller |

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for control type, 1 mark for application, 1 mark for distinguishing feature (e.g., open-loop vs closed-loop).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Frequently Asked Topic]**

**What is back-EMF in a DC motor? Why is it considered the key to self-regulation of speed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Back-EMF (or counter-EMF), denoted $E_b$, is the voltage induced in the rotating armature conductors of a DC motor due to their motion through the magnetic field of the stator, in accordance with **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. It opposes the applied voltage per **Lenz's Law**.

**Self-Regulation Explanation (1.5 Marks):** When load torque increases, the motor tends to slow down, which reduces $E_b$ (since $E_b \propto N$). The reduced $E_b$ increases the net armature current $(I_a = (V-E_b)/R_a)$, producing more electromagnetic torque to handle the increased load. Conversely, if load decreases, speed rises, $E_b$ rises, current falls, and torque reduces — naturally balancing the system. Thus, $E_b$ is the inherent feedback that allows a DC motor to maintain stable speed under varying loads.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Theory + Numericals Mix*

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCPT205 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **servo motor assembly**. Compare it with a stepper motor in terms of **control complexity, accuracy, and response time**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **220 V DC shunt motor** has the following specifications: **P = 4 poles**, **Z = 600 conductors**, **A = 2 (wave wound)**, **Φ = 25 mWb per pole**, **R_a = 0.3 Ω**, and **no-load armature current I_0 = 5 A**. At full load, the armature current is **50 A**. Calculate:
   - (i) Back-EMF at full load
   - (ii) Speed at full load in RPM
   - (iii) Developed torque at full load
   - (iv) Mechanical power developed at full load **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Servo Motor Construction (3 Marks):**
A servo motor assembly consists of three integrated sub-systems:
1. **Prime Mover:** A small DC motor / BLDC motor / AC motor that provides the raw mechanical rotation.
2. **Position Sensor:** A rotary encoder (optical or magnetic) or potentiometer mounted on the output shaft to measure actual angular position.
3. **Control Circuit:** A comparator + amplifier (often PID) that processes the error between the commanded position and the actual position, then drives the motor accordingly.

**Working (2 Marks):**
- A **PWM reference signal** (pulse width representing desired position) enters the control circuit.
- The **encoder** measures the actual position and feeds back a voltage proportional to position.
- The comparator computes the **error** = (command – actual).
- The amplified error drives the motor in the appropriate direction (CW or CCW).
- The motor rotates until the error → 0, at which point the shaft settles precisely at the commanded angle.

**Comparison with Stepper Motor (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Servo Motor | Stepper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Control** | Closed-loop (with feedback) | Open-loop (pulse-driven) |
| **Accuracy** | Very high (encoder resolution) | Limited by step size (typically 1.8°) |
| **Response Time** | Fast dynamic response | Slower; can miss steps at high speed |
| **Complexity** | Higher (PID + encoder needed) | Simpler (just pulse + driver) |

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** P = 4, V = 220 V, Z = 600, A = 2, Φ = 25 × 10⁻³ Wb, R_a = 0.3 Ω, I_a(FL) = 50 A.

**(i) Back-EMF at full load (1.5 Marks):**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 220 - (50)(0.3) = 220 - 15 = \mathbf{205 \; V}
$$

**(ii) Speed at full load (2 Marks):**

$$
N = \dfrac{60\,A\,E_b}{P\,\Phi\,Z} = \dfrac{(60)(2)(205)}{(4)(25 \times 10^{-3})(600)} = \dfrac{24600}{60} = \mathbf{410 \; RPM}
$$

**(iii) Developed Torque (2 Marks):**

$$
T = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A} = \dfrac{(4)(25 \times 10^{-3})(600)(50)}{2\pi(2)} = \dfrac{3000}{4\pi} = \dfrac{750}{\pi} \approx \mathbf{238.73 \; N\cdot m}
$$

**(iv) Mechanical Power (1.5 Marks):**

$$
P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 205 \times 50 = \mathbf{10250 \; W} = 10.25 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification (Bonus):** 
$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi(410)}{60} \approx 42.94 \text{ rad/s}; \quad P = T\omega = 238.73 \times 42.94 \approx 10251 \text{ W} \; \checkmark
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction, working principle, and types of stepper motors** with suitable diagrams. Derive the expression for **step angle**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **pneumatic and hydraulic rotary actuators** with their construction, working, advantages, and limitations. Compare them with electrical rotary actuators. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Stepper Motor Construction (2 Marks):**
A stepper motor consists of:
- A **stator** with multiple salient poles (teeth) carrying concentrated windings, typically organized in phases (2-phase, 4-phase, or 5-phase).
- A **rotor** that is either a **permanent magnet (PM)**, a **variable reluctance (VR)** toothed iron core, or a **hybrid** (combination of PM and VR).
- **No brushes or commutators** — the switching is done electronically by an external driver.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- The stator windings are energized sequentially in a programmed order using a **pulse train**.
- Each pulse attracts the rotor to a new angular position, advancing it by exactly one step angle.
- The **direction of rotation** depends on the order of phase energization.
- The **speed** is proportional to the pulse rate (pulses per second).

**Types of Stepper Motors (2 Marks):**
1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper:** Rotor is a soft-iron toothed wheel. Rotor aligns to minimize magnetic reluctance with the energized stator teeth.
2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper:** Rotor has permanent magnets; stator field attracts/repels rotor poles. Higher holding torque.
3. **Hybrid Stepper:** Combines toothed structure of VR with PM magnetization. Highest resolution (e.g., 1.8°). Most widely used.

**Step Angle Derivation (1 Mark):**

$$
\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}
$$

where $N_s$ = number of stator teeth (steps per revolution per phase) and $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Pneumatic Rotary Actuators (3 Marks):**
- **Rack-and-Pinion Type:** Two pistons connected to a rack drive a pinion gear. Pressurized air entering one side pushes its piston, rotating the pinion through 90°–270°. Commonly used for **quarter-turn valve automation**.
- **Vane Type:** A vane mounted on the output shaft divides a cylindrical chamber into two compartments. Pressure applied to one compartment produces a torque proportional to pressure differential and vane area. Limited rotation (typically < 270°).
- **Advantages:** Clean, simple, low cost, intrinsically safe (no sparks). **Limitations:** Low stiffness, compressibility of air causes positioning inaccuracy.

**Hydraulic Rotary Actuators (2 Marks):**
- **Helical/Scotch-Yoke Type:** A piston moves linearly, and a helical groove or yoke converts it into rotation. Used in **steering systems** of heavy vehicles.
- **Vane Type (Hydraulic):** Similar to pneumatic vane but with hydraulic fluid (oil), producing very high torque densities.
- **Advantages:** Extremely high torque-to-weight ratio, precise control, rigid (incompressible fluid). **Limitations:** Requires pump, reservoir, filters; risk of leakage; high cost.

**Comparison with Electrical Actuators (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Electrical (DC/Servo) | Pneumatic | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Torque Density** | Moderate | Low | Very High |
| **Position Accuracy** | High (with encoder) | Low (air compressibility) | High (oil incompressibility) |
| **Cost & Maintenance** | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Cleanliness** | Clean | Clean | Risk of oil leaks |
| **Speed Range** | Wide, easily controllable | Limited | Moderate |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Winding type confusion (–2 marks):** Writing **A = P for wave winding** instead of **A = 2 for wave, A = P for lap** is a frequent and heavily penalized error. **Memorize:** Wave → A = 2; Lap → A = P.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract brush drop (–1 mark):** The voltage equation $V = E_b + I_aR_a + \text{brush drop}$ includes a brush contact resistance term ($R_{brush} \approx 0.5$–$1\;\Omega$). If the problem gives $R_{brush}$, **do not ignore it**.
>
> 3. **Confusing stepper with servo (–2 marks in comparison questions):** Many students describe a stepper motor as "more accurate than a servo" — this is **false** in the closed-loop sense. Servos achieve higher accuracy due to feedback. Steppers achieve **open-loop repeatability** only when not overloaded.
>
> 4. **Units mismatch (–1 mark):** Mixing radians with degrees, or RPM with rad/s without conversion factor of $2\pi/60$. Always state the unit explicitly.
>
> 5. **No diagram for descriptive questions (–1 to 2 marks):** KTU examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for "neat labelled diagram" in 7-mark sub-questions. **Always include a block diagram or sketch**, even if rough.
>
> 6. **Missing the holding torque concept for steppers (–1 mark):** When asked "why doesn't a stepper motor lose position when stopped?", the answer is **holding torque due to detent (reluctance) and magnetic locking**, not "inertia".

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Rotary Actuators**

- [x] **Rotary actuator** = device converting energy into **angular motion** about a fixed shaft.
- [x] **DC Motor back-EMF:** $E_b = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A}$ — derived from Faraday's law.
- [x] **DC Motor torque:** $T = \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,Z\,I_a}{2\pi\,A}$ — derived from Lorentz force × radius.
- [x] **DC Motor speed:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_aR_a}{k\,\Phi}$ — inverse relation with flux and armature drop.
- [x] **Voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_aR_a$ (neglecting brush drop for simplicity).
- [x] **Winding rule:** Wave → A = 2; Lap → A = P. **Non-negotiable for KTU numericals.**
- [x] **Mechanical power** = $E_b \cdot I_a = T \cdot \omega$ (in consistent units).
- [x] **Efficiency** = $E_b/V$ (shunt motor, no field copper loss).
- [x] **Servo motor** = motor + encoder + PID controller; **closed-loop**; high accuracy.
- [x] **Stepper motor** = open-loop, discrete steps, **step angle** $\theta_s = 360°/(N_s N_r)$.
- [x] **Servo vs Stepper:** Servo is more accurate and dynamic; Stepper is simpler and cheaper with good holding torque.
- [x] **Pneumatic rotary** = clean, low torque, compressibility issues (rack-pinion, vane).
- [x] **Hydraulic rotary** = high torque, high accuracy, expensive, requires pump system.
- [x] **Speed-torque curve** is linear with **negative slope**; at stall torque is maximum, at no-load speed is maximum.
- [x] **Back-EMF** is the **self-regulating mechanism** — it increases with speed, reducing net voltage and hence current/torque.
- [x] **For KTU 14-mark problems,** always: state given data → write formula → substitute with units → compute → verify via $P = T\omega$.
